THE
PARLIAMENTARY DEBATES
(OFFICIAL REPORT)
IN THE
FIRST SESSION OF THE FOURTH PARLIAMENT OF NORTHERN IRELAND
APPOINTED TO MEET THE EIGHTEENTH DAY OF DECEMBER, IN THE
TWENTY-FOURTH YEAR OF THE REIGN OF

MAJESTY

VOLUME XVI.
THE SENATE.

---

Monday, 18th December, 1933.
The First Session of the Fourth Parliament of Northern Ireland, appointed to
meet on 18th December, 1933, in the
twenty-fourth year of the Reign of King
George V, was opened by the Governor
(His Grace the Duke of Abercorn, K.G.,
K.P.) in person.
The House met at half-past Eleven of
the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER (Right Hon.
the Viscount Bangor, O.B.E., D.L.) in
the Chair.
His Grace the Governor of Northern
Ireland, being seated on the Throne, commanded the Gentleman Usher of the Black
Rod to let the Commons know "It is His
Grace's pleasure they attend him immediately in this House," who being come,
The Clerk read the Proclamation dissolving Parliament as followeth :
WHEREAS it is provided by section
eleven of the Government of Ireland Act,
1920, as modified by and in pursuance of
certain enactments amending the said Act,
that the Governor of Northern Ireland
shall, in his Majesty's name, summon,
prorogue and dissolve the Parliament of
Northern Ireland.
AND WHEREAS it is desirable that
the present Parliament of Northern Ireland, which stands prorogued to Tuesday,
the fifth day of December next, should be
dissolved and that a new Parliament
should be called.
NOW, THEREFORE, I, JAMES
ALBERT EDWARD, DUKE OF ABER-
CORN, Knight of the Most Noble Order
of the Garter, Knight of the Most Illustrious Order of Saint Patrick, Governor
of Northern Ireland, by this Proclamation,
do publish and declare his Majesty's
pleasure that the said Parliament be as
from the date of this Proclamation dissolved, and that the Senators and Members
of the House of Commons be discharged
from their meeting and attendance on the
said Tuesday, the fifth day of December
next.
AND I do hereby make known to all
his Majesty's loving subjects his Royal
will and pleasure to call a new Parliament
of Northern Ireland, and do further
declare that I have taken order that writs
shall be issued forthwith in his Majesty's
name under the Great Seal of Northern
Ireland, in due form and according to law,
for calling a Parliament of Northern
Ireland to meet at Stormont, Northern
Ireland, on Monday, the eighteenth day
of December next, and for causing the
Senators and Commons who are to serve
in the said Parliament to be duly returned
to and give their attendance to his
Majesty's said Parliament on Monday,
the eighteenth day of December next,
which writs are to be returnable in due
course of law.
Dated this 10th day of December in the
year of our Lord One Thousand Nine
Hundred and Thirty-three.
ABERCORN.
GOD SAVE THE KING.
A
Speaker of the Commons
Members Sworn
THE SENATE
His GRACE was pleased to speak as
follows:
Members of the Senate and of the House
of Commons of Northern Ireland, In the
name and on behalf of his Majesty I have
to let you know that, as soon as the Members of both Houses shall have been sworn,
the causes of his Majesty's calling this
Parliament of Northern Ireland will be
declared to you; and, it being necessary
that a Speaker of the House of Commons
of Northern Ireland should be first chosen,
I declare his Majesty's pleasure that you,
Members of the House of Commons, do
proceed to the choice of a Speaker, and
that you present such person whom you
shall so choose here for his Majesty's
Royal approbation.
Then the Commons withdrew.
House adjourned during pleasure.
House resumed.
Speaker of the House of Commons.
His Grace the Governor, being seated on
the Throne, again commanded the Gentleman Usher of the Black Rod to let the
Commons know "It is His Grace's pleasure
they attend him immediately in this
House," who being come,
The SPEAKER-ELECT OF THE
HOUSE OF COMMONS (The Hon.
Henry George Hill Mulholland) said:
Your Grace, I have to acquaint you that
in obedience to His Majesty's commands His Majesty's most faithful
Commons of Northern Ireland have, in
the exercise of their undoubted rights and
privileges, proceeded to the election of a
Speaker, and that their choice has fallen
upon myself. I now present myself to
your Grace, and submit myself with all
humility for His Majesty's gracious approbation.
His GRACE: Mr. Mulholland, being
sensible of your zeal in the public service, and of your ample sufficiency to
execute all the important duties of the
position which his faithful Commons of
Northern Ireland have selected you to
discharge, I do, on His Majesty's behalf,
most readily approve and confirm you as
their Speaker.
The SPEAKER OF THE HOUSE OF
COMMONS: Your Grace, I submit myself with all humility and gratitude to
His Majesty's gracious commands.
It
is now my duty in the name and on behalf
of the Commons of Northern Ireland to
lay claim by humble petition to the rights,
privileges, and immunities assured to the
Commons of Northern Ireland by the
Government of Ireland Act, 1920, and
that the most favourable construction
shall be put on all their proceedings.
In regard to myself, I pray that, if in
the discharge of my duties I should inadvertently fall into any error, it may be
imputed to myself alone, and not to His
Majesty's most faithful Commons of
Northern Ireland.
His GRACE: Mr. Speaker of the
Commons of Northern Ireland, in the
name of His Majesty I do most readily
confirm the rights,
rights, privileges, and
immunities to which you have laid claim.
With respect to yourself, Sir, although
His Majesty is sensible that you stand
in need of no such assurance, His
Majesty will ever put the most favourable construction on your words and
actions.
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons, it will now be the
duty of Members of both Houses of
Parliament of Northern Ireland to take
the required oath.
To-day, at half-past Three o'clock, the
causes of His Majesty's calling this
Parliament of Northern Ireland will be
declared to you.
Then the Commons withdrew.
Members Sworn.
The Senators following took and Subscribed the Oath, or made and subscribed
the Affirmation required by law and
signed the Roll.

18 DECEMBER 1933

KING'S SPEECH.
His Grace the Governor, being seated
on the Throne, commanded the Gentleman Usher of the Black Rod to let the
Commons know "It is His Grace's
pleasure that they attend him immediately in this House."
The Commons being at the Bar with
their Speaker.
His Grace delivered the following
Speech from the Throne:-
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
I feel confident that the deliberations
of this, the Fourth Parliament of
Northern Ireland, now being inaugurated, will be conducted with the care,
wisdom and moderation which have in
past years characterized the work of
both Houses.
I am gratified to learn that a substantial increase in the volume of
employment has taken place during the
current year and that the number of
persons registering for work has fallen
by about one-fifth, the improvement
being particularly marked in our staple
industries of shipbuilding and linen
manufacture. There are indications of
still further improvement, so I am
not without hope that the progress
already made towards general industrial
recovery will not only be maintained,
but accelerated. In the course of
twelve months about 24,000 persons
have been assisted in securing employment through the medium of our
Employment Exchanges.
The identity of our economic and industrial interests with those of the rest
of the United Kingdom has been demonstrated very clearly during the past
few months by the figures for trade and
employment both in Great Britain and
in Northern Ireland, which coincide to
a remarkable degree. It is evident that
Northern Ireland is sharing fully the
advantage of all those far-reaching
changes which the British Government
have initiated in the sphere of Imperial
and International relationships. My
Government will continue to give sympathetic and unremitting attention to
all practicable measures for the development of the resources of the country
and will use every available means of
stimulating the demand for the agricultural and industrial products of our
area.
You will be invited to give consideration to a comprehensive measure designed to place Unemployment Insurance
on a sound and permanent footing and
to establish a new system for the ablebodied unemployed who are outside
insurance. By these proposals the social
standards of our unemployed workpeople will be maintained equal in every
way to those enjoyed by their fellowcountrymen elsewhere in the United
Kingdom.
Under the Agricultural Marketing
Act schemes are being prepared by the
Ministry of Agriculture dealing with
the Marketing of Milk and Potatoes.
It was found impracticable last Session
to consider proposals for the reform of
certain aspects of Local Government
Administration; a Bill dealing with
this matter will be laid before you.
our
The number of accidents on
roads, resulting in loss of life and injury
to persons and property is causing my
Government much concern; you will be
invited to give consideration to a Bill
designed with the object of remedying
this state of affairs.
A measure will also be laid before
you dealing with the qualifying period
of residence governing the registration
of voters in Northern Ireland.
Other Bills will be introduced regarding the following matters:-
Agricultural Marketing.
Destructive Insects and Pests.
Charitable Donations and
Bequests.
King's Speech

THE SENATE
King's Speech
Fisheries.
Railways.
Donaghadee Harbour.
Printers.
Coroners.
Members of the House of Commons.
The estimates for the public services
will be laid before you in due course.
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
I am gratified to learn that law and
order continue to be effectually maintained, and I am sure that the steps
taken by the authorities to this end
have the whole-hearted support of all
law-abiding citizens.
The policy of my Government in
aiming at a prolonged period freed, as
far as possible, from contentious legislation during which the business
community and our unrivalled agriculturists and artisans may pursue their
respective vocations meets with my
highest commendation.
Outstanding progress has been made
with the work of electrification, and
much encouragement can be derived
from the fact that not only rural communities, but also outlying industrial
establishments, are readily taking
advantage of the cheap power rendered
available to them by this method.
The educational system continues to
develop satisfactorily, and notwithstanding the difficulties occasioned by
the present strain on public finance,
substantial progress has been made in
the replacement of unsuitable buildings
by modern structures, with resulting
improvement in the well-being of both
scholars and teachers.
It is gratifying to learn of the marked
success which continues to attend the
skilfully organised voluntary efforts to
stimulate the demand within Northern
Ireland for goods produced by manufacturers at home and within the
Empire, and also the
measures
instituted to attract tourists to our
shores, and thus to turn to account
in the interest of our people generally
those natural assets with which our
country has been so generously endowed.
And I pray that the blessing of
Almighty God may rest upon your
deliberations.
Then His Grace was pleased to retire,
and the Commons withdrew.
House adjourned during pleasure.
House resumed.

Members Sworn.
The Senators following took and Subscribed the Oath, or made and subscribed
the Affirmation required by law and
signed the Roll.
The Right Hon. Viscount Charlemont,
V.L.
Thomas Stanislaus McAllister, Esquire.
Sir George Smith Clark, Bart., D.L.
John Porter Porter, Esquire, D.L.
Thomas McLaughlin, Esquire.
Debate on Address.
Mr. SPEAKER having read the
King's Speech,
The MAYOR OF LONDONDERRY
(Sir Dudley McCorkell) (in Morning
Dress): I beg to move
"That an humble Address be presented
to His Grace in the following words:
Your Grace,
We, His Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal subjects, the Senate of the Parliament of Northern Ireland, beg leave to
offer our humble thanks to your Grace
for the Speech which your Grace has
addressed to both Houses of Parliament."
In moving this resolution I am conscious
of the honour conferred upon me, and
the responsibility incurred by me, and I
would appeal to all Members of this
House to favour me with that kindly
consideration which has always been so
generously extended to the Mover of the
Address to the Throne.
The Gracious Speech makes mention of
many matters of moment, and foreshadows legislation both of great
importance and in many directions, but
I feel that what this House will welcome
most heartily is the indication that unemployment is on the wane, that the
future is bright with hope for a still
further increase in the activities of our
industries, and that we see passing away
that cloud of unemployment which to a
greater or lesser degree has hung over
our country for a considerable time. The
prospect of employment for 9,000 men in
our shipyards during the next few
months is a real and not an imaginary
sign of improving conditions. There is
something real and concrete in that, something we can see and believe in, and it
is a most welcome sign. The increased
activity in the linen trade is such as to
give cause for satisfaction.
The legislative measures mentioned in
the Gracious Speech will receive every
attention when they come before the
House; meanwhile, a brief mention of
several of these may not be out of place.
The Bill to be introduced to deal
with the appalling casualties due to
motor traffic is surely one which will be
welcomed in this House, where from time
to time the matter has been under discussion. Many remedies have been suggested,
but as yet it has not been possible to do
anything effective. Nevertheless, I cannot
help thinking that if the law as it stands.
were administered a little differently there
would appear to be under existing legislation opportunity for curtailing the list
of casualties if those responsible for the
administration of the law were to deal in
a
more appropriate manner than I am
afraid they do with cases of reckless driving. Legislation is undoubtedly necessary,
because something must be done; but with
the law as it is a great deal more could
be done to prevent the motor-car from
taking toll of life and limb of the ordinary
users of the road, whether they be
motorists or pedestrians.
Another point in the Gracious Speech
which will appeal to many people is the
intimation of the introduction of an
Agricultural Marketing Bill. Anything
which touches upon that great industry
and will benefit it is always welcome. That
industry is essential to the prosperity of
this Province, and it can never be too
strongly urged that urban and rural industries are so closely inter-dependent as
to be necessary to each other, and to the
community as a whole.
A Measure dealing with railways is one
of great public importance. The railway
companies employ large numbers of men,
and are custodians of much invested
money, a great part of which represents
the savings of comparatively poor people,
who more or less have been encouraged
to invest their savings in the prior
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Sir Dudley McCorkell]
charges of the railway companies. It is
desirable, therefore, that they should be
enabled to carry on their service to the
public on up-to-date lines, and freed
from such shackles of regulations as have
grown rusty, for there is no doubt that
the companies are sometimes hampered
by certain regulations which, I think, in
the light of modern practice would be
better removed.
Moreover, as pointed out in the
Gracious Speech, our economic and industrial interests are identical with those
of the rest of the United Kingdom, and
that being so it behoves us to equip our
part of the Kingdom with such traffic
facilities, both rail and road, as will
render good service when that return to
prosperity of which signs are not wanting-shall make itself fully felt.
There
is no doubt that we are emerging from
the bad times of unemployment to the
better times of more industrial activity,
and I think that one of the most essential things to have in this country is
proper transit, whether it is by rail or
road, both for passengers and goods.
There are many other points in the
Speech that could be touched upon, but
I will not further tax the patience of the
House, but will conclude by thanking
you, Mr. Speaker, and all right hon. and
hon. Members for their forbearance.
beg to move.
I
Colonel SINCLAIR (in military
uniform): I esteem it a high honour to
be asked to second the Address of Thanks
to His Grace the Governor for the
Gracious Speech from the Throne which
he has been pleased to deliver to both
Houses of Parliament to-day on the
occasion of the opening of the Fourth
Parliament of Northern Ireland.
Eleven years ago I enjoyed the
privilege of discharging the same honourable duty. We have to rejoice that the
circumstances in which we find ourselves
to-day are much more stable in regard to
the maintenance of law and order than
they were in 1922. In that year the
machinery of Government was not fully
established. Several public services were
not completely transferred, and many of
the officers of State were new and
untried. This state of affairs coincided
with crime and lawlessness on an unprecedented scale, and there was acute
public anxiety everywhere.
It speaks volumes in praise of our
Prime Minister and his Cabinet colleagues that calm, sagacious, and resolute
handling of many difficult situations.
during this interval of time places us in
the happier position to-day of feeling
more security and confidence in embarking upon our enterprises, and pursuing
our various occupations, than in the
troublous times in 1922. No finer
tribute to the wisdom and statesmanship
of Viscount Craigavon and his Ministers
could be rendered than their unopposed
re-election to office by a grateful and discriminating electorate in the Imperial
Province; surely a result without parallel
in any civilised country with representative government.
Let us hope that our Prime Minister,
now happily restored to health, may long
be spared to us to steer the ship of State
with his accustomed skill and fidelity.
The hon. Senator, the Mayor of Londonderry, has eloquently reviewed the details
outlined in the Gracious Speech in moving,
in appropriate language, the humble
Address of Thanks to His Excellency.
I desire to spare the Senate the tedium
of unnecessary repetition, but, with your
indulgence, I would refer to a few points.
We may take heart from the optimistic
allusions in the Gracious Speech to the
improvement in trade, especially in our
staple industries, and the consequent
twenty per cent. reduction in unemployment. May we not envisage a steady
enlargement of this prosperity, so that
the workers in other fields may be speedily
returned to duty, and thus to contentmen
and happiness. We shall welcome the
Measures for relieving the able-bodied
unemployed who are outside the scope of
insurance. Possibly agriculture,
greatest industry, may be indirectly benefited by this advance, as well as by other
advantages foreshadowed in the Gracious
Speech.
our
With regard to education, let me remark that economy may not in all circumstances be a virtue. I trust our exceedingly astute Chancellor of the Exchequer,
who deserves our thanks and congratulations for invariably balancing his Budget,
will not deem it an impertinence if I beg
of him not to withdraw or contract the
Government support to education, primary, secondary and university,
especially technical, so that the minds of
our young people may be fully occupied
and better equipped to enter on the fierce
and
I
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
18 DECEMBER 1933
competition in life. Is it too much to
say that I hope we are within sighting
distance of an addition of a year to the
school age? The noble Viscount, the
Leader of the Senate and Minister of
Education, may be trusted to urge his
claims on behalf of the Department he so
ably controls, and thus extend the beneficent influence which he wields for the
common good.
All will be grateful for the promised
Bill designed to reduce the dreadful holocaust of the roads from reckless driving of
motor vehicles. It is a pity that the
views of experts and the public are so
much at variance as to the best way of
regulating traffic. It looks like "So many
men, so many opinions." Much thought
and argument are inevitable before a
satisfactory solution is reached. In connection with this opportune Measure I
would earnestly plead for a really efficient
and generous safeguarding of casualty
hospital accommodation, and hospital
finance, lest these institutions become
over-run and exhausted by irresponsible
and selfish exploitation.
I am glad that a Fisheries Measure is
promised. This is of great importance if
fish are not to be well-nigh exterminated
in our waters by inadequate protection and
lack of co-ordination among the various
agencies concerned. Can we expect in this
new Parliament that fish, so important a
food of our people, may be protected and
multiplied by a Bill comparable to the
admirable Act for the protection of birds
passed in last session, which we owe to the
large humanity and earnest endeavours of
the hon. and learned Senator Campbell?
His Bill was a perfect model for the
Imperial House of Commons to follow,
and I can testify personally it was a powerful factor in securing the passing of the
Wild Birds Protection Act in Great
Britain, over which Imperial Members had
wrangled for years to no purpose.
It is to be regretted that no mention is
made in the Gracious Speech of the early
establishment of an air port and flying
facilities for passengers and mails in the
immediate vicinity of Belfast. It is desirable that the Imperial Province should
not be behindhand in advancing this
important service. I hope that the admirable speech in London recently of the hon.
Senator, Right Hon. the Lord Mayor of
Belfast, will be duly implemented in the
near future in this respect.
I have the utmost pleasure in seconding
the Address of Thanks to his Grace for
the Gracious Speech from the Throne.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House) I should like to congratulate
the mover and seconder of the Motion,
which is before the House, on the eloquent
manner in which they have performed
their tasks. The hon. Senator, the Mayor
of Londonderry, always commands the attention of this House, not only on account
of the fact that he is the representative
of the second city in Northern Ireland,
but also because we have learned to expect
from him shrewd and constructive contributions to our debates, in which he invariably brings to our help the outstanding
abilities which eminently qualify him for
the position he holds.
The hon. and gallant Senator who
seconded the Motion is one of our most
eminent citizens, and has been a member
of this House from its beginning.
Throughout that period he has on many
occasions shown by his learned speeches
his deep and sincere interest in all those
questions which affect the welfare of the
people and of the profession of which he
is such a distinguished representative.
trust that he will long continue to be
numbered with us, and that despite his
very many and varied other interests he
will on many future occasions give this
House the benefit of his wide experience.
(Hon. Senators: Hear, hear.) As is
perhaps natural, both speakers have referred to that portion of the Gracious
Speech which deals with the state of the
trade and industry of the Province. The
problem of unemployment is, unhappily,
still with us. I am sure the House, however, will share my gratification at the
improvement which has taken place in the
position as compared with a year ago. In
addressing the House a little over twelve
months ago I remarked that the depressing cloud of unemployment could only be
driven away by a genial trade wind; the
hoped-for wind has come, largely induced,
no doubt, by the financial and tariff
policies of the Imperial Government and
the legislation of our own Government.
Whilst it has not attained gale strength,
it has, at any rate, been of sufficient force
to produce such an improvement in industrial affairs that there are now, the House
will be glad to know, upwards of 15,000
fewer people registered for employment
than there were at the beginning of the
year. It is satisfactory, therefore, to
Debate on the Address

THE SENATE
Debate on the Address
Viscount Charlemont]
reflect that the approaching festive season
will be more cheerful for many of our
people because there are substantially more
bread-winners in employment than there
were last year.
Past experience obliges me to speak
with qualified optimism, but I may say
that the trade omens for the future are
more favourable than they have been for
some time, and I think there are
reasonable grounds for expecting that the
improved position of the past year will
be more than maintained in the year that
lies ahead.
The lot of unemployed persons who
fulfil certain statutory conditions continues to be ameliorated by the payment
of unemployment insurance benefits and
transitional payments, whilst work is
being created for many of them by the
promotion of grant-aided public utility
schemes.
for
Nearly 2,000 persons are at
the present moment directly employed on
these schemes who would otherwise be
unemployed, whilst a substantial amount
of consequential employment also results.
A Bill is at present before the Imperial
Parliament to amend the Unemployment
Insurance Acts, and to make provision
outside unemployment insurance
assisting and promoting the welfare of
able-bodied persons who are unemployed
and in need. A similar Bill will be submitted for the consideration of this
House during the present Session, in
continuation of the Government's
pressed policy of preserving the social
standards of those of our people who are
unable to find employment, on the same
level as those which prevail for their
fellow-countrymen elsewhere throughout
the United Kingdom, of which Northern
Ireland is an integral part.
ex-
In the field of agriculture vigorous
efforts are being made in practically all
countries to counter the effects of the
present depression brought about by the
heavy fall in prices which has occurred
in recent years owing to the large
quantities of surplus agricultural products thrown upon the market. I think
it may confidently be claimed that the
steps taken in this country with a view
to ameliorating the position of the agricultural community have been accompanied by a great measure of success.
For many years past the policy consistently pursued by the Government and
the Ministry of Agriculture of Northern
Ireland has been to bring about a great
improvement in the conditions of marketing agricultural produce, and also to
relieve farmers of burdens, such as rates
on agricultural land and buildings.
As a result of the legislation passed
by our Parliament the reputation of Northern Ireland produce on the British
market has never been higher, and our
producers have, therefore, been in a particularly favourable position to take
advantage of the recent changes in the
agricultural policy adopted by the
British Government, which have resulted
in a restriction of the quantities of
agricultural produce from abroad admitted to the British market.
exwith
the
conam
agree
Under the terms of the Agricultural
Marketing Act passed earlier this year
marketing schemes for pigs and bacon.
have already been put into force, and
the
despite
initial difficulties
in
connection
perienced
pigs marketing scheme, I
fident hon. Members will
that this scheme has already brought
about a very material improvement in
the whole position in regard to the
marketing of pigs in Northern Ireland,
and has enabled the producer to obtain
a price for his pigs more in accordance
with the costs he has incurred in their
production. It has also given the farmer
greater security by letting him know in
advance the price he will obtain. It is
intended that marketing schemes in connection with milk and potatoes will be
introduced in the near future, while,
subsequently, schemes dealing with the
marketing of eggs and poultry and fat
stock will be prepared.
It may confidently be hoped that the
adoption of the principles contained in
the Agricultural Marketing Act of 1933,
under which producers are given the
power to regulate the quantity of their
produce which shall be marketed and the
price at which it shall be sold will ultimately lead to a substantial improvement
in the position of the farming community;
but it is impossible to find a market for
inferior produce under any marketing
scheme and the foundation for the success
of any schemes framed under the Act
must, undoubtedly, rest upon the
improved quality of Northern Ireland.
agricultural produce brought about by
the marketing legislation in regard to
standards of quality already passed by
this Parliament in the years from 1922
onwards.
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
18 DECEMBER 1933
Of the measures to the introduction of
which the Gracious Speech refers, the
proposed Bill dealing with road safety is
one which will probably be of surpassing
interest to hon. Members. The mover
and seconder have referred to this
matter, which has indeed been exercising
the minds of all thinking people to an
increasing extent for the past decade.
For some time past the question of the
rapid development of motor transport,
and particularly the increase of goodscarrying vehicles, has caused considerable
concern. In the case of the classes of
vehicles referred to, there has been up to
the present no co-ordinate system of control or supervision, and undoubtedly
under present circumstances opportunities.
are afforded to unscrupulous owners to
place on the roads vehicles which by
reason of their defective construction or
condition are a danger to other users of
the roads.
At the same time, it has beome increasingly apparent that persons have
been employed in driving these vehicles,
and particularly heavy classes of vehicles,
who have not had sufficient training and,
by their inexperience and, possibly,
physical disability, are not capable of controlling these machines in traffic. It is
accordingly proposed to institute a system of
supervision and control over all classes of
goods-carrying vehicles with the object of
ensuring that the vehicles of this class
used on the roads are in proper mechanical
condition and that the drivers of them
are capable of controlling them properly.
of
The Bill, which is in course
preparation, will also contain other provisions dealing with traffic generally, and
designed with the object of effecting a
more complete system of supervision and
control and so reducing danger to road
users.
I do not propose at this time to enter
into details regarding the other Bills
which will in due course be brought before
you for consideration. They are aimed
at preserving for our people the amenities.
of civilisation, and the right to pursue
their lawful vocations with safety, health,
and profit. There are, however, one or
two matters which have been mentioned
with which I might deal very briefly.
The hon. Senator who seconded the
Address felt some fear lest the demands for economy should hamper the
development of our educational system,
and expressed a hope that at some no
distant date the period for compulsory
school attendance might be increased.
As regards the first point, progress during
times of depression is undoubtedly and
inevitably hampered by lack of ways and
means, but although naturally I hope for
increased and accelerated activity with
the return of more prosperous times, I
can assure the hon. and gallant Member
that as Minister of Education I have had
the loyal co-operation of the local education authorities and others in pressing
forward essential improvements within
the means at our disposal.
The increasing of the period of
compulsory attendance may perhaps
have something to commend it. On
the other hand, the provision of the
additional accommodation for pupils
which would thereby be rendered necessary would be a very severe strain on the
resources of our people, particularly in
the city of Belfast. Nor am I convinced
that public opinion is sufficiently matured
on this point to secure the desired effect,
even were the means available. Parliament can make laws, but an enlightened
public opinion is necessary for the successful administration of those laws. Before
leaving this question I might perhaps explain that although compulsory school
attendance ceases at the age of fourteen,
there is no bar to voluntary attendance at
elementary schools after that age. It is,
of course, also a fact that the Unemployment Bill at present being considered by
the Imperial Parliament provides for a
system of compulsory attendance, at any
rate of a part-time nature, for unemployed persons outside the present compulsory school age.
I
Mr. MCALLISTER: I must express
my great sympathy with the two very
popular Members of this House who have
respectively proposed and seconded the
reply to the Speech from the Throne.
do not think it would be possible to
deliver into the hands of any Member of
this or any other Parliament a proposal
to dress the political shop window of the
Government with such tawdry and shoddy
goods. When we consider that we are
called here with all the formality that we
have witnessed to-day to deal with a
Destructive Insects and Pests Bill we can
scarcely realise that for the last ten years
we have been living in such a state of
industrial depression. I always think it
is a compensating virtue in Parliamentary
life that when hon. Members are called
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. McAllister]
upon to discharge an impossible task,
knowing it to be an impossible task, they
always receive the sympathy even of
political opponents. I think it is strong
evidence of the futility of the legislation
we are called upon to deal with that the
Government chose as their spokesmen two
of their most able, and, undoubtedly,
two of their most popular Members.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
The Mayor of Derry has referred to the
probable employment of 9,000 additional
men in the shipyards. As a resident in
an urban area, I would be very glad to
see the shipyards completely and fully
employed, but I must say that even if
9,000 additional men are employed in
the shipyards it can be of no value, and
it can be no asset and of no service to
any part of the Six Counties except the
city of Belfast, which holds a monopoly as
regards shipbuilding. Even the city of
which the mover of the Address is such
an able representative will probably not
benefit, as its shipyards have been closed
for years.
With regard to the speech of the hon.
and gallant Member who seconded the
Address, I think he used the expression
that we were carrying on now under more
happy conditions and with a feeling of
greater confidence and security than prevailed in 1922. I would remind the
House that all sides must contribute to
that peace and security to which the hon.
and gallant Member referred, and if all
responsible men in Northern Ireland
would voice their views with the same
unqualified determination, yet with the
moderation and logic of the two hon.
Members who have moved and seconded
the Address, it would be a good thing for
Northern Ireland. It is not by such
speeches as were delivered by some responsible men in Northern Ireland on the last
12th July, for instance, when they openly
and unabashedly and in the most depraved
manner suggested the boycott of one-third
of the people of Northern Ireland, that
peace will be established. I say it is not
by those methods that we will secure a
better understanding and will be enabled
to carry on in peace and security. Those
are not the methods by which such a desirable state of affairs will be brought about.
Let us take another aspect of the last
eleven years. Take the question of agriculture. Eleven years ago there was not
another small country in Europe in which
agriculture was carried on more successfully than it was in Northern Ireland.
That was the state of affairs that existed
when this Government took control of the
Six Counties eleven years ago, and now
it is common knowledge that in no country
has agriculture passed through the same
depression as it has in Northern Ireland
during the régime of the present Government.
Sir EMERSON HERDMAN: What
about England?
Mr. MCALLISTER: I think the very
fact that an Agricultural Marketing Bill
is to be introduced into Parliament at
the end of eleven years of agricultural
depression is the surest evidence of the
neglect of that industry on the part
of the Government during the last eleven
years. I have always held that the
agricultural industry is the one indispensable and important industry in this
country. If the agricultural industry
prospers all urban and provincial towns
must progress in the same ratio, and,
therefore, I am sure Members on all sides
of the House will be only too willing to
co-operate in offering constructive criticism in connection with any efforts the
Government may make to improve that
But
industry.
if the ameliorative
Measures the Government propose to
introduce concern only insects and pests
how can
we be expected to offer any
criticism of a constructive nature.
Col. PAKENHAM. What about milk?
Mr. MCALLISTER: With regard to
the question of motor traffic, I can
possibly speak with a longer experience
of driving motor-cars than any other
Member on either side of the House.
As a motorist of that long experience, I
must say that, in my opinion, a very
great percentage of the motorists of Northern Ireland seem to think that they
have a monopoly of the rights of the
road in Northern Ireland. Any Bill
that can be introduced to safeguard the
public from negligent, careless, and irresponsible motorists will receive all the
support that we on this side of the House
can give it. But there is another aspect
of this question. There is the question
of third-party insurance, and I think
Debate on the Address
Adjournment
18 DECEMBER 1933
steps should be taken to ensure that the
poor agricultural labourer, the artisan in
the town, and the bread-winner in any
section of the community who is killed.
or injured in a motor accident should be
protected against the combine of insurance
companies, standing in with one another
and indemnifying one another, who make
it impossible for the representatives of
the ordinary wage-earner to carry his
litigation to the highest court in the
land, and to its ultimate conclusion.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move,
"That this debate be now adjourned.'
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjournment.
Resolved, "That this House do now
adjourn." (Viscount Charlemont.)
Adjourned accordingly at
twenty minutes before
Five o'clock.

THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 19th December, 1933.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Paper Presented.
Ministry of Agriculture:
Black Scab in Potatoes (Northern Ireland) Order (No. 2).
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act), and ordered to lie on the Table.
Deputy Speakers.
Resolved, "That the Senators following
be named Deputy Speakers of the
Senate during the present Session of
Parliament: Sir G. Clark, Mr.
McLaughlin."-(Mr. Long.)
Joint Committees.
Resolved, That the Senators following
be named of the Joint Standing Committee on Divorce Bills:-Mr. Leslie,
Colonel Pakenham, Mr. Quigley."-(Mr.
Long.)
Resolved, "That the Senators following
be named of the Joint Standing Orders
Committee:-Mr. Barclay, Mr. Leslie,
Colonel Sinclair."-(Mr. Long.)
Resolved, "That the Senators following
be named of the Joint Unopposed
Committee on Local Bills:-Mr. J.
Cunningham, Mr. McLaughlin."-(Mr.
Long.)
Resolved, "That the Senators following
be named of the Joint Kitchen and

J. Cunningham, Mr. Hill Dickson,
