Debate on the Address.
Order read for resuming Debate on
Question (18th December).
"That an humble Address be presented to His Grace in the following
words:
"Your Grace,
"We, His Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal subjects, the Senate of the Parliament of Northern Ireland, beg leave to
offer our humble thanks to your Grace
for the speech which your Grace has
addressed to both Houses of Parliament." (Sir Dudley McCorkell.)
Question again proposed.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I wish to join in
the congratulations which have been extended to the mover and seconder of the
Address in reply to the Speech from the
Throne. The hon Member who represents the city of Derry made a speech in
the usual incisive manner which he displays in this House, and the hon. and
gallant Senator Colonel Sinclair, made a
comprehensive survey of the whole position.
in the politics of Northern Ireland at the
present moment. With many of their
sentiments of course I profoundly disagree. But I do associate myself with my
hon. Friend, Mr. McAllister, when he
said that two more popular Members of
the Senate could not have been selected
to move and second the Address. I
would go even farther and say that two
abler Members could not have been
selected, and still farther, that two Members could hardly have been selected to
whose observations we in this House would
be more willing to listen with a ready ear.
Both of them speak always to the point.
They say what they mean and mean what
they say, however we may disagree with it.
Passing away from the personal aspect,
I do submit to the House that the legislative menu card is a very meagre and
fasting one. It is more arresting for
what it does not contain than for what
it contains. It is certainly a menu card
that will whet no man's appetite in this
Province. We are promised an Unemployment Bill. We must wait and see
what kind of a Bill we are going to get.
Will it be free from the taint of the poorlaw? Will it vest in the central authority
sole responsibility for the relief of the
able-bodied unemployed in this area?
Will it transfer to that authority whatever responsibility now rests on the local
authorities? We cannot forget that the
Bill now before the Imperial Parliament
and on which our Bill in Northern Ireland will apparently be modelled is very
bitterly resented by working-class leaders
in Great Britain.
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
19 DECEMBER 1933
The Gracious Speech from the Throne
holds out no hopes of the provision of
habitable homes for the poor workers of
this area. It holds out no hope of securing town tenants, who are outside the pale
of the Rent Restriction Acts, security in
their homes, security on fair terms of
A committee reported
rent and tenure.
on that branch of the law almost five
years ago and made recommendations
which, if adopted, would have approximated the law in Northern Ireland to the
law existing in Great Britain. That report has been, on the part of the Government, ignored and shelved. The
Pecksniffian self-satisfaction and selfcomplacency of the Government are quite
understandable. They live on in the
belief that they have got a lease of office
renewable for ever, that they have no constituencies to fear in the real sense of the
word, as the British Government has to
face constituencies and fear them.
day they are turning their attention to
deluding their dispirited dupes with the
fairy story that golden times are now in
store for them just round the corner.
To-
There was one matter on which the
Speech was conspicuously silent. The
hon. and
Colonel
gallant Senator,
Sinclair, contrasted the conditions of Northern Ireland in 1933 with the conditions
in 1921-22 when he became a Senator.
No mention whatever was made in the
Speech of anything in the nature of a
repeal of the Civil Authorities Act, which
is a matter of the greatest consequence to
the people of Northern Ireland. That
Act was passed at the time the
hon. and gallant Senator became a
Member of this House. It was passed
as an emergency Measure in a state of
emergency. Is the Province in a state of
emergency to-dav? Is the emergency
always to last. Is the emergency to be
coterminous with the partition of this
country? It is an Act which I venture to
say does not exist on the Statute Book of
any white community. Under that Act a
respectable man can be arrested and
detained-I invite the attention of the
House to that-detained at the ukase of
the Ministry, without offence charged.
without offence proved and without trial
had. An agent provocateur may send a
man to jail for an indeterminate period.
He has no redress. Northern Ireland is
part of the United Kingdom and yet an
Englishman, Scotsman, or Welshman may
be-
Mr. SPEAKER: I think I must ask
the hon. Member to confine himself to
what is actually mentioned in the Gracious
Speech. I do not think there is any
reference to the Act to which he is referring.
and
Mr. CAMPBELL: The hon.
gallant Senator contrasted the conditions
of to-day with those of 1923, and although
I bow to your ruling I submit that the
remarks made in that case entitle me to
reply generally. I did not introduce the
matter. But I think the Speech in express terms refers to the condition of
peace at the present time. It refers to
law and order being effectually main-
I do
tained at the present moment.
submit that it is within the province of
a Member of this House to criticise that
part of the Speech. I intend my remarks
on the subject to be brief, but I think they
It is a matter of very
are necessary.
great consequence to the civil population
of this area, because as the Act operates at
the present moment, as the law stands at
the present moment, I do not think
it is responsible for the conditions referred
to in the Speech from the Throne. They
are due to very different considerations.
I pass away from that, but I would
like leave to refer to one matter of very
great consequence affecting law and
order. Recently we had an order under that
Act banning in our territory the flag of
the Free State. I think that is a step
which is not in the best interests of this
Province. I think it is a step which is
an unfriendly act to a neighbouring
Dominion. Under that order a man in
this area can be sentenced to two years'
imprisonment for exhibiting the flag of
a neighbouring territory even if only the
size of a pocket handkerchief. The same
flag can be flown in London and Edinburgh, and on all other public buildings
throughout the British Empire. It is a
most remarkable state of affairs.
I thought the Gracious Speech would
have given us some hope that the question
of slum clearance would have been dealt
with by the Government. It is a pressing
and urgent problem, but I see no hope
of any remedy or redress or relief in this
respect. The Housing and Planning Act
which the Government passed two or
three years ago has not in any way been
of help in solving this problem. At the
present moment it is practically a dead
letter. No advantage is being taken of
the low cost of building or the low rate
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
of interest at present prevailing, and,
therefore, as far as one can see at the
present time, those areas in our Province
in which slums exist will continue.
The Speech attributes to the tariff
policy of the British Government an increase in prosperity and an improvement
in trade which is negligible. In so far
as shipbuilding is concerned the improvebe in part due to the operation
ment may
of the Loans Guarantee Acts, under
which, as we all well know, the Ministry
of Finance has made very heavy commitments of the credit of the taxpayers of
Northern Ireland. That is merely an
artificial stimulation of shipbuilding. A
similar artificial stimulation was abandoned six years ago by the British Government, and no Government at Westminster has since that time attempted to
revive it. This artificial stimulation of
shipbuilding was condemned by the Chancellor of the Exchequer this month in the
British House of Commons as not conducive in the long run to the wellbeing
of either shipowners or shipbuilders.
Then, again, we are told that the signs
of returning prosperity are due to the
tariff arrangements made by the British
Government with the Dominions. On
that point I would like to ask whether any
of the farmers of Ulster or any other class
of the community has benefited in the
least degree from the Ottawa Conference.
The Mayor of Londonderry, whose absence
from the House to-day I regret, was
at the Ottawa Conference as a direct
representative of Lord Craigavon. Sir
Dudley McCorkell has rendered Lord
Craigavon staunch service. He is uncompromising in his opinions, but he is a
clean, candid, straightforward foeman.
What triumphs has he brought home from
Ottawa? What orders for Ulster goods
have followed him hither? Well, one result
of that conference that I have seen is that
Lord Hailsham, who was at Ottawa, too,
was initiated into a tribe of Red Indians.
I do not know whether the same honour
was conferred on the Mayor of Derry. I
have not heard or read that Sir Dudley
was decorated with the war feathers or
girt with the tomahawk, or taught to beat
the war drum. Perhaps they thought
that a man from the Walls of Derry knew
enough about the drums. They say these
Red Indians have a practice called smoking
the pipe of peace. Sir Dudley may have
smoked the pipe of peace. Certainly his
mission ended in smoke.
The Speech expresses satisfaction at the
replacement of unsuitable school buildings by modern structures. Those modern
structures are being built entirely by
public money for the bigger section of the
community. The smaller section get 10/-
for every £1 the larger get for new school
buildings. The minority are abominably
treated in this matter. I will not mince
words. It is an amazing scandal that the
Government should express satisfaction at
the gross inequality now in process of perpetration. The minority are profoundly
dissatisfied, and with good cause.
The Speech holds out a prospect of some
effort to check the growing toll of death and
mutilation on the roads. About 2,000,000
people have been killed or injured in road
accidents in these islands since the War,
and about half-a-million more cars are on
British roads this year, compared with
last year.
A small ten horse-power car
will do sixty miles an hour, and
some cars of the latest type will do
over 100 miles an hour. Is every Tom,
Dick and Harry to be let loose on the
highways with cars of these speed capacities? Where is greater safety to be
found? Ought there not to be more control of speed at least in the towns and
villages? This is the very heart of the
matter. Ought we not to try to eliminate
the unfit drivers by tests for physical fitness and mechanical skill?
There is to be a Bill dealing with destructive insects and pests. What kind
of a Bill is that going to be. What are
the dangerous insects and what are the
pests. The noble Lord, the Leader of
the House, in his speech yesterday threw
no light on this paramount picture of the
coming session. Some of us would very
gladly welcome a Bill to extirpate some
other kind of insects and pests that are
over-running this Province at the present time, not in this House of course.
There is a delightful reference in the
Gracious Speech to the encouragement
that we in Northern Ireland give to
tourists. I think that is what Major
Bagstock would call a "devilish sly" reference to our Intoxicating Liquor Acts,
which make so entrancing and attractive
an appeal to guileless British tourists
visiting Ulster in quest of a holiday. I
think it is in a most delightful way that
we encourage these guileless and unsophisticated travellers who arrive at
Belfast or Larne on a Sunday morning.
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
19 DECEMBER 1933
The last Bill foreshadowed in the King's
Speech is Coroners." Again, I do not
know what is intended in that respect.
It
smacks of mortality, and after that the
rest is silence.
mere
Mr. LESLIE: I would venture to add
my own humble congratulations, perhaps
I may venture to say encomiums, to the
remarks already made respecting the
mover and seconder of the Address in
reply to the Gracious Speech. I do not
think that the House will regard the few
remarks I am going to make on that
part of the question as being a
formality. Let us look at the records of
the hon. Senators who addressed us
yesterday. We had first of all the Mayor
of Derry speaking to us, I suppose by
accident, on the eighteenth of December,
a date which those versed in the history
of the City of Derry may perhaps remember in connection with somewhat
historical closing of the gates. It was
certainly, if I may say so, a happy
accident that the Mayor of Derry should
have been addressing us on that day.
a
Senator Sir Dudley McCorkell himself
is one of the sons of our Province to
whom we owe much. We have watched
the work he has done in this House. We
have observed what he did across the
water at the recent meeting or convention-call it what you will at Ottawa.
We know he watched our interests there
in collaboration with more responsible
and more directly accredited ambassadors,
and we owe him a debt of gratitude for
his work there. We are also indebted to
him for having in his capacity as Mayor
of Derry broken the silence which some
of his predecessors imposed on themselves,
as I venture to think without any great
disadvantage to this House. I understand
that the tenure of office of the hon. Senator
is approaching its close. We shall all
regret that very much, and we shall hope
to see him again. There is an old Ulster
proverb that eaten bread is soon forgotten, and I am sure everyone here will
regret that a recent exemplification of
the truth of that proverb has been given
by some ward or other in the city of
Derry. For ourselves, this House will
not ever be unmindful of the services of
the gentleman to whom I have alluded.
Passing to the seconder, one whom we
hear not perhaps as often as we could
wish, we think of him as one whose life
has been mainly spent in alleviating the
sufferings of mankind. We remember,
we cannot forget, what he did during the
war. We know that he brought comfort
and relief to many sick and wounded.
We thank him for that, for the work
done in service abroad was work done
for Ulster. We remember, too, that he
represents the University in the Imperial
Parliament, and we feel sure that his
constituents are extremely satisfied with
what he does there.
It
Coming to the Speech itself, there is
no concrete Amendment before us, and
therefore I must not-indeed, you have
just so ruled-say anything about anything that might have appeared in the
Speech but does not. There are plenty
of pegs in the Speech itself on which to
hang observations on almost any subject
that is arousing attention at the moment.
I cannot, therefore, agree with the two
Senators who preceded me in saying
that the fare provided by the Gracious
Speech is very meagre and jejune.
seems to me that either of those two adjectives would be entirely inapplicable to the
Speech which introduces to our notice and
foreshadows legislation to be brought
before us dealing with the important question of the regulation, almost the rejuvenation, of the system of unemployment
insurance, and proposes to combine with
that system something further in the way
of public assistance to those able-bodied
would-be workers who are outside the
scheme of the present Unemployment
Insurance Acts.
For some years past we have all, I
think, been troubled with the question
of what was to be done for the man outside insurance in that way, whether there
was anything between him and the workhouse, as it would formerly have been,
or some form of outdoor relief, as is the
more modern practice. The matter has
engaged attention. The Financial Relations Committee, that is, the financial
relations of the State to the local authorities was not able to deal with it
very fully, but certain proposals were,
as a matter of fact, brought before
that Committee, but they were not
supported by either facts or figures, and
the whole question was one that could
not be dealt with at that time. Therefore, I think that particular Committee is
of no very great assistance to those considering this question.
But there is to be a Bill brought before
the Parliament of the United Kingdom.
That Bill is already in print, and it will
necessarily follow that, following our
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
usual practice of endeavouring to secure
for all our workers benefits equal to those
which they would have enjoyed if the Act
of 1920 had not been passed, we shall, if
we can, adhere to the same practice, and
we shall adopt, although it may be we
shall also have to adapt the Imperial
Measure to the circumstances of Northern
Ireland. I have said that it is our desire
that our workers should receive exactly
the same benefits as their confreres across
the water. I have always qualified that,
and I must still qualify it by saying that
we can only do that so far as our means
extend. Hitherto we have been fortunate.
With the assistance of the Reinsurance
Agreement, which was negotiated by one
of the most prominent Members of our
own Government, we have been able to
keep on level terms. But we all know that
not very long ago the situation was
dangerous in the extreme. However,
very happily that danger has been surmounted, and we hope we shall be able
to carry on on exactly the same lines.
But I must repeat that warning, that
there must be some equality of sacrifice.
We should not be justified in depleting
our Exchequer at the cost of those not
personally concerned in this matter in
order that those who are may receive
greater benefit than we can in reality
afford to give. I do not think any
Measure for seriously extending the scope
of the Unemployment Insurance Acts,
such as by including other classes,
domestic servants, and, it may be, agricultural labourers-I do not think such
proposals would meet with approval in our
Province. So far as I know nothing of
that sort is included in the Imperial Bill.
We have to remember in that connection,
and I shall refer to it again in a moment,
that the Province has not got a bottomless
purse. We must cut our coat according
to our cloth. We have dangerously, perhaps, risked overstepping the proper
bounds of prudent finance. We have
risked-I do not say we have actually
done it, but we must be careful in these
matters.
Now the Speech contains a reference to
agriculture. It is really too soon to say
anything about the success or otherwise
of the recent arrangements entered into
for the marketing of pigs. We all know
there was some trouble at the commencement, but we hope it will speedily pass
away, and that in the future matters will
proceed more smoothly and more satisfactorily. The Speech indicates that steps.
are to be taken as regards marketing of
potatoes. Those of us who grow potatoes
with a view to selling them would naturally welcome anything that would help
to obtain some sort of price for potatoes
when sold that would approximate more
to the cost of production, irrespective
of the question of profit. The present price of potatoes can be seen
in the daily papers, and anybody
with the smallest knowledge of the subject must know that they can only be
produced at a loss if sold at the price.
given in the papers.
The Speech refers also to some adjustment in the system of Local Government
administration. It probably will transpire that Parliament will be asked to
adopt some further parts of the recommendations made by the Committee that
examined the question, I think, so long
ago as 1923. It may be some of the other
recommendations of the Financial Relations Committee to which I have already
referred may be adopted. At all events,
there is to be a Local Government Bill,
and that is the sort of Bill which this
House is peculiarly competent, by the past
experience of its Members, to deal with.
area.
Again reference was made by the hon.
and learned Senator, who preceded me,
to the question of motor accidents on our
highways. Undoubtedly we are all
shocked at the present loss of life which
appears to be due to accidents caused by
mechanically-propelled vehicles. The
matter has not escaped attention in our
More than one attempt has been
made-rather indirectly, perhaps at all
events, an attempt has been made to
reduce the loss of life and to put a check
to really dangerous and reckless driving.
I have to say that I do not think some of
those attempts that have been made-and
most of those attempts were not adopted
with the unanimous approval of this
House-have been altogether successful.
A great change was made some two or
three years ago by transferring jurisdiction in cases of alleged dangerous driving
from the ordinary bench of magistrates to
the resident magistrates. It was thought
that that, coupled with the system of
minimum fines, would effect a great
improvement. I have not observed that
that has been the result. I have observed
more than once that the resident magistrates took advantage of the power very
wisely committed to them of suspending
19 DECEMBER 1933
Debate on the Address

Debate on the Address
The occupation of business premises as
defined in 1928, that safeguard to business premises was to remain. There was
a special exemption from the provisions
in the Act for any persons born in Northern Ireland.
licences. But I have found also that on
hearing that the man's driving was the
man's livelihood the
the suspension was
revoked. That is to say they enabled the
man to continue driving because it was
his livelihood, although he had been found.
guilty of conduct tending to deprive other
people not only of the means of livelihood,
but of life itself. I recently had the
advantage of hearing the views of one of
the Lords Justices on the subject of highway accidents. He said the best remedy
that he could think of was that all people
using the roads should endeavour to carry
out the Scriptural injunction to do unto
others as they would that others should do
unto them. That would make for really
what we want, that is to say, it would
make for prevention.
Hitherto legislation has concerned itself rather with the punishment of the
offender. That, of course, is a necessary
consequence of any orders introduced, but
it is not what we want. I hope, looking
forward to this new Bill, that it is likely
to contain some better regulation of
heavy motor lorries which do very much
damage to the roads and to the public,
and also that some steps will be taken to
ensure that the people driving these really
heavy vehicles shall have some understanding of what they are doing. It is very
often the case that a comparatively untrained man is put in charge of one of
those instruments of destruction without
having sufficient idea of the dangerous
capacity of the instrument he is wielding,
just as if the motor-car was a small thing
like a bludgeon which he waved above his
head. He controls the thing with his
hands, but not with his mind.
there will be an improvement.
We hope
Now we pass to another passage in the
Speech which I am sure is likely to arouse
very considerable attention, that is to say,
the alteration in the system of registration of Parliamentary voters; that is,
voters for the Parliament of Northern
Ireland. It may be remembered that in
1928, I think, this Parliament passed an
Act requiring that a person should not be
registered, or allowed to vote if he were
registered. unless he had been three years
resident in Northern Ireland. There
were, of course, certain safeguards. The
proposal, as I now understand, is that we
shall substitute for that requirement
a seven years' residence. I imagine
that the original safeguards will remain.
Those safeguards are of great importance.
I do not think we have any reason to
suppose that the effect of any such
measure as is adumbrated in the Speech
would be to produce a situation in any
way comparable with the situation in
Johannesburg which gave rise to the
Jameson raid in the middle nineties. I
do not think the situation will be like
that at all. I think it may be necessary
to consider the proposals with great care,
because we shall have to examine the
question of taxation without representation, which would be in issue, particularly
in the case of local government, in the
matter of rates. It would be more a matter of local government rates than
taxation, because the great bulk of the
taxation in this Province is levied in
accordance with legislation passed in the
United Kingdom which this Parliament
has little to do with. That question will
have to be considered, also the question
of safeguards.
I remember in 1928 I spoke of a
difficulty that was in my mind as to
whether domicile rather than residence
should not be made the test, but I thought
that residence, being the usual test for
the purpose of election, would be better
and the domicile matter, which, by the
way, is outside the powers of this Parliament, had better be left alone. It may
be that if we extend the period of
three years we may think it right to
give exemption to a person born in the
United Kingdom, that being an extension of the Act of 1928, which deals
with persons born in Northern Ireland.
The reason of that I think is a fair one.
Many Northern Ireland citizens were born
in England or Scotland. I will not go
into that. It belongs to the Bill itself.
There are numerous minor matters mentioned in the Speech, but these can wait
until we see the actual Bills. I should not
have referred to them except that something has been said about destructive insects and pests, and I may perhaps venture to remind the House that legislation
dealing with the Colorado Beetle was
passed fifty or sixty years ago, and it has
always been the practice to deal with any
danger that threatened by means of legislation.
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie
We all join in the satisfaction his Grace
expresses as to the supremacy of law and
order which now exists. The Speech
mentions, and with approbation, the
steps that have been taken by the Government to secure and maintain law and
order. The expression used is "authorities" not "Government." The hon. and
learned Senator in front (Mr. Campbell)
spoke of the Civil Authorities (Special
Powers) Act. I cannot help thinking
that that Civil Authorities Act was perhaps in the mind of his Grace or his
advisers when that passage in the Speech
was penned. I shall not say anything
about that Act. The House will remember that I appealed myself not long
ago for what I might call a revised edition
of it. That is an entirely different thing
to its repeal. I consider his Grace is
satisfied with the results which have
flowed partly from the existence of that
Act.
Then something is said, and we welcome
it, as regards the decrease in unemployment, particularly in our staple industries, in which shipbuilding is mentioned.
That would seem to be, as indeed the
hon. and learned Senator (Mr. Campbell)
admitted, an example of the bread we
cast upon the waters in the shape of the
Loans Guarantee Acts coming home.
The action of this Parliament as regards
those Loans Guarantee Acts has always
been a very courageous action, and it is
satisfactory to see that it has cost us very
little, and has brought us a very great
deal of good. There are few greater gifts
that can be given to this Province than
an advancement in our shipbuilding industry. The number of men normally
kept at work in our shipyards is very
great. I am not as conversant with
the details as some other hon. Members
on my left, but if we can keep shipbuilding going in Belfast we are well paid for
such risk as we may have incurred by
guaranteeing loans which have imposed
a liability on the Province which has not
yet been called on, and which is not
beyond our resources.
I venture to say-although I do not
think the hon. and learned Senator (Mr.
Campbell) will agree with me that we
have taken as regards those Loans
Guarantee Acts the long view in reality,
and we have also taken the short view
that what we want is relief for unemployment now. We want to keep our
people at work now in order that their
bread and butter may be assured at once
rather than the possibility of a banquet,
let us say, the year after next, when some
of them may have died, not perhaps of
starvation but of mal-nutrition, and have
ceased to require it.
Substantial progress is recognised as
regards electrification, and that, I am
sure, will appeal to us. The Speech
records that substantial progress has also
been made in education. An attack has
been launched this afternoon against the
educational policy of the Government on
the ground that some of the school buildings are not all that might be desired.
That no doubt is so, but the very wording
of the Speech gives the explanation.
Here it is:
Notwithstanding the difficulties
occasioned by the present strain on
public finance, substantial progress has
been made in the replacement of unsuitable buildings by modern structures,
I said a moment ago that I should
have to refer to finance again, and this
is what I was thinking about. We have
not got a bottomless purse. We know
that we have not yet attained perfection
as regards our school buildings, but we
do say that substantial progress has been
made. We recognise that perfection is a
moving equilibrium, and we will try to
keep up with it, but we must wait until
we get the necessary money. When the
revenue expands the extension of school
buildings will increase also. Technical
education undoubtedly causes a good deal
of expense, and there is a feeling which
has been voiced that perhaps too large
a proportion of the expense falls on the
taxpayers and not perhaps quite sufficient
on the local ratepayer, not to mention
the pupil himself. Again, the extension
of the school age was mentioned, and I
think the noble Viscount dealt with that
yesterday. I do not believe, especially
in the country districts, any extension of
the school age would be welcomed at the
moment.
I think I may close my remarks on a
note of general congratulation. After
twelve years in office the Government has
recently been, as the expression goes, to
the country. Very few seats were contested; roughly speaking, about
quarter. Only one seat was lost. If I
remember rightly it was Cicero, speaking
of a man called Cataline, who unfortua
I
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
19 DECEMBER 1933
nately still has his prototype in Ireland,
if not Northern Ireland, who said:
"When things are present what need is
there of words." Surely the results of
the last election just closed are things
present.
Mr. McMAHON: Gerrymandered constituencies.
Mr. LESLIE: The constituencies were
last altered at least five years ago, and
whatever the constituencies were like there
would seem no great reason why an
attempt to capture the constituencies
should not have been made. It is always
possible to contest a constituency. I do
not think, for instance, there has been
any change in the representation of the
Mourne Division of County Down, and I
think there were one or two other divisions
in which an attempt was made to induce
a change, but it did not occur. Therefore, if there was any wholesale gerrymandering of constituencies it does not
appear to have been uniformly successful,
and perhaps it is just as well that should
be so.
I do say, having regard to the
result of the recent election, that the
Government of the day may justly claim
that they are continuing to hold office by
the will of their people, and to continue
to hold office by the will of the constituencies is the acid test of success. I
venture to add that, to my mind, it is
the highest reward of faithful public
service. I have great pleasure in supporting the humble Address.
on
Mr. McMAHON: I would like to join
with other hon. Senators who have preceded me in congratulating the mover
and seconder of the Address, and while
I do not agree with everything they said,
I must compliment them
their
eloquence. I regret that no mention is
made in the Speech of the deplorable
plight that the minority in Northern Ireland find themselves in at the present
time. One would have thought that the
Governor, the representative of a King
whose country prides itself on being the
protector of minorities, would have called
the attention of the Government of the
Six Counties to the sufferings endured by
the minority under its jurisdiction.
the past thirteen years we have been the
special victims of the Government's
ineptitude and intolerance. Proportional
Representation, which was specially put
in the Act of the Imperial Parliament
For
establishing this Parliament, has been
abolished, and the constituencies have
been so shamelessly gerrymandered in
order to rob us of our rights that we are
unable to control County Councils where
there is a majority of our people.
In connection with education we are
also penalised. Palatial schools are being
built for Protestants free of cost, while
Roman Catholics are called upon to pay
half the cost of building their schools
and at the same time contribute as taxpayers to the upkeep of Protestant
schools. Many other things in connection with the administration of the
Education Act also bear heavily on
Roman Catholics. For instance, there
is the maintenance of the school after it
is built, and its cleaning and so forth,
for which we do not get a fair grant.
During the past seven or eight weeks,
especially during the election demonstrations, we heard speeches made by
Ministers of the Crown calling upon
employers of labour not to employ
Roman Catholics, and what Ministers
have been advising employers of labour
to do they have been practising themselves for a considerable number of years.
At an Orange demonstration quite
recently the Minister of Labour stated
that there was only one Catholic employed
at Stormont, and that he had a temporary
job. You will not find such a state of
affairs existing in any other place but
Northern Ireland. We are also penalised
in connection with the appointment of
judges. We have not one solitary
Catholic judge, and, considering our
numbers, very few Catholic justices of
the peace, and we have not got a solitary
Catholic Commissioner of Oaths. For
the past number of weeks our people have
been subjected to a reign of terror.
Catholic houses have been
indiscriminately and searched.
raided
Mr. SPEAKER: I think the hon.
Member is rather wandering from the
subject of the King's Speech. I do not
wish to interrupt him, but I must ask
him to confine himself to what is in the
Gracious Speech.
Mr. McMAHON: I am in the same
position as the hon. and learned Senator
(Mr. Campbell). Reference is made in
the King's Speech to peace and
tranquillity, and I thought I was entitled
to point out that the Government is
menacing the peace of Northern Ireland
421783
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. McMahon]
by their conduct at the present time.
That is the point I want to make.
Catholic homes have been invaded by the
police, and the houses of law-abiding
citizens have been raided at all times of
the night and morning.
You may say
that murder has been committed. I
condemn murder, whether it is the
murder of a police constable or a private
citizen, but if the law is to be vindicated
it should be with justice to all. Some
weeks ago a police constable was shot, and
from that day to the present raids and
arrests have been the order of the day. There
was a Catholic publican also shot, but no
arrests were made in connection with that
crime, nor were any arrests made when
his barman was shot during the troubles,
nor when his premises were burned out.
If you are going to vindicate the law it
should be with fairness to all.
You may tell me that the people you
have arrested are Republicans or rebels,
but then the Members of the Government
itself were once rebels, and if the men
they have sent to jail are rebels to-day
it is because they are apt pupils of Lord
Carson, whose monument holds pride of
place outside this House. All the ills
Ireland is suffering from to-day can be
attributed to Lord Carson, who threatened
an Ulster rebellion. They imported guns
into Ulster to fight the British soldiers.
If the King dared to dissent to the Home
Rule Bill they would kick the King's
crown into the Boyne and transfer their
allegiance to the German Kaiser.
The
only difference I can see between the
former rebels, that is the Government
to-day, and the rebels they have sent to
jail is that while the rebels occupy the
felons' cell the Government occupy the
front bench.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: On a point of
order. I hold it is out of order to disobey
the Speaker's ruling.
Mr. SPEAKER: I think that is entirely a matter for the Speaker. I have
given the hon. Member every possible
latitude, but I still hold that the hon.
Member has been wandering a good deal.
from the subject of the Speech.
I will
Mr. McMAHON: Thank you.
depart from that, but I will just say as
far as the minority is concerned I would
draw the attention of this House to the
fair treatment of the minority in the Free
State. In the Free State the minority
has all the privileges that a minority
should have. In fact they have more
than their due share. In Northern Ireland the minority does not want privileges; they want justice. In the Free
State fitness, not religion, is the only
qualification.
To the credit of the
minority in the Free State it is to be said
they have rightly recognised that fact.
The minority in Northern Ireland would
gladly pay tribute to Northern Ireland if
they were in the same position by fair
treatment, but they do not get fair
treatment. They are bound down by a
Government of political passion and prejudice, who vie with each other as to who
can curse the Pope the loudest.
Mr. GYLE: I mentioned to the Leader
of this House that I would like at this
stage to move the adjournment of the
debate, and I do so with your permission.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I cannot
agree to the Motion. I do not see any
reason for adjourning the debate.
Mr. GYLE: As a newcomer to this
House I fully anticipated that this debate
would be carried on for a much longer
period, and as I was extremely busy I had
not an opportunity of going into the
Gracious Speech, and therefore I thought
I would perhaps have been permitted to
speak on this matter to-morrow, when I
had more time to consider everything
that was in the Speech. However, as the
noble Lord has refused, I have no other
course open than to attempt to deal with
one or two points in the Speech.
Before doing so I wish, like other
speakers who preceded me in this matter,
to say how much I appreciated the speech
of the Mayor of Derry, and also that of
the seconder, Senator Sinclair. The first
thing that struck me yesterday when I was
listening to the Gracious Speech being
read was the fact that there was no mention at all of a matter that is agitating the
minds of a great many people in Northern
Ireland. I saw in the Press the other day
that the Portrush Council had passed a
resolution regarding the matter of landlord and tenant. This is a matter that I
raised in this House some time ago, and
although I did not get a great amount of
assurance that there would be anything
introduced in the near future, most of us
came to the conclusion that it would at
least have mention in the King's Speech
19 DECEMBER 1933
Debate on the Address

Debate on the Address
At least their
on in Northern Ireland.
actions would lead one to believe that they
know little about what they should be
looking after; that is, the interests of the
people they govern.
again, even if it had to be relegated to the
matters that could not come under the
consideration of the Government. We
thought that at least we would be told that
they were going to consider it again. But
it is quite evident that the bogey man has
got the best of the Government again.
The Leader of the House told us that
he attends Cabinet meetings, and so far
as he was concerned he was not able to
see any bogey man in the Cabinet, and,
therefore, we might take it for granted
that he was the bogey man himself. I
disagree entirely. We know that it is not
the noble Lord who leads this House who
is the bogey man in the Cabinet. I read
in the Press the other day that if given
three guesses the writer could guess the
individual first time. I think I could
name in one guess the bogey man in the
Cabinet, who is preventing this Measure
being brought in to give the people of
Northern Ireland the same rights and
privileges as the people in England have.
We have been told over and over again
that there is no man or woman in Northern Ireland who would be anything the
worse by being a member of this community. But here we have a thing we can
place our finger on. The Portrush
Council and the commercial community
in Belfast-I cannot at the moment
remember the particular body-have
passed resolutions quite recently.
We have found many other councils
right throughout Northern Ireland asking
for this Measure, and we are told why it
is that it will not be placed on the
Statute Book. Surely the Government
will listen to reason at some time. I ask
them again if they would see their way,
although it is not included in the Gracious
Speech, to try to lay the bogey man by the
heels as soon as possible and allow this
Measure to be put on the Statute Book. It
is a Measure that is really necessary if
our people are to be protected. If the
noble Lord knew as many cases as I do of
people who are really in a state of wonderment knowing scarcely what to do regarding
this matter, whose leases are expiring,
and who are being threatened with
a great increase of rent-an increase that
is going practically to put them to the
door-he would bring that matter before
the Cabinet, and inform them of some of
the things that are occurring.
It seems
to me that the Members of the Cabinet
are living in the air. They do not seem
to know anything at all of what is going
As I told you, I had not much time to
go through the Speech, and so my remarks
may be rather disjointed. I know the
House will forgive me for that reason. I
listened to-day to one or two things and I
hope you will not rule me out of order if
refer to them. I heard someone speak
of gerrymandering, and I heard someone.
talk about why the constituencies that
were gerrymandered were not contested.
I wonder if the hon. Senator who
made that remark about contesting
constituencies knows what it means.
It means, first and foremost, that
you have got to get a man to step
out of the rut and to say he is not afraid
to stand before his fellows and take the
retribution that comes to a man from the
Northern Government if he dares to
speak an independent thought. You
must have a man of that description.
Mr. SPEAKER: I do not wish to
interrupt the hon. Member, but he has
told us he was going to review the Speech,
and I have not heard one single solitary
syllable that has any bearing on the
Gracious Speech.
Mr. GYLE: I am exceedingly sorry.
I was following the custom in another
place, where we were usually allowed to
remark upon what had already been mentioned in the debate. That was what I
was doing. I had already heard remarks
made without any intervention from the
Chair, and I thought that perhaps I
was entitled to speak on the same lines.
However, I must bow to your ruling and
pass on.
Perhaps I will be allowed to come to a
matter-I am not sure whether I will be
allowed or not-which is in the Speech:
"It is gratifying to learn of the marked.
success which continues to attend the
skilfully organised voluntary efforts to
stimulate the demand within Northern
Ireland for goods produced by manufacturers at home and within the
Empire." I thoroughly agree with that,
and I believe that it is very necessary if
Northern Ireland is to get any sort of
success at all that we should try to purchase those goods manufactured within
our own borders-not so much manufactured within the Empire, but to provide work at home.
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Gyle]
Then I come along further and see
"also measures instituted to attract
tourists to our shores." Now, whether
I am in order or not, I would like to
draw attention to one or two little things
that I think at the present time keep
tourists out of our district. In the first place
I just wish to read this. I do not know
whether I am allowed to read from a
newspaper within this House, but this is
a cutting from a paper.
Mr. McMAHON: Is that the "Irish
News'?
Mr. GYLE: No, it is the "Northern
Whig and Belfast Post."
It is hard to realise that in the year
1933 we have such drastic licensing
legislation which compels one of the
best hotels in Northern Ireland to
issue the following notice to guests on
arrival: "Guests are respectfully reminded that all orders for liquor must
be placed with the chambermaid before
9-30 p.m."
Unless we do something to amend the
drastic licensing laws which compel the
management of one of our first-class
hotels to issue that notice to visitors
when they sign the visitors' book we shall
keep out of Northern Ireland many really
first-class tourists.
Mr. QUIGLEY: No.
Mr. GYLE: I am delighted to know
that at least one Senator does not agree
with that. He prefers the picture of a
visitor coming to Belfast on the Liverpool steamer, not on a Sunday morning,
but on the morning of a week-day, and
after a rough night's crossing, with seas
going over the ship-(laughter)-there
are two or three different ways of having
a heavy night's crossing, as I know.
After a heavy night's crossing the visitor
goes to one of our principal hotels, and,
as he is not feeling too well, he wants a
little something or other to warm the
cockles of his heart. He says he would
like a whisky and soda, and he is told
he cannot have it, because it is not ten
o'clock in the morning. Just imagine
that on a week-day morning. I am not
speaking of Sunday. That is the position
of affairs that prevails at the present
time affecting tourists arriving in Northern Ireland. We know that tourists
who are treated like that often say they
will never come back here any more.
no.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: Oh,
Mr. GYLE: I happen to know they
do. I am not going to discuss the question of the money that goes out of
Northen Ireland in consequence of these
restrictions. We know that the Great
Northern Railway Company run eight,
ten and sometimes twelve special trains
on a Sunday to take people out of Northern Ireland, but I am not going to
discuss that aspect of the matter.
Mr. SPEAKER: If the hon. Member
did so I should have to rule him out of
order.
Mr. GYLE: I took the precaution to
say I was not going to talk about it. I
wish to talk about something that will
induce tourists to come to Northern Ireland, and I think I am in perfect order
so far as that is concerned. I think we
are entitled to talk in this House about
the drastic licensing laws that prevent
tourists coming here. We know that
after ten o'clock at night in ordinary
hotels not only can a man not order liquor,
but if he has it with him for his own
use, under our Licensing Act he cannot
legally consume it, no matter whether he
is sick or not.
Mr. McMAHON: He can in a temperance hotel.
Mr. GYLE: Our present licensing laws
are so severe that they are keeping many
desirable tourists out of Northern Ireland. I happen to be a shareholder in
one of the largest hotel concerns, and in
talking the matter over with some of the
directors I have been told that it is an awful
shame the way they have to treat visitors
coming to Northern Ireland, and that they
are often told by tourists that they
will not come back again. I wish to impress upon this House and upon the
Government the fact that if we desire to
develop our tourist traffic we will certainly
have to amend our present licensing laws
in such a way that tourists coming to
Northern Ireland will have the same
rights and privileges that they have in
England and Scotland. We all know
that when we go to an hotel in England
or Scotland it is like our home.
should try to make the hotels in Northern Ireland the homes of the tourists who
visit them, and try to make them realise
that we are really a hospitable people and
that we want to treat them well.
We
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
19 DECEMBER 1933
We have a beautiful country, and
although there is an almost unlimited
supply of tourists we know we are not
getting anything like the number we
should have, and for the reasons I have
given. We certainly cannot put the
blame on the country. We may put it
on the number of accidents that occur on
the roads, although I do not think we
are any worse in that respect than they
are elsewhere. I think one of the blots
on
our licensing laws is the fact that
tourists cannot get the same accommodation, the same rights, and the same
privileges that they can get if they go
elsewhere for their holidays. I am sorry,
Mr. Speaker, if I have transgressed too
far, and I assure you it was not with
any intention of flouting your ruling, because I appreciate very much your kindness and indulgence.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: It
was not my intention to say anything this
afternoon, as I would not have done
SO but for the interesting manner
in which the debate has developed.
I would like in the first place to
pay a compliment to the mover and
seconder of the Address for the very
able and acceptable manner in which they
discharged their duty. Regarding the
speech of Senator Gyle, one would imagine.
that so far as licensing laws are
cerned this is the worst country in the
world. He must be very well aware of
the nature of the licensing laws on the
other side of the water, and he must know
that if a traveller dropped into a publichouse in the middle of the day he would
not obtain intoxicating liquor.
con-
Mr. GYLE: I was not speaking of the
general licensing laws. I was speaking
merely of the licensing laws regarding
hotels. Senator Cunningham is absolutely
wrong. If you are living in an hotel in
either England or Scotland you can have
liquor at any time you demand it within
the twenty-four hours.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: The
impression I gained from the hon. Member's speech was that he was dealing with
our general licensing laws. He said that
a person who arrived here after a heavy
crossing and went to a public-house-
Mr. GYLE: I never mentioned publichouse.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: If
you go to London you will find the publichouses closed in the middle of the day.
While I am no pussyfoot, the hon.
Senator has got to realise that in this
matter the Government are carrying out
the will of the people. This Government
has just been returned to office again, and
during the election there was no indication that the people desired any change
to be made in the licensing laws of this
country. The hon. Senator and his
friends should have put forward some
candidate to test the feelings of the
electors on this question. As it is, the
Government is merely carrying out the
will of the people as expressed at the last
two elections. There might be something
done so far as hotel accommodation for
tourists is concerned, and if representations are made to the Government on
that subject I am sure they will be considered. I for one would be agreeable to
something being done in that direction.
Senator Gyle some time ago in this
House referred to the bogey man of the
Cabinet. He has repeated that reference
to-day, and I am puzzled to know what
he means by it. To me the Members of
the Cabinet appear to be pulling together
very agreeably, and very satisfactorily
so far as they are interpreting the minds
of the people.
There are one or two points in the
Gracious Speech I would like to refer to
briefly. I think this is the first time we
have listened to a Speech from the
Throne which has been able to give us
some indication of returning prosperity.
Every King's Speech prior to the present
one has had to lament on the terrible
industrial depression that prevailed.
Everyone feels thankful that the corner
has apparently been turned, and that it
is probable we are at last going to enjoy
better times. Those who live in close
proximity to the shipyards can once
again hear the hammers going and all
the familiar noise of returning activity.
It is very pleasing to the ear, and there
is no question that the Government's wise
action in passing the Loans Guarantee
Acts has contributed in a large
measure to securing that happy result.
But, after all, it must be borne in mind
that the shipbuilding firms themselves
deserve some credit for having obtained
the orders, because, in the first instance,
they have got to put forward tenders that
are acceptable to the shipping firms before
they are accepted. It is not altogether
the Loans Guarantee Acts that brings it
here. It is the industry and foresight
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
and wisdom of the heads of the shipbuilding firms that help to bring work
here, with the assistance of the Northern
Government. I must say it is very
gratifying to find we have turned the
corner, and that there are better times
in store for us. I do hope they will come
along pretty speedily.
As far as motor accidents are concerned,
I am sure every Member who has spoken
in this House has expressed a desire that
something should be done to reduce the
number of casualties. I for one would
certainly be pleased to support any
Measure that would reduce that number.
There is a considerable number of careless
individuals with machines. I noticed last
night, going from this House, a car going
up Corporation Street. It was a breakdown van with a crane hanging from it.
I actually saw it turn out before another
car, which had to turn to save a smash.
There are cases like that that require to
be dealt with, and dealt with severely.
There are other matters in the Speech
which will receive the support of the
Members of this House. I must say most
of the criticism was pretty fair, and not
altogether unreasonable, with the exception of that speech we heard about our
beloved leader, Lord Carson, being a
rebel. That was an exception. Mr.
Campbell referred to the slum dwellings.
I would like to point out that that is not
altogether the fault of the Government.
The Government, I think, have done very
well. They have put forward only recently a subsidy to encourage the building of houses for the poorer class of people
at a smaller rent. It is the local authorities that are holding this up. I for one
feel very strongly on the matter. When
the Bill was being passed in this House
I asked the Government to use some pressure on the local authorities, but Mr.
Leslie contended that that could not be,
that the Government has no right to use
any pressure. I think it is a pity, because
the local authorities are holding up the
work. I know there are dozens of
builders in Belfast ready to go forward
and build houses at a low rent, and yet
they are held up by the local authorities
being obstinate and contrary in not agreeing to pass the statutory resolution that
is necessary by Act of Parliament.
I
think now that material is cheaper and
builders are walking about the streets
looking for employment the local authorities should be induced to build houses
for the poorer class of people.
With reference to some remarks by
Senator McMahon, who complained about
the suffering endured by some people, I
do not know that there is any suffering
endured in the North of Ireland by any
law-abiding people. The only people who
are suffering are the people who have
broken the law and are a danger to the
Northern counties. Let me point out
that the Government are protecting
Roman Catholics as well as Protestants,
because the Republicans are making a
great attack on the North of Ireland.
Only recently we saw the Nationalist
Members being opposed by Republicans.
It was regrettable to notice that one who
did much for the city of Belfast only
obtained election by a very small
majority. The Government are protecting decent Roman Catholics as well as
Protestants, and are being strict to put
the law into operation to keep these people
in their place. I think, instead of condemning the Government they should recognise that the Government is seeing that
the law is obeyed, and that some people
are not allowed to run loose to the danger
of respectable people.
Mr. QUIGLEY: As "the only one"
referred to by Mr. Gyle, I desire heartily
to support the Motion, and to congratulate the Mayor of Derry and the hon.
and gallant Senator on the very able
manner in which they respectively proposed and seconded the Address. It is
very encouraging to see that trade is
improving in Northern Ireland, and is on
the up-grade. As to the change in the
Unemployment Insurance Acts referred to
in the Gracious Speech it is pleasant to
note that the able-bodied unemployed are
to get consideration.
The suggested legislation on road safety
will give great satisfaction. That legislation has been long overdue, and I hope
there will be no delay in carrying it into
effect. In regard to hon. Senator Gyle,
who has given us a great oration, not by
any means a temperance address, I have a
different opinion on the Licensing Act of
1923. It has had a fair trial, and has
proved to be a great boon. Indeed I believe that some people in our city and in
the Six Counties, who were opposed to
the restriction in 1923, are now satisfied.
I could give proof of this from a publication. My Friend, Mr. Gyle, quoted from
the "Northern Whig." I am going to
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address

19 DECEMBER 1933
quote from a paper which I am sure he
knows very well, "The Northern Light."
There is an article here in this paper,
signed Louis Richards, and I read it very
frequently. He refers here to Sunday
closing. "I am surprised that the North
puts up as calmly as it does with such a
wallop on the jaw." The North puts up
with the Licensing Act because it is satisfied. Surely the hours from 10 a.m. to
10 p.m., twelve hours daily in the city, and
in the country and seaside nine are satisfactory. Does the hon. Senator (Mr.
Gyle) want to get back to the white slavery
of 7 a.m. to 11 p.m.-16 hours out of the
24. Indeed the licensing hours in Northern Ireland give better facilities even
than England, Scotland, or Wales. In
Blackpool the licensing hours are short.
Mr. GYLE: I was ruled out of order.
Mr. SPEAKER: Perhaps the hon.
Member would confine himself to the
Gracious Speech. To talk about the
Licensing Act is out of order.
Mr. QUIGLEY: The Bill was referred
to by the hon. Senator.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I join with the
other speakers in congratulating both the
mover and the seconder of the Address.
I would just like to refer to one or two
matters that have been raised. There is
the question of unemployment. I think
the Government should consider the question of giving to the urban councils and
the various rural councils more relief
works, and facilitating those councils in
having schemes whereby all able-bodied
unemployed men could be taken on.
Armagh we had great difficulty in some
schemes we brought forward. We found
In
we were obliged to go to the exchanges
for men while we had a number of men
yoing about the streets idle and starving.
We could not relieve them.
With regard to the markets I think the
Government should really consider the
position of the towns and see that the
markets of our towns are in no way endangered, and that the centres where markets
have been held will still be the marketing
centres. I hope the country people will
be encouraged to come into the towns to
sell their produce, and that the centre will
not be here in Belfast for produce lifted
at the farmers' door and brought here. I
want the Government to remember that
the old-standing markets and fairs are an
institution in this country, and are not to
be interfered with.
I see a reference has been made to the
education question, that it is going on in
a satisfactory manner. No one as far as
my constituency is concerned can say there
is anything satisfactory about education
or educational administration in the Six
Counties. We say that we are as good
citizens as any under your Government.
We are carrying through our duties as
best we can. We are anxious to help in
everything the Government are trying to
do legitimately for the welfare of the
citizens, but I do say that it is not good
statesmanship for the Government to be
bringing up our rising generation under
the conditions that exist as far as the
schooling of our children is concerned.
Let any of our people go round the Six
Counties and see the splendid schools that
are being erected for Protestant children
-I have no hesitation in saying it is for
Protestant children-and look at the
school alongside where the Catholic child
is being educated. What a comparison !
You have what might be called a palace
on the one hand, and on the other hand
you have what might be called a hovel.
Surely to goodness it is not suggested that
under conditions of the kind our children
will be brought up and made peaceful and
satisfied citizens. I say that as long as
the present state of affairs exists you are
bringing up children who will be dissatisfied. They will look back on their schooling days and see that their comrades have
been educated in schools supplied with
every modern requirement, whilst they
have been educated in schools that had not
the equipment they should have had.
Remember all this money is coming out of
the rates. I do say the Government
should be generous enough to re-open the
education question and to see if something
could be done. Our churches refuse to
accept your conditions, and I say it is
time something was done to re-open the
question.
A Bill is to be introduced to deal with
the railways. I am very glad to see that
the Government are going to tackle that
question, and when they do I would
respectfully urge them to remember the
people who have invested their money in
the railways and see that they get the
protection to which they are entitled.
We all know that up to now railway
stock was a trustee security, and a great
deal of money was invested by trustees
Debate on the Address
Adjournment
THE SENATE
[Mr. McLaughlin]
for children and widows. A large number
of widows and orphans and other people,
who are dependent on their dividends
from railways are now practically penniless.
The railways have been faced by competition which was anything but fair.
They were tied up by legislation, and I
trust that in any Bill the Government
may bring in they will remember the
unfortunate shareholders and try to help
them to regain their former position so
far as their stock is concerned.
With regard to the Destructive Insects
and Pests Bill, I would strongly urge on
the Governmtnt that if there are any
insects or pests in this House they should
get rid of them, present company, of
course, excepted. I hope the suggestions
that have been made regarding the
clearance of slum areas will be favourably
considered. It is largely a question of
the leases, because we find we cannot get
a sufficent lease of the ground or property
where we want to build. I hope the
Government will give this matter due
consideration.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Resolved: That a humble Address be
presented to His Grace in the following
words:
Your Grace,
We, His Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal subjects, the Senate of the Parliament of Northern Ireland, beg leave to
offer our humble thanks to Your Grace
for the Speech which Your Grace has
addressed to both Houses of Parliament.
:
Ordered That the said Address be presented to His Grace by Privy Councillors
of Northern Ireland.
BILL PRESENTED.
Dogs Protection Bill,
"to protect dogs by enabling the Courts
to disqualify persons convicted of cruelty
to dogs from keeping dogs," was presented by Mr. Campbell, supported by
Mr. McAllister, Mr. Gyle and Mr.
McLaughlin, read a First time, and
ordered to be printed. (Bill No. 1).
Henry's Divorce Bill.
Gilbert's Divorce Bill.

and read a First time.

adjourn until such date as Mr. Speaker
shall appoint."-(Viscount Charlemont).
Adjourned accordingly at Five
minutes after Five of the
Clock until such date as Mr.
Speaker shall appoint.
