Questions to Minister.
STRANMILLIS TRAINING
COLLEGE.
on
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House whether he can state the
total expenditure on
average annual
Stranmillis Training College, its staffs
and students; whether the average
the
annual expenditure per head
students at Strawberry Hill Training
College, London, is about £70; whether
the English Board of Education expend
the same sum per head on the training
of the English students there; whether
the average annual expenditure per head
at Stranmillis is about £90, including
overhead charges for buildings, repairs,
interest, etc., there being no such charges
on the public funds for Strawberry Hill;
and whether it is a fact that Strawberry
Hill was built out of private resources.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): The average annual total
payments out of the revenue account of
the Stranmillis Training College during
the three years ended 31st August, 1933,
amounted to £21,413 17s 1d.
The Ministry pays to the Board of
Education, London, in respect of
students from Northern Ireland who are
being trained at St. Mary's (Strawberry
Hill) Training College at the rate which
is allowed by that Board to the college
in respect of all students being trained
thereat. For the session 1931-32, the
rate was £69 13s 4d. In addition, each
student was required to pay a fee-understood to be £25.
The average actual payments per
student (men and women) at Stranmillis
Training College for the session ended
31st August, 1933. amounted
to
£89 5s 4d. Of this total £58 19s 10d
was met from Parliamentary grants,
£28 15s 2d from fees paid by students
and £1 10s 4d from bank interest, sale
of garden produce, etc.
The figures upon which the above
average of total payments are based include the actual payments for minor
works of renewal and repair during the
year, and also a sum transferred to a
depreciation and renewals account to provide for the depreciation in premises and
equipment. They do not, however, include any sum in respect of rent or of
interest on capital outlay, neither of
which charges has to be borne by the
college.
The Ministry has no further information in regard to the finances of the
Strawberry Hill Training College, nor
can it be said from what funds it was
provided.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Arising out of the
noble Lord's reply, I would like to ask
him whether a yearly sum is payable
as interest by Stranmillis to the Imperial
Government on foot of the latter's expenditure of £186,000 on the building;
whether this yearly sum is £9,300, computed at 5 per cent., equal to a further
charge of £42 a year per head of the
220 students; and whether the real ratio
per student per year is £70 in the case
of Strawberry Hill, and £131 in the case
of Stranmillis, in view of the building
of Strawberry Hill costing nothing to the
public.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The
Stranmillis Training College was built by
the Government of Northern Ireland and
is available for the training of students
of all denominations. The expenditure
of £186,000 to which the hon. and
learned Member refers was met from the
grant of £400,000 which was made available from the United Kingdom Exchequer for the provision of various
public buildings in Northern Ireland, pursuant to the Interim Report of the
Colwyn Committee.
No interest is, therefore, payable on
foot of the expenditure, and the remaining portions of the supplementary
question do not arise.
LOANS GUARANTEE ACTS.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House whether he can state the sum
of the loans guaranteed by the Ministry
of Finance to date under the Loans
6 MARCH 1934
Dogs Protection Bill-

Second Reading
Measure, opposition coming from no
quarter in either House of Parliament.
Guarantee Acts in respect of ship construction; how much of such sum is repaid and how much is outstanding.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In reply,
I would refer the hon. and learned
Senator to the last published quarterly
return for the quarter ended 30th September, 1933, from which it will be
noted that guarantees issued up to that
date amounted in the aggregate to
£12,608,307. Of this sum, £12,479,237
represents guarantees given in respect of
shipbuilding. Of the total of loans repaid to that date, shown in the return,
repayments in respect of shipbuilding
guaranteed loans account for £3,707,047,
leaving outstanding £8,772,190.
Further statements are in course of
compilation for the period ending 31st
instant, and the hon. and learned
Senator will, doubtless, not press me to
anticipate that statutory return to this
House.
Mr. MCALLISTER: In view of the
fact that practically the whole of this
colossal sum has been guaranteed in respect of shipbuilding, and therefore
appropriated entirely to Belfast, I would
like to ask the noble Lord whether, in
any future guarantees for loans, the provisions of the Act will not be administered entirely in the interests of Belfast
to the practical exclusion of the rural
areas.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Anything
that benefits Belfast benefits, indirectly,
the whole of the country. If the hon.
Member would like to put down a Motion
we could have a debate on the subject.
Dogs Protection Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move,
"That this Bill be now read a Second
time." This is a very simple Measure,
and it is a long overdue Bill. A short
time ago, in fact within the past twelve
months, a similar Bill went through a
Second Reading without debate in the
Commons at Westminster, and was
accepted by the House of Lords. In
fact, it was accepted by both Houses of
Parliament as a welcome unopposed
Parliament might seem surprised to
find that it had not long ago given to
dogs the protection which this Bill gives.
The Bill adopts in a short and simple
way the humane view of friends of dogs
that a person convicted of cruelty to a
dog should not be suffered, for a time
at least, to own or keep a dog. The
intention is to deprive a brutal owner of
the opportunity of repeating his cruelty.
The Bill will debar such a person from
obtaining or holding a dog licence, or
having the care of a dog during such
time as the court may think just. Magistrates who have to deal with an offender
deserving a stiff fine or a term of imprisonment will find the Bill helpful.
practice all offenders who permit or cause
cruelty to dogs can be dealt with under
this Measure.
In
The policy of the Bill is not altogether
novel. It has already found its way
to the Statute Book, because by the Protection of Animals Act, 1911, the court
can deprive a cruel person of the ownership of the dog and make an order for
the disposal of the animal. But that
Act falls short in this respect, that it
does not disqualify the offender from
having the custody of a dog for any
period. It simply takes out of his hands
the ownership of the dog cruelly dealt
with. This Bill goes a step further, and
I hope it will pass without challenge
from any quarter.
I do not intend to enlarge on any
sentimental truisms. We are all aware
of the refreshing fact that, alone among
animals, birds, dogs, and cats voluntarily adapt themselves to the society of
mankind, and alone among animals are
man's natural familiars. It is a peculiar
phenomenon, but it is one we are all
quite familiar with. I do submit to
the House that the very fact that this
Bill will be on the Statute Book will act
as a powerful deterrent against the mischief aimed at by the Bill.
I shall now deal very shortly with the
text of the Measure itself. I think the
Clauses are very plain and I hope they
are very clear. They are certainly very
short. Clause 1 enables the court to disqualify during such period as it thinks
right the person who has inflicted cruelty
to the dog from keeping a dog or obtaining a dog licence. The suspension con-
Second Reading
Dogs Protection Bill-

THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
tinues in force only during such period
as the court thinks right. I am perfectly
certain that the ordinary court of magistrates in petty sessions will not make
any order under this Bill except for good
and valid reasons.
The court is not bound to make an
order. It may make an order, but it is
left entirely to the discretion of the
tribunal whether or not to make one. If it
is a matter of humanity and a matter
of justice to make such an order as the
Bill contemplates the court will. Clause 1
gives the right of appeal to the quarter
sessions. It has been suggested that
power should be given to the court to
order the transfer of the dog from a
cruel owner to a more humane owner.
That may be a very excellent suggestion
and it is one that will bear investigation
at a later stage.
Power is given by the Bill under
Clause 2 to remove the disqualification
attached to a cruel owner. After a
period of six months he is perfectly free
to come in and apply. Doubtless, in the
ordinary case the application would be
acceded to if fair and reasonable. The
remaining Clauses 3 and 4 are verbal
and consequential and really explanatory. I submit that the Bill, simple as
it is, is a humane Bill and a moderate
Bill, and I do respectfully ask the House
to adopt it as a non-contentious Bill.
I
Mr. LESLIE: I rise, I need scarcely.
say, to support the Bill, and I imagine
it is a Bill which will find universal support in every quarter of the House. We
are all agreed, I think, that the dog,
more perhaps than any other animal, is
the friend of man. He has been described as his first and earliest friend. I
think we are all also agreed that any
person who is wilfully guilty of cruelty
to a dog is not fit to be allowed to retain
custody of so sensitive an animal.
think, too, we shall be prepared and
willing to congratulate the hon. and
learned Senator who has moved the Bill
this afternoon on his action in laying
yet another brick-if I mistake not it is
the third brick-in the wall of legislative
protection within which we seek to enclose the animal creation. Nor shall we,
I think, find any fault with the hon.
Senator for basing his Bill strictly on the
Act on the Statute Book of Great
Britain which received the Royal Assent
in May of last year.
We shall not offer any criticism on the
basis of the Bill, but looking at the Bill
as it would apply in Northern Ireland,
I am bound to say that there seems to be
a glaring defect. It is a defect which
may not be apparent in England, where
some of their rules or orders or possibly
Acts of Parliament have an application
which they have not in Northern Ireland. But there is a glaring defect so
far as we are concerned, and if it is not
remedied the Bill will fail to effect its
object. Accordingly, I shall at a later
stage submit a proposal which I think
will meet the particular case.
There are, it seems to me, two gaps to
be filled. First of all, although it is
scarcely a gap, I think the English Act
has allowed the necessary connection
with the Protection of Animals Act,
1911, to become a positive entangleabsolute hindrance.
ment and an
The other point is perhaps more glaring.
The proposal in the Bill deprives a man
of a dog licence and refuses him permission to take out another. But the
Bill says nothing as to what is to happen
to the dog or dogs which the man may
have. I cannot think it is the intention
to turn the unfortunate dog loose into
the street. There has been one case in
England since the Act was passed. In
that case the owner pleaded guilty and
very properly, if I may say so, was sent
to prison for fourteen days, but we are
not told what happened to the dog.
There are certain powers under the Act
of 1911 with regard to the disposal of
the animal that has been cruelly treated,
but these powers are limited to the particular dog in respect of which the act
of cruelty has been committed, and they
are circumscribed and limited to the
owner and so fall short of what is required.
I think it will be necessary to insert
words which will give the courts fuller
power.
Following from that it would seem to
me to be necessary to insert other words
which, while making that necessary connection of which I have spoken with the
main Act, will disentangle us and abolish
the necessity of construing the new Bill
entirely in terms of the old. I do not
think it is necessary to construe this Bill
with any other legislation at all. If it
is necessary, it would seem to me
6 MARCH 1934

Second Reading

Dogs Protection Billthat the code to which it should be
attached is the Dogs Regulation code and
not the Protection of Animals code.
I rather
There is another point.
gathered from what the hon. and learned
Senator said that it was his intention
to ask the House to amend slightly the
title of the Bill. I think he intends to
introduce the word "further," and that
I will let me in. There is a point which I
I cannot unthink ought to be made.
derstand why it has not been made
before. In the Protection of Animals
Act of 1911, the main Act-an Act
which I venture to say seems to have
been drawn in the interests of the larger
and more valuable animals, such
horses-there is a most admirable section
which gives power for the summary and
of
destruction
an injured
painless
animal. But rather unhappily, as I think,
that section of the Act has a little subsection at the end of it in which it defines
the animals to which it applies. These
animals are the horse, the mule, the ass,
the bull, sheep, goats, and pigs. Unfortunately the dog is excluded.
as
I shall
ask the House if it be necessary to make
a change in the title-I do not think it
will-to give to the unfortunate dog, for
instance the dog injured in a motor car
accident, the same protection that is
given to the sheep, pig, or goat. I cannot think why that Act has remained for
23 years unamended in that respect.
It only remains to say I cordially support the Bill, and perhaps I may add
that the suggestions which I have adumbrated this afternoon are made with the
generous concurrence of the hon. and
learned Senator who moved the Bill.
It
Colonel PAKENHAM: I rise to support the Bill introduced by the hon. and
learned Senator, and I do so because I
feel that the thing is very necessary.
is very necessary that we should try to
protect the dog. The only thing I feel
about this Bill is that the man or woman
who has once inflicted cruelty on a dog
is very likely to be cruel again.
person is cruel to a dog, he has a cruel
nature. I rather think the Senator might
go further and say that the man
woman who is cruel to a dog should not
be allowed to keep a dog again. In the
meantime I support the Bill as a step in
the right direction. The question as to
If a
or
who owns the dog afterwards can be very
easily settled one way or the other. I
support the Bill.
he
as
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I should
like, first of all, to congratulate the
hon. and learned Senator on his speech
on the Second Reading of the Bill. He
the
is now definitely established
champion of dumb animals in Northern
Ireland. The Wild Birds Protection
which
Act,
steered
successfully
through this House, may be called the
Magna Charta of the wild bird kingdom.
Personally, I am in favour of the Bill.
I think that any Measure that has for
its object the suppression of any form of
cruelty should have universal support.
At the present moment I am unable to
give any definite assurance that the Government will sponsor it in another place.
Colonel SINCLAIR: I do not rise to
object to the Bill, but I should like some
assurance from the hon. and learned
Senator who introduced it. Like other
Members of the Senate, I desire to congratulate him on the third attempt to
carry out humane principles and to
secure that unnecessary suffering shall
not be inflicted on any animal. I rise
rather to ask for an assurance on
point. With the general tenor of the
Bill I am in agreement, and I hope I
can support it cordially if I can get an
I think it is
assurance on this point.
possible to have a definition of cruelty,
and we do not know from the Bill who
exactly defines what cruelty to a dog is.
Who is to decide that point?
one
I should, at this stage, say I am a dog
lover myself, and my dog is duly registered in the Tail-Waggers' Club, and I
hope that anything I have got to say
will be taken as in favour of the general
tendency of this Bill. What I want to
know is, in the operation of this Bill
when it becomes an Act, is there any
likelihood of a charge of cruelty being
brought forward from the ordinary daily
occurrences, or on what happens, and
must necessarily happen, in scientific
establishments concerned with medical
research. Members of the Senate know
that the Privy Council of Great Britain
and Northern Ireland established, twenty
years ago or thereabouts, a Medical Research Council. It is drawn from the
highest scientists we know, and it is
specially charged with the responsibility
Dogs Protection Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Colonel Sinclair]
of making investigations into matters for
the relief not only of human suffering,
but animal suffering. I should like to
illustrate how dogs used in these experiments might benefit. A year or two ago
the Medical Research Council, by the
generous help of "The Field" newspaper,
carried out an investigation into distemper, which is the dogs' greatest
Scourge, and great benefit resulted from
that inquiry in regard to a measure for
the prevention of this distemper in dogs.
Therefore, even dogs themselves may
benefit from these experiments. The
benefits derived from scientific experiments of this kind are not always confined to human beings. For these and
other reasons I would like the hon. and
learned Member who is responsible for
this Bill to inform me whether, in the
administration of the Bill when it becomes an Act, there is any likelihood
of any embarrassment being caused to
or prosecutions brought against research
establishments which are engaged in
carrying out experiments that are considered necessary in the interests of
human beings as well as dogs.
For instance, take the researches
carried out by Dr. Banting some years
ago in Canada. Those researches resulted
in the discovery of insulin. The benefit of
that discovery has proved to be something colossal. It is estimated that
more than two million human beings in
two hemispheres have benefited in the
alleviation of diabetes, and in the extension of the lives of people who might
otherwise be doomed to an early death.
It is very easy to overstep the limit of
rational criticism and become sentimental, but I would very cordially support the hon. Member's Bill if I received
from him an assurance that experimental
research would not be harassed
thwarted, even though it entailed the
occasional use of dogs for the purposes
of research. I should be very glad if the
hon. and learned Member would relieve
my mind on that point.
or
Mr. CAMPBELL: I wish to express
my deep gratitude to the House for the
reception it has given to this Bill. I
wish particularly to tender my thanks
to the hon. and learned Member (Mr.
Leslie) for the most useful suggestions
he has made, directed entirely with a
view to the improvement of the Measure.
With regard to the appropriate and relevant remarks made by the hon. and gallant member (Colonel Sinclair), I should
be very slow to attempt to give a definition
of what amounts to cruelty. I am not
aware of any such definition, and I think
it would be almost impossible to frame
an exact and comprehensive definition,
or to draw a line of demarcation between
an act that is cruel and an act that is
not cruel, or to express in any definite
formula what is cruelty. I think this would
be best left to the good sense and sound
judgment of the tribunal that will deal
with the matter. In the great majority of
these cases the magistrates do act reasonably and fairly and in such a way as
commands universal acceptance. They
are humane, and they are as anxious as
they can be to protect animals.
With regard to the hon. and gallant
Member's other point as to the danger
of scientific experiments on dogs in the
interests of humanity being exposed to
peril under this Bill, I may just remind
him that the Measure is grafted on to the
Protection of Animals Act, 1911. Section
(1), sub-section (3) of that Act says that
nothing in the Section dealing with acts
of cruelty to animals will render illegal
any act lawfully done under the Cruelty
of Animals Act, 1876. I think the
latter Act envisages and contemplates
acts of an experimental and scientific
kind made in the interests of science, and
the Act of 1911 expressly says that
nothing in that Section shall in any way
at all render illegal any act lawfully done
under the Cruelty of Animals Act, 1876.
I think that that Act of 1911, incorporated in the present Bill, renders this.
Measure perfectly free from all danger to
any experimenters of that class. I do not
think that such a danger is within
the bounds of practical politics, and I
hope the hon. and gallant Member will
be perfectly satisfied on that point. No
one wishes to trespass on the efforts of
science to serve humanity, even if it
is done at the expense of some little
suffering on the part of animals, and I
am satisfied that such experiments will
not be impeded or interfered with by
this Bill, which I will now leave to the
good judgment of this House.
Adjournment-
Sittings of the House
6 MARCH 1934
On Question, Motion, "That the Bill
be now read a Second time," agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for the next sitting day.
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn
until such date as Mr. Speaker shall
appoint."
Mr. LESLIE: Can we be given any
idea as to how soon we may be expected
to resume our labours?
I am
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
afraid I cannot. It will certainly not
be before next week.

Adjourned accordingly at

o'clock until such date
as

Stormont Regulation and Government
Property Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Viscount
CHARLEMONT:
Before
actually moving that this House do resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill I
have a statement which I would like to
make in reference to some points that were
raised on Second Reading. Senator
Leslie pointed out that this Bill falls into
two parts-the first four clauses dealing
with the general management of the
Stormont Estate, and the fifth clause with
the acquisition and disposal of Government property in general. The honourable and learned Senator took the view
that these two matters ought to have been
kept distinct, each forming the subject of
a separate Measure. That view would, if
I may say so, be a reasonable one to take
if we had to regard only the question of
logical arrangement. But the Government, when framing a Bill which deals
solely with details of administration, must
keep the administrative aspect in view. I
need not labour the point that the Bill
now before the House is a Bill of this
kind.
When moving the Second Reading, I
explained that the Bill seeks to do two
things. It accords to the Government
the necessary powers to enable it to preserve the amenities of the Stormont
Estate, and it also makes general provision
with regard to land for the public service.
Now both these matters are, in our scheme
of administration, within the sphere of
the Ministry of Finance as successors in
Northern Ireland to the Irish Commissioners of Public Works. It is natural,
therefore, to combine in a single administrative Measure such provision as in the
judgment of the Government is necessary,
in order to enable the Ministry of Finance
to fulfil its functions as to public works,
both in respect of the Stormont Estate
recently handed over and also in respect
of other property.
I would remind the House that the
estimate of the amount of public money
annually required for both purposes is laid
before the House of Commons under the
head of "Public Works and Buildings,"
and is appropriated under the same head.
I am sure that Senator Leslie would be
the last person to prefer a theoretical
classification to one which accords both
with constitutional arrangements and also
with Parliamentary procedure.
The
In his Second Reading speech Senator
Leslie enquired whether the powers proposed by Clause 5 of the Bill are new
As I remarked at the
powers or not.
same stage, certain powers in this connection already reside in the Ministry of
Finance; but I should like to add that
those powers are not conferred in so clear
and definite a way as is desirable.
honourable and learned Senator will
realise what I mean if he refers to Section
2 of the Public Works (Ireland) Act,
1869, or Section 8 of the Public Works
Loans Act, 1892. Those enactments, of
course, had reference not to the Ministry
but to their predecessors, and they have to
be read with adaptations to the present
administrative scheme, having been
enacted for the purposes of a scheme which
is now superseded.
The power to dispose of land when not
required for the public service has always
been exercised without question, but it
was not referable-in the case of the
Finance Ministry's predecessors to any
express statutory provision. The Ministry
has for some time desired to have a definite
authority from Parliament in the matter.
The present Bill affords an opportunity
for giving such a power, and this opportunity ought not, in my judgment, to be
lost.
I should like to say one more thing:
Clause 5 expressly withholds from the
Ministry any power to sell, exchange, lease
or surrender the Stormont Estate, so far
as it is the gift of Great Britain. I hope
that the House will be satisfied as to the
appropriateness of the arrangement
adopted in the Bill. I beg to move,
"That this House do now resolve itself
into Committee on the said Bill."
THE SENATE
Stormont Property Bill-

Committee
not the case. I am not satisfied, as indeed I said on Second Reading, that
even if taken at their worst the words
would effect the purpose which I would
regard as an undesirable purpose.
However, the words as they appear
in the text do not help us
in
Mr. LESLIE: Perhaps I might follow
the noble Viscount for a moment, but
only so far as to say that I think the
House will be very glad to have the explanation which it has now been given,
and also I think the House will probably
prefer to have the explanation now rather
than to get it at a later stage when the
particular Clause which has been SO
much referred to is before us.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.

CLAUSES 1 (Power to make regulations as to Stormont Estate), 2 (Powers
and duties of rangers and police), 3 Prosecutions and application of penalties),
and 4 (Savings as to certain powers and

CLAUSE 5 (Provision as to taking
and disposing of land for the public
services).
(1) The Ministry of Finance may
purchase by agreement or take on
lease any land, where the same is required for the purpose of any powers
or duties of the said Ministry or for
the administration of any public
service in Northern Ireland.
ex-
(2) Subject to the provisions hereinafter contained in this section, the
Ministry of Finance may sell,
change, lease or surrender on any terms
any land for the time being vested in
that Ministry, and on any such exchange may give or receive any money
for equality of exchange; and that
Ministry may sell either by public
auction or by private contract, and
may make any stipulations, as to title
or otherwise, in any conditions of sale
or contract for sale or exchange, and
may buy in at any auction and may
rescind or vary any contract for sale
or exchange, and may re-sell or reexchange any such land.
in
Mr. LESLIE : I beg to move
Clause 5, Sub-Clause (2), line 34, to
leave out from "exchange" ("sale or
exchange, and") to the end of Sub-
Clause (2). The words which I suggest
that the Committee should take out of
the Bill before us are words which seem
to me to tend directly to confusion of
thought, and they do convey a suggestion that exceptional powers are intended
to be taken. I am assured that that is
any way, and taking them out will
not take away from the Ministry
any power which it ought to have. The
Ministry, if these words are taken out,
will be left with neither greater nor less
powers than an ordinary person would
have in the case of a similar transaction.
I do not think it is necessary for me to
labour the interpretation of 'the words in
the portion I propose to expunge word
by word and line by line. I think it
is sufficient to say that they do not help
us, and they do convey an undesirable
suggestion. Accordingly, I move that
they be left out.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I accept
this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 6 (Interpretation) and 7
(Short title) ordered to stand part of
the Bill.

be reported to the House.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause
1, paragraph (c), line 27, after "year"
the year of hiring ") to insert the
words or other shorter term." The
object of the suggested Amendment is to
meet those cases which are very common
in Northern Ireland where an agri-
But I must not disguise from the Committee that there might be thought to be
some difficulties in the way. In the
first place, the text of the Bill accurately reproduces the existing law, and the
law which has been in existence certainly
since 1889. If, therefore, the Committee
accept the Amendment they will be
making a very slight change, but still a
change, in the present law. Also, it
would be necessary that the consent of
the Imperial Treasury should be
intimated, and further, we should have to
remember that making this change might
throw the Bill slightly out of gear with
other codes of legislation, particularly the
company code. But those difficulties, I
think, if I may say so, will arise equally
acutely at a later stage in our discussion
if we deal with some Amendments to be
moved by the noble Lord, the Leader of
the House. But I have every belief that
the Committee will think the difficulties
are not insuperable, and that there is
nothing that should militate against this
Amendment, which is really an act of
justice which may be of some use,
although so far as I know, during the
period which has elapsed since 1889, no
question has, in fact, arisen.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, in Clause 1, page 3, to leave out
paragraphs (i), (ii) and (iii), and
insert,
"(i) the Unemployment Insurance Act,
1920, or any enactment for the
time being in force amending or

THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
viding for the payment of contributions under that Act for the
purpose of widows', orphans', and
old age contributory pensions;
and being amounts recoverable in
Northern Ireland by any Government
department or Minister administering
such Act in some part of the United
Kingdom."
This Amendment is of a somewhat
technical character. Subsequent to the
passage of this Bill through the House
of Commons, it was brought to the notice
of the Government that contributions
due under the Unemployment Insurance
Act of 1920 are still recoverable in the
Northern Ireland Courts whether due to
the Government authorities in Great
Britain or to the Ministry of Labour in
Northern Ireland, and that these contributions are to the same extent preferential debts in a Northern Ireland bankruptcy. The same position obtains
with regard to Health Insurance and
Pensions contributions, because these
are governed by the National Health
Insurance Act, 1924,
1924, which applies
throughout the United Kingdom.
Mr. LESLIE: I think we must all
agree that the Amendment does deal
with a very technical subject, but I
should like to say that I think that in
introducing the Amendment the Government has taken what I may call a very
expansive view of possibilities that may
arise in the future. We are proposing
to take power now to deal with any
enactment for the time being in force,
the Unemployment Insurance Act and
otherwise. That was already in the Bill
when it came to us from another place.
We are also taking power to transfer
our attention to any enactment which
may be substituted for an existing enactment. Having regard to the very up and
down, here and there, in and out, and
roundabout methods of progress which
have been adopted by these Insurance
Acts, that may be found of great help
to us in the future, and, therefore, I
should like to welcome not only the
Amendment before us but the two others
which the noble Viscount will be moving
in due course, and which are all more
or less connected together, as in fact
they naturally would be. I think the
Amendment before us will be found of
great use in the future, and I think
perhaps it will be found of greater use
than is apparent at the moment.
Amendment agreed to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, in Clause 1, Sub-Clause (10), line
38, after "1896," insert, "or of Section
twenty-eight of the Savings Bank Act,
1833. I am asking the House to take
this Amendment as well as those which
appear on the printed Paper, because it
meets a point to which the attention of
the Government has been called within
the last few days.
The words which I propose to move
will, as I am advised, secure that depositors in the Post Office Savings Bank
shall enjoy, in the case of a bankruptcy,
a safeguard similar to that which depositors in Trustee Savings Banks enjoy.
The latter class of depositor has a safeguard in Section 14 of the Trustee Savings Bank Act, 1863, which is preserved
by the Bill at present before the House,
with the effect that depositors under
that Act are not in danger of having
their savings distributed as assets in the
bankruptcy of a Savings Bank official.
But that event might perhaps happen to
a Post Office Savings Bank depositor, if
the Bill did not expressly preserve
another enactment also, namely Section
28 of the Savings Bank Act, 1833.
Mr. LESLIE: The effect of that will
be, incidentally-although the noble
Viscount did not point it out-that the
position of the depositor in Northern
Ireland will be better safeguarded by
Statute than the position of his opposite.
number across the water. It is undoubtedly an Amendment that should be
inserted in the Bill now that it has been
ascertained and decided that express
words are necessary to serve the purpose.
It is a very great thing that we in Northern Ireland should be able to say that
we are more careful to safeguard the interests of our depositors than is the case
in other and less favoured regions.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

2. References in this Act to enactments of the Parliament of the United
Kingdom shall be construed as
references to those enactments as they
apply in Northern Ireland.

certainly suit me much better than

long distance, and probably distance will
be no object to those other hon. Members
who possess a car. I would appeal to the
noble Viscount to agree to the House
sitting on Thursday instead of to-morrow.

would like to support the suggestion that

To-morrow there is to be a meeting of the
Belfast Corporation which might sit all
day. There would be no possibility of
either Senator Joseph Cunningham or
myself attending here to-morrow, and
on those grounds alone I would appeal for
an adjournment until Thursday.

Members had come up to Belfast in a

would see the advisability of staying here
when they are here.

in time for to-day's meeting I had to
start at eleven o'clock yesterday morning.

Grey Seals Protection Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): The Bill to which the House
is now asked to give its consent is intended to take the place of the Grey Seals
Protection Act of 1914, which, in its
application to Northern Ireland Parliament, has been renewing annually by
successive Expiring Laws Continuance
Acts.
Two distinct kinds of seal frequent the
waters around the British Isles, the
common seal and the grey seal. The
common seal is found in considerable
numbers around the Northern Irish Coast,
but the grey seal is rarely met with, their
principal habitat being the Hebrides.
Prior to 1914 the destruction of grey seals
in British waters had been so considerable
that there appeared to be a danger of
their total extinction, and, accordingly,
an Act was passed in that year giving
them complete protection. This Act,
which was of a temporary nature, has
been kept in operation by annual renewals
both in Great Britain and Northern Ireland up to the present year.
In 1927 and 1928 the Scottish Fishery
Board carried out exhaustive investigations into the breeding conditions and the
number of grey seals in the western
islands of Scotland. These investigations
showed that the grey seal population had
appreciably recovered and that there was
then no danger of its early extinction.
Whilst, however, it was recognised that
in the interests of the fishing industry an
excessive increase in the grey seal population should be prevented, the Imperial
Government considered that it would be
unwise completely to withdraw the protection afforded by the 1914 Act. Accordingly, the Grey Seals Protection Act
passed by the Imperial Parliament in
May of last year, though repealing the
1914 Act, provided for an annual close
season for grey seals, subject to variation
Grey Seals Protection Bill
Adjournment
THE SENATE
Minister of
[Viscount Charlemont]
at the discretion of the
Agriculture and Fisheries in England and
the Secretary of State in Scotland, and
prohibited the killing of grey seals except
under permit and by approved methods.
The Bill which is now before the
House applies corresponding provisions to
Northern Ireland, and is introduced with
a view to securing uniformity of practice.
in this matter throughout the United
Kingdom. I beg to move, "That this
Bill be now read a Second time."
Mr. LESLIE: I imagine that the only
point not dealt with is that the Bill before
us, following the English Measure of last
year, extends by six weeks the close season
provided in the 1914 Act, to which the
noble Viscount referred. That is the only
essential difference between this Bill and
the Act of 1914, because I do not regard
the provision of power to make order if
circumstances require, as an essential
matter at all. It is, on the whole, desirable that there should be power somewhere
to vary the close season if occasion really
does require that course. There is nothing
else in the Bill before us unless perhaps
we may notice that in this Bill as in the
English Act the term used, halichorus
grypus, has promoted the seal to the diphthong to which it is entitled, according to
the books on natural history. The essential difference between the grey seal and
the brown seal appears to be that the grey
seal has a hooked nose and is somewhat
larger than the brown seal. That, I
suppose, accounts for the fact that it is not
generally known round Northern Ireland
as one of our seaside summer visitors.
Mr. PORTER PORTER: Since the last
hon. Senator has been speaking as one who
knows such a lot about seals I would like
to know if there are any grey seals in
Northern Ireland. I know there are
plenty on the Donegal coast, but that is
in the Free State. I do not know of any
from Ballycastle round in Northern Ireland. The last speaker seems to have
knowledge and, perhaps, he can help me,
if the Government cannot, by telling me
if there is such a thing as a grey seal in
the North of Ireland.
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
I regret
that I cannot give any great information
on that subject myself, but if there are
grey seals on the Donegal coast it is not
unnatural to suppose that some visitors
may come into our seaports.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill read a Second time accordingly,
and committed to a Committee of the
Whole House.
Stormont Regulation and Government
Property Bill.
Order for receiving Report of Amendment read.
Moved: "That this report be now
received."(Viscount Charlemont.)
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: Before this Resolution is passed there is
just one question I would like to ask the
noble Leader. On page 6, line 24, where
it speaks of the statutory period, I cannot
make it out. I am sure it is correct,
but I cannot make out what it means.
It says a period comprising five days at
least, and then it goes on to say, not
being in any case shorter in duration
than ten days." I should like if the
noble Lord the Leader of the House
would just explain. I do not think there
can be anything wrong, but I should like
to know what it means.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will give
the information at a later stage. I am
not quite certain about the meaning myself.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

Bill.

received."(Viscount Charlemont.)

move, "That this House do now adjourn

Question to Minister.
SICKNESS IN NORTHERN IRE-
LAND AND IMPERIAL CIVIL
SERVICES.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM asked
the Leader of the House whether he can
state what is the rate of sickness in the
Civil Service of Northern Ireland as
compared with corresponding branches of
the Imperial Service.
Mr. LONG: The figures which I am
about to give cover the year 1932 in so
far as the Northern Ireland Civil Service
is concerned, but for the Imperial Civil
Service I propose to give figures relating
to the year 1931, since that is the latest
year for which particulars are available
to the Ministry of Finance.
I should add, however, that the figures
for the temporary staffs in the Northern
Ireland Employment Exchanges are not
included, as it was not practicable to
collect them in time for this reply. The
figures are:-

Ballymena Electricity Special Order.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, “That the
Special Order made and confirmed by the
Ministry of Commerce for Northern Ireland under the Electricity (Supply) Acts
(Northern Ireland) 1882 to 1931, for the
transfer of the undertaking authorised
by the Ballymena Electricity Special
Order, 1930, be hereby approved."
This Special Order made and confirmed by the Ministry of Commerce
makes provision to enable the electricity
undertaking of the Ballymena Urban
District Council to be transferred to
the Electricity Distribution Company,
Limited. The company has statutory
powers, under the County of Antrim and
District Electricity Special Order, 1932,
to undertake the distribution of electricity throughout the greater part of the
County of Antrim and the Western
portion of the County of Londonderry.
The terms of the transfer have been
agreed between the Council and the Com-

:-

Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the Loans
Guarantee Bill, the Exchequer and
Financial Provisions Bill, the Civil
Authorities (Special Powers) Bill, and
the County Officers and Courts Bill, to
which they desire the concurrence of the
Senate.
They agree to the Amendments made
by the Senate to the Stormont Regulation
and Government Property Bill, and the
Preferential Payments in Bankruptcy
Bill, without Amendment.
Loans Guarantee Bill.
Moved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Long.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): The object of this Bill is
to extend to the 31st day of March,
1935, the time within which guarantees
115 Loans Guarantee Bill—

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Viscount Charlemont]
may be issued by the Ministry of Finance
under the Loans Guarantee Acts. The
authority given by the existing Acts will,
in default of the passing of this Bill,
terminate at the end of the present
month.
In the calamitous depression from
which our shipbuilding industry is at
present suffering, hon. Members will, I
feel sure, have no hesitation in supporting this Measure. The Government is
convinced that the existence of the credit
facilities thus made available has in the
past been successful in attracting work
to our local shipyards, and we hope that
it will have a similar effect in the not
very distant future.
It will be noted that the Bill makes
no provision for any extension in the
total amount which may be guaranteed
under the Acts. Hon. Members may be
interested to know that on 31st December last the total amount guaranteed was
£12,131,247. Of this amount £3,330,100
had however already been repaid, so that
within the existing limit of £13,000,000,
approximately £4,000,000 remains available. I beg to move, "That this Bill be
now read a Second time."
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure we are all
quite at one with the noble Viscount
in the hope that the Measure to which
this House no doubt is about to give a
Second Reading may effect some good
purpose in the future, just as its predecessors have done good work in the
past. The only remark that one seems
called upon to make is to utter the hope
that the benefit when it comes will not
be confined to shipbuilding, which is the
matter the noble Viscount particularly
referred to. The Bill, of course, is not
limited in its application to guarantee
in respect of the building of ships. The
guarantees could, if occasion arose, be
made equally well for the furtherance
of other industries, and we hope that
other industries will share in the benefit.
The only other thing one is likely to
notice is that this Bill, following the
precedents of 1926 and 1929, extends the
period of the guarantee to two years instead of, as has been the more usual
custom, for one year. However, I do
not suppose that will occasion any
difficulty. One supports the Bill with
more, perhaps, hope than actual expectation.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
also would like to say how pleased I am
to see that the Loans Guarantee Act
is to be extended for a further period.
I think it is a pity that a considerable
number of people hardly realise the great
benefit the Loans Guarantee Acts have
been to the shipyards of the City of
Belfast. They have been the means of
bringing a large amount of work to the
city, and giving employment to a considerable number of men who would
otherwise have been walking the streets.
There are a number of people who, in
their anxiety to criticise the Government,
entirely overlook the great benefit that
has been conferred on the shipyards by
the successive Loans Guarantee Acts,
and, like the hon. and learned Senator
(Mr. Leslie), I sincerely hope this Act
will be the means of bringing further
orders to our shipyards, because they are
undoubtedly very much in need of them
at the present time. At the same time
it is a good thing to know the Government are fully alive to the position, and
that by further extending this Act they
are doing their best to encourage the
placing of orders for ships in the shipyards of Belfast.
Mr. BARCLAY: I support the
Second Reading of this Bill, and, like
the previous speakers, I agree that it has
in the past been of great benefit to the
working men of the City of Belfast. At
the same time, it is not altogether the
working men I am concerned about, but
the rising generation. If the shipyards
had been working normally a large number of boys would have been serving their
apprenticeship to some trade in the shipyards. As things have turned out those
boys who would under ordinary
conditions have been serving their
apprenticeship to shipyard trades have
now grown too old to enter into an
apprenticeship, and the result is that the
shipyards have practically lost a generation of skilled workers. I hope this
Bill will be the means of bringing orders
to our shipyards so that boys of from 14
to 16 years of age may become
apprenticed to one or other of the shipyard trades. If this is not done to some
extent at least, we are going to be greatly
handicapped in the future. Shipbuilding may revive in the course of a few
years, when many of the older generation
of shipyard workers have passed away,
and we would not have a sufficient number of skilled men to take their place.
117 Financial Provisions Bill-

21 MARCH 1933
Second Reading
We might have to apply for foreign
skilled labour to do the work that should
be done by our own countrymen. We
do not want in the future to have a community of labouring men, but a community of skilled craftsmen like their
fathers and grandfathers, who gave the
Belfast shipyards such a great name
throughout the length and breadth of the
world. I have great pleasure in supporting this Bill in the hope that it will be
of some benefit to the rising generation.
Mr. QUIGLEY: It is with interest.
and pleasure that I support this further
extension of the Loans Guarantee Act,
knowing the great amount of work which
has been obtained for our workpeople
through the medium of this Act in the
past. It should not be forgotten that
some fifty ships were built in the Belfast
shipyards that would not have been built
here had it not been for the operation of
the Loans Guarantee Act. Those ships,
it should also be remembered, were built
at a time when shipbuilding across the
Channel was at a very low ebb. I join
in the hope expressed by other hon.
Members that this Bill will lead to a
return of prosperity in our shipbuilding
industry, and I am optimistic enough to
believe that that will prove to be the
case. I trust that the time is not far
distant when our shipyards will obtain
valuable orders for ships which will enable our unemployed workers to return
to work.
On Question, Motion, "That the Bill
be now read a Second time," agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for to-morrow.
Exchequer and Financial Provisions Bill.
Moved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Long.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This Bill
provides the necessary statutory authority
for various financial operations involved
by recent Government policy, and contains, in addition, certain amendments of
existing enactments which experience has
shown to be necessary.
In Clause 1 provision is made for bringing into account, as part of the revenue
of the current year, the sum of £350,000,
received as an adjustment of Northern
Ireland's residuary share of reserved
taxation in respect of prior year's
revenue. Under existing legislation,
this sum would ordinarily have been used
to increase the Reserve Fund, but having
regard to the circumstances which led to
the necessity for this rather large adjustment-one of the main factors was the
unusually early receipt of tax payments
in response to the appeal made by the
Chancellor of the Exchequer at the close
of 1931-the Minister of Finance's
Budget proposals for the financial year,
1932-33, entailed the diversion of this
sum to the year's ordinary revenue.
Clause 2 deals with the scheme of short
term loans to farmers, recently introduced, and in Clause 3 power is taken to
increase, by £2,000, the maximum
amount which may be paid to aid the
Soldiers' and Sailors' Land Trust in their
scheme for the construction of houses for
ex-Servicemen in Northern Ireland.
The provisions of Clause 4 are designed
to meet the arrangements which have
been made for the financing of schemes
of road construction for the relief of unemployment and distress. As hon. Members are no doubt aware, it has been
decided to charge the Government share
of the cost of these schemes against the
Road Fund, but in order to avoid placing
the Fund under an obligation which
might prove too great for it to bear, it is
provided, in the first place, that the
amount shall be limited to the existing
commitments under this head, amounting to £350,000; and in the second place,
that this £350,000 shall be paid from the
Road Fund by equal amounts of £50,000
in each of the next seven years, and no
interest is payable. Any excess over this
amount will remain as a burden on the
Exchequer. I know that many hon.
Members dislike these proposals.
would, however, ask them to remember
that the alternatives lie in the raising
of special taxes, or rates, for the purpose,
and the Government is convinced that in
the existing circumstances we have
chosen the method which entails the
smallest amount of hardship for our
people as a whole.
I
It is I think, unnecessary for me to
delay the House with the details of the
remaining Clauses of the Bill. They are
designed to remove points of difficulty
which have arisen in connection with
Government borrowing, Government
loans, and Government investments, and
119 Financial Provisions Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Viscount Charlemont]
I shall be pleased to furnish on the Committee Stage any more detailed information which hon. Members may desire. I
beg to move,
"That the Bill be now read
a Second time."
Mr. LESLIE: I think we may take it
from what the noble Viscount has said
that the principal part of the Bill before
us may be regarded as authorising, or
sanctioning rather, transactions which are
more in the nature of book-keeping transactions than anything else. I think that
is a very fair view to take of the Bill.
Some of the Clauses do, of course, invite
more close examination and, perhaps,
some discussion. Clause 2, for instance,
the Clause relating to the provision of
funds directed to the assistance of agriculture, or rather the guaranteeing of
such funds may be regarded, in fact I
think it ought to be regarded, as to some
extent the answer of the Government to
the plea put up here and there I may
say in decreasing force-for the postponement or adjustment, or even for complete
cancellation of the land purchase
annuities due from tenants who bought
their land under the Wyndham Act.
That particular proposal has, as the
House knows, been ruled out of court
again and again, because it
it would
necessarily run counter both to the spirit
and the letter of the Government of
Ireland Act, and also because it would be
undesirable for other reasons. But this
Clause does indicate the intention of the
Government, with the support of Parliament, to do what it can to help the agriculturist through a period of very great
difficulty, a period of difficulty which is
just as great to him as the period of difficulty through which those of our men,
who would like to be at work in the shipyards and cannot get work, are passing.
This Clause is reminder that the
Province is inter-dependent, and that
each particular class of industry has its
own special problems.
a
Clause 3 will awaken almost enthusiasm
amongst everybody, because there has
long been a feeling that we were not,
perhaps, doing all that might be done to
help those very desirable schemes for providing houses for those who served in the
war. Clause 4, as indeed the noble
Viscount indicated, is a Clause which is
not very likely to command anything
approaching enthusiasm in any part of
the country districts. I am not quite
sure how those of our own Members, who
happen to be county councillors, would
view the proposals, but I am afraid that
if the House looks at it from the point
of view that this House, after all, is not
directly concerned with the financial provisions it will not be possible to bring
forward any grave objection.
It has
has been the practice of late
years in the Budgets on both sides
of the Channel, to lay less and less importance on the sources of individual items
of revenue, that is to say, the policy of
ear-marking this or that source of revenue
for this or that purpose has been tending
to disappear, and I believe that the
greater number of our financial experts
would like to see that course disappear
entirely. We have had
had plenty of
examples. When the last petrol tax was
put on in Great Britain it was supposed
to be put on as a method of financing
derating.
it
Looking at from the
Northern Ireland standpoint, the amount
of extra revenue raised from petrol would
not look at the cost cast upon the
Exchequer by the introduction of further
derating. Again, the additional cost of
that derating in Northern Ireland bears
very close, although
although a completely
accidental, resemblance to the amount
received annually from the payments
a
made under the 1903 Land Act. That is
a mere accident, but it is a fact that has
been noticed more than once.
do
not
We
Taking it all round, I
think the supporters of the Road
Fund can formulate any really strong
case against the proposals to which effect
is given by this particular Clause.
all know that unemployment must be
helped where practicable, and we feel it
is much better that men out of work
should be set to do something really useful rather than receive money for doing
nothing. If work can be found on the
foundation of the roads and for the
widening of narrow roads, and not
extravagant interference with existing
corners, it does seem not unreasonable
that the Road Fund should bear the cost
of that, or at least some portion of it.
The rest of the Bill is almost entirely
administrative, and I do not think it will
provoke any comment at all.
bound to say that, in my humble opinion,
it is quite an admirable Measure, to
which this House ought not to raise any
objection.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
I am
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for to-morrow.

125 Financial Provisions Bill
