Civil Authorities (Special Powers) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): The Bill, which I am now
moving, requires no long or detailed explanation. This Parliament has conferred
on the Government through the medium
of the Civil Authorities Act of 1922,
special powers for dealing with all forms
of unrest and attempts to create disorder
in our midst, and I think it may safely
and undeniably be claimed that the fact
that we have this Act on our Statute
Book, and the manner in which its provisions have been administered, have
resulted in a continued peace in our Province which compares favourably with
that in most other countries. The Government feel that it is their duty to ask
that the powers which they hold under
the Civil Authorities Act may be continued in order that they may be able
to deal with any situation or difficulty
which may arise in connection with the
administration of law and order.
The keynote of the Act of 1922 is to
confer on the Minister of Home Affairs
authority to take all such steps and to
issue all such orders as may be necessary
for the preservation of peace and maintenance of order, but, as the House is
aware, in cases of emergency it is frequently necessary to act with the utmost
promptitude, and neglect or inability to
act quickly may neutralise the steps which
should be appropriately taken.
Accordingly, it is felt that the Minister
of Home Affairs should have power in
cases of unavoidable absence or illness
to entrust his authority to the Parliamentary Secretary of the Ministry of
Home Affairs, and it will be noticed that
effect is given to this principle in Clause 1
of the Bill.
The Act of 1922 empowered the
Minister to delegate all or any of his
powers to any officer of police, but in
this connection it should be mentioned
that the Civil Authorities Act of 1922
was passed on the 7th April of that year,
whereas the Constabulary Act, establishing the Royal Ulster Constabulary, was
not passed until the 31st May of that
year. Accordingly, in order to harmonise
the two Acts we are now replacing the
expression "any officer of police," which
appears in the Civil Authorities Act of
1922, by the more appropriate expression
of
"any officer the Royal Ulster
Constabulary."
The Act, as I have said, has been a
valuable asset to the Government and to
the whole community, and it is an Act
that we must retain on the Statute Book
until the Government and Parliament
are satisfied that its provisions are no
longer necessary.
In former years steps have been taken
periodically to extend the provisions of
the Act of 1922, but we feel that in all
the circumstances the most appropriate
Civil Authorities Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
way of dealing with this matter is to
submit for the approval of the House a
clause to the effect that it shall remain
in force until Parliament otherwise
determines, a course which I am sure will
commend itself to every Member of this
House. I beg to move.
Mr. DORMAN: I take it for granted
that we are all proud of our inheritance
of the civil and religious liberties which,
as subjects of the British Commonwealth
of Nations, we enjoy. To gain these
civil and religious liberties has meant
centuries of contest between the people
and the high authorities in the State
before they, in the first place, abolished
the final right of Kings, and took from
the Executive, on behalf of the Crown,
the power of depriving the citizens of
their civil liberty when a charge was
preferred against them, and gave them
the right to appear before a court of
justice for the purpose of proving their
innocence of the charge which the
Executive might at any time accuse them
of. There is no part of the British
Commonwealth of Nations in which the
toast of our civil and religious liberties
is so frequently and extensively drunk
as it is in this particular area under the
jurisdiction of the Government
Northern Ireland.
of
Before encouraging the continuance of
this Act on the Statute Book it is well
to consider the difference in the conditions
that exist now compared with the conditions that obtained when this Act was
first placed on the Statute Book of Northern Ireland. Then there prevailed in
our midst a state of chaos, outrage,
assassination, incendiarism, and a state
of mass psychology under the influence
of which many of our citizens perpetrated
deeds which, had they not been under
the influence of religious and political
hatred, they would never have perpetrated. I have no desire to recapitulate
any of the incidents that occurred in
those days further than to state that
they were beneath the most degraded
savage of the human race. Under such
circumstances the Government was
justified in seeking powers which would
give the executive officers the right to
deprive the ordinary citizen of his.
liberties, which as British subjects we
have the right to be proud of.
I do not believe that any self-respecting or liberty-loving officer of the Royal
Ulster Constabulary will joyfully accept
the responsibilities which this Act is going
to impose upon him. The function of
the Royal Ulster Constabulary is to keep
order and to detect crime, to bring any
citizen suspected of being guilty of a
crime before a court of justices, and to
substantiate the charge by the production
of evidence to prove the guilt of the person accused of committing the crime.
believe the Royal Ulster Constabulary
enjoy the confidence and respect of every
citizen of this particular area, but this
Act is going to impose on them the right
to be not only the prosecutor but the
judge in the case of charges which they
themselves have originated.
It may be said, and it has been said,
that these powers have not been unjustifiably used in the past, but already, since
this Act was first placed on the Statute
Book, it has been put into operation in
regard to citizens of two other States in
the British Commonwealth of Nations.
Those persons were arrested and afterwards deported without any charge being
preferred against them by the authorities in Northern Ireland. It is at this
particular time in the history of Europe,
at a period when Constitutions are tumbling, and when Parliamentary powers are
being superseded by dictators everywhere
in Europe, that we are going to continue
on the Statute Book of Northern Ireland
an Act which gives the Executive the
right to deprive the ordinary citizen of
his liberty.
It has been said that this Act has not
been used, and it is not intended to be
used unjustifiably. On the first occasion
on which the Minister of Home Affairs
was present in this House I stated that I
did not think he or any other statesman
in this or any other country was Angelic
enough to have reposed in him the powers
given under this Act except in a case of
extreme urgency. We have just had his
Majesty's judges congratulating the grand
juries at assizes on the absence of
organised crime of any kind in the community, and yet it is at such a peaceful
period in our history that we are going
to perpetuate this particular Act to deprive citizens of the freedom of which they
are justifiably proud.
Since the Act was placed on the
Statute Book it has been used to
suppress the freedom of speech of
a political party opposed to the Government. I myself presided at a meeting
called under the auspices of the Labour
129 Civil Authorities Bill-

22 MARCH 1933
Second Reading
party in Templemore Avenue.
It was
attended by from 600 to 800 persons,
and after I had uttered my opening
remarks a drumming club turned out and
drummed out these people, who were
peacefully assembled to listen to expressions of opinion to which they might be
politically opposed. Was this Act used
for the purpose of preventing that unnecessary and unconstitutional interference with a peaceful meeting on the part
of a small section of their political
opponents? No. Not only were they
allowed to break up that meeting, but
they followed it up and broke it up on a
second occasion. That happened in spite
of the powers possessed by the Minister
of Home Affairs under this Act, and yet
meetings, including meetings of political
opponents of the Government, were not
allowed to take place in certain areas. I
do hope the House will refuse to consent
to the perpetuation of this Act, but even
if no other hon. Member votes against
the Second Reading I, at any rate, feel it
my duty as a British subject, who is proud
of the civil and religious liberty he has
inherited, to divide the House and to
record at least a protest against the attempt to undermine the principles of our
Constitution as proposed by this Act.
Mr. LESLIE: The main question before
us this afternoon which is raised by this
Bill is whether the Civil Authorities
(Special Powers) Act of 1922 should expire on 31st December of this year, or
whether it should be continued for a
further period, be that period definite or
indefinite. The point is whether the
Statute is to be allowed to lapse at the
end of December. I do not believe that,
with the exception of the hon. Member
who has just sat down, there is any
Member of this House who would be prepared to allow that Act of 1922 to expire
in December of this year. Certainly I
myself would have nothing to do with any
proposal that the Act should disappear in
that way when nothing has been provided to take its place. I want to make
my position on that matter perfectly
clear to the House, because I shall presently be speaking more definitely as
regards the period of continuation.
At the moment I should oppose most
strongly any proposal to terminate the
existence of the Act at the date at present provided. My hon. Friend (Mr.
Dorman) has reminded us very pointedly
that we are not in the year 1922, and
that our circumstances are very different
to what they were in 1922. In that I
am thankful to say he is right. There is
not the manifest and open disturbance
at present that there was ten or eleven
years ago, but I am afraid there are
indications of what I may call an undercurrent which would tend, and is intended
to tend, to force His Majesty to change
his counsels. I am afraid that that
undercurrent does still exist in the
Province.
Senator Dorman referred to the charges
delivered by the judges on circuit. A
very few days ago I was present at the
Antrim Assizes, and Antrim is a very
law-abiding county. There were five
persons in the calendar, one of them
charged with petty larceny, two with
shooting into a house-a crime which
had no political significance and two
others with offences of unlawful drilling
and possession and presentation of firearms. Last
summer, if I remember
rightly, at the Antrim Assizes there was a
case of an armed raid on a house at Glengormley. There were not many of those
matters, but they show that such crimes
do still form a considerable proportion
of the very small calendar common to all
law-abiding counties. Then again, the
hon. Senator suggested that the weapons
placed in the hands of the Minister by
this Act were only used against political
opponents.
Mr. DORMAN: I did not say they
were only used against political opponents.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sorry. I misunderstood you, and I will withdraw
that at once. I should like, however to take
the opportunity of bearing my testimony
to the extreme moderation with which
these powers have been used, to the fact
that, so far as I can judge, they have
served a useful purpose, and even more,
perhaps, to the extent to which those
special powers have enabled the Royal
Ulster Constabulary to go on from
strength to strength in their proper
duty of securing the peace and preservation of good order in the Province. I
believe they have to a great extent been
able to do that by means of the Act as
it was on the Statute Book. Therefore,
I cannot for a moment, support the
suggestion that it should be allowed to
lapse.
C
Civil Authorities Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Lesliej
As regards the Bill itself, there are
two Amendments brought forward. There
is nothing in the first Amendment of the
existing Act, which enables the Minister
of Home Affairs to delegate his powers
to the Parliamentary Secretary. I think,
a3 a matter of fact, that that could
be achieved by a more or less roundabout method by the use of the Ministers
Act which we passed in 1924, when the
present Minister of Home Affairs was
unhappily indisposed. But I imagine
the House will agree that it is better to
put it directly into the Act, because as
long as the Parliamentary Secretary to
the Minister is a Member of Parliament,
I cannot see that any difficulty can arise.
If he were a civil servant I should not
support the proposal, but the Parliamentary Secretary, except possibly for
some brief interval, must be in Parliament, and therefore I think no difficulty
can arise. The second Amendment
amounts to nothing but a revision of
nomenclature.
If the House agrees that the main
question has been decided, and that the
two proposed Amendments of the law are
not such as to give us any trouble, we are
then left with comparatively academic
questions to consider, and those comparatively academic questions are, of
course, the questions of the duration, and
control of the duration, of the continuance
of the Act of 1922. Those are questions
which I venture to suggest this House is
very competent indeed to discuss and consider calmly, certainly without prejudice
one way or the other, and with very considerable knowledge of the events of 1922
and the course of events since that year.
Undoubtedly the Act of 1922 does make
provision outside the ordinary law for the
exercise of various powers, and I am
going to suggest to the House that those
various powers can definitely be divided
into three classes the preventative powers,
the punitive powers, and the administrative powers. Of those three divisions the
first is the most important.
I need
scarcely remind the House that we are all
agreed that prevention is better than
cure, and it is obvious I think that prevention in the case of any particular
delinquent must begin to operate before
the question of punishment for his crime.
can possibly arise, because again, to
quote a familiar proverb, you must catch
the hare before you cook it. The third
matter of administration
ancilliary to the other two.
is merely
I do not intend to go much into details
as regards the preventative powers, the
most important of which are contained in
the Act of 1922. I do not think it would
serve any useful purpose to go into these
things at any great length to-day, and I
think perhaps one might possibly do
harm, because there would always be the
risk of somebody suggesting that there was
some difference of opinion amongst us on
principle, instead of some difference of
opinion as to how the principle should be
carried into effect. But I would say
before I pass from it that some of the
preventative powers which are in force
now and are occasionally exercised are not
to my mind very greatly different from
certain other powers which have been
used in England since the time of
Edward III., which is a very considerable
number of years ago. I do not think
Senator Dorman got quite right the
powers which are conferred on the
constabulary. I was not quite able to
follow him in what he said about that.
I am not going to say any more about
these preventative powers. I want to go
on to other matters that arise under the
Act.
or
I am going to mention three matters
rather for the purpose of directing
thought towards those three matters than
because there is anything material in the
points themselves. Under the Civil
Authorities (Special Powers) Act, 1922,
it is possible to inflict the death penalty.
for something less than murder
treason. That has been the law since
1922.
never
That penalty has
been
inflicted for those offences. I do not
think it ever would be so inflicted, but I
do not think that such a penalty should
be inflicted except in the face of such a
crisis as did unhappily exist in 1922, but
does not exist now.
Another point is the power which is
given of interference with the ordinary
law as regards inquests, and the third
point is the very extensive punitive powers
which are given to resident magistrates,
powers of imprisonment up to two years
without trial by jury, and subject only to
appeal to a county court judge sitting
alone. Those are, of course, serious
powers. They were meant to be. They
were required at the time, but they are
powers which, as I think, being still outside our ordinary law, ought only to be kept
Civil Authorities Bill-
Second Reading.
22 MARCH 1933
in force, or permitted to remain in force
under close Parliamentary supervision.
The position ought to be reviewed by
Parliament from time to time. For such
review the steps we have hitherto taken
have been accepted as sufficient for maintaining Parliamentary control. For some
years the duration of the Act of 1922 was
extended for a year by Expiring Laws
Continuance Bills. In 1928 it was
thought it might save trouble to give a
five years' extension at one time, and it
is that five years' extension that is to
expire next December.
We have to-day an entirely new proposal. It is now suggested that the Act
of 1922 shall continue in force
" until
Parliament otherwise determines." I
may digress to remind the House that in
1928, when the Continuance Bill was first
brought forward in another place, a proposal was then made to make the Statute
permanent, but the Government did not
persist in the proposal, and the period of
five years was adopted as an alternative.
The proposal to-day is that the Act of
1922 shall continue in force until Parliament otherwise determines. I do not like
that proposal at all. It seeks to put the
saddle on the wrong horse. To my mind,
where special powers are required it is for
the Government to come to Parliament at
relatively short intervals and ask for a
continuance of the special powers which
adverse circumstances might demand.
""
I imagine, of course, that attention
will be directed to the mystic words,
"until Parliament otherwise determines,'
because those are words which do not
usually find a place in an Act of Parliament. I cannot attach very much importance to those words. I know they are
used here, as they are used in every
Act where they occur, as a pointer
to the fact that the Parliament
of the day does not intend that
particular Statute to be permanent.
There are many examples which could be
given of the use of the words in that
way, but I cannot attach much importance to them because no Act of Parliament has got any longer currency than
Parliament is willing that it should enjoy.
There is no Act of Parliament which cannot be repealed by a succeeding Parliament at short notice, nor is there any
Act of Parliament which cannot be challenged in either House by a Private Member's Bill or by question and answer.
There is no Act that can be described as
absolutely permanent. Even the most basic
of all our Statutes, Magna Charta itself, in so far as it is a Statute, the Habeas
Corpus Act, the Bill of Rights, the Act
of Settlement, and even the Government
of Ireland Act, 1920, have been reviewed
by later Parliaments.
""
were
I said just now that these words, "until
Parliament otherwise determines,'
fairly commonly found in Acts of Parliament. May I remind the House of a
Bill we dealt with the other day, a Bill
for the better protection of grey seals.
Most of us referred to the original Act
of 1914, an English Act brought in as a
temporary Measure. The Act of 1914,
enduring for four years, had these very
words, "unless"-not "until"-Parliament otherwise determines." Now the
history of that Act is normal enough.
After the four years were up the
Bill was continued year after year by the
Expiring Laws Acts, until the other day
it was made permanent in England, and
we are making it permanent in Northern
Ireland.
we
That is one example. Another example
is this very
Bill itself, the Civil
Authorities Bill, because that wound up
in 1922 in its penultimate Clause, I
think, with "This Act shall continue in
force for one year and no longer, unless
Parliament otherwise determines." That
is the phrase used there, and, as
know, it has been continued for ten years.
I fear that if Parliament sanctions the
present proposal the result will be to let
in that policy, or lack of policy, which
is profanely called drift, of which we
have a rather striking example in the
case of our Local Government Act of
1921, the Emergency Act, which was
again given currency for one year unless
Parliament otherwise determined. Parliament has not otherwise determined year
after year, and in the Expiring Laws
Continuance Acts since 1921 that Act has
been continued.
I do not feel that we should be discharging our duty as a second chamber if
we allowed this most important matter
to drag on indefinitely. I think, on the
contrary, the time has now come for what
I would like to describe as a new edition
of the Act of 1922, and I think we must also
recognise that the time has come not only
for a new edition of the Statute, but for
recognising that some provisions which it
contains will necessarily have a very long
if not a perpetual lease of life. Northern Ireland has not an insular position,
Civil Authorities Bill-
THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Leslie]
and it seems to me idle to continue to
govern ourselves as though it had.
a
Let us look back. Since 1922 we have
enriched the Statute Book by the Criminal
Evidence Act, 1923, and by the Emer-
1926,
gency Powers Act,
General
Emergency Powers Act to be brought
into force if necessary by a proclamation
by His Grace, distinct from the Local Government Powers Act. We have also been
enabled to set up a Court of Criminal
Appeal in Northern Ireland. It seems
to me that these three facts alone justify,
even require, a re-examination of the Act
of 1922, and, indeed, of the whole statutory position connected with that Act.
When I say the whole statutory position,
I mean deliberately to include the Restoration of Order in Ireland Act, 1921, or
so much of it as is left, and to some extent the Explosives Act of 1883, and the
Firearms Act, 1920.
Holding the view that the time has
come not for letting the Act sink into
desuetude entirely, but for bringing it
more up to date. I propose to move, on
the Committee Stage, an Amendment restricting the immediate continuance of
the Act to three years in the hope that
advantage will be taken of that interlude to re-examine the whole question.
That re-examination might perhaps be
well undertaken with the help of a Parliamentary Committee of both Houses, a
Committee which certainly need not sit
in public. I believe if that course were
taken it would be found possible and not
even difficult to prepare a new Act which
might reasonably expect a long lease of
life, the long lease to which I have
already referred, because I think that
such an Act could be based on those
principles of Imperial law which commend themselves to us all. And I think
it would be found that what would be
necessary would not involve such a very
wide departure from the terms of the
existing temporary legislation as might at
first sight appear likely.
a
Ten years is
a long time and
good many things happen. Opinions to
some extent change, though facts do not,
as has been said before. Still I think
such a Statute could readily be produced.
It would be based on the principles to
which I have referred, and those principles, it will be remembered, while most
meticulously safeguarding the liberty of
the subject to which my hon. Friend referred, neither recognise nor admit any
right divine on the part of that subject
to conduct himself either feloniously or
treasonably. It is sometimes thought that
the liberty of the subject means the
liberty of everybody to do everything he
himself chooses without regard to what
effect that course of action may have on
I need
the liberties of other people.
scarcely add that I propose to support the
Second Reading of the Bill and that
nothing I have said is intended to be construed as opposing the necessity for continuing the Act to which this Bill refers.
Mr. BARCLAY: I just wish to support the Second Reading of this Bill,
because I think we should have this Act
continued for a considerable time yet.
Everything is not as peaceful as the hon.
Member (Mr. Dorman) would have us
believe. Although there are no acts of
violence manifested day after day, still
there is an undercurrent at work which
might explode some day and, perhaps, do
a tremendous lot of harm.
The hon. Member spoke of liberty. I
was thinking, when he was speaking of
liberty, what kind of liberty he meant.
I am
a lover of liberty, and I believe
every man should have liberty, but there
are two kinds of liberty. Some people
might claim the liberty which they
thought right, but which would interfere with the liberty of others, and might
interfere with the liberty of the great
majority of the community, and I would
say that such liberty would be wrong. I
think there should be some restraint put
on such liberty as that.
Now it is necessary that the Minister
who is responsible for the peace and order
of the community should be equipped
with power to take action at oncedrastic action if necessary.
Although
this Bill has been in existence for a
number of years there are no law-abiding
citizens who can say it has interfered
with them in any way as they went about
their lawful duty. And I have the
impression that this Bill has not been
used in the way it should have been. If
it had been used in the way it could have
been we might have saved a lot of trouble
last October in the City of Belfast. It
was no use putting it in force at the
eleventh hour, when the organisation was
complete and when the demonstration was
taking place. It was too late then. I
would have interfered at an earlier stage
and I would have prohibited these men
who stand at street corners and make
these inflammatory speeches to ignorant
Civil Authorities Bill-
Second Reading
22 MARCH 1933
working men, and make them in such a
way that they arouse ill-feeling, hatred
and enmity in the hearts of these men.
We have seen from our experience of last
The
October how these things finished.
with
lives of two citizens have been lost,
the result that the citizens of Belfast will
have to compensate these families, and it
is going to be a burden on the rates
during the coming year.
The Government has been too lenient.
It is necessary that the Ministry should
have these powers continued so that it will
be in a position, if any organisation
establishes itself in Belfast or in any other
city or town throughout Northern
Ireland with the object of overthrowing
the present Government, or any properly
constitued Government elected in Northern Ireland, to arrest and punish those
disloyal organisations that have, for their
object, the overthrow of government or
the overthrow of society, or of our social
system, or the disorganisation of our
trade and industry, or interference with
our civil and religious liberties in any
way.
The Ministry should have power to
interfere, and to act at once, to bring
these things to an end, either by deporting these people who come into our
country or, if they are citizens, imprisoning them. We cannot allow this to go
on, because we do not know where it will
end. We read in the papers how it
finished in other countries, such as Russia
and Spain, and it has finished in terrible
disaster in a good many places. I have,
therefore, great pleasure in supporting
this Bill, and I hope, in future, that the
police authorities, as well as the Minister
who is responsible for law and order in
the country, will keep a watchful eye on
these movements, so that we will not have
drilling by a disloyal organisation at the
very backdoor of the City of Belfast. We
had enough of that in 1920 and 1923.
We do not want any more of it at the
present time.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
cannot agree with my hon. and learned
Friend (Mr. Leslie) about bringing in this
Bill as the necessity arises. I think this
power is a power that should be in
existence and not have it brought up
before Parliament to be passed when the
whole of the country is in a state of confusion. It then looks, perhaps, like the
persecution of a section. Therefore, I
would like to see it made permanent. I
do not think it will be abused. If it is,
Parliament has the power of seeing about
it.
I was greatly struck by the fact that
in the late financial crisis in the United
States of America, we all admired how
promptly the President acted and took
The
power to look after the finances.
reason he was able to do that was because
in 1917 a Measure was passed that was
intended to be temporary, but it was
overlooked and not revoked, and within
the last fortnight the President of the
United States was able to put that into
force to the advantage, not alone of the
United States, but of the whole world.
If he had had to wait to get an Act
passed he would have lost some time and
Iwould not have been able to settle banking affairs as he has done. It was one of
those mistakes that benefit the world. I
hope that instead of making this Measure
temporary it will be made permanent and
we, in the Senate and in the other House,
will be able to question it if there are any
abuses. I heartily support the Bill.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I rise, with very
great pleasure indeed, to support the continuance of the Civil Authorities Special
Powers Act. Great necessity still exists
in Northern Ireland for this Act.
listened with astonishment to the speech
of Senator Dorman. I was thinking,
really, that he has been like Rip Van

Mr. DORMAN: No; I was awake.

Mr. QUIGLEY: Well, if he was
awake he must be well aware of what has
been going on in Northern Ireland since
1922 and the great difficulties the Government had to contend with. The hon.
Senator speaks of civil and religious
liberty, but this Bill gives liberty to
people who conduct themselves. He also
referred to the difference in the circumstances now.
That is due to this Bill still
being in force. I cannot understand at
the present time any person living in
Northern Ireland suggesting that this
important Bill should be done away with.
The time certainly has not arrived. The
difference in circumstances, as I have said,
has been referred to by Senator Dorman.
There are many cases of trouble in Northern Ireland that have not been detected,
but by having this Bill we have power
to deal with the people in Northern Ireland who are giving the trouble. There
County Courts Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Quigley]
are many persons opposed in every way
to our Government, and who use means
in different ways with a view to the detriment of our Government. It is up to the
Government to continue this
continue this Special
Powers Act and deal with all people
who are not inclined to abide by the laws
of Northern Ireland.
I
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The criticism of this Bill falls into two species,
that is, criticism in detail and criticism
against principle. Criticism in detail as
to the duration of the Bill can be better
answered, I think, at a later stage.
might say, however, in defence of the
proposal that it should be left to the
determination of Parliament, that the
Government feel it is an advantageous
thing to have a weapon of this kind up
one's sleeve, but not always advantageous
The Lord Mayor of Belfast
Armstrong, Mr. Henry B.
Barclay, Mr. William
Charlemont, The Viscount
Cunningham, Mr. Joseph
to draw attention to the fact that it is
there. They feel it would be better if
it was left to Parliament to determine
when the power should lapse, than to have
it brought into the limelight every year.
With regard to the criticism of principle made by Senator Dorman, I am sure
we are all agreed on the necessity for
these powers to be continued. As
Senator Leslie has said, Northern Ireland has not an insular position, and it
is an unfortunate fact that in such a
small community the reverberations of
political crime have a greater effect than
in a much larger community.
Question put, "That this Bill be now
read a Second time."
The House divided: Content, 14; Not
Content, 1.
CONTENT
Cunningham, The Right. Hon.
Samuel
Dickson, Mr. James H.
Dixon, Sir T. J.
Dobbs, Major A. F.
NOT CONTENT
Dorman, Mr. R.
County Officers and Courts Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This Bill
deals with miscellaneous matters affecting
county court judges, Clerks of the Crown
and Peace and other officers, and the
administration of the county courts
generally. In Clauses 1 to 6 will be
found the provisions relating to county
court officers. Perhaps the most important of these is that contained in
Clause 1, under which in future Clerks
of the Crown and Peace may be paid
a consolidated salary. These officers, in
addition to carrying out their duties in
connection with the County Courts
have also certain responsibilities in
connection with Registration of Title, and
the preparation of Voters' Lists, which
are matters reserved to the Imperial
Government under the Government of
Ireland Act, 1920, and they receive
separate remuneration in respect of each
of these three portions of their work.
The provision in Clause 1 of the Bill
carries into effect the recommendation of
a Viceregal Committee which issued a report on the duties of Clerks of the Crown
and Peace in 1920.
Lavery, Mr. T. R.
Leslie, Mr. James G.
Long, Mr. John A.
Quigley, Mr. A.
Sinclair, Colonel Thomas
In Clause 2 authority is given for the
appointment and payment of temporary
Clerks of the Crown and Peace. The
authorities which have up to the present
been relied upon for this purpose are
regarded as inadequate. Under Clause 3
registrars of county courts will be
appointed in the ordinary way as members of the county court service, instead
of by the county court judge personally,
as at present; the Clause will not, however, apply as respects any existing
county court judge, save with his consent.
By Clauses 4 and 5 the Clerks of the
Crown and Peace are absolved from the
necessity to give security, and from
personal liability in respect of anything
done in the course of their duties, whilst
Clause 6 contains various provisions designed to make the conditions of the
county court service in general correspond
more closely to those applicable to the
ordinary civil servant.
Clauses 7 to 10 of the Bill deal with
matters of county court administration,
and I think the wording is for the most
part self explanatory. Clause 11 is concerned with the jurisdiction of county
court judges, and it is hoped that the
141 County Courts Bill-

22 MARCH 1933
Second Reading
a
provisions of the Clause will make possible
more convenient and
and expeditious
arrangement of jurisdiction where the
circumstances so require. In Clause 12
we propose to remove the liability of the
county court judge for payment of salary
to a deputy during his illness or absence.
A similar amendment of the law relating
to English judges was made in 1919.
Clause 13 of the Bill has been rendered
necessary by an interpretation of Section
5 of the Jury Laws Amendment Act of
1926, as depriving the Attorney-General
and the private citizen of the right to
send up an indictment at quarter sessions
in a
case where the magistrates have
refused informations. The Clause
restores to the subject the right to the
protection of the county court.
I do not propose to delay the House
further with the provisions of the Bill
which, in view of their varied nature,
lend themselves to debate in Committee
rather than on Second Reading. I beg
to move,
"That this Bill be now read a
Second time."
Mr. LESLIE: Certainly, this Bill, as
the noble Viscount said, is of a very
miscellaneous character. It is, in fact,
rather more miscellaneous than I thought
it was going to be. I am almost inclined
to think if I had known more about the
Bill yesterday I would have suggested
that the Second Reading should not be
taken so soon. I agree with what the
noble Viscount said at the conclusion of
his speech, that it would be better to
take in Committee any points likely to
arise. The only objection to that is, we
will be faced with something like
Second Reading debate on each Clause.
This is inevitable in a miscellaneous Bill,
almost any clause of which might have
been the subject of a separate Measure.
a
The proposal to make the appointment
of the registrar a permanent one, independent of the will of the county court
judge, seems to me very closely to
resemble the position of the Under
Sheriff. Most of us remember that each
incoming High Sheriff had to appoint his
own Sub-Sheriff. It is only in the last
five and twenty years that the appointment of the Sub-Sheriff of the county has
been made permanent. Again, the proposal to allow county court judges
to assume a more peripatetic character
than they have already, appears very
closely to resemble the power we extended
to resident magistrates some years ago.
Resident magistrates have now jurisdiction throughout Northern Ireland.
Finally, there is a proposal in Clause
10 to appropriate, for the benefit of the
Exchequer, certain unclaimed moneys.
Well, that follows a precedent set in
England, though I think, by something
higher than the county court, and out of
the money the law courts at the corner of
Chancery Lane were, in fact, built;
power being, of course, taken to pay anybody who, in due course, established his
claim to the money. That brings up a
point on which I ought to warn the
Government I shall want to know something at a later stage. Sub-clause (3) of
Clause 10 provides that, in the event of
anybody satisfying the county court that
he is entitled to payment of some moneys
transferred to the Exchequer, the court
is to order payment and the Ministry of
Finance shall issue such sums as appear
to the Ministry to provide for said payment. I shall ask for a little more
explanation as to what discretion, if any,
it is proposed to confer on the Ministry.
may be in some difficulty about it,
because the matter deals with the Consolidated Fund, but I shall hope an
explanation may be given to us, because
I think one could not tolerate, for, a
moment, the suggestion that if the county
court judge said that x pounds and y
shillings should be given to a person, the
Ministry of Finance could step in and
say that y pounds and x shillings should
be paid to him. I do not think that that
can be the intention.
I
The only other Clause I wish to
mention is Clause 13, which relates to an
amendment of the Juries Act of 1926. I
cannot help thinking that if Section (5)
of the Jury Laws Amendment Act had
not been passed it would not now have
been necessary to amend it. I cannot pin
my memory to the numeral, but I rather
think that that was the Clause to which
I objected at the time, and I seem to have
an idea that I pointed out that one of
the objections to an alteration of the law
was this very fact, that the private
person would be deprived of his right to
present a private indictment at quarter
sessions. However, any mistake that was
made there should certainly be corrected,
and one would commend the Government
for proposing to correct it. The only
thing is that one is inclined to think
County Courts Bill-
Adjournment-
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
seven years is a rather long time to allow
to elapse. It only suggests that the
policy of drift is rather more widespread
than one would wish.
As regards the right of the Attorney-
General, I shall again want information.
The Attorney-General very rarely, in
Northern Ireland, presents informations
off his own bat. It seems to me that the
question is only likely to arise if some
bench of magistrates has done something
it should not have done; if it has heard
and determined a case which it was only
required to investigate, and so dismissed
or refused informations that should have
been granted, or else the matter may be
an original matter of the Attorney-
General himself in his capacity as adviser
of the Crown in those matters. I cannot
help thinking that one would prefer that,
in any case in which Mr. Attorney has to
bring forward the Crown case, he should
do it at Assizes or City Commission, before
a judge rather than before a court of
quarter sessions.
To what extent it is likely that Mr.
Attorney would bring forward informations at quarter sessions I have no idea.
I know little about quarter sessions
practice in Northern Ireland, but I
should like rather more information on
Clause 13, particularly as regards those
powers of the Attorney-General. I do
not know that any further difficulty will
arise under the new Sub-clause 1 (b)
which it is proposed to insert in the Act
of 1926. That, I think, is part of the
original alteration of the law made in
1926. I do not think there is anything
else with which I need trouble the House
at the moment, but I do think that a
good deal of information is likely to be
asked for on the Committee stage.
On Question, Motion, "That the Bill
be now read a Second time," agreed to.
Adjournment.
Moved,
"That this House do now
adjourn." (Viscount Charlemont.)
Mr. LESLIE: Could the noble Lord
tell us whether we are going to sit
to-morrow?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Yes.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Half after Four o'clock.

Question to Minister.
FERRY AND PLEASURE BOAT
TRAFFIC.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :
To ask the Leader of the House
whether any representations have been
received by the Government respecting
disabilities imposed on those engaged in
ferry and pleasure boat traffic by the
existing state of the law relating to
mechanically-propelled watercraft; and,
if so, whether is is proposed to take
any action in the matter.
Mr. LESLIE: I think that most of
us perhaps have received certain circulars
dealing with the matter to which this
question relates. The suggestion is that
the application of the Merchant Shipping
Act of 1894, as amended by subsequent
legislation, presses somewhat hardly on
people concerned with the keeping of
ferry boats and craft of that description
for the purpose of profit. The suggestion
they make is that such boats are not seagoing craft, and that the regulations introduced under the authority of the
Statutes I have referred to ought not to
be applicable to them, and that the
attempt to apply them results in loss and
trouble. Whether there is anything in
their plea or not I do not know. Therefore, I have put the question on the Paper
in order that we may receive the considered reply of the Government.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): The Government have
recently received from the Magistrates'
Association of Northern Ireland representations as to an alleged grievance on
the part of certain boatmen in having to
Loans Guarantee Bill
Adjournment
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
comply with provisions of the Merchant
Shipping Acts. The hon. and learned
Member, I am sure, appreciates that
under the Government of Ireland Act
this question is only within the jurisdiction of this House in so far as it relates
to inland waters.
a
It is true that a mechanically-propelled
boat may not carry more than twelve
passengers unless Board of Trade
Certificate has been obtained in respect of
such craft. This certificate is not given
until there has been a survey made.
Before granting a certificate the Board
of Trade officials are most particular to
ensure that a number of safety devices
are provided. Thus the surveyor
examines the situation of the engine in
the boat, the insulation of motor spaces,
and the adequacy of the fire-extinguishing appliances. He does not object to
the carriage of a small quantity of petrol,
not exceeding two gallons per motor, to
facilitate starting the engines, but otherwise he requires that internal combustion
engines should be driven by paraffin or
other similar fuel or heavy oil.
Surveys in recent years have shown
that the main faults of passenger-carrying motor-boats lie in the inadequacy of
fire appliances and buoyant apparatus,
and the absence of any proper insulation of petrol tanks or motors or any
device for preventing petrol from leaking
out into the main portion of the boat.
As the hon. and learned Member will
appreciate, this whole system of survey is
designed to protect the public and, quite
apart from the constitutional aspect of
this question, I am advised that it would
be a retrograde step to dispense with the
survey in the case of petrol-driven boats
carrying more than twelve passengers.
Mr. LESLIE: Thank you.

Questions to Minister.
PROVISION OF WORKING-CLASS
HOUSES.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the same of Mr. Joseph
Cunningham:-
To ask the Leader of the House
whether the Government
to
propose
introduce further legislation to expedite
the building of suitable dwelling-houses
for the accommodation of the workingclasses, and to provide much needed employment for the building trade workers.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
put this question down to ascertain what
assistance the Government propose giving
to municipal authorities to enable them
to cope with the serious housing problem
which they are compelled to face, and
also to learn if they intend introducing
legislation which will encourage Building
Societies to advance money for the
erection of houses. A Bill has been
passed through the Imperial House
of Commons which enables Building
Societies to advance 90 per cent. of the
value of houses to be built instead of
70 per cent. I am told by those who, I
believe, are in a position to give reliable.
information, that if the same facilities
were available in Northern Ireland a
very large number of houses would be
built by building firms. The trouble, at
present is that they are short of money,
but if these facilities that have been
granted in England were available over
here it would result in the erection of a
large number of dwellings.
Apart from the supply of houses,
there is another important matter, and
that is the absorption of unemployed
workers in the building trades. We hear
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
a great deal at the present time about
wise spending, and it could not be argued
that it would not be wise spending if
by the erection of additional houses we
were able to provide employment for the
unemployed workers in the building
trades. This morning I happened to get
particulars of the number of men in the
various building trades who are at present unemployed. I am informed that
there are 1,700 joiners unemployed, 450
painters, 500 plumbers, 180 plasterers,
and 500 labourers, making the total
number of unemployed persons depending
entirely on the building trades 3,300 for
the City of Belfast alone. In view
of those figures, if it is possible to do
anything to relieve that situation it
should be done at once.
I am afraid we are becoming careless
regarding the housing of our people.
Yet there must be a very large number
who cannot claim to have a home to live
in, and not until we are able to supply
this demand can we expect to do anything to provide better accommodation
for those who are compelled to live in
small unhealthy houses, which are often
described as slums. I believe it is the
duty of the Government and local
authorities to provide people with suitable.
healthy houses. I remember reading a
speech which was delivered by Lord
Roberts some years ago, when he was
touring the country endeavouring to
waken the people to recognise the danger
of war, for which he considered Germany
In the course of
was then preparing.
his remarks he said that the people should
all be provided with comfortable homes,
so that they would have a greater pride
in their country, which they might be
required to defend. While his warning
did not receive the serious consideration
which it deserved, we all agree with his
wise suggestion that the people should be
comfortably housed. Yet we must admit
that the working class are more favourably
situated here than in some of the larger
cities across the Channel. They have
never been required to live in dark,
damp cellars such as we read of, but that
is no reason why we should allow things
to drift, and lose our good reputation.
Unfortunately the building of small
working-class houses is no longer attractive
to the building firms, because they cannot build to let them at rents which the
people can afford to pay. It is therefore
necessary for the Government or local
authorities to give financial assistance to
bridge the difference. That is the reason
why I would like to know what the
Government are prepared to do. Since
the subsidy was reduced building has
almost come to a standstill. The Government formerly gave a subsidy of £100,
and some of the local authorities gave
£40. I think the Belfast City Council
gave that amount. During 1928, under
the subsidy, there were 1,286 houses
built; in 1929 there were 2,255 houses
erected, and up to 31st March, 1931,
there were 3,507 houses built. It is remarkable to note that since the subsidy
was reduced by the Government and withdrawn by the municipal authorities, in
Belfast only 42 houses have been completed, and 67 are in course of erection.
That is in respect of the past two years.
Surely that is sufficient evidence that it
is necessary to take some action.
The Imperial Government gave liberal
financial assistance in their Slum
Clearance Act. I think they made an
allowance for every person disturbed.
But we in Belfast are handicapped in
that respect, because before anyone can
be disturbed
we must first provide
alternative accommodation, which we have
not got. It is only with the assistance
of the Government that local authorities
can deal successfully with this problem.
It is not my desire to bolster up the
Irish Free State, but I should like to
point out that at the present time the
Free State is giving a subsidy of £70 for
the building of small working-class
houses, and I read in the Press the other
day that with the aid of that subsidy
they are able to build houses to let at a
rent of about 4/- a week. If that can
be done in the Free State I do not see
what is to prevent it being done in Northern Ireland. The cost of material and
labour has been considerably reduced
recently, and surely this is a good time
to make a special effort to solve this
problem. Let
say in conclusion,
that you have a splendid type of people
who deserve all that can be done to
provide for their comfort. I would
therefore make a special appeal to the
Government to provide generous financial
assistance, and thus enable the local
authorities to provide the houses the
people require, so that they will be able
to spend their days in that degree of
comfort and security to which they are
so well entitled.
me
28 MARCH 1933
Oral Answers

Oral Answers
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to support the
appeal so ably made by Senator Cunningham on behalf of some active operation
being set going in regard to the housing
of the working classes in Belfast. I do
not intend to occupy the time of the
house further than to say that probably
Belfast, with all its boasting, has a record
on this point unequalled by any other
city in Great Britain or Northern Ireland. In the interval between the last
two census returns at least one ward in
the city of Belfast had an increase of
100 per
cent. in
one-roomed
dwellings, and some other wards have increased considerably. I would impress
upon the Government this fact, that an
increase of over 100 per cent. in oneroomed dwellings in any ward in the city
of Belfast means a jump in the death rate
of 300 per cent. more than in the case of
families occupying houses with four
Mr. BARCLAY: I agree with all the
hon. Senator has said, and I should like
to support the appeal he has made to the
Government. The trouble to-day in connection with house building is the lack of
money and the problem of how to get it.
Senator Cunningham has suggested one
or two ways in which the money might
be found to build houses for the working
classes. He referred to slum property,
but we have no slums in Belfast, although
there are slums in other places. But in
Belfast we have a large number of
narrow streets with small houses that
could be taken down and
three
every
streets made into two. That would give
air space and living space for the
inhabitants of those districts. I dare say
that if that work was taken in hand temporary provision could be made for the
people who live in those small houses.
But here again the question of money
comes in. The Corporation is not undertaking that kind of work at the present
time, and building contractors say there
is not sufficient money to enable them to
do it.
I think that if the Government increased its subsidy from £25 to £35, and
the local authority gave £15, a total of
£50, it might solve the difficulty. I was
speaking this morning to a gentleman
who is in the building trade and who is
the leader of one of the building trade
organisations, and I asked him if a grant
of £50 would be sufficient to enable contractors to bridge over the gulf so far as
slum property is concerned, and he said
it would. I hope the Government will do
something in the near future to provide
work for those thousands of skilled workmen who are walking about the streets
at the present time. The grants given
by the Government in the past have been
principally for the benefit of the unskilled workmen. Although it is necessary that something should be done for
them, the skilled workers have been
greatly neglected in the past, and this
would be one way in which the Government could come to their assistance and
enable them to keep their hands in
practice. It would also be of some benefit to the Government itself, because if
these men were in employment they would
be contributing to the Unemployment
Insurance Fund instead of drawing benefit.
Therefore, I hope the Government
will take some action on the lines suggested in the near future.
over
rooms or over.
The difficulty in Belfast is that the
working-class people cannot pay the
economic rent that is demanded, and the
economic rent cannot be paid because of
the abnormal price of land inside the
borough area. I have stated in this
House that if the Government of Northern Ireland undertook to act on the advice
of the Royal Commission set up by the
Coalition Government after the war, and
built houses at the rate of twelve houses
to the acre, it would take over £25 per
house to pay the interest and Sinking
Fund in order to get the money necessary
to take over the ownership of the land.
I do hope the Government will give some
attention to this matter, and I hope the
local boards will give it attention.
The hon. Senator has stated that there
are over 3,300 workers of the building industry unemployed, but I state here that
if the conditions and provisions of the
Public Health Act were enforced as they
ought to be enforced by the officers who
are paid by the people to see that they
are enforced, there would probably be a
thousand wood workers employed in
bringing existing working-class dwellings
in the city of Belfast into compliance with
the provisions of that particular Act.
hope the right hon. the Lord Mayor and
the hon. Senator who put this question
this afternoon will take that back to the
Corporation and see that the sub-sanitary
officers enforce the provisions of the
Public Health Act in regard to the
residences of the working classes.
I
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH: I
JLLAGE
would not like it to go out to the public
that we have slum areas in the city of
Belfast. We have not. You have every
year, in the ordinary course of events,
a certain number of houses that gradually
fall out. That is all. Slum areas have
been cleared. You have here and there,
as the hon. Senator says, narrow streets.
I took the opportunity a short time ago
to go through some of these houses, and
really they are a credit to the people who
Occupy them. They are perfectly clean
and well kept, and they have those houses
at a rent at which you could not possibly
produce them to-day. There is just the
point that I would not like it to go out
that the sanitary authorities at the City
Hall are not doing their duty. I want
to tell you they are.
In every case that
I am aware of where a house is not fit to
live in it is ordered to be taken down.
Generally speaking, the authorities are
doing their work and doing it well.
an
Then there is another question of the
erection of houses, and I am heartily with
Senator Cunningham in making
appeal to the Government to try to do
something to give employment to the
skilled workers who are going about
anxious and willing to work. It would
keep their hands in. I think the Government and the Corporation should get together to try to provide some of the
people with houses at a price. The
trouble to-day is to get houses at a price
at which you can let them at a reasonable
rent. But the public may not expect to
get down to the pre-war prices of 3s 9d
houses or 2s 9d houses we used to know.
The thing is impossible. But if both
authorities get together there is no doubt
they might be able to produce houses
which would be within the reach of the
working man, and at the same time would
be reducing unemployment amongst their
ranks.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This matter has been the subject of a conference
between representatives of the Ministry of
Home Affairs and the Belfast Corporation, and is now engaging the attention
of the Government.
I am not in a position to say more at
the present moment, but perhaps the hon.
Senator will ask a question or raise the
matter again after the Adjournment and
I will give further details.
Mr. LESLIE: Arising out of that,
will the Government be able to give us
any information-if they have not got it
already. Perhaps they have-showing
how the cost of materials, the rate of
wages, allowing, of course, for the increased cost of living, and the rate of
interest on money compares at present
with what might be described as prewar rates; also will the Government be
able to tell us whether there is any
reason to believe that the operation of the
Rent Restriction Acts has any bearing on
the reduction of the number of buildings
to which reference has been made.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will
endeavour to get the information asked
for by the hon. and learned Senator.
Perhaps when we meet again he might put
a question on the matter.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM:
When the Government give this return I
would be glad if they could tell us if
there are any vacant houses in the city.
My reason for asking is that some years
ago one would not have seen notices on
houses that they were to let. Now I see
quite a number of houses vacant. I would
like to know how many vacant houses
there are in the city of Belfast. Another
thing. In speaking of the joiners out of
work on account of the state of the building trade, should a number of those not
be credited to the idleness in the shipyards? There is a man living opposite
me who is a joiner out of work, and so
far as I know he never worked at anything but shipbuilding. I think for
years he has been working at shipbuilding. Those are two things I would be
glad if the Government would take notice
of in making the return.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think
the information about vacant houses
would be rather difficult for the Government to obtain, but I will see what can
be done in the matter.
BREAKDOWN IN THE TELEPHONE
SYSTEM.
Major DOBBS asked the Leader of the
House whether, in view of the grave inconvenience caused to subscribers by the
recent breakdown in the telephone
system, the Government will make
representations to the Imperial Government so that steps may be taken to avoid
similar serious inconvenience in the
future.
Oral Answers
28 MARCH 1933
Adjournment
CHARLEMONT: As the
Viscount
hon. Senator will be aware, the circumstances which led to an interruption of
certain telephone services in Northern
Ireland a few weeks ago were quite exceptional, the weather conditions having
been, I am given to understand, worse
from a telephone point of view than at
any time during the preceding fifteen
years. Under abnormal conditions of
this kind it is impossible to preclude all
possibilty of an interruption in the service, but undoubtedly the best means of
averting serious breakdown is the provision of underground telephone cables.
From inquiries which have been made by
my right hon. Friend, the Minister of
Commerce, it has been ascertained that
extensive works are already in hand, with
the ultimate object of providing an
underground system not only between
towns but also between villages throughout the whole of Northern Ireland. As
the hon. Member will readily appreciate,
work of this nature is exceedingly expensive, and the carrying out of the scheme.
must necessarily be spread over a period
of years.
The progress already made,
however, is considerable and my right
hon. Friend, the Minister of Commerce,
assures me that, relatively to population,
the rate of progress towards completion of
the scheme in Northern Ireland is greater
than in most areas, if not, in fact, in
any comparable area, in Great Britain.
Adjournment.
Moved: "That this House do now
adjourn until such date as Mr. Speaker
shall appoint."-(Viscount Charlemont.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Ten minutes before One
o'clock, until Tuesday,
25th April.
Papers Presented
Oral Answers

Tuesday, 25th April, 1933.

from 28th March-met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
