Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act) and ordered to lie on the Table.
Question to Minister.
SALE OF ALCOHOLIC BEVERAGES
IN HOTELS.
Mr. GLENDINNING: In asking the
question standing in my name I trust
no one will accuse me of any sympathy
with an increase in the sale of intoxicants
in this country. On the contrary, all
my wish and desire would be to promote
temperance and respect for the law. The
noble Lord in his reply will probably
inform me that my question is premature,
inasmuch as the Measure has not become
law. But that it will become law in a
very short time is now beyond question.
There was a very large majority in favour
of the Bill when put to the vote in the
Imperial Parliament, and it is now
assured of Government support.
If a Bill on something like the same
lines were passed in the Parliament of
Northern Ireland this session visitors to
the country and tourists during the summer and residents also would then enjoy
more comfortable hotel residence during
the ensuing summer. Members may
recollect that when the Sale of Intoxicating Liquor Amendment Bill was before
the Senate I unsuccessfully endeavoured
to have a clause inserted excluding hotels
from some of the provisions of the Act.
I then gave what I considered unanswerable reasons which I do not intend to
repeat at the present time.
I hold that even those restrictions on
hotel managers and guests were unfair
and unnecessary. Experience has only
confirmed that opinion. Indeed, I would
go as far as to say that Members of this
House themselves do not fully appreciate
the extent of their application. To prove
that I would mention that a Member of
this House not long ago was morally
guilty of a breach of the Act. A friend
to whom he was extending hospitality
that
laughingly told him
owing to
recent legislation the thing could not
be done. I beg to ask the Leader of the
House whether it is the intention of the
Government to introduce legislation to
amend the law regulating the sale of
alcoholic beverages in hotels and restaurants on the lines of the Measure at
present before the Imperial Parliament.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Legislation of the character of the Bill referred
to by the hon. Member would constitute
161 Civil Authorities Bill-

25 APRIL 1933
Committee
S
a clear departure from the principles of
the Intoxicating Liquor Acts of 1923 and
1927. I would remind him that it has
already been stated on more than one
occasion that the Government would not
introduce any fresh legislation on the
question of intoxicating liquor, and there
are no grounds, I think, which would
necessitate or warrant a departure from
this considered decision.
Mr. LESLIE: Arising out of that
might one inquire whether the noble
Viscount, as representing the Government, does not think that the growth of
some of our seaside towns-what I might
describe as the gradual growth, towards
the necessary maximum of, I think,
10,000, affords some justification for
giving some little concession, at all events,
to these seaside towns which naturally
desire to become as attractive as possible
for visitors!
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think the
situation outlined by the hon. and learned
Member can be met by existing legislation.
Civil Authorities (Special Powers) Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In moving
"That this House do now resolve itself
into Committee on the said Bill," I may
say I am pleased to welcome the right
hon. Gentleman, the Minister of Home
Affairs. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSE 1 (Amendment of 12 and 13
Geo. 5, c.5, s.1 (2) as to power of delegation) ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Extension of duration of
the Act of 1922).
(1) The Act of 1922 shall continue in
force until Parliament otherwise determines.
*
*
*
*
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause
2, after "until" to insert "the thirtyfirst day of December, nineteen hundred
and thirty-six and no longer unless."
The Clause will go on to read "unless
Parliament otherwise determines.'
question before us is in reality a perfectly
""
The
simple one. It is whether the Civil
Authorities (Special Powers) Act of 1922
is to continue in force indefinitely, as the
Bill proposes, or whether its continuance
should be restricted for the present and
without prejudice to the power of Parliament to give a further continuance, if it
so decides. If the continuance is to be
restricted to a further period I suggest
three years as being a sufficient extension
for the present at all events.
I want, so far as I can, to keep the
debate strictly to the point at issue. I
want to do that for two reasons. First
of all, the main question has already been
settled. The Senate has agreed that the
Act of 1922 is not to be allowed to lapse
on 31st December next, as it would have
done had Parliament not interfered or
been about to interfere. The second is
that I am still of opinion that a debate
on the general question of the Act would
not, if it were allowed to become in any
way unduly discursive, tend towards the
public interest. It does not seem to me
difficult at this stage of the debate, at
all events, to take that course, because
there has been ample time since the
Second Reading of the Bill now before us
for those hon. Members who were not of
the Senate in 1922-and that is something
more than three-fifths of the existing
House to read up, not only the Act of
1922 itself, but the thirty or forty regulations which were incorporated in that Act,
and if they chose they could have gone
further and pursued sundry other regulations, mostly of an ephemeral character,
through the various volumes of the
Statutory Rules and Orders issued since
that time.
Also, of course, those hon. Members I
have referred to have had the opportunity
of referring to the debates in both Houses,
not only on the Bill of 1922, but on such
continuations of the Act through the
medium of the Expiring Laws Continuance Bills and also the debates on the
Bill which subsequently became the Act
of 1928. That was the Act which gave
a continuance of five years to the Act of
1922. Similarly, those of us who were
Members of the Senate in 1922 have had
opportunities of refreshing our memories.
by the perusal of documents, and we have
had further opportunity of considering
the powers conferred by, and under, the
Act on the civil authority. There is
certainly no occasion this afternoon to
emphasise the moderation and the general
propriety with which the powers given in
Civil Authorities Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie
1922 have hitherto been exercised. That
has been done more than once already,
and I may, perhaps, say that my own
voice has not been altogether absent from
the chorus of approval.
But we are dealing to-day not with
the past, nor, possibly, with the present,
but essentially with the future. It cannot be denied that the powers under the
Act are not only very wide and very
strong they were meant to be both at
that time-but also of a very far-reaching
character. That attracts our attention
at the very first sight, and it cannot be
denied that such powers contain
potentialities alike of error and of evil.
It is almost equally obvious, I think,
that any application of many of the provisions must necessarily involve some
slight interference-perhaps it may be
greater or less, but at all events it must
involve some interference with the perfectly proper proceedings or proposed
proceedings of perfectly law-abiding
people. I do not want, for the reasons
I have given already, to go
into detail as regards the regulations
than may be necessary, but I may perhaps instance the first three or four, not
that they are the most important. They
are not. One in particular which I
think we all have in mind goes much
further, but I just instance these as showing that there is a power of interference
which might affect perfectly law-abiding
people.
more
That being so, the question I think at
once occurs to us-it must occur to us--
whether Parliament ought to give an indefinite lease of power to the civil
authority; that is, as the Act defines, to
the Minister of Home Affairs of the day, or
whether as an alternative course Parliament ought not to give those powers under
such conditions as would ensure that the
civil authority-and the civil authority,
as I have just said, is the Minister of
Home Affairs of the day, and I desire to
emphasise that point very strongly because
it cannot be expected that the Minister
of Home Affairs of the day or the
Attorney-General of the day, or indeed
any Member of the Government, of which
they are both component and very important component parts, will continue.
in office for ever. It is impossible that
they should. The alternative then is to
require the civil authority to come to
Parliament from time to time, if the
occasion requires, at fixed, but I do not
say at too frequent intervals in order to
obtain from Parliament, if he needs it, a
renewal of the power, and in order that
he may give an account, if Parliament
requires, of any action which he may have.
taken.
I may perhaps digress for a minute or
two to remind the Committee that there
is a third course, and for that third
course the interval of three years suggested in my Amendment affords ample
opportunity. That is to make certain
changes in our general law. Such
changes can, I feel confident, be readily
made, and would give all that is really
necessary, and they could be accompanied
with some statutory safeguard which is
undoubtedly required, a safeguard which
to the best of my knowledge and belief
is already supplied, or has been supplied
in the past, by administrative action, but
which is not to be found in the Statute.
That, I believe, could easily be done.
There is ample precedent for taking
special steps to deal with special
difficulties which arise from time to time.
It may be that the Explosive Substances
Act of 1883, and even more perhaps our
own Emergency Powers Act of 1926, will
occur to the minds of the Committee as
very fair examples.
However, to return to the general
question, I say that there can be only
one proper course. Parliament ought to
adhere most strictly to constitutional
principles, for it is on constitutonal principles alone that Parliamentary action
can properly be based. The Committee
will remember that the celebrated Peace
Preservation Acts, both of 1881 and 1882,
were granted in the first instance for
limited periods. They were, as some of
us will remember, extended again and
again, and did not finally disappear until,
I think, the year 1898 or 1900, but at
all events they were originally brought
in as a temporary Measure and extended
again and again. They were never placed
permanently-if the Committee will allow
the expression-even temporarily on the
Statute Book. They furnish, I suggest,
a very admirable precedent.
I
If the Committee accepts my suggestion
that it is constitutional principle that
should be our guide in this matter,
think it follows that Parliament would
be wrong, very wrong indeed, if they
were to issue a blank cheque, perhaps I
should say a blank letter of credit,
to be cashed or to be drawn, Parliament
165 Civil Authorities Bill—

25 APRIL 1933
Committee
does not know when, and by Parliament
does not know whom. If that be so it
would obviously be equally wrong for this
Senate to relax that power of restraint
which can be exercised to-day, but which,
if once lost, can never be recalled.
The Committee will remember, and I
have no doubt but that they will,
individually and collectively, remember,
that if this blank cheque is once signed
-if the indefinite tense be grantedchange can never come save by the
will of that Government, which is able to
command a majority at that time in the
other House. This Senate could not act
alone. We sit here to-day, as it seems to
me, as trustees for the people of Northern
Ireland, for all the people of whatever
class, creed, or description.
are
trustees for one and all of them. We are
the guardians of their rights and liberties.
I suggest it is not for us to expose the
rights and liberties of any one of our
wards to any form of risk in the future,
for we do not know what the future may
hold for Northern Ireland. At present
I need say no more. I beg to move the
Amendment standing in my name.
We
The MINISTER OF HOME AFFAIRS
(Sir Dawson Bates): May I first of all
thank this House for allowing me to
intervene in this debate. I would not
have done so if it had not been for the
fact that I have been responsible for the
administration of the Act during the past
twelve years, and it occurred to me that
possibly criticisms might be levelled
against the manner in which the Act was
administered, in which event I might be
of assistance to the House.
Personally, I am sorry that the hon.
and learned Senator has moved this
Amendment. He has used arguments
which I certainly, and I venture to say
the majority of the Members of this
House, cannot subscribe to.
With many
of his arguments I would agree if they had
been made, say in 1914, but I would
remind the hon. and learned Senator that
a lot of water has flowed under the bridges
since those days. I would remind him
that he is speaking in 1933. I am just as
strong in upholding the principle of
democratic Government as any Senator
in this House, but my experience during
the past twelve years has been that if it
had not been for the existence of this
Act-which Senator Leslie has described
as being not of a constitutional character
-I venture to say
Mr. LESLIE: I am sorry to interrupt,
but I think the right hon. Gentleman
suggested that I described his 1922 Act
as not being of a constitutional character.
I did not say anything about that.
Sir DAWSON BATES: Perhaps I
misunderstood the hon. and learned
Gentleman. Perhaps he suggested it
was unconstitutional because the civil
authority was given too much power.
Mr. LESLIE: I did not suggest that
at all.
Sir DAWSON BATES: If it had not
been for the existence of the Civil
Authorities Act on the Statute Book, and
the powers which are utilised under it,
there would have been no constitutional
Government left in Ulster to-day. If we
look all over the world to-day we see
democratic Governments tumbling to
pieces. This, I think, is mainly due to
the fact that these Governments had not
the power or were disinclined to maintain
law and order when a crisis arose and,
therefore, the-if I may use the phraseautocracy of mob law prevailed or else a
dictator had to be appointed.
The hon. and learned Senator who
I moved this Amendment wishes to have a
time limit of three years put on the Act.
I think this House, with one exception,
agreed to the principle of this Bill. We
were hopeful that the time would come
when we could safely repeal the Act or
not re-enact it, but I ask the hon. and
learned Senator will he, with his experience of the past twelve years, be prepared
to say that at the end of three years the
necessity for that Act will have passed
away. I would remind the House that the
Parliament that has enacted the Act can
repeal it, but in regard to this we are told
by the hon. and learned Senator that the
result would be that, owing to a policy
which he describes as drift," on the
part of the Government, the Act would
remain on the Statute Book indefinitely.
Surely the answer to that is this that,
if it were the general feeling that conditions had improved to such an extent
that it was no longer necessary or desirable to keep the Act upon the Statute
Book, it would be impossible in this
democratic age for any Government, in
face of such general feeling, to resist the
repeal of the Measure.

I would like to ask the hon. and learned
Senator to visualise the position of the
Civil Authorities Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Sir Dawson Bates]
Government at the end of three years.
It might be at that time that outwardly
the situation might be perfectly peaceful
and there might be no outward sign of
pending trouble. The Government, however, with inside knowledge, might take
and have very good reason for taking an
entirely different view as to the prospects
of peace, and it might deem it necessary
to have to come to the House again for
the re-enactment of the Measure. The
Government would then be placed in the
position of giving reasons why they
thought it was necessary that this Act
should be re-enacted, and this, I will say
perfectly frankly, might be extremely
embarrassing to the Government in charge
of law and order. On the other hand it
might be said that the Government in the
midst of peace, or so-called peace, were
acting in a purely provocative manner.
Personally, my own view is that the less
this Act is talked about the better. I am
sorry it should be necessary to have such
an Act in Northern Ireland. As I said
before, it has been largely responsible for
the peace which we now enjoy in our community and, therefore, the Government
are determined to retain the weapon
which has had the effect of maintaining
peace which is so necessary. I hope the
hon. and learned Senator will, on reconsideration, be prepared to fall in with
this principle. I only say this, that if
such conditions arise in future as to render
the existence of this Act on the Statute
Book unnecessary the Government will be
only too glad to submit a Measure for its
repeal, and with this assurance I hope the
hon. and learned Senator may see his way
to withdraw this Amendment, so that
there may be no question that this House
is determined to back up the Government
in maintaining law and order.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not observe that
any other Member desires to take part
in the debate and, therefore, I rise at
once to reply. I am almost afraid that
I must tell the Committee that I have
some doubt as to whether the right hon.
Gentleman, the Minister of Home Affairs,
whom we are all pleased to see with us,
came here to-day entirely to deal with the
point before the Committee or to deal
with the Ardglass Bill, which is also to
have our attention. Passing from that I
may say that the right hon. Gentleman
directed his speech entirely to what has
happened in the past. He defended the
administration which I did not attack
and which was not attacked. He laid
stress on the results which flowed from
the Act of 1922 which were not in
question, but he gave us no reason whatever why the Government-
Sir DAWSON BATES: May I intervene just one moment? The hon. and
learned Senator is under a misapprehension. The reason I dealt with these
points was that in his speech the hon.
and learned Member dealt with the very
considerable inconvenience and trouble
that would be caused to law-abiding citizens if this Bill went through. He did
not confine himself to the three years'
limit.
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid, on the
other hand, the right hon. Gentleman
misunderstood me. What I tried to
point out was that the application of
certain provisions of the Act of 1922
must inevitably entail certain inconveniences to certain people. However,
that does not seem to be the point at
issue. I was about to say that the right
hon. Gentleman gave us no reason whatever why a self-respecting Government
with a mind conscious of rectitude should
not from time to time come back to
Parliament and ask for a renewal of its
powers. He gave no reason against that.
And if I may say so, although the right
hon. Gentleman displayed touching confidence in a mind conscious of rectitude
on his own part, I did not see that he
gave any reason whatever for his
apparent confidence in the existence of
such a mind and rectitude of conduct on
the part of his successors. That is the
point I would press. If we pass this
Bill we cannot tell what Minister of
Home Affairs we may have in future or
what Government we may have in future.
The right hon. Gentleman also said that
the House could repeal the Bill. I laid
some stress I think on the point that this
House alone could not repeal the Bill.
Any repeal must be by action from the
Government.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I said
Parliament.
Mr. LESLIE: My note is House.
Parliament could repeal just as Parliament could exercise the
power of
extending the duration.
I do not think there is anything else
to answer. In fact, most of what was
said seemed to me to be beside the point.
I can only say, anxious as I am to assist
County Courts Bill-
Committee.
25 APRIL 1933
in the preservation of law and order, I
should fail in my duty if I withdrew
this Amendment. The Committee must
exercise their own individual opinions on
it.

County Officers and Courts Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Before
moving that this House do now resolve
itself into Committee on
the County
Officers and Courts Bill, I should like to
offer a short explanation with regard to
two matters which were mentioned by
Senator Leslie during the Second Reading debate.
The hon. and learned Senator asked for
some explanation as to what discretion,
if any, it is proposed to confer on the
Ministry of Finance by Clause 10, subclause (3) of the Bill. The Clause is not
intended to give to the Ministry any
"discretion" in the full sense of the term,
but to allow the Ministry to use its best
judgment to provide whatever funds are
necessary. Precedents for the wording of
the sub-clause will be found in Section
253 of the Companies Act (Northern
Ireland), 1932, and Section 85 of the
Supreme Court of Judicature (Ireland)
Act of 1877.
Leslie, Mr. James G.
Pakenham, Col. H. A.
Porter, Mr. John Porter
Quigley, Mr. Andrew
Sinclair, Col. Thomas
The hon.
Senator also
and learned
asked for some further information with
regard to Clause 13.
Under the provisions of Section 5, subsection (1), of the Jury Laws Amendment
Act, 1926, grand juries at quarter sessions
were abolished, but it was provided that
after the appointed day an indictment
might be presented at quarter sessions
without having been found by a grand
jury, provided it was an indictment
against a person committed for trial at
that court.
Cases may arise from time to time and,
in fact, have arisen, since the Act to
which I have just referred was passed, in
which informations were refused by a
court of petty sessions, and consequently
the defendant was not committed for
trial." It followed that there was no
longer any method by which the Attorney-
General, or indeed any private person,
might exercise the common law right of
presenting a Bill at quarter sessions in
such cases, an effect which I think the
hon. and learned Senator who mentioned
this Clause on the Second Reading will
agree could hardly have been intended
by the legislature. The Clause in the
Bill at present before this House is
designed to remedy this position. I beg
to move That this House do now resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill.”
(6
On Question, Motion agreed to.

Mr. LESLIE: On Clause 6, sub-clause

the sub-section comes to us from another

Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, in Clause 9, sub-clause (2), page
6, line 28, to leave out the words "other-
""
wise expressly," and to insert instead
thereof the words, or statutory regulation otherwise."
It has been pointed out by the Postmaster-General that the regulations
dealing with British Savings Certificates
make no specific provision under which
county court funds could be invested in
them. This has also brought to notice
that the Ulster Savings Certificates Regulations have no such specific provision
either. It might of course happen that
certain funds in the county court could
comply with some of the provisions in
either set of regulations and be invested
accordingly, but each case would have to
be settled on its merits.
In order, therefore, that the Act might
not appear to attempt to override the
regulations themselves, it has been
arranged with the Post Office that an
Amendment should be introduced in
Clause 9, sub-clause (2), as shown on
the Order Paper, the exception then
running as follows:- Except where
any enactment or statutory regulation
otherwise provides." The addition of the
words "statutory regulation" ensures that
the Clause does not override the regulations, while the word "expressly" is
omitted because the statutory bar may
not in all cases be stated in express
language but only inferentially.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 10 (Disposition of dormant
or unclaimed funds in the county court).
Question proposed, "That the Clause
stand part of the Bill."
Mr. LESLIE: I should like to take
the opportunity of thanking the noble
Viscount for the information he gave us
on the Motion, That the House resolve
itself into Committee. I quite recognise
the force of the precedents which have
been quoted, and I am quite satisfied in
my own mind that in, at all events,
ninety-nine cases out of every hundred,
the particular words to which I should
have been disposed to object have no
meaning whatever. In view of those precedents there is no object in taking them.
out, even if that course were possible in
this Chamber. The alternative would be
to object to the entire Clause, but as the
words mean nothing there can be no
objection to the Clause.

Mr. LESLIE: I confess I have some
little difficulty regarding sub-clause (2) of
Clause 2. I want to know whether this
sub-clause relates solely to what I might
describe as the trespassing county court
judge, or does it include also the other
county court judge whose territory is
being encroached upon. If the intention
is that the direction shall only be given
ir order to aid in the discharge of work
which has become too much for one man
there seems to be no difficulty, because
both judges can be consulted if that is
the intention. But if the direction is
also intended to be given in the case of
the illness or incapacity of a judge whose
territory it is proposed to encroach upon
it occurs to me that there might be some
difficulty in the wording of the sub-clause;
that is to say, if the judge incapacitated.
is required to give his consent before the
other judge can come in. That, also, is
perhaps a matter which the noble Lord
might like to stand over to a later stage.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will give
the definite information at a later date,
but it would appear to be common sense
not to assume that the consent of the
incapacitated judge would be necessary,
because he might be in such a state that
it would not be possible for him to give
any form of reply.
Mr. LESLIE: The point is that if he
were incapacitated and could not give his
consent the Act would be inoperative.

Mr. LESLIE: I have again to thank

the intention of the Act of 1926 to

that time had. There is no use going back

the right taken from them then has now
been restored after a period of seven years
-I am afraid I must use the word-drift.
There is nothing in Clause 13 that attracts
attention in itself. I presume I am right
in thinking that the Vexatious Indictments Act is not affected. I think that
was saved in the Act of 1926, but it is a
point that should not escape attention.
I am afraid that we shall be left with the

Attorney-General to go before a petty

by this Bill.

Ardglass Dock and Harbour Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LONG: The provisions of this Bill
are so explicit that I feel very little
explanation is required from me. Ardglass is the main centre of our East Coast
herring fishery, and the harbour there has
been Government property since the year
1876. At the north-west end of the
harbour there is a small dock, which
remained in private ownership until a
few years ago, when it was acquired by
the Ministry of Commerce from the
persons named in the Schedule to the
Bill. In 1878 the Ardglass Dock Order
conferred upon the owner of the dock, a
Mr. Beauclerk, statutory powers with
respect to the dock. That Order and the
Ardglass Harbour Act, 1876, have
remained in operation up to the present
time.
Probate Bill
Company Law Bill
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
In this Bill we propose to repeal the
Dock Order and to make the dock part of
the harbour undertaking so that the Act
of 1876 shall apply to both. Under
Clause 3 the harbour master is empowered
to control the laying up of vessels in the
dock. I may add that although the
traffic in goods landed at the dock is small
-only four cargoes of coal having been
brought in during the past year-the dock
is a somewhat valuable adjunct to the
harbour by reason of the fact that fishing
vessels and small steam vessels may lie
there in safety during rough weather. I
beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Probate and Letters of Administration
Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This is a
short Bill designed to remedy certain defects which experience has disclosed in the
law relative to Probates and Letters of
Administration.
The duties and responsibilities attaching to executors and administrators in
administering an estate are frequently
both difficult and onerous, and in fact
require technical knowledge and skill,
which in many instances renders it necessary for executors to obtain professional
assistance to enable them to carry out
their duties.
In these circumstances it will, therefore, be readily understood that testators
-particularly if property to any considerable extent is involved-might desire
to join with some relative or personal
friend, a bank or other similar trust corporation, as executor, to work in collaboration in looking after the affairs of
the estate. The bank, or trust
corporation, being a corporate body,
would never be without a legal representative, and the expense of appointing new
trustees, which sometimes arises in the
case of ordinary executors, would
avoided. Moreover, most of the banks
undertaking this type of work have highly
skilled technical officers in constant touch
with the money markets, who can, therefore, be depended upon to safeguard the
testator's investments.
be
As the law stands at present in Northern Ireland it is not possible to issue a
grant of probate to a bank or to a personal executor jointly, or to a bank
alone, although Letters of Administration
can be issued to a syndic or nominee of
a bank if the other executor renounces.
The main provision of the present Bill
is intended to remove this difficulty and
to bring the law in this respect into line
with that in England. Clause 1 of the
Bill follows closely upon the provisions
of Section 1 (6) (1) of the Supreme Court
of Judicature (Consolidation) Act of
1925, and, as will be seen, enables grants
of probate or administration to be issued
to trust corporations.
This
The second provision incorporated in
the Bill is contained in Clause 2.
is a very technical matter, but I understand that under the existing law in
Northern Ireland if a person dies without
any personal property a grant of probate
or administration cannot be given,
although such person may have in fact
freehold property, or property in the
sense of claims on some other estates.
This defect in the law has been
remedied in England by Section 2 of the
Administration of Justice Act, 1932,
upon which Clause 2 of our Bill is
modelled, enabling Probate or Letters of
Administration to be granted in cases
where there are no assets.
The remainder of the Bill consists
solely of consequential and drafting provisions, is self-explanatory, and does
not call for any explanation from me.
beg to move.
I
"" That
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move,
the debate on this Bill be adjourned until
to-morrow."
If this
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
House agrees with the Motion of the hon.
and learned Senator I have no personal
objection if the adjournment is limited
until to-morrow.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Debate accordingly adjourned until
to-morrow.
Company Law Amendment Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This brief
Measure is put forward for the purpose
of removing points of difficulty that have
arisen in connection with the administration of the Companies Act (Northern
Company Law Bill-
• Adjournment.
25 APRIL 1933
Ireland), 1932. The most important of
its provisions is associated with the powers
conferred upon liquidators in voluntary
winding-up cases. Representations have
been made to the Ministry that wider
powers should be conferred upon liquidators, including a power to dispose of
property in parcels by means of fee farm
grants or sub-grants and leases or subleases. A liquidator finding it impossible
to secure an outright sale of property may,
in some cases, be able to dispose of it in
parcels by means of fee farm grants or
leases, and thereafter to sell the rents so
created. Under Clause 3 liquidators will
have the power to make such fee farm
grants and leases, while Clause 5 rounds
off the recital of the powers of liquidators in voluntary winding-up cases by
the addition of a short paragraph to
Section 220 of the Act of 1932.
The remaining provisions of the Bill are
of less importance and follow upon discussions with the Incorporated Law
Society and other interests. In Clause 2
of the Bill we propose to repeal the proviso in Section 158 of the Act of 1932,
which enables the High Court to direct
that winding-up proceedings shall be conducted by the local bankruptcy court.
This provision has been in existence for a
long time but apparently has never been
used.
As regards Section 156 of the Act of
1932, which deals with the case of a contributory who has become bankrupt, the
words "his assignees or trustee in bankruptcy" are being substituted for the
words" his trustee in bankruptcy." The
latter words were used in the Companies
(Consolidation) Act, 1908, but inasmuch
as the assignees in bankruptcy act in most
cases, it seems better to alter the wording.
Hon. Members will gather that this is
only a small drafting Amendment.
In Clause 4 we are making a small
amendment of Section 198 of the
principal Act. In a case where a
resolution to wind up a company is passed,
say on a Thursday afternoon, it is not
easy to publish the statutory notice within
the seven days prescribed by the section,
for the "Belfast Gazette" is published
weekly and goes to press on Thursday. It
is not unreasonable to extend the time
limit to ten days.
The Ministry has been in communication with the Incorporated Law Society
and other representative interests and is
satisfied that the clauses of the Bill meet
with general approval. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: The only remark, I
think, that one has to make on this little
Bill is to point out to the House that it
contains nothing, in my judgment, which
could properly have been done in the Consolidation Bill of last session. In that I
shall hope to have the concurrence of the
hon. Member on my left, who was one of
the Committee on that Bill. There is
nothing, I think, that could properly have
been done. I must say if the proposal had
been made as regards the "Belfast
Gazette" I should have felt tempted,
having regard to the fact that the "Belfast
Gazette" had no existence when the Act
of 1908 had been passed.
There is in Clause 2 a very interesting
departure from precedent in regard to the
method of citation. It is a departure
which is thoroughly justified by the particular circumstances and which, perhaps,
has possibilities of development in future.
I was glad to notice that the Minister of
Education in introducing the Clause took
no exception to what I might call the
split subjunctive. I presume that method
of dealing with the subjunctive has the
approval of the Minister of Education.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will not
say that.
Mr. LESLIE: At all events, if it has
not his full approval he will agree with
me it might have been infinitively worse.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjournment.
Moved "That this House do now
adjourn until to-morrow."-(Viscount
Charlemont.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Twenty minutes after
Four o'clock.

Civil Authorities (Special Powers) Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I beg to move,
"That this
Bill be now read the Third time.'
Mr. LESLIE: I do not propose, of
course, to oppose the Third Reading of
this Bill because, having voted for the
Second Reading, that course would be,
as it seems to me, entirely inconsistent.
Therefore, much as I regret the decision
which the Committee came to yesterday
afternoon, I shall offer no opposition to
the Bill. But that decision is not the
only matter which I regret. I regret
very much that the right hon. Gentleman, the Minister of Home Affairs, is
not in his place this afternoon in order,
if one may say so, to take his godchild,
shall we say, a further step towards confirmation.
It will be within the recollection of
the House that in the course of what did
not appear to be a wholly unprepared
speech, the right hon. Gentleman charged
me with having stated that the Act of
1922 was unconstitutional. When he was
controverted
on that he shifted his
ground, and said perhaps I was alluding
to the administration of the Act. Being
challenged on that also he left the subject, and he neither withdrew nor
attempted to substantiate his remark. I
think it is unfortunate that he has not
availed himself of his opportunity to-day
to take one or other of those two courses.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am sure
the hon. and learned Senator is under
a misapprehension in thinking that the
right hon. Gentleman, the Minister of
Home Affairs, could possibly accuse him,
of all the Members of the Senate, of
thinking that the action of the Government was unconstitutional.
On Question, Motion ("That this Bill
be now read the Third time ") agreed to.
Moved, That this Bill do now pass."
-(Viscount Charlemont.)

of Commons to acquaint them that the

County Officers and Courts Bill.
Order for receiving Report of Amendment read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT :
Before
actually moving "That this Report be
now received," I should like to give a
little information which I was asked for
yesterday. On Clause 6 Senator Leslie
asked how many persons would become
entitled to superannuation rights as a
result of the passing of this Clause. The
answer is that the Clause does not confer
any superannuation rights additional to
those already conferred by the County
Officers and Courts Act of 1925. It is
designed merely to clarify the provisions
contained in Section 2 of that Act.
In relation to Clause (2) Senator Leslie
suggested that there might be some difficulty in obtaining the consent of an exist
ing county court judge on account of
illness or incapacity. Undoubtedly such
difficulty might arise, but it could only
be of a temporary nature, and the
Government is not disposed to amend the
Probate Bill-
26 APRIL 1933
Second Reading
Clause merely for the sake of avoiding
this contingency. I now beg to move,
"That this Report be now received."
Mr. LESLIE: One has only to say that
one is obliged to the noble Viscount for
the information he has been kind enough
to furnish.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The said Amendment was accordingly
reported and agreed to by the House.

Probates and Letters of Administration
Bill.
Order read for resuming adjourned
debate on Question (25th April)-
That the Bill be now read a Second
time." (Viscount Charlemont.)
Mr. LESLIE: The Bill which we began
to consider yesterday afternoon will, I
believe, be found to be of considerable
convenience to quite
quite a considerable
number of persons. The Bill deals with
very technical matters, or rather, perhaps
one should say touches on matters of
some technical interest and considerable
technical difficulty. The Bill must have
been under consideration for a very long
time. I use the expression "very long
time" advisedly, because I think it must
have been on the stocks, as the saying
goes, for two or three years, if not for a
longer period, and the Bill must have
given a very great deal of trouble in its
preparation.
It is a Bill which demands something
more than passing notice at the hands of
this House. I believe I am right in
saying that they did not get this matter
of allowing Trust Corporations to act
directly as executors or administrators
into order in England completely until
the year 1925, and it was not, if I am
right, until St. Patrick's Day of last
year, when the Second Miscellaneous
Provisions (Northern Ireland) Bill received the Royal Assent, that we were
in a position to deal with the matter in
Northern Ireland. Now that we have got
the Bill I am afraid we must admit that
it is not, at first reading at all events,
very easy either to follow or to appreciate. It is, if I may say so, essentially
one of those Bills where the presence in
this House of some solicitor with a practical working knowledge of the details
of the steps which have to be taken to
deal with the property, if any, of a
deceased person, would be of great help
to us.
However, even if the Bill is difficult in
places, it seems to me that we must be
careful to avoid creating difficulties for
ourselves, and I think that on the whole
we can perhaps do that by looking at
the Bill to see what it does not do
rather perhaps than what it does. As
to what the Bill does, I am going to
suggest that it does not really change the
law at all. What it does seek to
do is to introduce new and important
machinery for giving effect to the law
already existing. That, I think, is a
fair description. Secondly, we are not
under the necessity of concerning ourselves very much, if indeed at all, with
the diversities which exist in the law in
Northern Ireland and the law of England as regards administration in cases
of whole or partial intestacy, and if we
recognise that fact we shall save ourselves a very considerable amount of
trouble.
Probate Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
Again, the Bill does not purport to be
in any way a consolidating Measure, and
that probably is one of the principal
reasons why there are so comparatively
few definitions in the Interpretation
Clause of the Bill. I shall be speaking
again about the Interpretation Clause,
for, as the Bill presents something more
than a strong family likeness to the two
English Statutes of 1925 and 1932, one
is rather inclined to wonder whether it
might not be desirable for the sake of
attracting any decisions there may be to
define certain particular terms which
occur more than once throughout the Bill.
The words in my mind are the apparently
simple words, "estate," "intestate," and
"will." The general question as to whether
those ought to be defined or not can
readily be settled by taking the case of
any one, and when the House is doing
that, either to-day or in Committee, the
House will bear in mind not only that
there are likely to be different aspects
to such questions, but that this Bill is
only an enabling Bill to be operated either
through the High Court itself or through
the District Registry in Londonderry,
which, of course, would merely function
as the representative of the High Court.
I hope, I may say in passing, that the
House will acquit me of any desire of
pressing for the insertion of
any
definition the necessity for which cannot
be clearly established, but only for the
insertion of such definitions as are thought
likely to be of really essential service. I am
not sure whether there will be any necessity to put anything down on the Order
Paper as regards those words I mentioned.
I rather think that the subject can be
dealt with generally, possibly on the
Motion to go into Committee. I shall,
as a matter of fact, have two Amendments on the Paper. Of the first I am
very doubtful whether the trivial alteration I suggest is one that ought to be
made, or whether it would have any
great effect if it were. The second I do
think is necessary, and I will refer to that
in a moment or two.
Going through the Bill, there is nothing
to say until one gets to Clause 4, which
is the Interpretation Clause. But I
cannot deny myself the pleasure of drawing attention to two words in line 31
on page 2. The two words, testator
or" are inserted in the Bill, but they do
not appear in the corresponding Section.
of the English Act. The corresponding
Section of the English Act is, I think,
Section 161 of the English Consolidation
Act of 1925. Those two words are not
in the English Act, and their absence
from the Northern Ireland Bill would,
I think, occasion very great difficulty.
Therefore they are being put in. I am
inclined to think that if they were not
there the whole Clause would be almost
entirely useless.
Coming now to the Interpretation
Clause, I am afraid I have to say that I
do not think the definition in paragraph
(a) of a trust corporation—and after all
it is with trust corporations that the Bill
purports to deal-I cannot say that I
think the definition, "a corporation
appointed by the High Court in any particular case to be a trustee," is at all
lucid. That has nothing to do with
Northern Ireland. The words used are
exactly the words that occur in the
English Act. I think that the intention of
the words is that any corporation, which
has once been appointed by the court to
act as a trustee, is to be regarded as available for selection to act as executor or
administrator on any subsequent occasion.
I think that is the intention, but I think
we ought to be definitely told at some convenient stage, very possibly when the Bill
is in Committee. Anybody who has compared this Clause with the corresponding
English Section will notice the absence
from the Clause in the Bill of any reference to the Public Trustee or to the
Treasury Solicitor. In the original
English Bill the Public Trustee was
mentioned, and, as the House knows, we
have no Public Trustee in Northern
Ireland. Therefore, we cannot make use
of the Public Trustee as a possible
executor or administrator under this Bill.
Of course, the English Public Trustee is a
corporation.
As regards the Treasury Solicitor, he was
added in England by statute a year or two
after the main Act of 1925, and I should
like to be told whether it is not thought
that it would be a convenience here if a
provision were inserted as regards the
possibility of the Treasury Solicitor acting.
I expect to be told, in fact I am sure I
shall be told, that the answer must be
that we cannot do anything. Incidentally,
I believe our Treasury Solicitor is not a
corporation, but, at the same time, it
would seem that the insertion of some
official person like that might be of some
Probate Bill-
Second Reading
26 APRIL 1933
practical assistance. It may be thought
that anything of that nature would be
outside the scope of the Bill, but I think
we ought to have some definite statement
from the Government, if only in order
that the statement may appear on our
records as showing that the matter did not
escape our attention.
An interesting little point arises when
we come to paragraph (iii).
It is a very
odd little occurrence, and the necessity for
a change seems to owe itself rather to the
difference in manner, in which two
separate documents have been printed
rather than to anything else. Finally, I
support the Bill as likely to be useful, but
I would urge that the Committee stage
should not be taken for some days to
come, as any questions that will arise are
likely to require rather more than usual
research and the dipping into statutes, of
which there are a large number relative
to the points at issue, and it will be
remembered that the Bill does not
definitely connect itself with any of our
existing statutes, although it is naturally
to be regarded as ancillary to the Act of
1857, which was the foundation of our
modern probate practice. The Bill is not
to come into immediate operation-it will
become operative on 1st July next-so
that there will be ample breathing space
for the benefit of solicitors and others who
are likely to be concerned.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am quite
willing to give sufficient time for the
further consideration of this Bill, and I
think it will be reasonable if we take the
Committee stage next week. I should
like to answer one or two points that have
been raised by the hon. and learned
Senator in the course of his speech. One
was with regard to the definition of "trust
corporation" in paragraph (a) of subclause (1) of Clause 4.
I am advised that paragraph (a) means
just what it says, that is, that a corporation appointed by the High Court in any
particular case to be a trustee may, in
another case, be granted probate or letters
of administration. Presumably the court
in the first case was satisfied of the
solvency and suitability of the corporation
appointed to act as trustee. The hon. and
learned Senator, of course, realises that
the Bill is an enabling one. The High
Court is not obliged to appoint any particular corporation, but may do so if
satisfied that the appointment is a suitable one.
The hon. and learned Senator raised
another point about the Public Trustee.
We would have been very pleased to
include the Public Trustee within the
definition of "trust corporation," but,
unfortunately, it was not found possible
to do so. The Public Trustee was consulted through the appropriate channels
and asked if he would be willing to act.
He has expressed the opinion that even
if appointed by the High Court in
Northern Ireland, it would be ultra vires
for him to act, as the Public Trustee Act
of 1906, which constituted the office,
does not apply to Northern Ireland. It
would also be outside the powers of this
Parliament to confer powers or duties
upon the Public Trustee. I am not sure
whether the same reasons do not apply in
the case of the Treasury Solicitor, but I
will find out. I am glad the Bill has
received such a favourable reception from
the House.
On Question, Motion ("That the Bill
be now read a Second time ") agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
Company Law Amendment Bill.

resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Viscount Charlemont.)

CLAUSES 1 (Amendment of s. 156 of
principal Act), 2 (Amendment of s. 158 of
principal Act), and 3 (Amendment of s.
176 of principal Act) ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move the insertion of the following new
Clause after Clause 3:-
-
Amendment of s. 178 of principal
Act.
4. In sub-section (7) of section one
hundred and seventy-eight of the
principal Act (which relates to the constitution and proceedings of a committee of inspection) the words "person
qualified to be a member under subsection (1) of this section" shall be substituted for the words creditor or
contributory."
""
Company Law Bill
Adjournment
THE SENATE
(Viscount Charlemont.)
The purpose of this Amendment to
Section 178 of the Companies Act
(Northern Ireland), 1932, is to make it
possible for a vacancy arising upon a committee of inspection to be filled by a
person holding a general power of attorney
from creditors or contributories where the
company is being wound up by the court.
Such a person is eligible for appointment.
to a committee of inspection when such
committee is first constituted, and there is
no apparent reason why he should not be
eligible to fill a vacancy. I beg to move
that the new Clause, as set out on the
Order Paper, stand part of the Bill.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSES 4 (Amendment of s. 198 of
principal Act), 5 (Amendment of s. 220
of principal Act), 6 (Interpretation), and
7 (Short title, construction, and citation),
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
Preamble and Title agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill be reported as
amended to the House.
Ordered: That the House do now
resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported with Amendment.
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
this House do now
move,
"That
adjourn."
Mr. LESLIE : I presume until
to-morrow?
Viscount CHARLEMONT indicated
assent.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Fifteen minutes before
Four o'clock.

Question to Minister.
APPLICANTS FOR TRANSITIONAL
BENEFIT.
Mr. DORMAN: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether he can
state the number of applicants for transitional benefit for the year ending 31st
March, 1933; the number who were
granted transitional payments at the
statutory rate, the number granted at
reduced rate, and the number turned
down on the ground of not needing
assistance.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): Out of a total number of
96,903 determinations given by Public
Assistance Committees on applications for
transitional payments referred to them
during the period 3rd April, 1932, to
1st April, 1933, 53,590 were allowed at
the full benefit rate, 36,697 were allowed
at lower rates, and in 6,616 cases the
needs of the applicants were held not to
justify payment.
These figures relate to applications and
not to individuals, and they include
initial applications, renewals and
visions.

Mr. LESLIE: I mentioned this small

defined," as they appear in the text, are,

corporation appointed by the High Court
in any particular case to be a trustee.
That is the corporation referred to in
paragraph (a) of the sub-clause.
intention is not to include such a
corporation. In order to qualify as
a company competent to be a component of another company under subdivision (iii), it is necessary that the
company should have the qualifications
contained in sub-division (i) or subdivision (ii) of paragraph (b) of the subclause. It is, I am afraid, rather a
difficult matter to put in short language,
but the effect is very simple. I beg to
move the Amendment standing in my
name.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept this Amendment.

part of the Bill.

Probates and Letters of Administration
Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this Bill be now read the
Third time."
Mr. LESLIE: I think it will be within
the recollection of the House that one or
two minor questions were raised on
previous stages of the Bill, and I am not
sure whether it was intended to reply to
them on the Third Reading or whether
it was thought, on further consideration,
that the necessary replies had been given
in the course of debate. The principal
point was the question of the Treasury
Solicitor, the point being that we were
not satisfied that the Treasury Solicitor
in Northern Ireland was a corporation
and could possibly be included in the Bill,
and I am not quite sure whether we were
to have had further information on the
subject.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I remember the point.
The Government came to
the conclusion that the suggestion to
include the Treasury Solicitor of Northern
Ireland in the Bill was not feasible, owing
to the fact that the Treasury Solicitor was
not a corporation.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Resolved, That this Bill do now
pass." (Viscount Charlemont.)
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill with
an Amendment, to which they desire
their concurrence.
Motion for Adjournment.
ACCOMMODATION FOR MEMBERS.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn."
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I
desire to raise a question in regard to the
comfort of the Members who attend the
sittings of the Senate. My complaint has
to do with the discomfort of the present
seating accommodation in this Chamber.
We miss here the comfort of the Senate
Chamber in the old House, because in
this Chamber the front seats are so far
away from the Table that Ministers and
Members on the front benches have no
place on which to put their papers. This
Chamber is very grand and very nice, but
it is really most uncomfortable. It
would be a great improvement if, during
the Summer Recess, the front benches
could be moved near to the Table, and I
would ask that you, Mr. Speaker, should
make that suggestion to
the proper
authorities. If that could be done it
would be a great convenience both to
Ministers and to Members. I have
spoken to the Leader of the House on the
subject, and he agrees it would be a great
improvement if something on the lines I
have suggested could be carried out. I
have been a Member of the Imperial
Parliament, and if the example at Westminster had been copied here it would
have been a good thing. At Westminister the front benches are so near the
Table that Ministers can put their legs
up and go to sleep.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am very
sorry indeed that the hon. and gallant
Member should think I would be better
employed with my legs up on the Table
and asleep. I think we had better postpone consideration of the matter until
after the Summer Recess.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Twenty-five minutes after
Three o'clock until such
date as Mr. Speaker shall
appoint.
Local Bills.

Business of the House.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I beg to move, "That Standing Order No. XXV be suspended for
this day's sitting for the purpose of
taking the Second Reading of the Motor
Vehicles (International Race) Bill, the
Moneylenders Bill, and the Appropriation
Bill."
Mr. DORMAN: I would ask the
noble Viscount to withdraw the Moneylenders Bill from his Motion. Very few
of us have had an opportunity of studying this Bill, and certainly no Member
has had an opportunity of resorting to
the Parliamentary Library in order to
look up the Statutes dealing with this
particular subject. No one in this House
would do anything to retard the progress
of the legislation which the Government
thinks it necessary to place on the Statute
Book, but on numerous occasions protests
have been made in this House against
the Government disregarding requests
made by the Senate for consideration in
affording Members a proper opportunity
for studying Bills brought before them.
Therefore, I ask the right hon. Gentleman to agree to delete from his Motion
all reference to the Moneylenders Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: I think that in the first
instance I may point out that the noble
Viscount did not give us any reason for
the suspension of the Standing Order,
and perhaps when replying to the point
raised by Senator Dorman he will tell
us why he wishes to suspend the Standing
Order in respect of the three Bills he has
mentioned. Two of them, perhaps I
may say, would not provoke any opposition to the proposal, but as regards the
Moneylenders Bill I am bound to
support to some extent what has been
said. I quite agree that there has not
been time between Friday morning,
when most of the Members of the House
received their Bill, and this afternoon,
for a proper and detailed study of the
various Statutes to which the Bill
happens to
reference
to refer, for
to
the debates in the Imperial Parlia-
Business of the House
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
ment, and so on. But I am inclined to
think that if the noble Viscount can give
us what we can accept as a good reason
for the suspension of the Standing
Order, and will tell us that he does not
propose to take the Committee stage
until next week, perhaps the opposition
to the Motion might subside. I do not
myself see, on further consideration,
that there is anything which need prevent us giving a Second Reading to the
Bill to-day, but I do not think
ought to hasten its subsequent stages
Being satisfied on that point, I would
appeal to the hon. Senator (Mr. Dorman) not to object to the Motion,
subject to good reason being given for
expedition, and ample time being
afforded between the Second Reading
and the Committee stage.
we
a
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The three
Bills mentioned have been placed together
in this Motion really to give some work
to Senators who have come from
distance to attend the meetings of the
Senate. With regard to the Moneylenders Bill, I would like to make some
kind of bargain with hon. Members that
if I agree to postpone the Second Reading of the Bill until next week they will
not ask too much time to elapse between
the Second Reading and the Committee
stage. The reason for the expedition in
regard to this legislation is a very simple.
one. The writs for the election of the
new Senators will go out on June 11th,
and therefore we have to dispose of all
legislation that comes to us from
another place by that date. It would
be a very singular thing, in fact, I think
it would be absolutely unprecedented, to
pass two stages of a Bill in one Senate
and leave the remaining stages as a
legacy for the new Senate. Indeed, I
do not think such a thing could be done.
Therefore all the outstanding legislation
we have to deal with will have to be
finished by June 11th, and that is the
reason for the haste. However, I have
no objection to postponing the Second
Reading of the Moneylenders Bill, and
omitting it from this Motion.
Mr. DORMAN:. I would be very
glad, so far as I am concerned, to facilitate the further progress of the Bill if the
Government will postpone the Second
Reading. To put the matter in order,
I will move an Amendment to delete
the words, "the Moneylenders Bill"
from the Motion before the House.
Amendment agreed to.
On Question, Motion, "That Standing Order No. XXV be suspended for
this day's sitting for the purpose of
taking the Second Reading of the Motor
Vehicles (International Race) Bill, and
the Appropriation Bill," agreed to.
Ballymena Urban District Council Bill.
The Amendments made by the Joint
Committee were considered and agreed
to.
Question to Minister.
DIVORCE LAW IN NORTHERN
IRELAND.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie:
a
To ask the Leader of the House
whether he is aware that during the
calendar years, 1930, 1931 and
1932, respectively, two, five, and three
petitions for divorce were submitted
to the Parliament of Northern Ireland; that during the first four
months of the year 1933 five such
petitions have been submitted; that,
owing to the fact that a divorce requires at the least two months for its
passage through Parliament, it is
usually only possible to obtain
divorce during the Spring Session;
that the inclusive cost to the petitioner of a Divorce Bill in Parliament
is from £100 to £200; and whether,
in view of the powers given to this
Parliament by the Northern Ireland
(Miscellaneous Provisions) Act of
1932, it is the intention of the Government to introduce legislation in
the immediate future in order to confer a more effective jurisdiction on the
High Court of Northern Ireland, and
so avoid the delay, expense, and other
inconveniences of the existing system.
Mr. LESLIE : The object of the
question which I have on the Paper today is not to suggest for a moment that
there is an undue number of divorce
cases in Northern Ireland. Such figures
as are obtainable perhaps would show
rather the contrary. It is a matter of
something like thirty cases during a
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
23 MAY 1933
period of seven years in a population of
a million and a quarter. It was not my
object to draw attention to that phase
of the question at all, but the object of
the question is to draw the attention of
the House to the view which, as you Mr.
Speaker know, has been expressed by
successive Divorce Committees, that this
matter of divorce is a matter rather for
the law courts than for a Joint Committee of Parliament. The question is
put now because we have recently been
enabled, by the passage of the Act of
Parliament, which is referred to in the
printed question, to give further powers
to our courts, such powers as we could
not hitherto have given owing to the restrictions imposed by the Act of 1920.
Perhaps, as the transfer, so to speak,
of this work to the Joint Committees
took place so long ago as 1925, I may be
permitted to remind the House as now
The
constituted of what happened.
question deals, of course, only with that
class of divorce which is described as
a vinculo matrimonii, a complete severance of the matrimonial chain, which
enables the parties to contract a further
and valid legal marriage with any person they may wish. Prior to 1925 a
person domiciled in Northern Ireland
who desired such a divorce could only
obtain it by a Bill in the Imperial Parliament. They had first then to go
through the process they still have to go
through, of going to the law courts for
what is called in Northern Ireland a
divorce from bed and board, and what in
England is called a judicial separation.
Having done that they approached the
House of Lords, and an Act of Parliament was probably passed in their
favour.
In 1925 the House of Lords, in effect,
informed the Parliament of Northern
Ireland that in future Northern Ireland
must attend to its own domestic matters,
and petitions from Northern Ireland
were no longer to be received by the
House of Lords. As a consequence, the
Standing Orders of both our Houses were
amended, and when this House was
asked to give its consent to the Amendment, the then Leader of the House, the
noble Marquess (Lord Londonderry) informed us that our Standing Orders
would conform as closely as possible to
those in force in the House of Lords.
Three Members of this House, His
Majesty's Lieutenant for the County of
Armagh (Mr. Armstrong), the
the late
Senator Greer, and myself, all voiced the
opinion that such a matter was one for
the law courts and not for Parliament,
and the earnest hope that we should very
soon be relieved of the duties cast upon
us.
The whole question of divorce, as
everybody knows, gives rise to great
differences of opinion nowadays. Nobody desires, I think, to force their
private opinions on other people. Still
less does anybody think that the opinion
against divorce should be enforced or
sought to be enforced by any artificial
means, such as that of imposing
obstructions in the way of the parties
who desire to obtain a divorce on recognised grounds.
I draw attention at the conclusion of
my question to three main points, delay,
expense, and inconvenience. First as
regards the question of delay. It is, I
think, only right that I should tell the
House that that cuts both ways. In the
course of the investigation which I undertook preparatory to putting this
question on the Paper, I discovered.
there were
cases in which, under our
existing system, complete divorce was
obtained
more quickly than it could
have been obtained under the English
system, the apparent discrepancy arising
from the fact that in England the
practice is to obtain a decree nisi from
the law courts, which normally suspend
a decree absolute for six months, unless
the court choose to exercise its discretion,
as it sometimes does, for shortening the
period. But that delay in England
is used for a useful purpose.
In the interval there is an opportunity
for the intervention of the King's
Proctor, who is a kind of counsel for
the holy state of matrimony, and there
is an opportunity of making sure that
the court has not been deceived, and in
the event of the King's Proctor not being
satisfied there is an opportunity for
again bringing the matter before the
court. As regards ourselves, our own
requirements work very unevenly.
Sometimes divorce can be obtained as
quickly or more quickly than in England. It depends on the accident of the
date on which the petition is lodged.
Owing to the fact that our Parliamentary Sessions do not endure throughout
the whole year, I am suggesting, subject to the correction of the noble Lord,
that it is in fact possible to secure
a
Motor Vehicles Bill 208
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie
divorce only during the Spring Session.
Speaking generally, there is undue
delay, and the extent of that delay is
a question of accident.
must
Then there is the question of expense.
It is obviously impossible for me to give,
or to ask the noble Viscount to furnish
a true estimate of the expense, which
in individual cases.
vary
It depends to a great extent on the number
of witnesses who have to be summoned
before the Joint Committee,
to appear
and the distance those witnesses have to
travel. It will be seen at once that, in
the ordinary case of first application to
the court for a decree from bed and
board which witnesses have to attend,
and after a short delay the witnesses
have to be summoned again, the expense
is doubled, and that there is a very
severe burden on our people.
no
As regards actual inconvenience, one
may mention that this building where
Joint Committees are held, usually in
one of the rooms appropriated to the
House of Commons, is some considerable
distance from the law courts, and it is
not very convenient for legal practitioners to come here to attend the Joint
Committee. Those are the material inconveniences. There is the other great
inconvenience you have to expose the
parties to-hitherto there has been only
one practitioner-and that is that they
have to tell their painful story a second
time. That is a very grave inconvenience, SO we suggest. It is also
suggested that this business serves
useful purpose. A Parliamentary Committee perhaps is not well equipped to discharge its duty of ascertaining that there
has not been collusion between the parties.
It is suggested that it is a matter for
trained lawyers exclusively, and therefore for the court. In making the suggestion which underlies the question I
think I may say, not only for myself but
for those who agree with me that the
question should be put, that we are prepared to ask for legislation, although we
know the form of legislation will give
rise to certain differences of opinion, and
we also know it will probably result in
the appointment at the expense of the
State of an official known as the King's
Proctor. I must apologise to the House
for the time I have occupied in asking
the question which stands in my name.
on the Paper.

Motor Vehicles (International Race) Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Long.)
The
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This Bill
is similar in all respects to that passed
by this House in connection with the
Tourist Trophy race held last year.
race is to take place in Ulster again this
year, and we propose, therefore, to confer upon the promoters-the Royal
Automobile Club-the right to impose a
charge not exceeding 2s 6d in respect of
each car conveying spectators into the
area of the race, in order to supplement
the funds raised by other means towards
meeting the necessary expenses.
It
may be of interest to hon. Members
to hear that the amount raised by the
sale of badges under the powers granted
by last year's Act was slightly more than
£600.
The provisions of the Bill are, I think,
sufficiently clear without further explanation from me. I should, however, like
to express the gratitude of the Government to Sir William Morris for undertaking to present the prize money for the
race, amounting to £1,500. His
generosity has lessened to a considerable
degree the responsibility of the promoters in connection with the financing
of the project. I beg to move,
That this Bill be now read a Second
time.'

Colonel PAKENHAM: I was going

am glad to hear it was £600. I think

Mr. LESLIE: I do not rise to suggest that we should do anything other
than pass the Bill, as is invariably the
custom of this House, but I think we
ought to draw attention to some of the
figures which are contained in the
Schedule. Hon. Members who have
looked at Part II of the Schedule cannot fail, I think to be struck with the
enormous cost of the contributions to the
Unemployment Fund, amounting to
over £1,750,000, with the heavy cost of
the social services old age pensions, and
so on, amounting to £1,358,000, and
with the expense of educational services,
amounting to very nearly £2,000,000.
It is not for us to provide the means of
meeting this vast expense, but I think
that we may fairly point out that we
cannot but view with some concern the
question as to whether the resources of
Northern Ireland are sufficient to support the expenditure of such huge sums
of money in return for what no doubt
are valuable services, very desirable in
themselves, subject always to their not
being carried on at a cost in excess of
that which we can afford. We cannot
suggest here any particular reduction,
but I do think that we should record our
feeling that the expenses are disproportionate to our income and our
savings, and that, if possible, some very
serious reduction should be effected.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

Suspension of Standing Order.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That Standing Order No. 25 be
suspended for this day's sitting for the
purpose of taking the Second Reading of
the Agricultural Marketing Bill.”
Mr. LESLIE: It seems to me scarcely
necessary to say that the proposal of the
noble Viscount, taken strictly on its
merits apart from the consideration of
attendant circumstances, is a perfectly
monstrous proposal. The Bill in question
covers 25 pages and contains, I think,
22 Clauses, and has three or four
schedules. It was in the hands of hon.
Members, in the form in which it left
another place, at half-past two this afternoon. Hon. Members, therefore, who
wished to make what I might venture to
describe as an intelligent contribution to
the debate had exactly three-quarters of
an hour in which to prepare themselves.
Passing from that to the surrounding
circumstances, which are within the
knowledge of the House, I suggest if the
House met me, so far as to allow the
Second Reading debate to be opened and
then have the debate adjourned until
Thursday or Friday, there would be a
better opportunity of discussing this
most important matter.
Speaking on the subject of the suspension of Standing Orders, I would like to
remind the House of some observations
which fell from the noble Viscount on
the approaching event on the 10th June
next. The noble Viscount suggested that
it would be impossible for this House to
213 Suspension of Standing Order.

30 MAY 1933
Oral Answers
sit during the progress of the election of
half its Members. I would like to say
that there is nothing in the Government
of Ireland Act to prevent this House
from sitting during a vacancy in its
membership, but, of course, as a matter
of convention, nobody would suggest an
important Second Reading should be put
through or that the House should do
anything but formal Reports or Third
Readings.
The whole question is a little difficult,
but it must have been foreseen. After
all we have had twelve years' notice.
One hopes that after approximately the
middle of June the House will meet once
more perhaps to swear in Members and
to perform another grateful duty and
then presumably adjourn. I hope we
shall meet after the elections, for I do
not think it would be well to leave things
for three months in an incomplete condition. For the moment I do not definitely oppose the proposal of the noble
Viscount, but I do hope he may be able
to meet the views that obtain in more
than one quarter that the debate on the
Second Reading should not conclude this
afternoon.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I would like
to support the hon. and learned Senator
in his suggestion that the Second Reading be opened to-day and that we should
have further time to consider the matter.
Amendments which have been proposed
in the other House have only been handed
to us a quarter of an hour ago or perhaps
half an hour ago, and we have not really
had time to consider them. This is one
of the most important Bills in connection with the agricultural industry in
Northern Ireland and while, perhaps, we
are bound in a measure to support the
Bill that has gone through the Imperial
Parliament, we would really like to consider how it will affect us in our Six
County area. I certainly would like to
see the Second Reading adjourned to-day
in order that we may have time to consider the Bill further.
: This
Viscount CHARLEMONT
Motion that I proposed is rather
analagous to the fisherman who casts a
fly to see whether any fish will rise, and
I have had the satisfaction to-day that
is very often denied me with the fishingrod, of catching two at once.
I am
quite agreeable to the suggestion of the
hon. and learned Member that my Second
Reading speech should be delivered
to-day and perhaps two speeches afterwards, and then the debate should be
adjourned until Thursday. But I am
afraid the pressure of business consequent
on the necessity of sending out the writs
for the election of new Senators at the
end of next week will necessarily hurry
up business very considerably, but I
would like to say that, taking the beginning of the Second Reading debate
to-day, I would propose to have the Committee Stage on Tuesday. And then I
am afraid that either on Tuesday or the
following day I would have to move the
suspension of Standing Orders to take
the remainder of the Bill on Wednesday
night, because another place will meet on
Thursday and, of course, it is possible
they may not agree to any amendments
we put forward, and they must necessarily
have a day to consider them. Our
business must be concluded on Wednesday night, and within those limitations
I am willing to give any concession I
I prefer, personally, to take the
adjourned debate on Thursday rather
than Friday.
can.

Viscount CHARLEMONT: Yes.
had forgotten Monday, but it is Whit
