Mr.
Question: to Minister.
DEMOLITION OF SLUMS.
I
JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
wish to ask the question which stands in
my name on the Order Paper to-day.
The last time I referred to housing I was
advised to put down a question at a
later date, when I might get further
information. In the meantime, I have
had the pleasure of seeing and hearing
His Royal Highness the Prince of Wales
deliver his speech at the Guildhall,
London, on the housing question, and it
was quite evident that the large number
of representatives assembled there were
very much impressed by that speech.
His Royal Highness made a fearless con-
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
demnation of slums through the country,
and particularly in the part of his speech
where he referred to the sickness which
these people suffer and also where he
stated that the slums were simply a breeding ground and spreading place for
disease. I have no doubt that the delegates will go from that particular meeting
to the various districts determined to try
to influence their colleagues in the country
to endeavour to remove this blot, which
was very properly described as a blot on
the civilisation of our country.
I expect that the local authorities of
Northern Ireland will be very anxious to
know what provision our Government are
going to make or what financial assistance they are likely to give to enable
these bodies to do something to keep pace
with those across the Channel. It is
certainly no good for us to turn our eyes
up and simply thank God we are not as
bad as others, and do nothing further.
In that case I must inform you we would
be in a very short time left disgracefully
behind, because there are a very large
number of unhealthy houses in congested
districts and in cities such as Belfast. But
we must first recognise that other accommodation is necessary before any of these
poor people can be disturbed, and it
makes the position worse when we know
that there is a shortage of these small
dwellings at present.
The Minister of Home Affairs stated
in another place that the building of
small houses had almost ceased since the
reduction of the subsidy, yet considerable numbers of the building trade
workers are still unemployed and in
receipt of State benefit or outdoor relief.
To me, it appears to be a silly policy to
pay skilled workmen to walk the streets,
while there is such a demand for houses,
which they could build with an increased
payment. The whole community would
benefit because it would mean an increased
number of ratepayers for the local
authorities, a reduction in the payments.
of unemployment benefit, and, above all,
an improvement in the health and comfort of the people. I would also like
to point out that unhealthy houses cause
discontentment amongst the dwellers,
where the Communists angle for recruits,
and surely it is our duty to save the poor
people, if possible, from falling into the
clutches of such doubtful friends, who
would be delighted to make capital out of
their unfortunate plight.
I am very glad to say that the workers
of Northern Ireland are not easily deceived or coerced by these adventurers,
but we should endeavour to do the best
we possibly can for them, for they certainly deserve it.
I am well aware that the Government,
like individuals, require to be careful
how they spend, especially the public
money, during these hard times, but
surely this might be considered
spending, which is so much advocated at
present.
wise
In conclusion, I would like to quote
the closing sentences of His Royal Highness the Prince of Wales:
"Every generation has a dominating
social task, so let our age, our generation, be remembered as the one in
which we swept away this blot that
disgraces our national life."
Again he said:-
"This is an age of planning and
building, let us build a new Britain
and provide houses worthy of the dignity and greatness of our race.'
I hope our Government will find it possible to assist in carrying out this sane
policy. Do not let us be content to follow; let us lead in such a laudable effort.
I believe the people of Northern Ireland
are capable of solving this problem if the
Government will only give them the little
assistance and encouragement that is required.
I beg to ask the Leader of the House
whether the Government has considered
the strong appeal made by H.R.H. the
Prince of Wales for the demolition of
slums, and if it has decided to encourage
the local authorities of Northern Ireland
to join in the five years' campaign to rid
the country of slum property and sweep
away what has been described as a blot
that disgraces our national life; and
whether he can state what financial assistance will be granted by the Government
for the erection of the kitchen type dwelling-house, suitable for those disturbed,
and for other low-paid workers, to make
it possible for the houses to be let at a
rent within the reach of those for whom
they are provided.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Government have the question of housing
policy under consideration, but I am not
in a position to make an announcement
on the subject as the present moment.
Oral Answers
30 MAY 1933
Oral Answers
ERECTION OF RURAL COTTAGES.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Joseph
Cunningham:
To ask the Leader of the House,
whether he can state the number of
rural cottages for which application
was made by the Rural Councils in
1931, under the schemes which the
Government were unable to accept
owing to the financial crisis; and what
decision the Ministry forwarded to the
Association of Rural Councils of Northern Ireland, who, in November last,
asked for the Ministry's approval to
proceed with a smaller scheme, merely
to replace the worst houses.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: When
a similar question was put in this House
on a previous occasion representations
were made to me on this matter from the
rural districts. I was informed that in
the rural districts the position is very
bad. It was pointed out to me that in
rural districts a considerable amount of
money is spent in erecting good, healthy,
sanitary schools, and yet the children who
attend these palatial schools live in mean
and unhealthy dwellings, and they have
to return to these dark and unhealthy
cottages after school hours. That is the
reason I put down this question, and I
hope I shall get a more definite answer
than the one I received to the last
question.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: At the
time is was decided in 1931, for financial
reasons, to suspend operations under the
Labourers Acts, one Rural District
Council had made definite progress with
a scheme involving the erection of 333
cottages. At the same time four other
councils contemplated schemes involving
448 cottages.
The Ministry of Home Affairs received
representations from the Association of
Rural District Councils suggesting that
small schemes should be proceeded with,
and that extensive repairs to existing
cottages in private ownership should be
subsidised. In the financial circumstances
existing, however, the Ministry
unable to adopt these suggestions.
was
Mr. HILL DICKSON: In connection
with this question, I should like to point
out that in the Newtownards rural
district and in other rural areas these
labourers' cottages have been erected at
considerable expense, and the labourers
who occupy them object to paying a rent
of 14/- per month. When the ratepayers have to subsidise these houses, in
addition to the Government grant, to
enable them to be let at 14/- a month, I
do not think it is reasonable that the
occupants should ask for a lower rent,
because that would mean that an additional burden would be placed on the
ratepayers. The rent of the labourers'
cottages built under the original schemes.
is now about 9/- a month, and before
we embarked upon the new scheme the
applicants for these houses stated they
were prepared to pay a rent of 14/- a
month. Now they want that rent reduced, but if that is done the cost of
the reduction will have to be borne by
the ratepayers of the area, who probably
have no more means of paying than have
the occupants of these cottages. Unless
the tenants of these houses are prepared
to pay the present rent of 14/- per
month, not per week, I do not think it
is up to the Government to provide
additional houses for them.
Mr. BARCLAY: I would like to draw
the attention of the House to the fact
that all houses occupied by the working
classes do not belong to City Councils or
to Rural District Councils. A great
many of them are owned by landlords,
and I think the houses owned by landlords are in a worse condition than those
owned by local authorities. Some pressure
should be brought to bear on landlords to
compel them to have their houses put in
a proper state of repair, both inside and
outside. If that was done the health of
the people who have to occupy these
houses would be a good deal better than
it is at present. A great many of these
houses have been put up as a speculation,
and others have been erected in connection with factories or mills, and the persons who occupy them are not required
to maintain them in the way they should
be kept.
I think there should be a
periodical inspection of such houses, and
the persons responsible should be compelled to keep them in a proper state of
repair.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the Agricultural Marketing Bill, and the Unemployment (Temporary Provisions) Bill, to
which they desire the concurrence of this
House.
219 Agricultural Marketing Bill-

THE SENATE
First Reading
Agricultural Marketing Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."—(Mr. Long).
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In introducing the Agricultural Marketing Bill
to this House I should like to give hon.
Senators a brief outline of the circumstances which have led the Government
to introduce this Bill to Parliament.
The question of assisting the agricultural industry has been one which has
exercised the minds of successive Governments in these islands since the war and
especially in view of the decline in agricultural prices which set in round about
1921 or 1922, and which has continued in
a greater or lesser degree ever since, and
which has been particularly marked
during the last few years. An examination of the position in regard to the
agricultural industry in the United Kingdom showed clearly that one of the
greatest disadvantages under which the
farming community were labouring was
their weakness as sellers. Agricultural
production is carried on on large numbers
of relatively small isolated farms. There
are some four hundred thousand agricultural holdings in England and Wales,
between seventy and eighty thousand in
Scotland, and about one hundred
thousand in Northern Ireland.
Each farmer is producing in isolation.
and, what has been even more important
in the past, he has also been selling in
isolation, and very often in direct competition with his neighbours who are selling exactly the same description of
produce. The weakness of the farmer as
a seller of produce is thrown into even
sharper prominence when his position is
contrasted with the position of manufacturers of industrial goods. In the case
of almost every industrial commodity production is concentrated in the hands of a
relatively few firms, and it frequently
happens that these firms are members of
some kind of federation or other organisation which controls the prices at which
their products shall be sold.
An interesting example of this type of
organisation is that which was recently
established by the authority of Parliament in the case of the coal industry in
Great Britain. There are about 1,400
coal firms engaged in production in Great.
Britain and, as hon. Senators are
probably aware, a scheme of restriction
of output has been brought into force
through the operation of the Coal Mines
Act, 1930.
With the object of enabling farmers
themselves to form marketing boards,
which would exercise a greater degree of
control over the conditions of sale of
agricultural produce, the Labour Government, which was in power in 1931, introduced the Agricultural Marketing Bill
which, with the approval of the Conservative Party in that Parliament,
became law in the summer of 1931. The
object of this Measure was to enable
farmers to control the marketing of their
produce by limiting the quantity of produce which might be sold in accordance
with market requirements. Although it
was recognised that the principle underlying the Agricultural Marketing Act of
1931 was an extremely valuable one it
was felt, in the opinion of many people,
that this Measure did not go far enough.
It is one thing to restrict the quantity
of produce produced in the British Isles
which can be placed on the market, but
unless there is a corresponding restriction of imports from abroad the effect of
restricting home sales may be simply to
open the way for increased imports of
dumped produce from abroad. This fact
was recognised by the National Government which came into office in the autumn
of 1931. They recognised that the Agricultural Marketing Act of that year
provided a means for the organisation of
producers at home, so as to enable the
British farmer to regulate his sales of
produce in accordance with the demands
of the market.
The National Government then proceeded to complement the Act of 1931
by a further Agricultural Marketing Bill
under which power is taken to regulate
the importation of agricultural products
from abroad, and also sales of homeproduced agricultural products. This is
the Measure which is at present before
the Parliament at Westminster. When
this legislation is passed it will be open
to the United Kingdom Government,
subject to certain conditions, to regulate
the quantity of any agricultural product
imported into the United Kingdom. If,
however, imports of agricultural produce
from abroad are restricted by means of
a quota it is apparent that the British
Government in the interests of consumers
must be assured that an adequate supply
of home-produced agricultural produce
221 Agricultural Marketing Bill-

30 MAY 1933
First Reading
will be forthcoming in order to satisfy
the requirements of the consuming
public.
In the absence of organised bodies,
which are in a position to control the
marketing of agricultural produce at
home, it would be obviously difficult for
the Government to obtain this assurance.
This is one of the reasons why it has been
considered essential that marketing
boards should be established in Northern
Ireland, and why the Government has
consequently introduced this Bill. There
is, however, another aspect of the question. The primary object of restricting
imports from abroad is to obtain a better
price for British agricultural products
by bringing the total supply of produce
sold into line with market requirements.
If, however, the first effect of a restriction of imports was to stimulate a very
rapid expansion of home production the
result might be to bring about a slump.
in prices on account of the rapid increase in production at home. Consequently, as a natural corollary to the
limitations of imports from abroad, the
United Kingdom Government is taking
power in the Agricultural Marketing Bill
at present before Parliament at Westminster to regulate the sales of homeproduced agricultural products. The
natural way in which this regulation of
home scales will be effected will be
through marketing boards established
under the 1931 Act, but in default of a
marketing board the authorities at Westminster are in a position to make regulations designed to bring about the
restrictions of sales of home-produced
agricultural products which they have
decided upon.
Hon. Senators will agree, however,
that it is infinitely to be preferred that
a marketing board should exercise these
restrictions rather than that they should
be brought about by means of administrative regulations. Moreover, in Northern Ireland it would be a positive.
danger to us to allow sales of our produce
to be restricted in accordance with regulations made from Westminster which
would naturally be designed primarily to
suit conditions in Great Britain and
which might, therefore, be altogether
unsuitable to our conditions here.
The circumstances I have outlined wil
make it clear to hon. Senators that the
Measure, which is being introduced to
this House this afternoon is necessary in
order to safeguard the interests of Ulster
farmers. There will be an opportunity
at a later stage to discuss in detail the
various Clauses of the Bill. At present
it is sufficient for me to say that the Bill
is purely an enabling one. It enables
schemes to be framed for the marketing
of agricultural products. The circumstances in which these schemes may be
framed are set out in Clause 1. The
power to prepare schemes rests with the
Ministry of Agriculture, but that Department is required to take into consultation representatives of producers
and of other interests affected, while in
Schedule I of the Bill provision is made
for the hearing of objections to schemes.
framed by the Ministry.
I may say that the Ministry of Agriculture proposes to interpret the provisions of the Bill with regard to consultation in a most liberal manner, and
that the very fullest consultation with
producers and others affected will take
place before a scheme is brought into
operation. Once a scheme has come into
force it may be annulled by either
House of Parliament passing a resolution
to that effect, and, in any event, it will
lapse at the end of two years unless
during that period it is approved by a
positive resolution of each House of
Parliament.
The reasons why the power to prepare
schemes is vested solely in the Ministry
of Agriculture is largely on account of
the necessity which exists for securing co-
'ordination with developments in Great
Britain. In regard to special products
which may be the subject of a Quota
Order under Part 1 of the United Kingdom Act it will be essential that we
should prepare a scheme in Northern
Ireland, otherwise it would be left open
to the Imperial Authorities to make regulations governing the conditions under
which that product could be sold in
Northern Ireland. In the case of certain
other products which may not be the subject of a Quota Order it may, nevertheless, be essential that our scheme in Northern Ireland should complement the
Great Britain scheme.
In these circumstances the preparation
of schemes must be undertaken by the
Ministry of Agriculture which is in the
best position to take into account all the
circumstances and to decide upon the
policy which is in the best interests of
producers as a whole. Moreover, it is to
be remembered that the task of preparing
Motor Vehicles Bill
Moneylenders Bill-
THE SENATE
Viscount Charlemont]
a marketing scheme under the Act will be
one of considerable difficulty requiring a
wide knowledge of the economic position
and prospects of the industry. Legal,
financial and economic considerations are
all involved, and it would be extremely
difficult for this knowledge to be supplied by producers themselves.
It is significant that in England the
Government has adopted the practice of
appointing the re-organisation commissions charged with the duty of preparing
marketing schemes under the 1931 Act
and submitting these schemes to producers
for their consideration. Commissions for
pigs and pig products, for milk and for
fat stock have already been established,
and others are foreshadowed. The
position in Northern Ireland will not,
therefore, radically differ in practice from
the position in Great Britain, and the
Ministry will virtually be in the position.
of a permanent reorganisation commission
charged with the duty of preparing
schemes in consultation with producers.
The remaining Clauses of the Bill are
largely concerned with the matters which
may be provided for in a scheme.
It is
apparent, however, that the exact provisions of a scheme will depend upon circumstances and the particular product
which is being regulated, and, as I have
already indicated, every scheme will be
the subject of consultation with producers
and other interests before it is brought
into operation. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move,
this debate be now adjourned."

Bill.

a First time."-(Mr. Long.)

CLAUSES 1 (Order of Ministry of
Home Affairs regulating entry into Race
Area) and 2 (Short Title) ordered to

Appropriation Bill.

And a Message was sent to the House

Moneylenders Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In this
Bill the Government proposes to impose
upon moneylenders similar restrictions to
those contained in the Imperial Act of
1927, and to bring the law regulating
moneylending in Northern Ireland into
close accord with that which exists in
Great Britain. The Bill, however, contains, in addition, some clauses which are
specially designed to meet the local
circumstances of our particular area.
First and foremost, the Bill endeavours to secure that moneylenders,
both individuals and firms, shall be of
reputable character. An examination of
Clauses 1, 2 and 3 will make clear the
procedure which must be followed, in the
event of the Bill becoming law, by a
person who wishes to transact moneylending business. He must, in the first
place, apply annually to a bench consisting of Resident Magistrates only for
Moneylenders Bill-
Second Reading
30 MAY 1933
a certificate authorising the grant to him
of an excise licence. This certificate will
show the applicant's true name, his
business name, and his office address, and
the issue of a certificate may be refused
on certain grounds, which are clearly laid
down in sub-clause (6) of Clause 2. Subclause (7) of this Clause provides an
appeal to quarter sessions in the event of
a refusal by the magistrates to issue a
certificate. Having obtained a certificate,
the moneylender must then take out
annually an excise licence, the fee for
which is £15. The Government does not
consider this to be an unreasonable fee,
having regard to the large demands which
this form of trading makes upon the time
of the courts.
In Clause 3 it is provided that where
the holder of a certificate is convicted of
an offence under the law relating to
moneylenders, the court may, subject to
appeal, suspend or forfeit, or may suitably endorse his certificate, or may disqualify him from holding a certificate for
such time as the court thinks fit.
The Bill then proceeds to lay down
certain restrictions and directions as to
the manner in which the registered
moneylender shall transact his business in
general. He may not, for instance, issue
circulars save in response to written
request, or employ convassers. He must
comply with certain provisions of the
Companies Act with regard to disclosing
alien nationality of directors, and must
clearly show his registered name on office
stationery and advertisements. These
provisions will be found in Clauses 4
and 5.
Then follow provisions for the protection of borrowers. Clause 6 requires in
respect of each loan a memorandum or
agreement setting out full details of the
transactions, including the rate per cent.
per annum of interest charged, which
must be signed by the borrower, and the
borrower's husband, if the borrower
happens to be a married woman wholly
dependent on her husband.
By Clause 8 a borrower is entitled to
demand, on payment of a fee of 1s, a
complete statement of his loan account.
Clause 9 restricts moneylenders' interest
in bankruptcy cases to 5 per cent., pari
passu with other creditors' claims, without prejudice to his claim for additional
interest to which he may be entitled after
payment of other proved debts in full,
and stipulates the manner in which
moneylenders' claims are to be proved.
With regard to rates of interest applicable to moneylending transactions
generally, hon. Members will recollect
that a court has, under Section 1 of the
Moneylenders Act of 1900, power in
certain circumstances, to reopen transactions if the terms seem to be harsh and
unconscionable. Some difficulty has,
however, been experienced owing to the
fact that no standard is laid down by
which the court may gauge the fairness
or otherwise of the rate of interest
charged. We propose to make an
attempt to remove this difficulty by providing in Clause 10 of this Bill, that where
the interest charged exceeds 48 per cent.
per annum it shall, in the absence of
proof to the contrary, be deemed to be
excessive. It should, however, be clearly
understood that the setting up of this
standard is entirely without prejudice to
the right of the court to regard as
excessive a rate of less than 48 per cent.
The figure 48 per cent. may, at first
sight, seem rather high, but I would
remind hon. Members that the figure,
which is identical with that contained in
the Act of 1927, was decided upon after
very exhaustive consideration by the
Select Committee. It will be noted that
in Clause 11 we propose to accord to the
court the right to reopen certain transactions where it might be held that the
powers granted by the Act of 1900 were
insufficient for the purpose.
Clause 12 prohibits the charging of
fees for expenses in connection with the
issue of the loan.
I have now, I think, dealt briefly with
the main points of the Bill. The remaining Clauses deal, for the most part, with
matters arising out of or ancillary to
these earlier provisions, and I do not
propose at this stage to delay the House
by further explanation of their provisions. I shall, however, be happy to
supply any more detailed information
which hon. Members may desire at a
later stage. I beg to move,
"That this
Bill be now read a Second time."
Mr. DORMAN: I wish, in the first
place, to congratulate the Leader of the
House on the very clear exposition he
has given of the various Clauses of this
Bill. It appears to me to be one of the
most important Measures that have been
brought before this House for a very con-
Moneylenders Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
siderable time. To-day we see the statesmen of the world running to and fro over
the face of the earth trying to evolve
some scheme to settle satisfactorily the
state of chaos existing in the world today. That state of chaos is undoubtedly
due to the fact that in the various
countries of the world the mediums of
exchange both in the international and the
national sphere are in the possession of
private individuals who are permitted to
control and manipulate the mediums of
exchange for the purpose of enriching
themselves. In a play of one of the
great dramatists he said, "He owns my
life who owns the means whereby I live,
and it may be truthfully said that the
people who own the means of exchange,
the necessary machinery whereby alone
the needs of the community can be satisfied day by day, practically own the lives
of the people, who must have that means
of exchange if they are to live.
This is not a new question so far as
legislation is concerned. If you look up
the Acts passed by the Irish Parliaments
in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries you will find numerous Measures
that were brought in to restrain the
usury then exercised by merchants and
others who controlled the medium of exchange. In this Bill now before the
House the maximum rate of interest that
may be charged is fixed at 48 per cent.
per annum, but in the legislation introduced in the Irish Parliaments in the
seventeenth and eighteenth centuries a
number of Bills succeeding one another
reduced the amount of interest to 10 per
cent., 8 per cent., 7 per cent., and 6 per
cent. In that legislation they have good
economic reasons why they restrained the
exorbitant interest demanded at that
time, not by what we call moneylenders,
but largely on the part of those merchants
who, seeing that industry was
domestic character, purchased from the
people their surplus produce and placed it
on the market. When the purchaser was
not able to meet the demand for payment
immediately, the merchant proceeded to
demand certain rates of interest on the
outstanding portion of the debt. Thus
it came about that at that particular
period Parliament found it necessary to
intervene and curtail the tendency of
those who controlled the medium of
exchange.
of a
The moneylenders who are referred to
in this particular Bill are not the only
moneylenders. All our great banking in.
stitutions are more or less moneylending
institutions. They are not necessarily
engaged in lending money of their own,
but they trade on the uncalled balances
of their depositors.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: Not at 48 per
cent. interest.
Mr. DORMAN: That is true, but at
the same time they trade at a rate of
interest which is not under the control
of the community as a whole, and not
under the control of the industries which
are often handicapped because of the
variation in that rate of interest. That
variation in the rate prevents them from
successfully entering into competition
with the industries of other countries in
the world. Therefore, I wish to compliment the Government on taking this
step to try to control this system for the
benefit of the community. One has only
to look at the returns of the large moneylending institutions in the various
countries of the world to realise that,
while these institutions are flourishing beyond the dreams of avarice, the industries and trades of these same countries
are crying out for assistance. Millions of
our fellow creatures are deprived of the
right to work simply because of the inability of the statesmen of the world to
evolve a better system than that which
exists to-day, as the Minister of Finance
said in another place the other day.
In looking over the accounts of the
three leading banks in Northern Ireland
I find that while our shipyards and other
industries are languishing, and our merchants are compelled to deposit securities
in the form of shares and the deeds of
their premises in order to obtain the
necsesary medium of exchange, these
banking institutions are flourishing to the
extent that they made a profit of well
over a quarter of a million pounds.
There are one or two points in the Bill
I should like to refer to briefly. I think
the licence fee of £15 is rather too high.
That is an exorbitant amount to charge,
because it may have the effect of driving
out of this particular calling a large
number of men who are engaged in only
a small way of business. Moneylenders
are not always men who have control of
a balance at the bank. Some of them
must necessarily go to the bigger institutions for the purpose of getting the money
they require, and they will be handi-
Moneylenders Bill-
Second Reading
30 MAY 1933
capped if they have to pay this fee of
£15. Another thing I object to is the
differentiation which is made in the Bill
between a pawnbroker and the ordinary
moneylender. Pawnbrokers will be allowed
to charge interest at the rate of only 20
per cent., while the ordinary moneylender is not to be subject to this restriction.
There are some admirable Clauses in
the Bill, especially that relating to the
custom of lending money to married
women without the consent of their
husbands. I know of several instances
of tradesmen in Belfast in a fair way of
business who have paid debts incurred
by their wives rather than expose themselves to action by moneylenders. I
would like to ask the right hon. Gentleman, the Leader of the House, a question with regard to Clause 13. It is not
clear to me what the meaning of that
Clause is. Paragraph (a) of the Clause
states: :-
If during the period of twelve
months aforesaid or at any time within
any subsequent period during which
proceedings may by virtue of this proviso be brought, the debtor acknowledges in writing the amount due and
gives a written undertaking to the
moneylender to pay that amount, proceedings for the recovery of the amount
due may be brought at any time within a period of twelve months from the
date of the acknowledgment and
undertaking;
In the case of a man who borrowed £100
and agreed to repay it at £10 per month,
if he becomes a defaulter at the end of
six months and gives to the moneylender
a written acknowledgment of his debt,
does it mean that the twelve months
would begin to count from that date,
and not from the date of the original
loan? If he later again becomes a defaulter and again gives the moneylender
a written acknowledgment of his indebtedness, would another twelve months
begin to operate from the date of that
second acknowledgment? If that is so
it appears to me to be a suspension of the
ordinary Statute of Limitations of six
years. I would like to have some information on that point.
Mr. LESLIE: I strongly support the
Bill which has been introduced to the
House this afternoon. I regard the
Measure as distinctly overdue.
It may
be within the knowledge of hon. Senators
that the Government in another place
was twice interrogated on this matter, I
think in the years 1927 and 1928, but it
was stated at that time that it was
thought better to wait for experience of
the English Measure, which, of course,
was the Act of 1927. There have been
five years to gain that experience, but I
hope the Province is now going to derive
the benefit of whatever that experience
has taught. Now, the Bill before us is
based on the Act of 1927. I think the
noble Viscount reminded us of that. The
Act of 1927 had a very chequered and
somewhat prolonged career. It was the
ultimate outcome, one may say, of a long
series of private Bills introduced into this
or that House of Parliament, and these
Bills met with varying fortunes. One for
instance, introduced by Lord Carson in
the House of Lords, passed all its stages
in that House but did not get any
further. Finally, the Parliamentary
ballot was very kind to a gentleman
named, I think, Burman, who got a Bill
through the House of Commons in 1927,
and Lord Darling took it through the
House of Lords in that year. It is on
that Bill the Bill before us is based.
The Bill follows the Act of 1927 very
closely indeed, almost, perhaps, in one
or two instances too closely. But it does
follow the Bill with great exactness,
although there are certain innovations
and certain adaptations. The adaptations explain themselves, but I propose
to direct attention to one or two innovations. Although the Bill follows the
English Bill very closely I am glad to
observe that the Bill preserves this House
from the introduction of one perfectly
marvellous word which occurs in relation
to a firm in the English Bill. It is a
word I do not remember to have seen
before, and which I am not wholly able
to appreciate. The word is "unincorporate. The omission or correction of
that is almost the only form of correction
I observed in the Bill.
I do not want to take up the time of
the House by dilating unduly on the
merits of the Bill, especially as there is
nothing whatever in the principles of the
Bill to which I can take exception. I
am not shocked as hon. Members in both
places have been, I know, by the 48 per
cent maximum. I am prepared to
support that, not that I believe 48 per
cent. is in itself a proper amount to
charge, but because I do not think our
Moneylenders Bill-
Second Reading.
1 HE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
courts ought to be given a lesser jurisdiction than their confrères across the
water. 48 per cent is in the English Act
as a maximum, and I do not think it is
desirable to alter it in Northern Ireland.
To refer for a moment to the limitation
on the pawnbroker alluded to by the hon.
Gentleman on my left (Mr. Dorman), I
venture to point out that the pawnbroker
who lends at 20 per cent. has certainly
the security of the pledge on which he
lends the money.
He has that tangible
object to recover on if necessary, but the
moneylender at 48 per cent. probably has
no security at all. It is because of that lack
of security he is able to exact, if not
necessarily able to obtain, so much higher
terms.
I think there is nothing in the
principle of the Bill that I want to talk
about, but there is a great deal, I think,
to be said on the question of machinery.
As I expect the noble Viscount will desire
to take the Committee Stage of this Bill
on Thursday I think, perhaps, the House
will allow me to go a little more into
details than would otherwise be right.
But, even so, I do not want to be more
elaborate than I can help, especially
as
several amendments will naturally
group themselves on the several points.
The first thing that occurs to me
is the difficulty of the moneylender himself. The moneylender of the future in
Northern Ireland is to be essentially the
child of statute. He is to be begotten,
if I may use the expression, by licence out
of certificate. As regards the man who
is already in office or at work, he must get
himself regenerated before the 1st of
January next. There seems no difficulty
there.
But what about the man of the future,
the man who is not a moneylender to-day
but wishes to become one in future? He
wants to put his foot on the first rung
of the ladder which leads to philanthropic benevolence, or wants to put his
foot on the top stair that leads elsewhere.
There will be many who desire to become
moneylenders, and to do so will be taking
out certificates. It seems to me that we
do require words of inclusion to cover
that case. They can, I think, be put in
without serious disturbance of the text.
Next, following England, it appears
to me that the Bill introduces some confusion as to what is to be authorised by
what. I think there is verbal confusion,
at all events, certainly not inextricable confusion, but here, on the contrary,
I think we require certain words of
exclusion-half a line in one place and a
line in another. I am not very sure
about that, especially as the noble
Viscount quoted some of the words that
are in my mind when introducing the
Bill.
Passing to a more serious matter, the
question of the court which deals with
the application for the certificate, we are
going further than they went in England.
First of all, we are saying that the court
must be constituted by resident magistrates sitting alone. In England they
had a similar provision relating to the
stipendiary magistrate, but they limited.
it to the stipendiaries in the Metropolitan Police District. They did not
extend the exclusive jurisdiction of the
stipendiaries to Glasgow, Leeds, Birmingham and other places. They drew a distinction between London and the other
parts of Great Britain. I think there
was probably a reason for that, and the
reason is that the English stipendiary is
limited in his commission to his own
locality. Here, as we know, the arrangement is wider. The resident magistrate
who is empowered to sit in Belfast can
sit anywhere else in the Six Counties and
vice versa.
I do not object in this case to the jurisdiction being limited to resident magistrates. It seems to me that the resident
magistrates for this purpose occupy a
rather convenient half-way house between
the local authority, who might be expected
to take the place of the commissioners
of customs and excise to which the earlier
Acts applied, and the county court. I
think the resident magistrate, especially
as he has jurisdiction throughout
Northern Ireland, is a very convenient
official for the issue of certificates. I
make no complaint about that. We shall
probably find that nine-tenths of the
moneylenders who will come in under this.
Bill will be operating in Belfast. I do
not know the exact numbers, but I am
going to suggest that there are something
like 230 moneylenders in Northern
Ireland, that 200 operate in Belfast, and
only about 30 in other districts. It
seems to me the one thing that is wanted
here is uniformity in practice in the
matter of issuing certificates. You will
certainly get that better by limiting the
Moneylenders Bill-
Second Reading
30 MAY 1933
number of authorising officials. I therefore entirely agree about resident
magistrates so far as that part of Clause 2
goes.
But I do not agree at all as regards
the court which is to be the medium of
the pronouncements. The text refers to
the court having jurisdiction in the district in which the business is carried on.
It seems to me that that is a very unnecessary provision and is not likely to
help us very much. I will suggest an
alteration as to that at a later stage. I
do not, of course, propose that a man who
wants to operate in Belfast or Londonderry should get a certificate in Antrim or
Downpatrick, but I do not see any particular reason why a man who wants to
operate in Belfast and Antrim should not
get a certificate, or rather, two certificates, in Belfast. I think that would
save time and trouble and make for the
general convenience.
It is necessary, presumably, that certain regulations should be made, and the
Ministry of Home Affairs is perhaps the
appropriate authority to make them. I
am asking the House to look for a moment
at sub-clause (5), of Clause 2. There
are three paragraphs, (a), (b), and (c).
Now (a) and (c) have their exact counterpart in England, but paragraph (b) has
no such counterpart, and I question very
much whether we ought to introduce it
here. I do not think we ought to give
the Minister of Home Affairs power to
regulate as to the persons entitled to be
represented at the hearing of the application, or any other hearing. It seems
to me that the parties to be represented
is solely a matter for the court. That,
again, I shall have to bring before the
House when it is in Committee.
Passing on, we find two of the matters
that I have called innovations towards the
end of sub-clause (6), the requirement
that the applicant shall be resident in
Northern Ireland for twelve months, and
the prohibition of the grant of a licence
to an applicant if he is interested in the
sale of intoxicating liquor. Neither of
these proposals is to be found in the Act
of 1927. I do not know that anyone
will object to the first of them, and I
am quite certain that nobody with any
knowledge of this kind of thing in Ireland will raise the slightest objection to
the prohibition of the granting of a
licence to a man licensed for the sale of
intoxicating liquor. That is one of the
more admirable features of the Bill.
As the licence gives certain privileges
so it imposes certain liabilities. We have
to deal with the question of what is to be
done with the moneylender who misbehaves. He is subjected to certain penalties. The certificate may be brought up
and endorsed as in the case of a motor
driver. But when the certificate has been
endorsed, the question arises what ought
to be done to publish the fact of that
endorsation. The proposal in the Bill
is that the endorsement should be communicated to the authority by whom the
certificate was granted and to the Ministry,
and the Ministry means the Ministry of
Finance, who issues the licence. I do not
know exactly what they will do with the
information when they get it.
That
would appear to be their business.
With regard to sending information to
the authority granting the certificate,
that must mean the petty sessions court.
I can see no use in that whatever.
If a
man gets a certifiate in Downpatrick and
misbehaves and loses his certificate, that
man should not be allowed to go to Coleraine and get another licence there.
What you want is something central. I
am going to suggest that the endorsement shall be sent to the Ministry of
Home Affairs, who may acquaint all the
resident magistrates. That, I think, is
a simple method and an effective one.
Now we come to Clause 5, a very admirable Clause, dealing with the limitations of advertisements and things like
that. Everybody who has read this Bill
speaks with enthusiasm about that
Clause. But before I leave it I want an
assurance from the noble Viscount, and if
I can I should like to get it this afternoon that the powers taken in sub-clause
(2), of Clause 5, are sufficient to meet, prohibit, extinguish and generally abolish a
certain class of advertisement. I want to
be sure that the powers in that subclause will meet such advertisements as I
hold in my hand, and which I am going
to read to the House. I am not going to
advertise or pillory the names of the
people responsible for the advertisement.
This advertisement appeared in a Belfast
paper as recently as 5th May, but I have
not seen it during the last few days. The
advertisement runs as follows:
arrange loans for all responsible
householders from £10 upwards; special
terms offered to R.U.C. and Civil
Servants, strictest privacy guaranteed.
Moneylenders Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
The other advertisement is not quite so
bad. It appeared in an evening paper
yesterday:-
Loans granted in town and country
to farmers, dealers, school teachers,
Police, Civil Servants, and others.
I say that advertisements like that are
dead against public policy, particularly
in Northern Ireland, and if the noble
Viscount is unable to assure us that the
Clause has sufficient power to stop them,
then I think it will be incumbent upon
us to put down an Amendment which, I
venture to suggest, could be framed of
sufficient strength and very considerable.
heat. Indeed, speaking of heat, the
House might consider that the penal
portion of the Clause I am referring to
is not quite strong enough to meet such
a case. In any case, I think a trival
correction is required in the next Sub-
Clause. I am quite certain the House
would support any Amendment, if an
Amendment is necessary, which would
effect the purpose to which I have just
referred.
I called Clause 5 an admirable Clause.
Clause 6 has already been alluded to by
the hon. Senator on my left (Mr.
Dorman). It demands a stronger and
more appreciative adjective. It strikes,
I think, a very strong blow, indeed,
against one of the curses of the present
system, and it will, if the House accepts
the principle of this Clause, as I am sure
it will, give the married woman, and
particularly the married woman in the
humblest walks of life, and the home of
that woman, a protection which she has
not got now and which she ought to have
been given years ago. So far as I have
been able to ascertain, Northern Ireland
will be ahead of Great Britain in giving
that protection. But I am not certain
even yet whether the phrase we have used
is strong enough. I am afraid of the
expression "married woman totally dependent on her husband." It seems to
me to raise a somewhat difficult question
of fact in every particular case. I would
like to ask the House to think over some
better phrase. I confess that the only
phrase that occurs to me at the moment
is the somewhat old legal expression
"woman under coverture." That would
not be of assistance to the moneylenders
in question.
That is all I have to say about the Bill.
There will be certain Amendments
suggested, and they will really be Amendments rather of the English Act in so
far as it is incorporated in our own Bill
than of anything we have done ourselves.
I most strongly support the Bill, and I
venture to assert it will do a large
amount of good which will continue for
very many years.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
also congratulate the Government on introducing this Bill. It is a most difficult
subject to deal with, and I have no doubt
the House will discover that it can be
improved by some minor Amendments.
To my mind there are two great benefits
that will be derived from the Bill. One
is that when a person borrows money
from a moneylender he will be in a
position to know what the contract
actually is, and that should do away
with a great deal of heart-burning. I
take it that when this Bill becomes law,
if a person who borrows money is not
given a copy of the contract the moneylender would not have a good case against
him in the courts in the event of the
borrower being sued.
Another great advantage will be that
a borrower will in future know from
whom he is borrowing, because the
identity of the moneylender must be
stated. I think these two clauses will do
away with a great deal of the hardship
experienced in the past. I support the
Bill, and I think it is a first-class
Measure.
I
are
Mr. BARCLAY: In supporting the
Second Reading of this Bill I agree with
Senator Leslie that it is long overdue.
It will be as great a blessing to the
working classes as was the removal of the
spirit grocers from the street corners.
hope that Clauses 1 and 2 will bring
under the notice of those responsible for
the administration of the law a certain
class of moneylender who is a great evil
in the back streets. There women
lending from a shilling and two shillings
up to five shillings to other women for
the purpose of backing horses, and when
a woman borrows a shilling on a Monday
morning she has to pay back in principal
and interest one and sixpence on the
Friday night. When this Bill becomes
law it is up to the authorities to try to
ferrit out these people who are bleeding.
the poor, not in the public thoroughfares,
but in the back streets. The moneylender who has a large office in the centre
Moneylenders Bill-
Second Reading
30 MAY 1933
of the city is well-known and can easily
be watched, but the people who ought to
be carefully watched and caught are those
who will have neither certificate nor
licence and who will try to evade the law.

I agree with what has been said by
Senators Leslie and Dorman with regard
to advertisements issued by moneylenders. Senator Leslie quoted one or
two advertisements he had seen in the
newspapers, but they were not quite so
attractive as one I would like to quote
now. This is one for the working
classes: Workers, don't worry. Cash
in advance." It also says this cash will
be sent on receipt of a letter, and they
can have from £1 to £500 to pay their
bills. A working-class man or
reading that advertisement would gain
the impression that this was money for
nothing, but when they get the money
they find they had got to pay dearly for
it. Here is another advertisement:-
woman
Look here, you people. If you want
a loan same day as application, apply
to me. You know our straightforward
dealing.
It would appear to me that all moneylenders are not straightforward, because
in several advertisements I have seen this
point is emphasised. I am very glad
that under this Bill these advertisements
will be restricted to a certain extent, and
that may be the means of saving a great
many people from falling into this trap.
I support the Bill because I believe it
will be of great benefit to both rich and
poor.
Sometimes people who are well off
become entangled in the toils of moneylenders and find it difficult to get out of
their clutches. Therefore I welcome this
Bill, even though it has been brought in
at this late date.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am very
pleased that the Bill has had such a
favourable reception. I will postpone a
reply to some of the points raised to a
later date, but I would like to give
Senator Leslie the assurance he asked for
with regard to advertisements issued by
moneylenders. If hon. Senators will
consult Clause 5, sub-section (2), they
will see that a moneylender can put only
certain particulars in an advertisement,
that is to say,
any authorised address at which he
carries on business as a moneylender
and the telegraphic address and telephone number thereof, any address at
which he formerly carried on business,
a statement that he lends money with
or without security, and of the highest
and lowest sums that he is prepared to
lend, and a statement of the date on
which the business carried on by him
was first established.
That precludes his being able to put into
an advertisement any special terms for
any particular section of the community.
I am afraid it would be impossible to
bring within the scope of the Bill those
persons referred to by Senator Barclay,
namely, those who are willing, without
being registered moneylenders, to provide
a certain amount of money one day for
backing a horse. I dare say that many
hon. Senators have lent money occasionally in the ordinary course of events, and
we could scarcely be expected to deal
with them under a Bill such as this.
As regards Senator Dorman's suggestion that the licence duty is too high, it
is very desirable that the duty should be
the same here as it is in Great Britain
because obviously if it was lower here
Northern Ireland might become a seat of
moneylending activity. Therefore I think
it is advisable to keep the licence duty as
it is in the Bill, although the Government would of
course consider any
amendment that might be put forward.
The hon. Member's interpretation of
Clause 13 was quite correct.
This proto one
vision overrides the Statute of Limitation by reducing the period of six years
year. Any other information
asked for I shall be pleased to give at a
later stage of the Bill.

be now read a Second Time," agreed to.

Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn
until Thursday next."
Mr. LESLIE: Until Thursday? The
noble Viscount cannot I suppose give us
any further open information now? We
rather left it on the discussion of the
Standing Order. We did not accept
Friday and Monday did not seem very
popular. I think we may take it that we
shall not sit on Friday and that on Thursday we shall be told whether we shall sit
on Monday?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think
much will have to depend on the course of
events. If things go favourably Friday
and Monday can go out. If not I should
like to have them up my sleeve.
