Question. to Minister.
VEHICLES PASSING STATIONARY
TRAMS.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Quigley:-
To ask the Leader of the House whether
in view of the large number of accidents
in Belfast, he will consider the advisability of introducing legislation to prevent
motor and horse vehicles and motor and
push bicycles from passing tramcars which
have stopped for the purpose of taking on
and letting off passengers, in order to
secure the greater safety of the public.
Mr. QUIGLEY: In asking the question
standing in my name, I do so in the interest of the safety of our people. Many
fatal accidents occur each year
in our
city, and it is probable that some of these
might be averted if the regulation suggested in my question received proper
attention. I believe such a regulation is
in force in Dublin, and also in a number
of cities in the United States. One
minute's stop might mean the saving of
a
valuable life. I admit that many
motorists are very careful and thoughtfully do stop, but unfortunately there
are others who have a little or no consideration for other people. In addition
to the fatal accidents, many persons are
injured and have to be taken to our hospitals for treatment. I would like to
refer to another matter. All vehicles,
motor-cycles and bicycles should have
rear reflectors affixed, and if this regulation were strictly carried out it would
be in the interest of vehicular traffic
generally. I am afraid, however, that this
important regulation is more honoured in
the breach than the observance. Many
vehicles and bicycles are not provided
with rear lights or reflectors, and some of
those which are affixed are practically
useless for the purpose.
I would like to read a letter bearing
on the subject which appeared in our
morning and evening newspapers during
this week. It is headed, "Cyclists and
Road Dangers, and is from Mr. H. R.
Oswald, late H.M. Coroner for the
County of London. It reads as follows:-
""
Sir,-All those who, like myself, have
had to investigate road accidents will
welcome the plea of Mr. Herbert Morrison, who speaks with experience as
Minister of Transport in the Labour
Government, to compel the use of a
rear light on cycles in place of the ineffective reflector at present permitted.
It is significant that the worst hour
for accidents, according to statistics, is
between 10 and 11 o'clock at night. It
is not the cyclists themselves who alone
are the sufferers, but motorists are
almost unanimous in saying that it is
the cyclists without rear lights who
cause great anxiety and strain, which
in turn lead to reduced driving efficiency
and dreadful accidents.-Yours, etc.
H. R. OSWALD, late H.M.
Coroner, County of London.
Let us adopt as our watchword "Safety
First."
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): The matter to which the
hon. Member refers has frequently been
the subject of prolonged and careful consideration by the Police Committee of the
Belfast Corporation, the police authorities, the Roads Advisory Committee and
the Ministry of Home Affairs.
The difficulty of satisfactorily enforcing
such a regulation and the possible
injurious effects which would be produced
have convinced each of these bodies that
a regulation is not desirable. If made,
it could not be enforced only in the centre
of the city and at the other special points
where danger is greatest. To prevent
confusion it must apply equally throughout the full length of each tramway
route. It would unquestionably congest
traffic in the centre of the city and elsewhere, and would thereby cause inconvenience and danger which must result
in more accidents than the present procedure occasions.
The police already possess ample power
to deal with vehicle drivers, including
cyclists, who endanger tramway passengers, and it is only through co-operation
between tramway employees, passengers
and vehicle drivers that accidents can be
prevented.
The number of accidents caused
annually to tramway passengers by passing vehicles is not considerable. For the
most part they are of a trivial nature,
and during the past two years
improvement has been indicated, mainly
because tram conductors and motorists
have been co-operating.
an
The question of rear lights on cycles is
not mentioned in the hon. Senator's
question, but if he would like to put
down a question on that subject at a
future date I shall be very pleased to
answer it.
Major DOBBS: Might I ask the noble
Lord, the Leader of the House, if he
would consider and, if he thinks it well,
use his influence with the proper
authorities to bring about the abolition
entirely of the Belfast trams, and substitute in their place trolly buses. The
trolly buses would take their current
from the existing overhead cables. They
have the great advantage of being able to
draw up to the curb to take up and set
down passengers, and by doing that they
would entirely eliminate these accidents
to which attention has been drawn in this
question. They have been found to be of
great benefit in various towns in England,
not only from that point of view but
also from the point of view of being a
practical solution, or almost a practical
solution, of the congestion of traffic.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
would like to ask the Leader of the House
if it is not a fact that the conductors on
the trams of the Belfast Corporation cooperate with the authorities to avoid
accidents. The manager of the tramways continuously instructs conductors
that when their tram is about to stop
they should put out their hand so that
oncoming traffic receives a warning that
the tram is about to stop. As far as I
am aware that is invariably done by conductors, and I believe there has been a
considerable reduction in accidents of
that kind. The conductors carry out this
instruction very satisfactorily, and they
also put out their hand when the tram is
about to turn a corner.
As regards the question of abolishing
the trams, that would have to be very
carefully considered by the Belfast Corporation before they agreed to such a
proposal. It is admitted that no bus
service can carry the same number of
passengers as the double-deck trams
Major DOBBS: Trolly buses.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
Or
trolly buses. They could not move the
great number of people that have to be
moved by the trams on our routes in
such a short space of time. This
question has repeatedly been very carefully considered by the Tramways Committee of the Belfast Corporation, and
there is no intention at the present time
of scrapping the system. Only a short
time ago the track was relaid, and the
Tramways Committee will take good care.
that it is worn out before they make any
change.
Mr. QUIGLEY: With regard to the
number of accidents-
Mr. SPEAKER: I am sorry to interrupt the hon. Member, but he must
remember that when he puts a question,
whether he makes any remarks before he
puts the question or simply puts it, it is
equivalent to making a speech, and therefore he is not entitled to speak any
further unless he wishes to put a supplementary question.
245 Agricultural Marketing Bill--

1 JUNE 1933
Second Reading.
Mr. QUIGLEY: Thank you.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I should
like to agree with Senator Cunningham
in his remarks about the tram conductors
in Belfast. It is my personal experience
that they always raise a hand before the
trams stop, and give motorists behind an
opportunity of stopping. I think, as a
matter of fact, a good many of these
accidents to which Senator Quigley has
referred in his question have been due
not to people getting off the trams, but to
people who, observing the tram stopping,
make a convenience of it for coming out
from the side of it, and who are not
actually passengers, and whom the
motorists behind cannot possibly see before he is actually on them. I do not
think it is a matter of people getting off
the trams so much as of the people I
have referred to. As regards the tramway system in general, I think the hon.
and gallant Senator had better refer his
question to the Lord Mayor of Belfast,
who is a better person to answer it than
myself.
Agricultural Marketing Bill.
Order for Resumed Debate on Second
Reading read.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not rise this afternoon for the purpose of opposing this
Bill.
I think that that course would be
altogether impossible. We all recognise
that something has got to be done, and
I think we all recognise more clearly,
perhaps, this afternoon than we had an
opportunity of recognising on Tuesday
last, that we are required to legislate
if not completely in the dark, at all
events, in the twilight. Well, it sometimes happens that one must do things
when daylight is failing or before dawn
comes, and I suppose this is one of those
occasions. I think that everybody
throughout the Province recognises that
some steps on the lines indicated in this
Bill-perhaps I should say suggested
rather than indicated-but something
must be done on the lines suggested by
the Bill as a last hope for the agricultural
industry.
And I think we see that, following the
Ottawa Conference, it has to be accepted
that what is known as the quota system
has come to stay, and it is on those lines
that the agriculturist and those interested in him must proceed, if they are
to be enabled to carry on their business
at all, that is to say, if they are to be
enabled to secure a reasonable reward
for their labour and expense. We have
to accept the quota system and we have
to do that, it seems to me, as part of
the United Kingdom of Great Britain
and Northern Ireland, and therefore it
follows that we must model and conform
our own procedure to that which is done
in Great Britain.
But that which is done in Great
Britain will be found to be based partly
on what is described in the Bill as the
Act of 1931, and partly on what is described as the United Kingdom Act at
present before the Imperial Parliament.
The United Kingdom Act, I think I am
correct in stating, has got through perhaps not a wholly untroubled career in
the House of Commons, and is at the
moment before the House of Lords.
are taking power, or the Bill is, to make
such adjustments as prove necessary in the
event of any serious Amendment of the
United Kingdom Bill being made before
it becomes law. The noble Viscount
spoke the other day I think-I am sorry
I have not been able to check my
memory as the Debates are not yet in
print of this Bill as an enabling Bill.
We
Needless to say, that is so to a great
extent. In fact, it is entirely an enabling Bill, but it is not wholly a permitting Bill, because there is one passage
in the proviso at the top of the second
page which practically requires the
Ministry of Agriculture to prepare a
scheme in certain circumstances. I
think that what we will have to consider
is what will happen here if we do not
accept the proposals in this Bill. It
would seem to follow from the United
Kingdom Bill, so far as I have been able
to follow it, if we do not do anything
ourselves it will have to be done for us
across the water. Whether that would
be or would not be to our advantage I
confess I am not very clear. I should
rather hope that during the course of the
debate we may get some enlightenment
on that. It seems to me we are so bound
up with England and Scotland in the
matter that there is very great danger,
in the event of there being a surplus of
some product, we may be left with our
surplus, and there is also a danger that
the consumer across the water may be
left with a deficiency. I should like
further light upon that, and I should
like to get it as soon as practicable.
247 Agricultural Marketing Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Leslie
Our Measure differs very much in one
important way from the Bill before the
Imperial Parliament. By the arrangement under the United Kingdom Act it
is intended that a draft scheme is to be
submitted to Parliament, and if Parliament approves of the scheme the Minister
will bring it into operation by Order.
Here we are proposing to proceed entirely
differently. The Minister is after due
consultation there is ample provision in
the Bill for the consideration of every
interest-after all this has been done the
Minister is to make a scheme by Order,
and then bring it before Parliament for
confirmation or rejection as the case may
be. There is not much said in the Bill
to provide for the possibility of amendment of the scheme by Parliament.
Doubtless we shall hear more about that.
The main difference between the two Bills
comes in this way. In Great Britain
Parliament is consulted first; in Northern
Ireland it is proposed to consult Parliament second. The Parliament of
Northern Ireland will be presented with
an accomplished fact. The scheme, if
there is one, will come before Parliament
on the proposal of the Minister of Agriculture, and the Minister of Agriculture
I will have behind him the Cabinet and the
support of his party in another place.
That is to say, if the scheme is to be
opposed in this Parliament the full
strength of the Party Whip will be
brought into operation in order to support the scheme. I should like to hear
that when either House is considering the
scheme no attempt will be made to put
pressure on the vote of individual Members. The party system is all very well
and necessary, as we know, in Chambers
of first instance, but not so necessary or
perhaps so desirable in second Chambers.
Still it is there. But there are occasions,
and I submit this is one of them, when
what we may describe as the "committee
upstairs," to use the Westminster
expression, affords a far better way of
hammering out something satisfactory
than having the consideration of a scheme
in an assembly governed by party considerations.
From the fact that the scheme has to
be brought into operation, the question
seems to arise as to what powers Parliament has in the way of getting rid of a
scheme which has not proved satisfactory.
Assuming it has been approved by
Parliament there does not seem to be any
definite arrangement for getting rid of
the scheme. Presumably, the Minister
would, himself, revoke it, or I imagine a
Resolution might be moved in either
House requiring its revocation. To
secure revocation of a scheme in that
manner would seem a cumbersome proposal. The only thing we can hope for
is that the schemes will be such that they
will not require revocation.
After all, the success of this Measure
must depend on the schemes framed
under the Bill. There are certain
essentials necessary to success. There is,
first, that the scheme should be administered with the strictest equity. That is,
if we
are dealing with potatoes there
must be no unfair choice of the producer
whose potatoes are taken. It must not
be a case of one lot shall be taken and
the other left. There must be absolute
fairness. I cannot help thinking there
may be difficulties in securing that that
will be done. Another consideration is
economy. There must not be any undue
expense and, indeed, the less expense the
better in the working of the scheme.
Apparently all the expense in regard to
this matter is to fall upon the producer.
They are to be assessed as fairly as may
be no doubt, but the producer has got
to bear the burden of the cost, and if he
is to bear the burden of the cost he can
only do it by means of receiving an enhanced price for his product. I do not
think anybody concerned of late in the
production of potatoes would say there
was any margin left to pay for unnecessary or even necessary expense. It
is a very grave consideration to my mind
whether there will be that margin of
profit to provide for expense which must
come before increased profit.
And again, these schemes are to be
administered by boards who are to be
representative of the various producers.
It is most important that there should
be the freest possible choice of representatives. In England it seems to me
it is proposed to have a somewhat
cumbersome system of election, which
must be horribly expensive and, I think,
not very likely to induce the right people
to come forward. There must be the
freest choice to get representatives.
There are farmers who do not belong to
the Farmers' Union or other organisation, but they will be producers and they
ought to be considered.
249 Agricultural Marketing Bill-

1 JUNE 1933
Second Reading
Then again there is the question of
efficiency-the efficiency of the scheme.
That seems to follow, if it is to be
attained at all, from the satisfactory
outcome of all the other matters. We
need say nothing about that. And,
finally, there is the interest of the consumer. It seems to me it is in the
interests of the consumer, rather than
in the interests of the producers, that
the quota system has been adopted in
opposition to the alternative of the imposition of a general tax. But the
interests of the consumer have got to be
considered. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.) Because, to quote Major Elliott
across the water, in the disagreement cr
contest between the factory and the
plough we may take it, I think, that the
factory worker is not going to pay more
for his food, if he can possibly help it,
in order that the man behind the plough
may derive a greater profit. The two
things have to be equitably balanced.
The man behind the plough, I imagine,
will stop walking behind that plough
if he cannot make anything out of it,
but all the same there is that gulf to
bridge. You must consider the interests
of the consumer.
on
The noble Viscount, speaking
Tuesday, I think, referred to the case
of coal. He spoke, may I say, of interference with the ordinary methods of
coal mine owners. I do not think the noble
Viscount succeeded in showing that the
arrangements made for restricting the
output in the coal mines had the effect
of reducing the price of coal to the cousumer. It cannot be argued, for
moment, that that has been the result.
On the contrary, it seems to me that
during the last thirty years the price of
coal in Northern Ireland has about
doubled. We do not want the prices
doubled to the consumer.
a
Now, that brings me to a more direct
question I have to put, and I shall not
be surprised if the House receives it with
something approaching
approaching laughter. I
want to be assured definitely that the
Government is satisfied that this Bill is
intended to include live stock. "Live
stock" is not included in the definition
given in the Bill. The definition "agricultural product," which appears in the interpretation Clause-Clause 21, I thinkis the same as that in the United
Kingdom Act of 1931, and it may be
argued with some force that it does not
specifically include live stock. It may be
that while in England they have in some
of the other Acts operating in that
country something that will safeguard
them, we may be left without that safeguard in Northern Ireland. I am quite
sure the Bill is meant to include live
stock. Anybody turning to the second
Sub-Clause of Clause 9 will see a reference to live stock. In the Bill the
powers proposed to be taken are strong
enough to include such things as bread,
beer and whisky, as well as oats and
flax.
But we ought to consider whether we
have caught not only live stock, but
butter and eggs and honey and so on.
If there is any doubt whatever about it,
the Government ought to take steps to
set that at rest. The importance of that
particular matter seems to me to lie in
the fact that if you have power to include this and that in a scheme, and do
not include either this or that, no harm
is done, but if you include something
in the scheme which you have not power
to include, then the scheme is waste
paper and trouble will ensue. There is,
perhaps, a method provided in the Bill
that gets round a difficulty of that sort and
to which it will be my unpleasant duty
to direct the attention of the House,
when and if the Bill gets into Committee.
I will not talk about that this afternoon.
It does not appear that the agriculturist of Northern Ireland is to get
any financial assistance in working this
Bill. It seems to be that the English
agriculturist is rather more liberally
treated. All the expenses are to be met
by ourselves, and there is a provision in
the United Kingdom Bill that certain
expenditure which can be ascertained as
attributable to Northern Ireland is to be
met by a deduction from the Northern
Ireland residuary share of taxation. We
are not given much. We are required
to risk a great deal, but in saying that
the House must acquit me of any desire
to deceive them as regards the matter of
guaranteed loans for which there is provision in the Bill. On the whole, if the
House accepts this Bill, and if the Bill
becomes law, we shall be requiring our
farmers in so far as they are producers
of whatever may be a regulated product
to come in compulsorily into a scheme
of co-operation which they are to carry
on at their own expense. They are to
bear the risk of the losses, and what
Agricultural Marketing Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie
assurance they are to have for increased
profits I do not know, nor I am afraid
can anybody tell me.
There will be certain details arising at
a later stage, and I shall not take up
time to-day with them. There will be
questions about prosecutions and offences,
and the comparatively usual provision
that a prosecution is not to be undertaken without the consent of the Minister
of Agriculture. That is a proposition
which has its advantages, but I am not
quite sure that I can see how you can
apply that to the special case of proceedings on indictment. I do not think that
that perhaps could be allowed to stand.
Et seems to me you cannot allow the
Minister of Agriculture or any other
Minister to interfere with the discretion
of the Attorney-General as to preferring
an indictment. We require the same protection here as we put in one of the other
Agricultural Acts of bringing a prosecution for possible misdemeanour on the part
of some official where the sanction of the
Ministry should not be required before
the prosecution. We should be careful
to see that our board is properly described.
There is one case in which money is
involved where it seems to me it is
assumed that marketing board and committee are synonymous terms.
That we
must look into. It is merely detail.
Then we
have to consider smaller
things, particularly, perhaps, that proposal which I referred to at an earlier
moment in my speech-the proposal to
allow a modification of the Bill to be made
by Order in Council. It is not a very
desirable method of procedure. This
House has sanctioned it more than once
in connection with unemployment insurance and matters of that kind. It is
not a sound thing to do. I do not know
whether the House will consider that on
the present occasion. With the Bill
actually before Parliament across the
water there will be no occasion to object.
I am bound to admit that in this particular case the proposal is a matter of
practical convenience, and I am pretty
certain we may rely on the Cabinet, who
will advise his Grace as regards the Order
in Council. This Government will be
responsible for that Order in Council and
I think we may rely on the Government
to see that power, if given, is not abused.
I come to the final point to which I
wish to draw attention, and that is how
the Bill is going to affect things produced outside our area, but brought within it. The House will find a reference
at the bottom of page 21, also, curiously
enough, in Clause 21 there is the term,
"regulated product." It is not to include any product in so far as it is produced outside the area to which the
scheme is applicable. I presume that the
Government are satisfied that there is no
probability of any loophole through which
a product in entering Northern Ireland
could be disposed of in complete contravention, not only of the definite provision
but of the purposes of the Act. That is
a matter to which the Government should
direct attention. Finally, I shall certainly not oppose the Second Reading. I
shall, on the contrary, support it in the
hope that it may effect some, at least, of
the good which I know it is the desire of
its authors should be effected. One can
only hope that the system will be found.
not too complicated and not too expensive
in working, and that it may receive the
support and confidence of our agricultural community.
Mr. McCORKELL: I do not intend to
oppose this Bill either. Like the last
speaker, it is my intention to support it,
but I am sorry I cannot agree with him
that we are all in a better position this
afternoon to understand the Bill. As
far as I am concerned the Bill only came
into my hands this afternoon. I understand that on last Tuesday, when the
House was sitting, the Members were
presented with copies of the Bill, but,
unfortunately, I was not here. If
Senators remained at home waiting for
the post, and if the authorities waited
for Senators to come here, it seems to me
we should be like the two armies in the
low countries, which never met once and
never got anywhere. I do not, for a
moment, assert that the Government will
say this is of no importance. Assuming
it is of some importance I think, with
all respect, that Members of this House
should be given a lengthened period in
which to study the Bill and make themselves cognisant of its details. It is very
difficult to get through a thing of this
sort in a short space of time. Personally
I find my intelligence, if any, at fault
in trying to understand the provisions
of this Bill. I hope, in the future, that
we may be given an opportunity of
having these Bills longer in our possession.
Sometimes the less one knows about a
thing the better and, therefore, I can
only express the pious hope that the Bill
253 Agricultural Marketing Bill-

1 JUNE 1933
Second Reading
may do something. I was sorry to hear
the hon. Senator (Mr. Leslie) make
reference to the fact that the factory
worker will not pay more for food in
order that the man behind the plough
may get more for his product. I think
it will be a pity if such a thing got
abroad. If there is any real harmony
and good-will between workers I do not
think that the town dweller, who is
drawing good wages would grudge paying more in order that his brother in the
country may have a living wage and not
a pittance. I hope the Bill will succeed
in doing something effective.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I, like my
hon. Friend (Mr. Leslie) intend to support the Bill, because, in my opinion, it
gives the only chance that agriculture
has of getting on to a sound basis. We
are now, by this Bill going right off the
policy of laissez faire-let go as you likeand adopting a policy where we can coordinate and endeavour to make the
thing a success. I should like, for a
moment, to go into the genesis of this
Bill. There were a good many people
who thought when we got committed to
a policy of tariffs it would remove all the
evils to which agriculture is subject.
That has proved a poor weapon for
various reasons, which I will attempt to
describe to the House. A tariff cannot
really compete against an article that
comes under a bounty. We have an
example of the 40 per cent. tariff on
cattle from the Free State. It will
interest the House to know that the
numbers of cattle from the Free State
coming in under that heavy duty have
nearly doubled themselves in the course
of a year. That tariff has no power to
control an import which is fed by bounty.
It cannot compete either against stocks
overcharging the market, because they
keep coming in fed by other meansbounties, etc. A tariff cannot compete
where the exchange varies rapidly. That
goes against it. What is most important
of all is that a tariff cannot control
quantities. It cannot stabilise quantities,
and therefore cannot stabilise prices.
What we want is a stabilised price. The
farmer wants to know from year to
year, what he is likely to get.
much more hope in this Measure than I
have ever seen in any other agricultural
Measure introduced into any House.
When the Government discovered that
tariffs could not do all that is necessary
they threw
threw their mind in another
I see
direction and came to the conclusion that
control and co-ordination of the industry
would probably have more effect than a
tariff itself, hence this Bill, and hence
the reason we have for all the Clauses in
this Bill. The schemes, of course, have
got to be formed, and that will require
a great deal of care.
Various Commissions have been set up
on the other side of the water to deal
with the different articles of agricultural
produce. A very interesting one is the
Pig Commission under the chairmanship
of Lord Linlithgow. It has been in
action for some time, and it may interest
Members to know that this Commission
by their action in controlling imports
and prices has raised the wholesale price
of bacon in England from 75s. in last
December to 100s. at the present time.
That looks a very good ideal of what a
Commission should set itself to do. That
is an increase of 25 per cent., and we
might hope for even better things than
that.
I do honestly believe that this is a
bold endeavour to try to put some life
into the unfortunate agriculturists of
this country. It will depend on the
farmer himself whether the thing will be
a success, and I believe you will be able
to get the farmers in Northern Ireland
to see that this is the only way in which
they can be benefited. Voluntary work
and individualistic work is no good. I
give the Bill my blessing and hope it
will be a success.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I do not wish
to detain the House further than to say,
as a representative of the agricultural
community, that we welcome this
Bill. I think every Member of the
House will give the Measure his support.
The hon. and learned Gentleman (Mr.
Leslie) opposite has gone into all the details on which I intended to make some
remarks, but I do not wish to repeat them.
The only thing I desire to point out is
that this is mandatory. If we intend to
keep our markets with Great Britain we
are bound to accept this Bill. In my
opinion we are under a great debt of
gratitude to the Government, and
especially to the Ministry of Agriculture,
for the interest they have taken in
following the Bill through the Imperial
Parliament, and drafting a Measure to
suit the conditions prevailing in Northern Ireland. I beg to give my support
to the Bill.
255 Agricultural Marketing Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading.
Major DOBBS: We have had various
efforts to improve our cattle, our pigs,
our butter, our eggs, and our apples,
but this, to my mind, is a Bill of far
greater importance. It is one not to
improve our agriculture but to make it
pay. On the face of it it is a lawyer's
Bill, and not of very much interest to
some of us, but in that it enables the
Pork Bill and the Butter Bill to be
brought forward, it is of paramount
interest to many of us. Twenty years
ago such a Bill with its financial powers,
registers, boards, and in the background
its quotas and committees arbitrarily
fixing prices would, I think, have been
stillborn, but to-day we welcome it as
the only means of helping our agricultural population. Twenty years ago
supply and demand were the only
legitimate factors governing the price of
anything. To-day that is changed, and
I think rightly so.
The Leader of the House has referred
to what has been done in the coal trade
by federation. It has stopped cutthroat competition, and has raised and
stabilised prices. I would like to call
attention to what has been done in the
tin-mining industry. Out of chaos, and
from what was a state practically verging
on bankruptcy, it is now emerging, and
I am sure that in a few months' time
we shall have the price of tin stabilised.
But it will be stabilised at a price fair
to both producers and consumers. That
has not been done by the law of supply
and demand but by quotas, restrictions,
percentages, and international agreements. I look upon this Bill as a statesmanlike effort to save agriculture. It is
not going to give the farmers something.
for nothing. They do not ask for that.
It will ensure, provided they work hard,
that their labours will be rewarded, and,
I trust, adequately rewarded. I have
great pleasure in wholeheartedly supporting this Bill.
this
Mr. QUIGLEY: I cannot speak as a
farmer, but I have gone through this Bill
and I have also seen that the organised
farming community are quite in favour
of it. They believe that it will meet the
requirements of
of agriculture in
country, and that by it the farmers will
have protection for their produce. It will
mean that the farmers will get a decent
price, and I think that is a very
important matter. I have very great
pleasure in supporting the Second Reading of the Bill.
Mr. DORMAN: I am not competent
as an authority upon agriculture to speak
on the various provisions of this Bill,
but I desire to congratulate the Government on introducing a Measure which is
an acknowledgment on their part of the
failure of the old goddess competition.
We were told until very recently that
competition was the life of trade, and to
introduce any philosophy calculated to retard the intense competition of the individualist in supplying the needs of the
community was an extremely foolish
proceeding; but Bill after Bill is now
being introduced by the Government here
and in Great Britain each of which is an
acknowledgment of the failure of the
present system of society to satisfy the
needs of the community. This Measure
is especially one of that character.
A state of chaos in agricultural marketing has actually existed during my
lifetime. Some fifty years ago it fell
to my lot to be responsible for the receipt
of agricultural produce in the shape of
barley probably to a larger extent than
under any other single roof in the world.
I had spent the previous part of my life
at sea, and I was struck at that time
with the foolishness of the producers of
the product for the receipt of which I
was responsible in glutting the market
when the malting season opened.
moment the period of the year arrived
when there was a reduction of the temperature which made it possible to enter
into the process of preparing malt for the
purpose of brewing and distilling, I have
known the producers of barley inside
three weeks by glutting the market to
reduce the price of their product by 2s
per barrel.
The
That happened when there was a very
intense agitation going on in the country
with regard to the rack rents to which
the agriculturists were subjected, and I
was surprised as a young man to discover
that the agriculturists by lack of coordination and the relating of the supply
to meet the demands of the market were
actually under-cutting themselves to the
extent of from £1 12s to £2 8s per acre
of the ground set apart for the purpose of
producing the barley. In most cases the
tenant farmers who were producing the
crop were themselves not subjected to a
rent of that amount for that portion of
the farms which they had set apart for
the production of barley, as the land on
which barley is grown is not of the richest
quality.
257 Agricultural Marketing Bill-

1 JUNE 1933
Second Reading
I have seen many instances of farmers
endeavouring to co-operate for the purpose of supplying the needs of the market,
but their organisation was not backed up
by the force of law. There was no legal
penalty attached to the violation of the
voluntary contract they entered into with
one another for the specific purpose
organising a better market for their product. In the absence of any penalty
under the law the farmers were at a loose
end, and there was instance after instance
where when a few pence was added to the
price they went outside the organisation
they had voluntarily entered into.
It may interest the Mayor of Derry to
know that forty years ago the agricultural producers in the area of Innishowen
and in a circle around Londonderry found
they were handicapped by reason of the
cross-Channel freights on their eggs, and
fowl, and other agricultural produce. A
large number of the farmers invested in
an old paddle-steamer, owned by the
London North-Western Railway Company, for the purpose of trying to reduce
the freight charges. I found that one
farmer invested £200 in that steamer.
Then the steamship company in competition reduced their freight upon
packet of eggs to a figure 6d below what
they had been paying
on their own
steamer. This farmer actually sent his
eggs by the rival boat in order to obtain
the benefit of this 6d. After a few years
the Stanley was taken from the water,
and he lost his capital.
a
I wish now to refer to the question
of bacon. Those of us who travel the
country, taking an interest in the common life of the people, know that
numerous demands have been made to
co-ordinate the supply so that it may
reach the market as soon as possible.
With regard to what the hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Leslie) has said in reference to the protection of the consumer, I do not think that this scheme
when it is established will tend to the
protection of the consumer in the home
market. I think it will have rather an
opposite effect. If we proceed to export
in
agricultural produce larger
quantities than we do now, the probability is that unless the Government and
the Ministry are very careful the consumers in the home market will suffer
more than they would otherwise suffer.
our
In illustration of that, let me say that
now we can buy in Belfast Australian
butter at 9d and 10d per lb. I know
that those who are producing the butter
fat in the Australian Colonies, out of
which this butter is made, are really
paying in their centres of population
from 1s 1d to 1s 4d per lb. The reason
for that is that they are exporting their
butter in large quantities in bulk and in
refrigerating ships, and the home consumer there to-day has to pay thirty to
forty per cent. more for the product of
Australia than we are paying for it in
Belfast.
Before closing I would like to ask the
Leader of the House a question with
reference to Clause 14. Clause 14, subclause (3) reads:
Any person who removes or attempts
to remove any agricultural product
from Northern Ireland into Great
Britain in contravention of the provisions of any regulation made under
paragraph (b) of sub-section (1) of
this section shall be liable on conviction
to imprisonment for a term not exceeding three months, or to a fine not
exceeding one hundred pounds, or to
both such imprisonment and fine.
It seems to me that that gives to the
boards administering these schemes a
monopoly in the exportation of the agricultural produce of Northern Ireland
and deprives individual farmers from
entering into competition with
with the
farmers of England in the English
markets. I have much pleasure in
supporting the Bill, because I believe
that ultimately it will result in the
Ministry further endeavouring to
ordinate and plan production so that the
farmers of Northern Ireland, after they
get the greatest possible proportion of
production from the soil, may know
what hope there is of a market for their
produce outside their own area.
CO-
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am glad
to hear from the debate that this Bill
has been received with approbation and
hope. I would like to answer one or two
questions that have been raised by the
hon. and learned Senator (Mr. Leslie)
who spoke first. He asked what would
have happened if the Northern Government had not introduced this Bill. The
answer is that we would have been at
the mercy of any scheme which might
have been introduced into England, and
which might not fit in with our conditions here, and also that under the Bill
we will have a chance to control imports
E
Moneylenders Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
into Northern Ireland, which otherwise
we might not have had.
He asked again the reason why the
schemes under this Act would come into
force prior to formal approval being
given by Parliament.
The reason for
that is that we must of necessity keep
in step with Great Britain, and the
proviso to Clause 1 affords an instance
of why Parliamentary approval comes
second in this instance.
I
Another point he raised was with regard to the definition of live stock.
am advised that the definition in the Act
does include live stock, except poultry.
Mr. LESLIE: Except poultry?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Except
poultry. With regard to the question
asked by Senator Dorman, I would be
obliged if he would ask it again at a
later stage of the Bill, as I am not quite
certain of the answer.

now read a Second time") agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause
1, page 2, line 38, to leave out "authorising him to do so" ("a proper moneylender's excise licence authorising him to
do so").
The words which I propose to omit

regard to the word "proper" which comes
just before. In fact, these words get
rather in the way of subsequent Clauses,

Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept this Amendment.

CLAUSE 2 (Certificate required for
grant of moneylender's excise licence).
(1) A moneylender's excise licence
shall not be granted except to a person
who holds a certificate granted in
accordance with the provisions of this
section, authorising the grant of the
licence to that person, and a separate
certificate shall be required in respect
of each separate licence. Any moneylender's excise licence granted in contravention of this section shall be void.
Moneylenders Bill-
Committee
1 JUNE 1933
(2) Certificates under this section (in
this Act referred to as "certificates")
shall be granted by a court of summary jurisdiction, constituted of a
resident magistrate sitting alone
with other resident magistrates, and
having jurisdiction in the district in
which the moneylender's business is to
be carried on.
*

or

(5) The Ministry of Home Affairs
shall make regulations with respect to

regulations may prescribe-
(a) the notices to be given of
intention to make an application;
(b) the persons entitled to be
represented at the hearing of the
application; and
(c) the form of certificate to be

The following Amendment stood on
the Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie :
Clause 2, page 3, leave out line 23
("authorising the grant of the licence to
that person').
Mr. LESLIE: I should tell the Committee that since I put this Amendment
on the Paper I have been shown a copy
of a form of certificate which is in use in
England and which it is proposed to use
here, and, in view of the terms of that
certificate, I think it would be desirable
not to move this Amendment. The
words had better stand. I do not move.
Amendment not moved.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:
Clause 2, page 3, lines 31 and 32,
leave out from ("magistrates") ("with
other resident magistrates') to the end
of line 33, and insert:—“ Provided that
a certificate to carry on business as
moneylender in a county of a city shall
be granted only by a court having jurisdiction in that county of a city where the
certificate is to have effect."
a
Mr. LESLIE: Here, again, inquiries
show that the balance of convenience
would not lie in inserting the words I
have on the Paper. Accordingly I move,
instead of the words as printed, the following words, of which I think you have
a copy, Mr. Chairman. I propose in line
32 to leave out the four words, "having
jurisdiction in the" ("and having jurisdiction in the district in which") and to
insert instead thereof the words, "being
a court having jurisdiction in the petty
sessions." That will confine and define
the jurisdiction, and is on the whole, 1
would submit to the Committee, the most
convenient means of dealing with a case
which is of some little difficulty. I shall
be moving later on two other Amendments dealing with the circulation of endorsations on certificates which really will
cover the ground which my original
Amendment was intended primarily to
cover. As it is, what I move is to leave
out the words "having jurisdiction in
the," and to insert, "being a court having
jurisdiction in the petty sessions." I beg
to move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
2, page 4, lines 24 and 25, to leave out
paragraph (b).
This question is rather more difficult.
The Committee will recollect that I mentioned it on the Second Reading. I am
proposing to leave out paragraph (b).
The effect of that omission, if the Committee agree to it, would be that the
Ministry of Home Affairs would not have
power to make regulations prescribing the
persons entitled to be represented at the
hearing of the application-that is the
application for a certificate authorising a
moneylender to carry on business at suchand-such an address and under such-andsuch a name. I do not in the least
object, and I am sure the Committee
would not object to the Ministry of Home
Affairs making any regulations they
might think fit to provide for the admission of persons to appear before the court
dealing with this application or with any
other matter. If, therefore, the intention of the paragraph is merely to
allow the Minister of Home Affairs to
prescribe for the appearance, let us say,
of the police at such a hearing, I should
not in the least object. It is, however,
the other side at which I am looking,
that is the question of excluding some
person who might wish to come and be
Moneylenders Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
heard before the court. The exact wording, as the Committee will see, is "entitled to be represented," not "entitled
to be heard." It seems to me that the
question of whether this or that person
has any locus standi is a matter entirely
for the court and not for the Ministry of
Home Affairs. I have no objection to
inclusion, but I do object to exclusion.
Therefore, I suggest the complete omission of these words in order that, if necessary, other words may be suggested, or
that we may receive some explanation
which it is possible to accept.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I regret
that I cannot accept this Amendment.
The provisions governing the granting of
certificates by courts of petty sessions are
among the most important in the Bill
and, in the opinion of the Government, it
is essential that a court in considering an
application for a certificate should have
before it all the relevant information
necessary to enable it to come to a proper
decision. It is not intended that this
provision should be used to effect the exclusion of any person who ought properly
to be heard on the application.
Mr. LESLIE: Thank you. I think
the Committee will be satisfied with that
and permit me to withdraw the Amendment as this provision is not to be used
for exclusion purposes.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Suspension and Forfeiture
of Moneylenders Certificates).
(1) Where any person, being the
holder of a certificate, is convicted of
any offence under this Act or under
section two or section four of the
Betting and Loans (Infants) Act,
1892, or the Moneylenders Act, 1900,
the court-
(a) may order that any certificates
held by that person, and, in the
case of a partner in a firm, by any
other partner in the firm, shall
either be suspended for such time.
as the court thinks fit, or shall be
forfeited, and may also if the court
thinks fit, declare any such person,
or any person responsible for the
management of the moneylending
business carried on by the person
convicted, to be disqualified for
obtaining a certificate for such time
as the court thinks fit; and
(b) shall cause particulars of the
conviction and of any order made
by the court under this sub-section
to be endorsed on every certificate
held by the person convicted or by
any other person affected by the
order, and shall
copies of
those particulars to be sent to the
authority by whom any certificate
so endorsed was granted, and to the
Ministry:
cause
Provided that, where by order of a
court a certificate held by any person
is suspended or forfeited, or any person is disqualified for obtaining a
certificate, he may, whether or not he
is the person convicted, appeal against
the order in the same manner as any
person convicted may appeal against
his conviction, and the court may, if
it thinks fit, pending the appeal, defer
the operation of the order.
(2) Any certificate required by a
court for endorsement in accordance
with the foregoing provisions of this
section shall be produced, in such
manner and within such time as may
be directed by the court, by the person by whom it is held, and any person who, without reasonable cause,
makes default in producing any certificate so required shall, in respect of
each offence, be liable on summary
conviction to a penalty not exceeding
five pounds for each day during which
the default continues.

Clause 3, page 6, to leave out lines 2
and 3 ("authority by whom any certificate so endorsed was granted, and to the
Ministry:”), and to insert instead thereof "Ministry and to the Ministry of
Home Affairs."
This deals with the question of
acquainting other courts of the conviction of some holder of a certificate of any offence under this Act.
I take it that this Act means not
only the Bill before us but also the Act
of 1900-so much of it as is left-because this Act is to be construed with
the other Act. I presume I am correct
in assuming that an offence under the
Act of 1900 is also included in this provision. Oh, yes.
It says so expressly.
Moneylenders Bill-
Committee
1 JUNE 1933
I did not see that. In any case of an
offence under this Act, or under the
Betting and Loans (Infants) Act, or the
Act of 1900 the certificate may be endorsed. It seems to me to be of the
greatest importance that the endorsation
of the certificate should be communicated to every person and every court
likely to be concerned with the granting
of a further certificate. That can be
done conveniently by sending the particulars of the endorsation not to the
authority which grants the certificate,
that is the petty sessional court, but to
the Ministry-that is the Ministry of
Finance-which issues the licence and
also to the Ministry of Home Affairs.
When the Ministry of Home Affairs get
it they will, I presume, have established
a small register and will be able to
acquaint all the resident magistrates of
the misdemeanour and of the endorsation
of the certificate, whether by way of
suspension or by way of cancellation. I
think the Committee will agree that it is
very desirable that the suspension or
cancellation of a certificate should be
published as widely as may be necessary
in order that it may become effective.
That would prevent a man, let us say,
authorised to perform his operations in
Downpatrick and misbehaving himself
in that district, from going to Coleraine
and getting a certificate from the resident.
magistrate in Coleraine, who, but for
this intimation, would have no knowledge of the Downpatrick proceedings.
Accordingly, in the first instance, I beg
to move to leave out lines 2 and 3 on
page 6, with the intention of following
it up by inserting "Ministry and to the
Ministry of Home Affairs." I beg to
move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.

penalty not exceeding."
Mr. LESLIE: I do not propose to
move that. We can do without it.
Amendment not moved.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

CLAUSE 6 (Form of moneylenders'
contracts).
(1) No contract for the repayment
by a borrower of money lent to him
or to any agent on his behalf by a
Moneylenders Bill-
Committee
THE SENATY
moneylender after the commencement
of this Act or for the payment by him
of interest on any money so lent, and
no security given by the borrower or
by any such agent in respect of a contract for the repayment of money so
lent or interest thereon, shall be
enforceable-
(a) unless a note or memorandum
in writing of the contract be made
and signed by the borrower and,
where the borrower is a married
woman totally dependent on her
husband, signed also by her husband,
and a copy of the note or memorandum be delivered or sent to the
borrower within seven days of the
making of the contract; or
(b) if it is proved that the note
or memorandum was not signed by
the borrower and, where the borrower
is a married woman totally dependent on her husband, signed also by
her husband, before the money was
lent or the security was given, as the
case may be.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 6, page 9, line 21, to leave out
from "a" to the end of line 22, and
insert, "woman under coverture."
"6
I do not know what the Committee will
say about this Amendment. It will be
remembered that on the Second Reading
I pointed out that the expression in relation to a married woman of " totally
dependent on her husband," raised a perhaps difficult question of the fact in
every individual case, and if that should
happen to be the general opinion of the
Committee it would be necessary to have
an alternative phrase. The only phrase
I can think of that satisfied myself is the
somewhat old-fashioned phrase, woman
under coverture." There is a good deal
to be said with regard to this question on
both sides. The Committee, as a whole,
will probably desire to be fair to the
moneylender, but they will be equally
anxious to protect the husband, the unknowing husband, of the married woman
from the consequences of her folly, and
really what the Committee have to do is
to try to balance the one against the
other and to arrive at a conclusion as to
whether it is wise to insert such a phrase
as "totally dependent" or not.
I have not myself that intimate
acquaintance with the moneylender which
perhaps is possessed by other Members of
the Committee who seem to have found
them unworthy of Christian recognition,
but I cannot help thinking it may be that
the moneylender will be very fairly able
to take care of himself as regards that.
The moneylender is not very likely, I
should imagine, to lend money to a
married woman without her husband's
confirmatory signature, unless he is very
sure of his ground. The question of
total dependence will be ultimately decided by the court. It may be the Committee will say that the words ought to
stand, and that they are better than the
words I am going to suggest as an alternative. In the meantime, in order that
the question may be considered and discussed, I beg to move, to leave out from
"a" to the end of line 22, that is to
say, "married woman totally dependent
on her husband," with a view to a subsequent insertion.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I regret
that I cannot accept this Amendment.
It is not the object of this provision to
interfere with transactions between
moneylenders and married women who
have private means and are not dependent
on their husbands for their livelihood. It
is further considered that the expression
proposed to be inserted is wider than is
necessary for the purpose of giving proper
effect to the objects of the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not wish to pursue
the subject.
It is very doubtful.
The CHAIRMAN: Amendment withdrawn?
Mr. LESLIE: Please.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

Marketing of Dairy Produce Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: One of the
principal problems in connection with the
creamery industry in Northern Ireland
has been that of enabling the creameries
to keep going during the winter
months. Winter dairying has proved an
uneconomic proposition in the Six
Counties and, consequently, the supply of
milk during the winter months is
inadequate to enable our creameries to
keep working. In order to provide
employment for creamery staffs during
the winter, premises
premises registered
creameries under the Marketing of Dairy
Produce Act of 1929 were specially
authorised to undertake the process of
purchasing imported bulk butter and
cutting this butter into rolls without
liability to be registered as a butter
factory.
as
It has been found, however, that the
market for imported butter cut into rolls
has been undermined by the competition
of blended roll butter. By the use of a
blend it is possible to obtain a butter
which spreads easily in cold weather and
which is more palatable than straightcut imported butter. As a consequence,
there has been a distinct tendency for
creameries in Northern Ireland to sacrifice their position as creameries and to
register as butter factories. In this way
they have obtained the right to blend
butter of any description and have placed
themselves outside a large part of the
Act of 1929.
It is now proposed to give the Ministry
power to authorise creameries to undertake the process of blending butter. As
will be seen from lines 8 and 9 of Clause
1, this authorisation will be "subject to
such conditions as may be prescribed."
273 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

1 JUNE 1933
Second Reading
The conditions which will be prescribed
will be a matter for regulations, but I
may indicate that it is intended to make
it conditional upon an authorisation to
blend being granted that the quality of
the butter which is used for blending will
be up to a high standard, which will be
defined by the Ministry.
In the case of imported butters which
are subject to a system of Government
inspection in the countries from which
they come, the Ministry will recognise
certain of these Government grades as
being up to the quality set out in the
conditions.
In the case of other butters
for which a system of export inspection
does not exist it will be necessary for the
creameries to make special arrangements
for satisfying the Ministry that the butter
is of a quality which complies with the
prescribed conditions.
I should like to emphasise that no possible question of mis-description in regard
to blended butter sold by our creameries
can arise. The question of the marking
of imported butter is fully covered by the
Merchandise Marks Act, 1926. Under
the terms of that Order all imported
butter must bear an indication of origin
when sold in the United Kingdom. The
various forms of indication of origin.
which have to be applied in the case of
blended butter are fully set out in the
Order. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: This little Bill has been
subjected to very great but undeserved
abuse outside, chiefly, I am afraid, on the
part of those people who have not taken
the trouble to read and compare it with
the Act of 1929. I think this Bill is
likely to be of some use, and cannot do
any harm. The governing condition of
the whole thing is " subject to such conditions as may be prescribed." If the
Ministry keep the conditions as they
should be, then there will be no difficulty
whatever. I support the Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
Unemployment Insurance (Temporary
Provisions) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LONG: This Bill is designed to
extend for a further period certain enactments with regard to Unemployment
Insurance which would otherwise come to
an end on the 30th June, 1933. A
Measure of reform of the Unemployment
Insurance Scheme is in contemplation
following the Report of the Royal Commission which recently held an inquiry
in Great Britain. The continuation of
the statutory provisions referred to in the
Bill will preserve the existing position
until it is possible to consider the proposals for reform.
The enactments to be continued are set
out in Clause 1 of the Bill. These temporary Measures effected a considerable
number of Amendments of the Unemployment Insurance Statutes, some of which
are of a very minor character, and it will
perhaps be sufficient for the purposes of
the House if a few of the more important
matters are indicated.
The "genuinely seeking work" condition for benefit, which gave rise to such
difficulties in administration, was temporarily abolished, as was also the condition that applicants for transitional
benefit should prove that they had a
reasonable period of employment in the
last two years
The classes of persons in respect of
whom dependents' benefit is paid were
extended, and were made to include,
amongst others, the claimant's father or
stepfather, and his younger brothers and
sisters.
The rate of dependents' benefit for
adults was increased from 7/- to 9/- per
week. Under the terms of a subsequent
Order this amount now stands at 8/- a
week, but if this Bill is not carried into
law the rate of benefit will be reduced
to 7/- again as from the 1st July.
Provision was made for the purpose of
removing anomalies in the operation of
the unemployment machinery. It dealt
with such cases as seasonal workers, persons employed for less than a full week,
and women ceasing work on marriage.
In assessing the needs of applicants
for transitional payments, public assistance committees were required to have
regard to certain considerations. The
persons affected are those in receipt of
disability pensions, workmen's compensation or health insurance benefit, or who
had small investments. The Transitional
Payments Act secured to such claimants
a certain minimum standard of treatment. It is in the interests of the
claimants that its provisions should not
be allowed to lapse.
275 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Long]
The enactments which I have mentioned will be continued in force under
the terms of the Bill for a period of not
more than one year.
It is possible that, pending the introduction of legislation in Great Britain to
Ideal with the recommendations of the
Royal Commission, the British authorities not only continue the Measures
may
which in Great Britain, as here, are of
a temporary nature, but may take the
opportunity of carrying out minor
amendments. If this is done, it will be
essential that similar steps should be
taken in Northern Ireland. It is accordingly proposed in the Bill that powers
should be given
given to his Grace the
Governor in Council to make such
amendments applicable in Northern Ireland, and to provide for the necessary
alterations of the law. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: So far as I am aware,
a Bill to deal with the first Clause of
this Bill is at the moment before the
Imperial Parliament, and therefore I do
not myself raise the least objection to
Clause 1. With regard to Clause 2, the
provision allowing his Grace the Governor
to legislate by Order in Council is, as I
mentioned a few minutes ago in another
connection, a practice we have allowed
on more than one occasion. I do not
like it, and I do not think any of us do,
but I am sure the Parliamentary Secretary will be able to assure us that there
is no intention of allowing such an
Order to operate except in regard to
literally very trivial amendments, such as
those that may be required to be made
from time to time. I think we ought
to be told, if not now, before we pass
from the Bill, something as to the
finance of the matter. I understand
from the White Paper that the net
extra cost to the Exchequer is likely to
be some £3,000 during the current
financial year, and I presume the
Minister of Finance has provided for
that £3,000 in what is called his Budget
Estimate.
But the whole question of the finance
of these schemes is one that cannot but
cause us great and increasing anxiety.
I believe I am correct in saying-and I
should like to be corrected if I am wrong,
either now or before we part with the
Bill-that at the moment we are not
receiving anything under the Unemployment Re-Insurance Agreement between
Great Britain and ourselves. I am sure
the circumstances of that agreement and
re-agreement are more or less within the
memory of the House. I think myself
that there was a limitation of one million
pounds as from the richer fund to the
poorer fund, but I think I am right in
assuming that on the present basis of
unemployment in Great Britain and Northern Ireland we are receiving nothing.
and cannot expect to receive anything
during the present financial year.
It is impossible, I think, to oppose the
Bill. We have to recognise it, and regard
a disagreeable necessity, and we
can only hope it will not involve any
serious expense.
it as
Mr. DORMAN: I join in expressing
regret at the tendency to increase legislation by Order in Council. The distinguishing feature of the great revolution which we will be celebrating next
month was that it restrained the then
King, and was intended to restrain
future kings, from continuing to legislate
by Order in Council. I think that in a
Parliament in this particular area, where
we so sincerely commemorate that particular historic epoch, we should hesitate
before we extend the right to legislate by
Order in Council. Forty years ago there
was introduced in the British House of
Commons a Bill which would have
settled all this unemployment question.
It was entitled, The Right to Work or
Maintenance Bill." Those who were
then behind the introduction of that Bill
said that if society, by its laws, precluded a large proportion of the community from the right of access to the
means of life, and if society excluded
individual citizens, who had no property
in their own right, society was under a
moral obligation to supply to those
citizens the right to work or maintenance.
No solution short of that will ultimately
settle this great question of unemployment.
""
Mr. LONG: Some of the points raised
by the hon. and learned Senator (Mr.
Leslie) I will deal with at a future date.
I may say that the amendments which
are expected to be made by the Imperial
Parliament are anticipated to be of a
very minor character, and they will only
relate to the three Bills that are
mentioned. I quite appreciate the point
made by Senator Leslie and Senator
277 Loans Guarantee Bill-

1 JUNE 1933
Second Reading
Dorman as to the undesirability of legislation by Order in Council, but it is
exceedingly desirable, even essential, that
we should keep in line with Great
Britain in these matters. If this House
continued to sit until these Amendments
were known no doubt this procedure
would not be necessary, but hon. Members may rest assured that they will be
merely minor Amendments. Senator
Leslie is correct in his statement as to the
financial provisions. They are set out in
the White Paper, and I understand the
Minister of Finance is fully prepared to
deal with that matter.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
Loans Guarantee (No. 2) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LONG: I think that hon. Members will have little hesitation in
according a Second Reading to this short
Bill. Its object is to enable the Ministry
of Finance, in cases where loans have
been guaranteed, to facilitate borrowers
in effecting, by conversion of securities, a
reduction in the rate of interest payable,
and thus to secure a reduction of the
Exchequer's contingent liability. I beg
to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I really ought to
apologise for inflicting myself upon a
somewhat attenuated House, but, unfortunately, there is a certain difficulty
about this Bill. I am quite sure the
House will accept the Bill so far as it
goes, because it can only tend, I think,
to reducing the contingent liabilities of
our Exchequer. But it has been put up
to me that difficulties are experienced in
certain quarters as to the exact intention
and scope of the Measure, and if it
goes no further than its words suggest it
is suggested that it ought to be amended.
The difficulty experienced is this. It can
best be put by referring to paragraph
(a) of sub-clause (1):-
Where the authority or body by
whom a guaranteed loan has been
raised propose at any time while the
guarantee is in force-
(a) to redeem the guaranteed loan
in exercise of powers reserved to
them in that behalf by the deeds or
instruments relating to the guaranteed loan; and.
The point put up is, supposing that the
particular instrument relating to a
guaranteed loan is silent on this matter,
and supposing that the borrower can go
to his lender and say to him, "I borrowed £100,000 from you when money
was at 5 per cent. Now it is at 3 per
cent. If I can arrange with the Ministry of Finance to carry on the existing
guarantee will you meet me in that way?"
It seems a very natural and ordinary
business transaction, but the question
arises, if that
were followed,
whether under the wording of the Bill the
Ministry would have power to continue
the guarantee.
course
The
It seems to me that the Ministry is
completely protected by the proviso as
regards incurring, on behalf of the
country of course, any additional liability
in respect of either principal or interest,
but it does appear that there is some gap,
whether intentional or otherwise, in the
provision it is proposed to make.
original intention of these loans guarantees was, of course, to free capital for
the promotion of industry, and it might
be argued that a mere re-borrowing does
not free new capital, and therefore does
not particularly assist industry, but I do
not know that that would have any particular bearing on the present case.
I should therefore like to ask the Parliamentary Secretary whether I am to
take it that the position is that unless
the instrument relating to the loan specifically provides for redemption by agreement at an earlier date, it would be impracticable for the borrower, even if he
agreed with his lender to take advantage
of this Bill. If that is so the question
will arise whether an Amendment should
be introduced in the Bill. I suggest
that it would be competent for this House
to make such an Amendment, because it
would be neither appropriating revenue
or imposing additional taxation. It
could be done either by an Amendment
in this House or by introducing a Loans
Guarantee (No. 3) Bill. It is a thing
we have not done before. We have been
rather careful not to interfere with Loans
Guarantee Bills, perhaps because the
necessity has never arisen, but this is a
distinct difficulty which has been put up
to me, and therefore I must bring it before
the House.
Loans Guarantee Bill-
Sittings of the House
THE SENATE
Mr. LONG: I regard the point raised
by the hon. and learned Senator as one
of great importance. I may say, however, that the Ministry knows of no
guaranteed loan where the instrument
does not specifically indicate the period
of the loan. I have received representations in support of the views expressed
by the hon. and learned Senator, and
also in the opposite direction, and as the
position is one of some complexity, I
propose at a later date to ascertain the
views of the Minister on the matter, and
I will communicate them to the House.
Mr. LESLIE: Might I ask whether it
will assist the hon. Member if I put
something on the Paper for the next
stage-something
committal ?
more
or
less
non-
Mr. LONG: There will be no objection
to that.
Mr. DORMAN: The object of this
Bill is not to safeguard the interests of
our Exchequer, but it is that existing
borrowers may take advantage of the
lower rate of interest.
Mr. LONG: I might say, in addition
to what I have already stated, that
supposing a firm or a company built ships.
and the Government guaranteed a loan
for the purpose, and had to pay 5 per
cent. interest on the money, it is quite
conceivable that in the present state of
the money market they could borrow at
a rate of interest considerably less than
that with the Government guarantee
attached, and the new loan would, therefore be to the mutual advantage of the
borrower and the Government.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.

Tuesday next."—(Mr.

to sit on Wednesday as well. Tuesday
and Wednesday?
Mr. LONG: Probably. Yes.

Five minutes after Five
o'clock until Tuesday

Agricultural Marketing Bill 282
Oral Answers
6 JUNE 1933
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 6th June, 1933.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Question to Minister.
EXPERIMENTS ON CATTLE.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:
To ask the Leader of the House
whether he can give any information as
to the case of certain cattle alleged to
have been exposed to considerable
suffering while undergoing experiments
in the neighbourhood of Lisburn during
the month of July, 1932.
Mr. LESLIE: The question on the
Paper arises from a printed report which
has been widely circulated through the
Six Counties. I do not propose to read
the report, that seems to be unnecessary;
but if the report is untrue, or if it is
exaggerated, the sooner it is contradicted
or corrected the better. If the report is
a true report and a fair report it would
seem that an authoritative statement from
the Government, accompanied by any explanation that may be available, ought to
be given to the House. I beg to ask the
question standing in my name.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): It is presumed that the hon.
and learned Member's question refers to
the experiments which are being carried
out by the Ministry of Agriculture in the
neighbourhood of Hillsborough with Mr.
Spahlinger's anti-tubercular bovine vaccine. These experiments are under the
direct control of the veterinary research
officers of the Ministry of Agriculture, and
I can assure the hon. Member that none
of the animals has been exposed to unnecessary or undue suffering.
Agricultural Marketing Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill."
Mr. LESLIE: On that proposal there
are three things I want to say very
briefly. The first is that it is found that
the time between the Second Reading and
the Committee Stage in this House is in
practice unduly short. I am afraid the
shortness of time will appear apparent
during the afternoon. Arising out of
that, I want to take this opportunity of
saying that I am not moving the first two
Amendments on the Paper. I think I
ought to say that now lest other Members
might be hampered in the matter. Thirdly,
I want to ask the noble Viscount if he
will take an opportunity, at the appropriate Clause, or perhaps later on during
the passage of the Bill, of explaining to
the House a statement, or rather I should
say amplifying rather than explaining a
statement he made to the House at the
conclusion of the debate on the Second
Reading to the effect that by this Bill we
will have a chance to control imports into
Northern Ireland, which otherwise we
might not have had. That information
seems to be information it would be a
help to the House to have before it parts
with the Bill. I do not, of course,
oppose the Motion.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: 1 am not
quite sure whether I should give the explanation asked for by the hon. and
learned Senator now or at a later stage.
I can give it at any moment.
Mr. SPEAKER: I think it would be
better to give it later.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair].

283 Agricultural Marketing Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
Clause 1, page 2, line 34, after the
first "be" insert "amended or".
Clause 1, page 2, line 34, after the
second "be" insert "so amended or".
Amendments not moved.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
1, page 3, line 7, Sub-clause (7), to
leave out the word conclusive,'
and
insert instead thereof the word
"sufficient." It seems to me that if we allow
this word "conclusive" to stand there is
a very grave danger that if a controversy unhappily arose and we hope it
would not-but if a controversy did arise
I am afraid that it might be held that
the use of the word "conclusive" estopped
any person from setting up before the
Court the fact that the Minister's Order
was not within the powers conferred upon
him by any Act. The word "conclusive"
does appear in somewhat parallel passages
in a Statute of the Imperial Parliament,
but as regards the Act of 1931, and still
more I think as regards what is called in
the Bill the United Kingdom Act, the
Bill now before the Imperial Parliament,
the word is used in this connection as
applied to a scheme which has already
received the sanction of Parliament.
Here the proposal is that the use of this
word "conclusive" may be expected to be
interpreted to mean final as applied to a
scheme not yet approved by Parliament.
I do not say that the Courts would
necessarily accept that interpretation,
because so far as I know there is no
final decision on the subject, but there
is grave danger that the word "conclusive"
might be interpreted as precluding anybody from urging anything to the
contrary, and suggesting that there was
something ultra vires in the scheme or
in the Order confirming the scheme.
On the other hand, the word "sufficient" goes very nearly as far as the word
"conclusive," and, I think,
goes sufficiently far for our purpose. The message
which I suggest the word sufficient "
conveys to the Court is, "Here is evidence
before you which Parliament regards as
very good evidence, and evidence which
Parliament expects you to accept. But
if it should be that anybody has anything
to say to the contrary, the Court is not
precluded from hearing it and deciding it
according to law." The definition given
of the term "sufficient evidence" is that
it is enough evidence to go to a jury, but
the other side is not precluded from contradicting it by other evidence. We want
to use a term here which will be strong
enough to discourage frivolous objections,
but which will not be so strong as to
deprive any of His Majesty's subjects of
their right of access to the Courts, and
will not have the effect of making the
Minister, instead of the Court, the interpreter of the Act of Parliament, and the
arbiter as to what is and what is not
to be the law of the land. I could not
reconcile it to my constitutional notions to
allow such a phrase to pass. I know
there have been attempts made to give
Departments more and more power, and
to move more and more in the direction
of ousting the courts of law, and to that
policy I shall always maintain unwavering opposition. I move the omission of
the word "conclusive," and if that is
acceptable I shall follow it up by moving
the insertion of the word "sufficient."
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The word
"conclusive" is used in the corresponding
Section of the Agricultural Marketing
Act of 1931 in Sub-section 8 of Section 1.
The word "conclusive" is also used in
relation to Development schemes in Subsection 3 of Clause 4 of the Agricultural
Marketing Bill at present before Parliament at Westminster. It is admitted
that in neither case are the English
schemes final until they have been
approved by Parliament, but under
the provisions of the Northern Ireland
Bill Parliament can annul a scheme immediately it has been made, while the
Northern Ireland schemes are temporary
and will run out after two years unless
they are formally approved by resolution
of each House of Parliament. There are
numerous precedents for the making of
an Order by a Government Department
being treated as conclusive evidence of
the requirements of an Act having been
complied with. The earliest precedent
is the English Local Government Act of
1894, Section 9, Sub-section 7 (c). A
further precedent which may interest the
hon. and learned Senator is to be found
in the Economy Act of 1931. For these
reasons I am afraid I am unable to accept
the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I regret to say I am
entirely unconvinced. It seems to me to
be a matter of first-class consequence, and
I must take the opinion of the Committee,
Question put, "That the word 'conclusive' be left out."
The Committee divided: Contents, 5;
not Contents, 11.
