Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Sub-
Clause (3), line 42, to leave out "two
years" ("of at least two years') and insert "one year. This is really one of
the most difficult passages in the Bill.
The intention of the proposal in the text
is an intention with which I am sure we
are all in sympathy. We want these
schemes to be launched in the most favourable circumstances. We want to put the
best crew in charge of the ship and the
best skipper in charge of the crew. But
the whole Clause is a little difficult.
If we look back a little-I mention
this in case the Government may desire
to suggest some alteration on the Report
stage-we shall see there is an executive
committee of eleven, and the executive
committee of eleven was, if my memory
serves me correctly, introduced into the
Bill during its passage through the other
House. It was not in the orginal Bill,
The provision has been inserted that the
chairman, vice-chairman, and three members of the board appointed by the
Ministry are to be included in that executive committee. But we are not told
definitely what is to happen if the chairman selected by the Minister is one of
the three members. It seems to me the
position is left a little hazy. There is no
instruction to the Minister as to whether
he shall or shall not choose the chairman
from these three members.
I think that that, although apparently
a digression, has some bearing on the
question of the period of two years. This
period of two years is, undoubtedly, the
287 Agricultural Marketing Bill—

THE SENATE
Committee
[Mr. Leslie]
The
man
critical time and you want the best men
you can get in charge. What troubles
me about this is really that instead of
moving to omit "two years" here I would
very much rather have moved it in page
2 which we have passed, line 40 (Clause 1,
(5)). That is, I would rather that the
experimental period was increased from
two years to three. That makes the
whole difference. If we allow the two years
to stand the board may have an unfitting
chairman, whom they do not want, for
the entire currency of the scheme.
scheme may not last more than two years.
That seems to be the difficulty. The
Committee must weigh the two problems
one against the other. If the
appointed is a good man, at the end of his
term of office one may reasonably expect,
the board being a good board, he will be
re-elected. If he is a bad man; that is,
a man who does not get on with the board
or if he is a man, however excellent, that
the board cannot get on with, he may go a
long way to wreck the entire scheme.
have to consider, therefore, whether it is
wiser in order to get what we all wantthe best man in charge of the experimental period-to have so long a period
as two years. On that point other
Members of the Committee will have an
opinion. For the moment, I beg to move
to leave out "two years" and insert "one
year."
We
I
Colonel PAKENHAM: I must say I
do not agree with my hon. Friend.
think it is a great pity to switch over the
chairmanship of a scheme like this in one
year. No chairman would be able to see
the results of a scheme in one year.
do not go so far as my hon. Friend and
say he is bound to be a bad chairman-
I
Mr. LESLIE: I did not say that.
Colonel PAKENHAM : I oppose this
being changed to one year, and I think
the Government will too.
The hon.
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
and gallant Member is right in his assumption. The term of two years during which
the chairman will be nominated by the
Minister is very definitely a probationary
period for a scheme, since it is only at the
end of this period and after a resolution
in favour of the scheme has been approved
by both Houses of Parliament that the
scheme may be regarded as having been
finally launched. It is felt that it would
be highly undesirable to have the risk of
a change of chairman during the initial
two years of the scheme, which will necessarily be the most difficult period of its
operation. For that reason I am unable
to accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I should like to have
heard that the noble Viscount is adopting
my suggestion of looking between this and
the Report Stage into my earlier suggestion.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am looking into it.
Mr. LESLIE: In that case I beg leave
to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
CLAUSE 3 (Register of producers and
lists).
(1) Every scheme shall provide for
the registration of any producer of the
regulated product who makes application for that purpose, and for the
removal from the register of the names
of persons who have ceased to be such
producers.
(2) The Ministry, as soon as practicable after any scheme has been framed,
shall cause a list to be compiled containing-
(a) the names of all such persons as the
Ministry has reason to believe to be
producers of the regulated product,
not being persons to be exempted
from registration under the scheme;
and;
(b) the respective addresses of such
persons so far as known to the
Ministry.
*
*
*
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :
"Page 4, line 19, after "persons" insert "in Northern Ireland."
Mr. LESLIE: This is a case in which
the Amendment on the Paper does not
in the least correspond with the typewritten Amendment which is now in the
hands of hon. Members. The House may
remember the story of the man who was
caught in somebody else's farmyard with
duck in his possession. When the
subsequent proceedings took place and he
was charged with stealing the duck the
man explained that he had eight different
a
289 Agricultural Marketing Bill-

6 JUNE 1933
Committee
much
defences, and said he would be
very
obliged if the court would accept one of
them. That is my position in regard to
this Amendment in line 19 (first line of
paragraph (a)) about persons.
It seems
to me that the persons may be obtainable
from three localities, so to speak, and it
seems important that we should define
exactly what should be the composition of
the list the Minister is required to
compile.
It appears to me that the compilation
of the list is mandatory on the Minister.
Therefore it is important that it
should be laid down from whence
he is to draw his candidates. It may be
from everybody in the world at large
whom he considers persons likely to be
producers whether those persons live in
Northern Ireland, the Irish Free State,
the United Kingdom, or anywhere else.
It seems to me that that could not be the
The
right answer.
answer which is
probably right, is what appears in the
typewritten paper, that is, the persons are
to be drawn from the area to which the
scheme applies. There is a passage
suggesting that in the corresponding provision of the Act of 1931, across the
water. Therefore, I think it possible that
should be the answer here.
On the other hand, considering how
small our area is, and I think the Ministry
has power to make a scheme applicable to
Northern Ireland or any part thereof, I
was not sure of the intention, but I am
sure that it is desirable that Parliament
should state quite definitely what the
intention is. I think now the intention is
that the persons to be included are those
"within the area to which the scheme
applies," and therefore that is the addition
to the Bill which I suggest. I beg to
move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

and encouragement of co-operation,

(1) A scheme may provide for all or
any of the matters set out in one or
more of the following paragraphs, that
is to say:-
(a) for empowering the board to
buy the regulated product, to produce
such commodities from that product
as may be specified in the schemes,
and to sell and (subject to regulations
made under sub-section (2) of this
section) to grade, pack, store, adapt
for sale, insure, advertise and transport the regulated product and any
commodity so produced by the board;

or any kind, variety or grade thereof, or such quantity thereof or of
any kind, variety or grade thereof
as may from time to time be determined by the board, only to, or
through the agency of, the board;
(c) for empowering the board to
buy, and to sell or let for hire to
registered producers, anything required for the production, adaptation for sale, or sale of the regulated
product;
or
(d) for empowering the board to cooperate with any other person in doing
anything which the board are
might be empowered to do by virtue.
of paragraph (a) or paragraph (c)
of this section;

time to time-
(i) of the kinds, varieties,
grades or descriptions of the regulated product which may be sold.
by any producer;

above which, the terms on which,
and the persons to, or through the
agency of whom, the regulated
product, or any kind, variety,
grade, description or quantity
thereof, may be sold as aforesaid;

producer,
however, that the

the board to prescribe it, and, without prejudice to the generality of the

wholly or partly by reference to the

2 of this Clause made a man a producer
willy-nilly who could not say whether he
was a producer or not, but he becomes one
by transfer in some of the methods.
indicated above. It seems to me that in
Northern Ireland before a man can be
prosecuted and subjected to the various
penalties imposed by the Bill, it should
be put beyond all question that his misdemeanour was a wilful one, that is, that
he wilfully contravened the scheme.
I know the word "wilful" does not
appear in the corresponding passage of
the English Measure. When the United
Kingdom Bill of 1933, now before the
House of Lords, was introduced, subclause 2 of this Bill did not appear. It
has been put in since. Whether their
Lordships across the water when they
295 Agricultural Marketing Bill—

THE SENATE
Committee
[Mr. Leslie]
have considered the matter will insert
this word "wilful" or not I cannot say.
The point I stand on is this. I have the
greatest horror of anybody being caught
unawares in the meshes of rules and
regulations and obscure Acts of Parliament which he has never seen. I am not
altogether sure that it is safe in these days
to rely on the old maxim that a man must
not be regarded as guilty unless he has a
guilty mind.
Although it will not accord with what
they have hitherto done in England, I
think we ought to insert this word
"wilful," remembering, perhaps, that
the people of Northern Ireland, especially the people likely to be affected by
this Bill, are not, generally speaking, so
apt to know all about everything as some
of their confrères in England. I know
that if this scheme is to succeed at all,
which I cannot discuss now, there must
not be, so to speak, any monkeying with
the business. People must play fair. All
the same I do not think that we should
expose a man to a prosecution unless his
contravention has been wilful, and
accordingly I move the insertion of the
word "wilful" in line 39.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Government is not prepared to accept this
Amendment. The sub-clause to which
this Amendment relates makes it an
offence for any producer to sell a product
which is regulated by an agricultural
marketing scheme unless, in accordance
with the provisions of the scheme. This
is the most important provision of the
Act with regard to the enforcement of
schemes, and the Government cannot
agree to any weakening of the provisions
of this sub-clause.
It may be added that in both the
Agricultural Marketing Acts of 1931
(sub-section 3 of Section 6), the word
"contravention" is used without the
qualifications of "wilful," and it may
prove most important that the powers to
enforce the operation of marketing
schemes in Northern Ireland should be
as great as the powers to enforce marketing schemes in Great Britain.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not in the least
object to strong measures being taken
against the man wilfully guilty, but at
the same time I think the proposal of the
Government is rather a hard one.
However, I do not observe that any Member proposes to stand up in defence of the
more or less injured innocent or guilty,
therefore I beg leave to withdraw the
Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendments stood in
the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Page 9, line 2, leave out from "on" in
line 2 to "to" in line 5 ("on summary
conviction to a fine not exceeding five
pounds, or') and insert "conviction to a
fine not exceeding fifty pounds and."
Page 9, lines 7 and 8, leave out "on
summary conviction" ("imposed on summary conviction").
Mr. LESLIE: This is really another
difficulty. This is a provision for, in presumably similar circumstances, trying a
man by indictment instead of summarily.
The same provision exists in the Bill
across the water, and it is not in itself
in the least objectionable. The difficulty
I see is that the provision as it appears in
the Bill can scarcely stand with Clause 17
further on which gives the Minister of
Agriculture power to say whether there
shall or shall not be a prosecution. I
am not going to move either of these two
Amendments, but instead I shall move
the typewritten Amendment I have
handed in when we come to Clause 17.
I must ask the Committee to pardon me
for making an explanation on Amendments which I am not moving.
Amendments not moved.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand.
part of the Bill.

CLAUSE 14 (Power of the Ministry
to make regulations and orders).
(1) The Ministry may by regulations
make such provision as appears to the
Ministry to be necessary for all or any
of the following purposes:
(a) for giving full effect to this
Act;
(b) for regulating the removal
from Northern Ireland into Great
Britain of any agricultural product
the marketing of which is regulated
by a scheme for the time being in
force under this Act or under the
Act of 1931, and in particular (without prejudice to the generality of the
foregoing provision of this paragraph) for the following matters:
(i) the registration of persons
carrying on business in Northern
Ireland as dealers in the regulated
product;
(ii) the exemption from registration of such classes of persons
carrying on business as aforesaid
as the regulations may specify:
(f) for giving effect to the provisions of a development scheme.
under Part II of the United Kingdom Act, in so far as that develop-
*
ment scheme is applicable to an agricultural product regulated by a
marketing scheme administered by a
board under this Act.
(2) Subject to any directions which
may be contained in regulations made
under this section, it shall be the duty
of boards administering agricultural
marketing schemes under this Act to
exercise their powers in such manner
as appears to them to be necessary for
securing that the regulations are not
contravened.
*

*
*
*
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move in Clause 14, page 17, line 6, after
"1931" to insert "the quantity of the
product or of any description thereof
which may be so removed, and the descriptions of the product which may be
so removed."
The object of this Amendment is to
bring this Sub-clause into line with the
provisions of Clause 24 of the Agricultural
Marketing Bill at present before Parliament at Westminster. In view of the
fact that Clause 24 provides the authority
for conferring on the Ministry the power to
make regulations and orders contained in
this Clause of the Northern Ireland Bill
it is essential that our legislation should
be consistent with the United Kingdom
Act. The alternative to this Amendment
would have been to have employed the
powers conferred by Clause 20, so as to
modify the Northern Ireland Act, and I
think hon. Senators will agree that this
course should only be adopted when no
other means of procedure is open to the
Government.
Mr. LESLIE: Hear, hear.
Amendment agreed to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT : I beg to
move in Clause 14, page 17, line 28, after
"product" ("to an agricultural product")
to insert "the marketing of which is."
This is a drafting Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move in page 17, line 29, to leave out
"marketing" ("regulated by a marketing.")
This is also a drafting Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
299 Agricultural Marketing Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move in page 17, line 33, to leave out
"agricultural marketing" ("administering agricultural.")
This also is a drafting Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 15 (Inspection of premises
and furnishing of returns) ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 16 (Restrictions on disclosing information or returns).
(1) Any person who discloses any information obtained by him in the exercise of any power conferred on him or on
the Ministry or on any marketing board
by or under the provisions of this Act
shall be liable on conviction to a fine
not exceeding fifty pounds:
Provided that nothing in this subsection shall apply to the disclosure of
any information in so far as it is required to be disclosed for the purposes
of a prosecution or other legal proceedings under this Act, or under any
scheme or regulations made under this
Act, or in so far as the disclosure is
required or authorised by this Act or
any scheme or regulations made thereunder.
(2) No individual return or part of
a return made to the Ministry in
accordance with an order of the
Ministry under this Act shall be disclosed without the authority of the
person by whom the return was made,
except in so far as the disclosure is
authorised by the proviso to sub-section
(1) of this section.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause
16, page 19, line 16, after "who" ("Any
person who") to insert "unlawfully.'
Here is another small difficulty. The
Clause is dealing with that class of
people who disclose improperly information which they have obtained by the
exercise of their powers under this Act,
whether they have obtained those powers
as officers of the Ministry or as officers
of the board. Anyhow, speaking generally,
Parliament is not meticulous as regards
possible disclosures on the part of its
officers. For one reason the officers employed by the Ministries do not as a
rule misbehave themselves in that particular way, and for another there are
two or three protections which I need
not go into at the moment.
In this case we have to deal not only
with officers of the Ministry but officers
of the marketing board, and we have inserted a particularly strong proviso.
What I am afraid of is that we have
made the proviso so strong that we have
given a privilege against disclosure by
order of a court of justice. It seems to
me very questionable whether we have
not gone a little bit further than we
intended. There is a good deal of
precedent of one sort and another, but
in nearly every case the safeguard is
simply that information may be disclosed
to persons engaged in the operation of
the Act and so on.
I think we have gathered all that in in
the Clause, but I am very doubtful
whether we have not expressed ourselves
so strongly that we ought not to put in
a little safeguard. If we put in a safeguard-and I am suggesting the insertion
of this word "unlawfully" after "who"
-it becomes questionable whether we
must not follow it up by the second Amendment on the printed paper in line 32. I
certainly would not suggest that Amendment in line 32 if I could help it, but if
we insert "unlawfully" we may decide
whether we can do without it. In the
meantime, unless the Government can
tell me definitely they are satisfied, there
is nothing in this Clause which overrides
the law that evidence shall be given if
required by a court, I think we must take
some steps. As a step, I suggest that we
insert the word ""
unlawful.
""
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This is a
legal point entirely, I am advised that
this Clause does not override the common
law. Therefore, I cannot accept the
Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: In that case I shall ask
leave to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendment stood in the
name of Mr. Leslie: Page 19, line 32,
after "is" insert "required by law or
specifically."
Amendment not moved.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause
16, page 19, line 18, to leave out
"marketing" ("on any marketing").
This is simply a drafting Amendment;
we only discovered it this morning.

this Amendment.

fines)-
17 (Prosecutions
and
(1) Except in a case where this Act
otherwise expressly provides, all offences
under this Act shall be prosecuted, and
all fines thereunder shall be recovered,
in manner provided by the Summary
Jurisdiction (Ireland) Acts; and a
person aggrieved by a conviction or
order made by a court of summary
jurisdiction on determining any information or complaint under this Act
may appeal in manner provided by the
said Summary Jurisdiction Acts.
the
(2) The Ministry may for
purposes of this Act by order require
any producer of, or person carrying on
business as a dealer in, a regulated product to make to the Ministry at such
time or times and in such form and
manner as may be prescribed by the
order, returns as to the production,
sales and stocks of the regulated product and also, in the case of a dealer,
returns as to purchases of the regulated
product.
Any person who fails to comply with
any requirement of an order under this
sub-section within the time prescribed
by the order, or knowingly makes any
false statement in reply to such requirement, shall for each offence be
liable on conviction to imprisonment
for a term not exceeding one month, or
to a fine not exceeding twenty pounds,
or to both such imprisonment and fine.
The following Amendment stood in the
name of Mr. Leslie: Clause 17, page 19,
lines 34 and 35, leave out from the
beginning of line 34 ("except in a case')
to "all" in line 35 ("all offences').
Amendment not moved.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause
17, page, 20, line 1, leave out sub-section
2, and insert the following sub-section:
(2) A prosecution for an offence
which under this Act may be prosecuted before a court of summary jurisdiction (other than an offence committed by an officer of the Ministry or of
any board established under this Act)
shall not be instituted except by or
with the consent of the Ministry.
Sub-clause (2) runs as follows:
"A prosecution for an offence under
this Act shall not be instituted except
by or with the consent of the
Ministry."
I said just now, in referring to the
Amendment dealing with indictments
which was not moved, that the two
clauses could not very well stand
together, but, as it is, in fact, desired
to retain the power of proceeding by
indictment in serious cases, we have left
the earlier part of the Bill alone, and we
now attack this sub-clause. I propose
to do so by means of cutting it out
altogether, and substituting for it this
new sub-clause. The Committee will see
that that limits the power of interference,
or regulation, whichever you like, of the
Ministry to proceedings before a court of
summary jurisdiction, and enables us to
maintain the policy which we have found
very successful in almost all our agricultural Acts. At the same time we do
not run counter to the special difficulties
attendant on proceeding by indictment.
As regards the other matter, we have to
make a saving in the case of officers of the
Ministry which we have inserted in recent
agricultural Acts. That is a matter of
common form and is of no importance. I
beg to move, therefore, firstly, the
omission of the sub-clause as it appears in
the Bill, and, secondly, the substitution
of the words on the typewritten paper.
Perhaps I had better read these again, as
I do not think all members of the Committee have a copy. The new subclause is:
"A prosecution for an offence which
under this Act may be prosecuted before
a court of summary jurisdiction (other
than an offence committed by an officer
of the Ministry or of any board established under this Act) shall not be
instituted except by or with the consent of the Ministry."
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
:-

CLAUSE 21 (Interpretation).
*
**
"Agricultural product" includes any
product of agriculture or horticulture
and any article of food or drink wholly
or partly manufactured or derived from
any such product, and fleeces and the
skins of animals.
"Regulated product"
means,
in
relation to any scheme, any product the
marketing of which is regulated by the
scheme, but does not include any product in so far as it is produced outside
the area to which the scheme is
applicable:
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause
21, page 21, line 22, to leave out "any"
("any product of agriculture or horticulture') and to insert "live stock,
poultry, and any other."
The suggestion to insert the words.
"live stock, poultry, and any other"
here really had its origin in something
that was said by the noble Viscount
during the Second Reading debate. He
assured me that live stock was included
within the purview of the Bill, but that
the term "live stock" did not include
poultry. It seemed to me, especially
having regard to the frequent references
to
turkeys in another place, that
it was desirable that we should
have
power to include poultry, at
all events in our own area if we could
not adapt ourselves to Great Britain in
that matter, because if live stock did not
include poultry here presumably it did not
include it in Great Britain, and, therefore, a joint scheme would not be possible.
As regards ourselves, I thought poultry
certainly ought to go in and, therefore,
305 Agricultural Marketing Bill-

6 JUNE 1933
Committee.
I had to put it in this way. I trust the
form of words I have used does not offend
against any recognised legal canons, but
I think that, as live stock does not include poultry, the words ought to be
inserted.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This
Amendment cannot be accepted. The
existing definition of "agricultural product" is supposed to cover poultry, and,
therefore, my answer about live stock
was misleading. "Agricultural product"
is supposed to apply to poultry rather
more than live stock," but any way it
is included in the Bill: that is to say there
are the two definitions. "Live stock"
and "agricultural product" cover poultry.
Poultry is included.
Colonel PAKENHAM: That may be
all right, but I must say that to call
poultry an agricultural product is rather
a peculiar way of talking of a hen. There
are a great many people who keep
poultry and who have nothing to do with
agriculture or agricultural products, and
I must say it is straining the English
language to include poultry as an agricultural product. I think the Government might have allowed the word
"poultry" to go in. It makes it very
clear. It does not mean more than the
changing of five or six lines and clears
the whole situation. Nobody
honestly tell me that a turkey hen is an
agricultural product.
can
Viscount CHARLEMONT: If the
hon. and learned Senator will allow me
I will consider the matter before the
next stage of the Bill. I do not say
whether the Amendment will be accepted
or not, but I will give it consideration.
Mr. LESLIE: If the Amendment is
moved at a later stage it will probably
not be in exactly the same terms as those
moved now. However, I shall ask leave
to withdraw, as the noble Viscount has
been kind enough to promise to look into
the matter.
Amendment, by leave, witdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page
21, lines 39 to 41, to leave out from
"scheme" ("regulated by the scheme")
in line 39 to the end of line 41
("applicable").
I am not at all sure whether any Member of the Committee will be brave
enough to stand up and say he understands the meaning of the words I am
proposing to leave out. The whole paragraph runs:
66 6
Regulated product' means, in
relation to any scheme, any product
the marketing of which is regulated by
the scheme
""
and goes on to say
"but does not include any product in
so far as it is produced outside the area
to which the scheme is applicable."
I suggest that those latter words had
better be left out unless some explanation
can be given. As the Committee is no
doubt aware the words occur in the
English Act of 1931, and they will be
repeated either directly or by reference
in the United Kingdom Act of 1933, but
they leave me in almost complete doubt
as to what is to happen to anything that
is produced outside Northern Ireland but
manages to get inside. It is very, very
complicated, and I am not at all certain
that it is desirable for us to use in this
Bill words which the great majority of
people in Northern Ireland certainly will.
not understand. It may be that the noble
Viscount, as Minister of Education, may
be able to give us some thoroughly clear
definition which we can understand, and,
if that be so, I think the Committee will
probably be disposed to accept the words,
In the meantime, I beg to move that
they be left out.
It would
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
be altogether improper for the Minister
of Education to interfere in the affairs of
the Minister of Agriculture. (Laughter.)
I regret that I cannot accept the Amendment. The reason why the words the hon.
Senator proposes to omit are necessary is
that there might be a scheme operating
for not the whole of Northern Ireland,
but simply in some part thereof.
The
words appear necessary for the same reason as had led the hon. Senator to insert
an Amendment to line 19 of page 4 of
the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: Everybody seems to be
content that the words should stand.
Therefore, I must ask leave to withdraw
the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

Loan Guarantee Bill-
Adjournment
6 JUNE 1933
The
CHAIRMAN: Although the
powers of this House in regard to amending Bills dealing with money are not very
clearly defined, it has always been my
opinion that this House should not
amend either Bills or clauses of Bills
which have been introduced on Money
Resolutions in another place. I regret,
therefore, that I must rule the two
Amendments on the Paper out of Order.
Mr. LESLIE: It is impossible to
challenge your Lordship's ruling in any
particular instance, but I will take
another opportunity of raising the point
to which the Amendments are directed.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Short title and citation)
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
Preamble and Title agreed to.
Ordered, That the Bill be reported,
without Amendment, to the House.
Ordered,
That the House do now
resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported without Amendment.
Moved, "That this Report be now
received."(Viscount Charlemont.)
Mr. LESLIE: Perhaps I may take this
opportunity of suggesting that the object
of referring a Bill based on a Money
Resolution to the House in Committee
does not now seem very clear, but that,
again, I think we might consider some
time after the Recess, in order that your
ruling may be embodied, perhaps, in the
Standing Orders, to prevent these
troubles. The question of the particular
Amendments I will raise on the Third
Reading. But I think we ought to be
clear on these things.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjournment.
Resolved, "That this House do now
adjourn." (Viscount Charlemont).
Adjourned accordingly at
Twenty Minutes after
Four o'clock.

THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 7th June, 1933.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Business of the House.
Resolved: "That Standing Order No.
XXV be suspended for this day's sitting
for the purpose of taking the remaining
stages of the Agricultural Marketing
Bill." (Viscount Charlemont.)
Question to Minister.
ALLEGED BOYCOTT OF ULSTER
TEACHERS.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Joseph
Cunningham:-
To ask the Minister of Education
whether, in view of the fact that Ulster
Teachers' Union is excluded from
membership of the World Federation,
whose Congress assembles in Dublin at
the end of July, he will consider the
withholding of official support and see
that the head of Stranmillis College
is not
attend,
permitted to
announced, till this boycott of an
organisation embracing a very large
body of Northern teachers is removed.
was
set
the
as
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM :
Before formally asking the question
which stands in my name on the Order
Paper, I should like to make an explanation of the position. When our Northern
Government
Ulster
our
up
teachers were required, in fact almost
compelled to sever their connection with
the Irish National Teachers' Organisation owing to
that
fact that
organisation was run on political lines.
The Ulster Teachers' Union was formed,
and has, I believe, carried on verv satisfactorily since that time. They have a
very capable secretary in Mr. Smyth,
and I know that so far as the teachers
are concerned the Ulster Teachers' Union
looks after their interests very effectively
in the Northern area. When it became
known that this World Federation of
Education Associations was holding a
congress in Dublin the secretary of the
Ulster Teachers' Union, Mr. Smyth,
wrote saying his organisation would be
very pleased to become affiliated with the
Federation, and to that letter he received
a very courteous reply, stating that the
Federation would be very pleased to have
the assistance of the Ulster Teachers'
Union in carrying out their ideals.
The Ulster Teachers' Union were satisfied that in due time they would receive
notification to attend the Congress, but
some months later they received a communication to say that their application
for affiliation had been turned down by
One can
a majority of the directors.
only come to the conclusion that those
who were making the arrangements for
the congress in Dublin, the Irish National
Teachers' Organisation, had evidently
used their influence with the directors to
prevent the Ulster Teachers' Union being
represented at that congress.
The members of the Ulster Teachers' Union feel
very humiliated to think that their application to take part in this congress has
been rejected.
A circular came into my possession
detailing the arrangements made for this
conference, and I was surprised to find
that the list of those who are to speak at
the congress included the names of such
distinguished Northern Ireland representatives as Sir Richard W. Livingstone,
Vice-Principal of Queen's University,
Belfast; Professor W. J. McCallister, and
Mr. H. E. Wynn, M.A., Principal of
Stranmillis Training College, Belfast. It
is evident that while the Irish National
Teachers' Organisation in Dublin do not
desire the assistance of the official body
representing Ulster Teachers, at the same
time they are prepared to select from
Northern Ireland one or two representatives whom they are prepared to use
for their Own particular advantage.
Therefore, I have put down this question
with a view to ascertaining whether it is
the intention of the Minister of Education to encourage the Principal of the
Stranmillis Training College to attend
this congress in Dublin. I feel that it is
rather an unpleasant position for the
three gentlemen whose names I have
mentioned to be placed in. I do not
know what they think of it, but I know
what I would think of it.
My opinion is that the Dublin people
simply want to use them for their own

the purport underlying the reply, but it
was not mentioned.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Certainly.
The Government would not give any
approval of that. The Government cannot help what the educationists of Northern Ireland do in their spare time.
particular purpose, and I should be very
glad to hear the Minister of Education
say that so far as he is concerned he will
not give his approval to the attendance
of the head of Stranmillis Training
College at the conference until those responsible remove this usual Irish weapon
of the boycott from the Ulster Teachers'
Union. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
I do not see why the Ulster Teachers'
Union should not receive all the assistance it is possible to give them. This
organisation was set up for the purpose
of looking after the interests of the
teachers of the Northern area, and why
there should be any attempt to boycott
it I cannot understand. It is probably
due more to political influences than to
anything else, but I would be very glad
to hear the Minister's view of the matter.
Mr. QUIGLEY: In view of how the
Northern Ireland teachers have been
treated by those responsible in the Irish
Free State for the arrangements in connection with the congress of the World
Federation of Education Associations,
which will meet in Dublin next month,
I not only hope, but I expect, that the

his question.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I have nɔ
official knowledge of the exclusion to which

of the Stranmillis Training College attends the congress in question, he will do
so in his private capacity, and during the
vacation period; his participation in the
proceedings of the Congress will have no
official significance.
The question of withholding official
support from the Congress does not arise,
since no official support has, in fact, been
given by the Ministry of Education.

315 Agricultural Marketing Bill--

THE SENATE
Report Stage
Agricultural Marketing Bill.
Order for receiving Report of Amendments read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In moving
"That this Report be now received," I
should like to amplify, to some extent, a
statement which appears under my name
in the report of the debate on the Second
Reading of the Bill to the effect that
"under the Bill we will have a chance
to control imports into Northern Ireland
which otherwise we might not have had."
I did not mean to imply that the Bill conferred any fiscal powers upon this Government-this would, of course, be manifestly
impossible. What I did intend to convey
was that by passing this Bill, Parliament
would enable the Government to undertake the necessary negotiations with the
authorities in Great Britain, so
secure that the special requirements of
Northern Ireland should be recognised in
the general arrangements for the United
Kingdom which it is expected will develop
as a result of marketing legislation.
as to
The hon. and learned Senator, Senator
Leslie, raised a point upon Clause 2 of the
Bill in connection with the constitution of
boards to administer schemes, to which I
promised to give consideration. I propose
to move two Amendments to the Subsection which, I think, will be an improvement and will meet the point raised.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
CLAUSE 2 (Constitution of boards to
administer schemes).
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
(2) when the members of the board
as fixed by a scheme exceed eleven in
number, the board shall appoint an
executive committee of eleven with full
board powers for the administration of
the said scheme: Provided that the
chairman and the vice-chairman of the
board and the three members of the
board appointed by the minister shall
be included in the executive committee
aforesaid as chairman, vice-chairman
and members.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move in Clause 2, Sub-Clause (2), to
leave out from the word "committee'
("executive committee of eleven") to the
end of the line ("powers") and insert.
"consisting of not more than eleven
members of the Board and having the
full powers of the Board."
This Amendment is designed to allow the
Committee to consist of a smaller number
than eleven, if so desired. The House
will realise that difficulties might arise if
it were necessary to adhere rigidly to the
number eleven in the case of a Board,
for instance, where the total number of
members was only twelve in all. That is
the first Amendment I propose to move.
Amendment agreed to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move in line 35 to leave out from "board"
("vice-chairman of the board") to the
end of line 36 ("Minister"), and insert
"(whether being members appointed by
the said Minister or not), and also any
members of the Board appointed by the
said Minister and not being such chairman or vice-chairman."
This Amendment is designed to meet
the point raised by the hon. and learned
Senator Leslie during the Committee
stage of the Bill. It will be seen that the
chairmau and vice-chairman of the
Board must be included in the Executive
Committee, whether they are appointed
by the Minister of Agriculture or not.
Mr. LESLIE: I might perhaps say,
as the noble Viscount says, that these
Amendments were put down really in
consequence of something I said yesterday. I should like to say further they
quite meet my view as to the little
difficulty one did at the moment experience. I think there
can be no
further difficulty now on that point.
The Amendments really go together.
*
Amendment agreed to.
CLAUSE 21 (Interpretation).
*
*
*
"United Kingdom Act" means any
Act which may be passed in the present session of the Parliament of the
United Kingdom to provide for regulating the importation and sale of
agricultural products and for organising the production of secondary
agricultural products, to amend the
law with respect to the marketing of
agricultural products, and to make
further provision in connection with
the matters aforesaid.
*
317 Agricultural Marketing Bill-

7 JUNE 1933
Report Stage
Mr. LESLIE: This is what I hope the
House will regard as an old friend in a
somewhat new guise. It will be remembered that during the Committee stage
of Bill there was a certain amount
of conversation, I may say, on the subject
of the inclusion of poultry definitely and
specifically in the Bill. It was thought
something should be done to keep the
discussion alive and perhaps enable the
House to reconsider the decision of the
Committee certainly to give the noble
Viscount the opportunity of giving the
House some further information. Accordingly, to attain these ends, I put the
words on the Paper which are now in
possession of the House, and, having put
the words on the Paper, it becomes incumbent upon me formally to move them
in the shape of an Amendment.
to move in Clause 21, page 22, after line
21 (end of Sub-Clause (1)) to insert as a
new Sub-Section: "A scheme under this
Act may provide for the application
thereof to any class of poultry within the
definition of poultry contained in the
Poultry Act, 1911."
I beg
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am advised that the term "agricultural product," as defined in Clause 21 of the Bill,
has the widest possible application, and
that the specific mention of either live
stock or poultry would not improve the
definition, but might, indeed, detract
from its generality. Legally, if not
mathematically, it appears to be an
axiom that if you add to a definition you
subtract from it. The same definition is
used in the Agricultural Marketing Act
of 1931. In an official handbook on that
Act it is stated that
"The definition is similar to that used
in the Agricultural Produce (Grading
and Marking) Amendment Act, 1931.
It covers all primary products, whether
crops or stock, produced or in course of
production. It also covers secondary or
processed products in the form of any
human or animal foodstuff or drink.
I may remark that the development
scheme recommended by the Lane-Fox
Re-organisation Commission as regards
pigs and bacon, and also Part II of the
present United Kingdom Bill, are based
upon this view. As our Bill is linked up
with the United Kingdom Bill I think the
learned Senator will agree that we ought
to preserve uniformity of phrase wherever
possible.
Colonel PAKENHAM: In view of the
answer of the noble Viscount I shall not
protest against hens being included as
agricultural products, but I do think,
with due respect to the framers of this
language, that it is a very awkward way
of describing hens. I shall remain convinced to the end of my life of the fact
that hens are not agricultural products,
and should not be rightly so described.
In this case, however, I bow to the
superior knowledge of the experts in
language.
Mr. LESLIE: I entirely concur with
the non-mathematical axiom laid down
by the noble Viscount. I hate anything
to do with mathematics, and that adds
to my pleasure in asking leave to withdraw my Amendment, which, I think,
is a formal necessity.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
(6
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, That this Bill be now read the
Third time."
Mr. BARCLAY: I would like to
support the Third Reading of this Bill.
On the day of the Second Reading we
had so much business that I remained
quiet, and did not support the Second
Reading as I intended to do. My reason
for supporting the Third Reading is that
I believe it will be a great benefit to
those who cultivate the land and to those
who sell the produce of the land. I think
this is another proof of the Government's
intention to assist in every way this
important industry which we have in
Northern Ireland, and if the farmers
embrace the opportunity afforded by this
Bill they will find that by working in
co-operation with each other they will
have a great deal more money in their
pockets than they have at the present
time.
Undoubtedly the price of farm produce during the past few years has been
very small. No doubt the land has been
hard to cultivate owing to the scarcity
of labour, but I think that if we had
the old spirit of the farmer back again
things would be quite different. In view
of the Acts of Parliament which have
been passed during recent years the
farmers should be a great deal better
off than their fathers and grandfathers
were. There is money in the land if
the farmer takes it out. The farmer
now has his part to play. The Government has played its part in providing
good seed, in providing markets, in pro-
Agricultural Marketing Bill—
Report Stage
THE SENATE
[Mr. Barclay]
viding live stock of a good strain, and
also in making it easy for transport by
making roads and bridges, and so on.
The Government has done its part well,
and it is up to the farmers in the country
to do their part, and if both the Government and the farmers work in conjunction
they will find that the agricultural industry will improve, that the farmers
will not be so hard up, and that we will
not hear so much about the hard times
we read of in the papers. If the farmers
take off their coats the way their fathers
did and say, "There is gold in this field
and we are going to have this gold," and
work as their fathers did they will find
that they will get the money. They will
have the money in their pockets and there
will be no necessity for holding meetings,
here, there, and everywhere, and raising
the cry that they are not able to pay
their land annuities. They should be
more independent. They should be in a
position to say, "We are going to be independent, we are going to pay our land
annuities, we are not going to be parasites living on the taxpayers of the
country. That is the kind of spirit
we want the farmers to have.
""
There are four commodities which the
farmer produces year after year which
would be of great benefit if properly
handled. These four commodities are
We
wheat, potatoes, pigs, and milk.
will take wheat. I dare say hon. Members will say to me that this country
cannot produce wheat that will be as
good as the wheat imported into this.
country. I admit that we cannot get
as good flour from the wheat we grow
in Northern Ireland as from imported
wheat, but we can produce wheat which
produces flour. I do not know if any
hon. Senators have seen the loaf which
is manufactured from this wheat. I have
seen it, and I ate some of it to-day. This
loaf is called "Ireland's Own.' It is
put upon the market, recommended by
doctors as wholesome and good for all who
eat it. I think we could do a good deal
more in regard to the production of this
kind of bread for the people of Northern
Ireland. The farmers, if they set themselves to it, have plenty of good land on
which to produce wheat, and if they produce it they will get a price which will be
a benefit to them.
Then we have milk. I do not know
whether the House will agree with me or
If
not, but I think that the milk industry
should be more centralised than it is at
the present time. We have too many
small creameries all over the country.
all those responsible for the carrying out
of the business were trained in the agricultural college, under the Ministry of
Agriculture, they would all produce the
same article, and being the same article
would, no doubt, command a better price.
As it is, no two creameries produce the
same article. I think if the industry was
centralised and all the farmers were producing the same article they would benefit
a great deal more financially than they
are doing at the present time.
Next we have pigs. We have not
developed the pig industry to the extent.
we should have done. There is a great
market for our pork and our bacon, and
we have a great market for our young pigs,
and so the pig industry can be built up to
an enormous extent if the farmers take
advantage of all the Acts of Parliament
upon the Statute Book. We can have
pig breeding, we can have pig fattening,
can have pig killing, curing, and
smoking, and also pig distribution.
we
Colonel PAKENHAM: You have got
the wrong pig.
Mr. BARCLAY: Then, again, we
have potatoes. The potato market has
not been so good during the past year,
but I think that even if we have not a
good market for potatoes we need never
be hungry as long as there are plenty of
potatoes. Potatoes also can be used for
feeding pigs, which will bring in a good
return. In 1930, I think it was, Great
Britain for bacon alone paid to foreigners
£52,000,000. I cannot see why the
farmers of Northern Ireland should not
have a lion's share of that £52,000,000
that is paid for bacon alone. I believe
that if this Bill is taken advantage of it
will be a great benefit to the whole farming community, and will also provide a
great amount of employment both on the
farms and at the different places of industry to which the agricultural products
would be brought. Therefore, I have
great pleasure in supporting the Third
Reading of this Bill."
Mr. LESLIE: I should like verv
briefly to bid farewell to the Bill now
before us and, perhaps, to express a final
wish that its sojourn in this House had
been somewhat longer. But, having done
321 Agricultural Marketing Bill

Moneylenders Bill
what one could to assist in the discussions
on the Bill, it is especially incumbent on
me to say that, although, as the House
knows, I do not regard this Bill as a Bill
entirely without a blot, I do recognise,
and I think the House recognises and
the country should recognise that it is
a well meant effort, very well put together,
to improve matters in the agricultural
field generally, and that it is the fruit of
very many months of very, very hard work
on the part of the Ministry and of those
who have been consulted in connection
with the matter.
With regard to the effect of the Bill, I
hope there will not be too many boards
established, and that
that a sufficiency of
satisfactory candidates will be forthcoming to fill the places on the boards. But
as a result of the efforts which have been
made it is towards a rise in prices and to
the certainty of a market that I would
look rather than further hard work on the
part of the farmer himself. I think the
farmer generally to-day is a very industrious person, and his efforts in the
past have been very ill-rewarded. I
hope, therefore, that better prices and the
certainty of a market may bring him.
better times. To that end we trust this
Bill may serve.
I
Mr. DORMAN: I too wish to bid farewell to this Bill, and to express the hope
that the agriculturists of Northern Ireland will take advantage of all the
facilities that it will afford them in the
future to compete in the great markets
of Great Britain. I am not one of those
who, like some of the British statesmen of
the middle of last century, think that
Ireland was intended by Providence as a
great cattle ranch to provide mutton
chops and beef steaks for the teeming
industrial centres of Great Britain.
believe that Ireland was intended by
Providence to enable the people to produce.
from its surface and from the bowels of
the earth the greatest possible quantity
of those things that are necessary for
human happiness and the continuance of
human life from day to day. Unfortunately there are a good many of our
countrymen who do not look upon our
country from that point of view, and who
are more concerned with external markets,
often forgetting that it is quite possible
to preserve and develop the foreign
market, while depressing the standard of
the comforts of life of the people within
the area from which the products come.
I hope the boards and the agricultural
producers of Northern Ireland will not
neglect the importance of the local market
and that they will not suffer as a result
of concentrating their attention exclusively
upon the foreign market thereby, perhaps,
depressing prices and producing for consumption in the home area a depreciated
product. I wish the Bill every success,
and hope that the agriculturists will
take full advantage of it.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I
only wish to say a few words upon this
question and to express the hope, though
I greatly doubt it, that the Bill will have
the effect referred to. Yesterday I went
through County Down, through most
beautiful country, where one should see
a large agricultural population, and I
hardly saw an agricultural field turned
up the whole way from Downpatrick.
The hon. Senator on my left says that
the land at present is used for producing
animals to make mutton chops for
England. The land in County Down was
never intended for a cattle ranch, which
it is at present. I do hope that this Bill
will have the effect of changing the face
of the country and of doing something
to provide for the population and for the
good of the Province.

Loans Guarantee (No. 2) Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Moved, "That this Bill be now read
the Third time."-(Mr. Long).
Mr. LESLIE: This is another Bill on
which I should like to bestow my good
wishes. I also want to express the hope
that it may be found possible to follow
up this Bill with a No. 3 Guarantee Bill
brought in by the Government to carry
the benefits of this Measure somewhat
further by allowing the guarantee to continue in the event of a change of lender.
It seems possible that a borrower might
be able to obtain better terms from some
third party than he can at the moment
obtain from his original lender, whether
he approaches the original lender within
the powers conferred by the instrument
creating the loan or not. It does not
seem to me that that course would
seriously impair the national finances or,
indeed, that it would have any effect at
all, provided, of course, that nothing was
done to which the Minister of Finance,
as representing the taxpayer, could not
give his consent. I think anything in
that direction would be within the spirit
of the remarks made by the right hon.
Gentleman, the Minister of Finance,
when moving the Second Reading of the
Bill in another place. I do not know
whether the Parliamentary Secretary
(Mr. Long) has any further information
to give us, because all the remarks which
I have just uttered were made on the
Second Reading of the Bill in this House.
Mr. LONG: In reply to the hon. and
learned Senator, I might explain that
there are three sources in general from
which borrowers obtain loans which are
the subject of guarantees under the Loans
Guarantee Acts. Firstly, from the public
by market flotations; secondly, from
bodies, like insurance companies, which
have moneys waiting to invest; and,
thirdly, from joint stock banks. In the
last-mentioned case the bank is very
often the borrower's usual bank at which
he carries out his financial transactions,
and has, therefore, a substantial interest
in the concern itself irrespective of the
guaranteed loan.
The Clause of the Bill pre-supposes
what I may briefly term a conversion
operation, that is to say, a re-borrowing
at a lower rate of interest. This reborrowing may be from any one of the
type of lenders to which I have referred,
but it is obvious that where a Government Department is concerned there must
be no element of compulsion in the conversion operation, otherwise the whole
basis of Government credit would disappear. It is not right, for instance, to
expect a bank, which has invested money
for a specific period and at a specific rate
of interest, at the bidding of the Government to accept a reduced rate and possibly
leave this money outstanding for a longer
period than it had originally bargained
for, or, alternatively, to accept a repayment of its loan. The only type of case
where the Government seeks the power to
re-guarantee is where what I may call a
definite break is provided for in the loan
instruments, and where no breach of
faith can arise. Moreover, a borrower
himself may try to bring pressure on the
Ministry of Finance in order that its
influence may be used to cajole the bank
into accepting a lower rate of interest.
This is a position in which no Government Department should be allowed to be
involved.
325 Loans Guarantee Bill-

7 JUNE 1933
Adjournment
I may say, however, that in the past
the Ministry of Finance has found banks
exceedingly reasonable, and cases have
occurred where they have voluntarily,
after a certain time, accepted a rate of
interest lower than the original arrangement provided for. The acceptance of
such a lower rate does not involve the
issue of a new guarantee, as the concession is an ex gratia one on the part
of the lender. I trust, therefore, that
with this explanation before him the hon.
and learned Senator will find no difficulty
in supporting the Third Reading of the
Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: I am much obliged.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Resolved: "That this Bill do
pass."-(Mr. Long.)
now
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill without any Amendment.
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
this House do now
move:
"That
adjourn."
Colonel SHARMAN - CRAWFORD:
Until when?
Viscount CHARLEMONT : Until

Royal Assent
Adjournment of House
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 13th June, 1933.
The Senate met at Ten minutes after
Twelve of the Clock, Mr. J. G. LESLIE
(Deputy-Speaker) in the Chair.

29 JUNE 1933
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 29th June, 1933.
The Senate, which had stood adjourned
from Tuesday, 13th June, met at Twelve
noon, Mr. LESLIE (Deputy Speaker) in
the Chair.
Mr.
Election of Senators.
informed
DEPUTY-SPEAKER
the House that he had received certificates from the Clerk of the Crown, That
the Right Hon. Samuel Cunningham, of
Fernhill, Belfast; Mr. James Hill
Dickson, of Ardmore, Ballygowan; the
Right Hon. Sir Thomas James Dixon, of
Cairndhu, Larne; Mr. James Woods
Gyle, of 77 Holywood Road, Belfast; Sir
Emerson Crawford Herdman, of Carricklee, Strabane; Mr. Thomas Robert
Lavery, of Lakeview, Newtownards; Mr.
John Andrew Long, of Culmore,
Highlands, Limavady; Mr. Thomas
McLaughlin, of Ogle Street, Armagh;
Mr. James McMahon, of Bettyville, 16.
Norfolk Drive, Belfast; Richard Crosbie
Maxwell, Viscount Bangor, of Castle
Ward, Downpatrick; Colonel Hercules
Arthur Pakenham, of Langford Lodge,
Crumlin; and the Right Hon. Robert
Gordon Sharman-Crawford, of Crawfordsburn, were, on the 23rd day of June,
1933, duly elected Senators to serve in
the Parliament of Northern Ireland.
Members Sworn.
The Senators following took and subscribed the Oath required by law, and
signed the Roll:-
The Viscount Bangor, O.B.E., D.L.
Mr. John Andrew Long, D.L.
Colonel the Right Hon. Robert Gordon
Sharman-Crawford, C.B.E., D.L.
Mr. James Hill Dickson.

Right Hon. Samuel Cunningham.
Sir Emerson Crawford Herdman,
K.B.E.
Colonel Hercules Arthur Pakenham,
C.M.G., D.L.
Election of Speaker.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I feel greatly
honoured that the duty of proposing the
election of the Speaker of this Chamber
has been placed in my hands, and I feel
almost as if some of the greatness inherent in that position has temporarily
been reflected upon myself. In proposing
that Viscount Bangor be elected Speaker
of this Chamber I am not going to give
the House a long historical sketch of his
career, because his work and duty, and the
way in which they are performed are
well known to all Members of this House,
and, indeed, to the people of Northern
Ireland. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
What more particularly affects us is his
fitness for the position into which we
hope to put him. The duties of a
Speaker of any Chamber are very
multifarious and include all sorts of
things. In this particular Chamber the
Speaker has generally found that we are
a very amenable quantity. We are not a
noisy crowd; we are not people who withdraw Maces from their proper places; we
do not take articles of furniture and
throw them at the Speaker, and we conduct ourselves as far as possible as
ordinary human beings and Christians.
Those of us who have sat in this House
during the last three years during Lord
Bangor's previous Speakership are all
firmly convinced that he is in full
possession of all those qualities which go
to make a good Speaker. (Hon. Members.
Hear, hear.) He has tact, dignity,
commonsense, and a great sense of impartiality. He has, moreover, what I
think one of the finest qualities, a great
sense of humour. He must sometimes
wonder in this Chamber whether or not
We are at all humorous. There are
occasions when we are far from being
humorous. At all events, his Lordship
has a very keen sense of humour, and I
am confident that in electing him as
Speaker we are doing our duty towards
ourselves. His career as a soldier and as
a citizen, as I have said before, require
no encomiums from me, but as a fellow
soldier I would like to say how proud I
am that those two qualities and virtues
of discipline and self-command find so
fine an exponent in Viscount Bangor. I
have the pleasure to propose the election
of Viscount Bangor as Speaker of this
Chamber.
Moved, "That Viscount Bangor be
elected Speaker of the Senate."
THE SENATE
Election of Speaker

Adjournment
I said it would be my earnest endeavour
to keep up the high traditions and lofty
standard of conduct so admirably maintained by him. In this respect I have
received the greatest support and cooperation from all sides of the House,
and my work as Speaker has not only
been easy but pleasant, for I felt I was
among friends.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
consider it a great pleasure to have the
opportunity of seconding the Motion
moved by the hon. and gallant Member.
I certainly agree with much that he has
said regarding our Speaker's ability. We
are very fortunate in having the services
of one so very well fitted for the position,
and our experience of him during the
past term has been such as to prove to
us that he is able to act in a strictly
fair and impartial manner. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) Any time that
the House required his guidance, or when
we fell out of the rule of procedure in
any way, we were rebuked in a very
courteous and very kindly way. I feel
we have no alternative indeed only to
re-elect our Speaker to the position.
When we suffered the loss of our previous
Speaker we thought it might be very
difficult to get someone to fill the position,
and we were fortunate in having Lord
Bangor to occupy the position here which
he has done so satisfactorily. I have
great pleasure in seconding the Motion
moved by the hon. and gallant Member,
Colonel Pakenham.
On Question, Motion ("That Viscount
Bangor be elected Speaker of the
Senate') agreed to.
The Senate having again unanimously
called Viscount Bangor to the Chair, he
was taken out of his place and conducted
to the Chair by Colonel Pakenham and
Mr. Joseph Cunningham.
Mr. SPEAKER: I feel deeply grateful
to the hon. Members, my Friends the
proposer and seconder of my election as
Speaker of this House, for all the very
kind things they have said about me,
and also to all the other hon. Members
present for their concurrence. When the
Members of this House did me the honour
of electing me as Speaker in succession
to the late Marquess of Dufferin and Ava,
In a football match, if the players
keep the rules the game progresses satisfactorily, but if they do not you hear
the referee's whistle, which leads to delay
and irritation. Similarly in this House
we have our Standing Orders, which
govern our procedure, and we have our
rules, which have to be observed.
these rules are kept then everything goes
smoothly. For twelve years, during
If
which time I and others in this House
have been Members of the Senate, I think
I may safely say the rules have been
generally well kept, and it has been a
very great pleasure to me to preside over
your deliberations.
In once more thanking all the Members
of this House for the great honour they
have conferred on me in re-electing me
to preside over their deliberations, I would
like just to say this one word, that if
I have been successful in winning the
confidence of the House during the past,
I can only hope that I may be able to
prove in the future that I am worthy of
the trust.
Adjournment.
Resolved: "That this House do now
adjourn until such date as Mr. Speaker
shall appoint."-(Viscount Charlemont.)
Adjourned accordingly at Twenty
minutes after Twelve noon
until such date as Mr. Speaker
shall appoint.

Question to Minister.
BUILDING AND ALTERING OF
ELEMENTARY SCHOOLS.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether he can state
the
number of provided elementary
schools built, or in process of building,
under the Education Acts (Northern Ireland) and the cost thereof; the number
of voluntary schools transferred under the
Acts; the number of transferred schools
altered and re-conditioned since transfer,
and the public cost; the number of
Catholic voluntary schools transferred;
the number of Catholic voluntary schools
built, or in process of building, under the
Act of 1930, and the Ministry's contribution and the managerial contribution to
the
Catholic
cost; the number of
voluntary schools altered or re-conditioned under the Act of 1930, and the
Ministry's and the managerial contribution to the cost; and whether the denominational teaching called "Bible Instruction" is given in all provided and
transferred schools.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): According to the information before this Ministry 73 new provided
public elementary schools had been
erected by the local Education Authorities up to the 31st December, 1932, at a
cost of approximately £680,000. Twentytwo further schools have since been completed or are now under construction at a
cost estimated at about £120,000. There
were 474 transferred schools in operation
on the 31st December, 1932. Since that
date a few of these have been discontinued consequent on the opening of
new provided schools or other causes.
Twenty-five voluntary public elementary
schools have been transferred since 1st
January, 1933.
Works of enlargement or major
improvement have been carried out in the
case of 218 transferred schools up to the
31st December, 1932, at a cost of
£227,736. The Ministry has not at
present the actual figures in regard to
similar works to
transferred schools
during the year commencing 1st January,
1933, but such works have been approved
in a number of cases. There has been
hitherto no case of the transfer of a public
elementary school by a Roman Catholic
clerical manager.
The Ministry has sanctioned grants
under the Act of 1930 amounting to
£53,215 towards the erection of 13 new
voluntary schools under Roman Catholic
management. Of these schools 5 are now
in operation, 2 are almost ready for
occupation and 3 are in course of erection.
So far as the Ministry is aware, the erection of the remaining 3 schools has not
337 Administrative Provisions Bill-

17 OCTOBER 1933
Second Reading
yet been begun. These grants have been
based on estimates only and the final
grant can only be determined when the
actual cost is known. This grant will be
one of half the actual approved cost, the
other half being defrayed by the local
parties interested in the schools.
The Ministry has similarly sanctioned.
grants amounting to £6,088 towards the
alteration or improvement of five existing
voluntary schools under Roman Catholic
managers, and the work has been completed in the case of two of these schools.
As in the case of the new schools these
grants are for the present based on estimates only and represent one-half of the
estimated cost, the other half falling to
be borne locally. The Ministry has at
present under examination a considerable
number of further applications by Roman
Catholic managers for similar grants towards the erection of new voluntary schools
or the improvement of existing schools in
accordance with the provisions of Section
10 of the 1930 Act, but the examination
of these cases has not yet reached the
stage at which a grant can be sanctioned
owing to the need for examination of the
plans, submission of satisfactory evidence
of title, etc.
To the best of my knowledge Bible Instruction as defined in the Act of 1930 is
given in provided and transferred schools.
almost without exception. I should like,
however, to impress upon the hon. and
learned Member that the Act expressly
defines Bible Instruction as excluding instruction as to any tenet which is
distinctive of any particular religious
denomination.

Administration Provisions Bill
(Northern Ireland), 1933.
Resolved: "That this Bill be now
read a First time."-(Mr. Long).
Mr. LONG: In this short Bill there
have been gathered together various provisions which deal with the details of
administration. The first two clauses of
the Bill make changes in the existing
practice designed to effect savings in the
expenditure of public money, whilst in
Clauses 3 and 4 are certain amendments
-or rather I should say adaptations-of
the law so as to bring it into line with
the circumstances of the present day.
may, perhaps, save discussion at a later
stage if I give a brief explanation of
each clause.
It
Clause 1 enables the Ordnance Survey
service to be transferred from the
Ministry of Agriculture to the Ministry
of Finance. It is considered that, having
regard to the extensive survey work which
is now being carried out by the Ministry
of Finance in connection with revaluation, the work of Ordnance Survey can
be more economically carried out by the
Valuation Department of the Ministry
of Finance than by the present separate
Ordnance Survey Department under the
Ministry of Agriculture. The clause provides that the change shall take effect as
from a date to be fixed by Order in
Council.
Clause 2 provides that where a statute
requires the presentation of an annual
report to Parliament the provisions of
the law may be complied with by the
presentation of reports at intervals of not
longer than three years. I do not think
that hon. Members of this House will
find any difficulty in accepting this proposal, more particularly since it will be
found that sub-clause (3) provides that
reports or returns relating to public
receipts and expenditure are excluded
from the operation of the clause.
an
Clause 3, sub-clause
(1) makes
adaptation rendered necessary by the removal of certain Government departments
to Stormont, while sub-clause
remedies an omission in one of the
adaptation orders made pursuant to the
Government of Ireland Act.
(2)
Clause 4 provides that reference to
the Lord Lieutenant in Acts passed by
the Parliament of Northern Ireland
before the passing of the Irish Free State
(Consequential Provisions) Act, 1922,
shall, unless the context otherwise requires, be construed as reference to the
Governor of Northern
clause is designed to
Ireland. The
remove certain
Financial Provisions Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
doubts which have arisen on this point.
I beg to move, "That this Bill be now
read a Second time."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill read a Second time accordingly
and committed to a Committee of the
