Exchequer and Financial Provisions
(No. 2) Bill.
Moved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."—(Mr. Long).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Mr. LONG: This Bill is for the most
part designed to make certain changes in
the existing law, which are rendered
necessary as a consequence of proposals
made by the Minister of Finance in his
Budget Statement in respect of the current year.
In Clause 1 we have, in the first place, a
provision for the transfer to the Reserve
Fund from the Capital Fund of the
ascertained surplus of the latter fund
arising on the 1st March, 1933. It will
be remembered that the Capital Fund,
under the Exchequer and Financial Provisions (No. 2) Act of 1930, was earmarked for the service of a debt of three
million pounds, representing moneys lent
to the Unemployment Fund by the Exchequer prior to 1st October, 1926.
Under agreement with the Imperial
Treasury, the Unemployment Fund is to
be relieved of this debt by progressive
amounts of £100,000 per annum, beginning on 1st March, 1930, so that on the
1st March, 1959, the Exchequer will
have assumed liability for the complete
debt of three million pounds. It is estimated that the amounts at present
standing to credit of the Capital Fund
are more than sufficient to accumulate to
the sum of £3,000,000 on the required
date, and it is, therefore, proposed to
transfer the ascertained surplus, which
amounts to £450,000, to the Reserve
Fund.
Clause 1 of this Bill also provides for
payment out of the Reserve Fund of
sums not exceeding £150,000 in any
financial year, for the purpose of meeting
charges falling on the Exchequer in respect of loans guaranteed under the
Loans Guarantee Acts.
Finally, the Clause authorises a transfer from the Reserve Fund to the
Exchequer of the ultimate annual surplus of the year ended 31st March, 1932.
The amount involved is £30,000, and the
provision is necessary to enable this
amount, which is the final adjustment of
the Northern Ireland residuary share of
reserved taxation for the year in question, to be treated in the same manner
as interim adjustments were dealt with
pursuant to the Exchequer and Financial
Provisions Act passed earlier in this
Session.
Clause 2 of the Bill deals with the
Land Purchase Sinking Fund. Hon.
Members are no doubt aware that this
fund has been built up by allocations
from ordinary revenue, and the income
from the fund will, in due course, be
available to preserve in perpuity the
annual revenue represented by the existing land purchase annuities, which were
handed over to Northern Ireland under
the Government of Ireland Act, 1920.
In calculating the perpetual annual value
of these annuities, no account has
hitherto been taken of the charges falling
upon our Exchequer in respect of sales
under the Irish Land Acts of 1903 and
1909. These charges will terminate
along with the existing annuities, and it
is obviously reasonable to take this fact
into account in calculating allocations to
the Land Purchase Sinking Fund. As
a result, the fund discloses a surplus, and
sub-clause (2) of Clause 2 authorises the
transfer of an amount not exceeding
£100,000 of this surplus to the
Exchequer.
Clause 3 directs the transfer to the
Exchequer of the profits realised in the
investment of the Capital Fund No. 2.
This fund consists of the balance of the
sum of £400,000 paid by the Imperial
Exchequer to Northern Ireland in 1924
for the provision of certain public buildings. A further transfer to the
Exchequer is the subject of Clause 5, the
amount involved in this case being a
small balance accumulated in the Constabulary Reward Fund, which is not
now required for any of the purposes for
which that Fund exists.
Clauses 5 and 6 deal with comparatively minor matters. The former
charges the payment of the expenses
incurred in the issue of Government
securities on the Consolidated Fund
instead of on the Votes for the
Criminal Justice Bill-
Second Reading
17 OCTOBER 1933
year, whilst the latter transfers from
the Consolidated Fund to Votes the cost
of furniture provided for new or reconstructed police barracks.
Clause 7 proposes a change in the
method of financing the expenditure incurred under Section 10 of the Education
Act of 1930, in respect of grants for the
erection or improvement and equipment
of voluntary schools. Hitherto it has
been the practice to pay these grants from
revenue, but having regard to the present
heavy demands on the Exchequer, and
the capital nature of the work in aid of
which these grants are paid, the Government has decided to provide the necessary
finance for each grant by annual payments over a period not exceeding
twenty-five years. I beg to move, "That
this Bill be now read a Second time."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move "That this
Bill be now Committed."
On Question, Motion negatived.
Ordered: That the Bill be read the
Third time to-morrow.
Criminal Justice Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Long.)
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In moving,
"That this Bill be now read a Second
time." I should like to say that the
objects of this Bill are to amend certain
provisions of existing Statutes, and there
is in fact no close connection between the
subject matter of the four main clauses.
Clause 1 relates to the sending of youthful delinquents to the Borstal Institution.
and, shortly, the alteration which it
makes in the existing procedure is as
follows. At the present time when cases
against youthful delinquents are brought
before Courts of Summary Jurisdiction
the latter Courts can, if they come to the
conclusion that the cases are proved, and
that from the lads' previous character
Borstal training is the best thing for
them, send a recommendation to the next
Quarter Sessional Court that the Presiding Judge there should give Borstal
sentences, which, of course, is outside the
powers of a Summary Court.
Hon. Members will appreciate that it
often happens that a Court of Assize is
held before the Quarter Sessional Court,
and it is, of course, most desirable that
the earliest opportunity should be taken
of having the sentence pronounced.
Accordingly, under this Clause like jurisdiction is conferred on Courts of Assize,
and these cases may be dealt with by
Coneither Court as is most convenient.
sequential on this proposal it becomes
necessary to amend our Northern Ireland
Criminal Appeal Act of 1930 in order
that appeals may be permitted in the
of youths sentenced to Borstal
detention from a Court of Assize in just
the same way as an appeal lies at the
present time from such sentence passed
by a Court of Quarter Sessions.
case
The subject matter of Clause 2 was, I
may say, brought under notice by an hon.
and learned Member in this House. Its
purpose is to remove certain doubts which
exist at the present time in connection
with offences against children or young
persons, and similar action was taken in
Great Britain by the Criminal Justice
Act of 1925, to clear the matter up. A
doubt arose in the working of the
Children Act of 1908 as to whether in the
case of a child actually killed this
would come within the meaning of the
expression "Involving bodily injury."
If it did come within this definition then
any person charged with the offence was
competent but not compellable to give
evidence, and the wife or husband of such
person was similarly competent but not
compellable. If it did not, then the provision of the Act in this respect did not
apply.
This Clause declares that the expression
"Any other offence involving bodily injury to a child or young person" includes
the murder or manslaughter of a child,
or young person, and also infanticide,
and consequently makes it competent for
any person charged with the offence to
give evidence, and the wife or husband of
such person is similarly competent but
not compellable.
In turning to Clause 3, I am sure that
hon. Members will appreciate the temptation which must exist from time to
time for persons receiving pensions in
respect of service in the Civil Service,
or as teachers, to mortgage their pension
and thus imprudently lose the benefits
of having an assured future income. In
most cases when this mortgage has taken
place I have no doubt that the pensions
which are paid out of public funds have
helped to swell the profits of professional
money-lenders, an object for which such
pensions were certainly never designed.
Criminal Justice Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
If this clause is passed it will be illegal
for any person to receive, detain, or have
in his possession, any such pension document as a pledge or security for a debt,
and I feel sure that this step will meet
with the approval of every Member of
the House. A similar provision was
enacted in the Criminal Justice Act,
1925, which did not apply to Northern
Ireland, and the British authorities are,
of course, interested in our clause in so
far as it will stop pensions issued by a
British pension authority being pledged
in Northern Ireland.
In connection with Clause 4, I should
inform hon. Members that when applying for relief under the Poor Relief Acts
an obligation is placed on applicants to
give full information as to their circumstances to the Board of Guardians, so
that the latter may be in a position to
judge whether their circumstances are
such as to justify the grant of relief,
and further, applicants who obtain relief.
undertake to give immediate notice of
any change in their circumstances. I do
not suggest for a moment that the great
majority of applicants are not entirely
truthful and accurate in the information
which they give, but there have been a
number of cases in which grants of relief
have been obtained by persons who were
not entitled to receive them on false in-
Under
formation given to the Board.
the existing law these offenders are indictable and the offenders are tried at
Quarter Sessions or at Commissions. It
will be recalled that at a City Commission
held in Belfast earlier in this year the
Grand Jury passed a resolution suggesting that these cases might more properly
be heard by Courts of Summary Jurisdiction. The present procedure is
cumbersome and expensive. usually only
comparatively small amounts being involved, and it is evident that those cases
could be more properly and expeditiously
dealt with at petty sessions in a similar
way to cases coming under the National
Health Insurance and Unemployment
Acts, and this will result from the provisions of Clause 4.
I do not think that hon. Members will
find any difficulty in appreciating fully
and supporting the objects of the four
main Clauses of this Bill. I beg to
move.
Mr. LESLIE: I rise for the purpose
of supporting this Bill, and perhaps also
of making a few remarks respecting it.
The Bill is, as indeed the noble Viscount
admitted, a miscellaneous Bill directed to
effecting sundry improvements in
criminal law. It is, with the exception
of the fourth Clause, to which reference
has just been made, based on the English
Act of 1925, which I think the noble
Viscount quoted to us. The Act of 1925
was rather a long Act. It dealt with
very many subjects of a very
miscellaneous character, and it dealt very
extensively with matters relative to
Courts of Summary Jurisdiction.
looking at the Bill before us one misses
anything that has very special reference
to general summary jurisdiction procedure. I do not think that that should
be held up as an objection against the
Bill, because a Summary Jurisdiction
Procedure Bill should properly, one may
suggest, be confined to matters relating
to summary jurisdiction procedure.
In
But there are one or two other things that
one would rather like to have seen in the
Bill, and particularly I am a little surprised that the opportunity has not been
taken of incorporating in this Bill a section of the 1925 Act which deals with
the theoretical coercion of a wife by the
husband. That is a matter of general
criminal law, and I should have thought
the Government might have dealt with
it, had they wished, in the Bill before
I am not going to move any Amendment on that particular subject in Committee, because it is perhaps rather a
thorny one, but I do suggest it is a
matter that should receive consideration.
us.
There is another matter, although it
is perhaps more particularly concerned
with the summary jurisdiction I have
alluded to, and that is conferring power
on such a Court to impose a fine instead
of sending an offender to prison. That
has been asked for several times by representative magistrates in connection with
small larceny charges. I think that is
an improvement that ought to be made,
but it ought to wait for a general Summary Jurisdiction Bill rather than be
incorporated in the Bill before us.
Indeed I should not have mentioned the
subject at all but for the introduction
in another place of Clause 4 during some
of the later stages in the history of the
Bill.
Looking at the Clauses of the Bill
itself one begins naturally at the beginning. I believe that Clause 1, dealing
with improvements relating to sending
Criminal Justice Bill—
Second Reading
17 OCTOBER 1933
boys to Borstal, will be found of great
help to us, and I cannot pass from the
subject without paying my humble
tribute to the success which the Borstal
treatment appears to have been in
Northern Ireland. I think I am correct
in stating that about 80 per cent. of the
young fellows who pass through a course
of Borstal treatment turn out well. And
if we remember that those of our younger
citizens who go to Borstal are scarcely
good material to start with, I think 80
is a very high and very encouraging percentage, which reflects very great credit
on those immediately concerned in the
administration of the Borstal institution.
Coming to Clause 2, which I welcome
very cordially, I regard it as an act of
wisdom rather, perhaps, than an act of
necessity. One can only say about that,
that we have gone just a step further
than they went in England by providing
that there shall no longer be any doubt
as to the extension of the provisions of
the Criminal Evidence Act, in our case
1923 and in their case 1898. It does
leave it open to consider whether-preferably again not on a Bill of this class
but on a Bill relating to the law of
evidence the opportunity should not be
taken to clear up the somewhat anomalous
position which may arise in some of the
cases suggested here. An unfortunate
woman may be charged with infanticide
and convicted of another offence, a misdemeanour, and there are cases of persons charged with an offence to which
Section 4 applies, and the husband or
wife gives evidence and the defendant
is convicted of some other offence, in
regard to which, if it formed the original
charge, that evidence would not be
admissible.
There is the case of conviction of concealment of birth on a charge of
infanticide, and there is the much commoner case, that is, a charge of a certain
class of assault which very often results
in a verdict of common assault. The
evidence of the husband or wife is
admissible in the first case but not in the
second. I think something ought to be
done sometime or other, but again, this
is not the convenient opportunity.
Clause 3 is based on the Clause of the
English Act of 1925, but again, we go
further. We have introduced the
Teachers' Superannuation Fund, and I
do not think there is anything answering to that across the water, so there
again we are in advance of our friends.
Clause 4 has been recommended to the
House by the noble Viscount on the
recommendation of the Grand Jury of
the City of Belfast and of the Lord Chief
Justice himself. Perhaps I might
venture to suggest that the fact that the
recommendation was made by the Grand
Jury of Belfast and adopted by the
Government, as it will be later by Parliament, affords an instance of the great value
of the Grand Jury and the useful nature
of the services rendered by it to the community. But looking into the Clause
itself, which is based on a section of the
Unemployment Insurance Act of 1920, I
shall suggest at a later stage that we
introduce the possibility of inflicting a
fine instead of imposing a sentence of
imprisonment.
It is not the practice in these later
days to pass any new legislation which
provides for imprisonment and does not
give the court the alternative of imposing
a fine. Following that I shall also suggest that we ought to make the maximum
term of imprisonment six months instead
of three, and so keep in strict line with
the Insurance Acts, as now in force.
Moreover, if I am right that at the
moment there is no method of obtaining
a conviction for such offences except on a
charge of false pretences, which is a
serious matter involving a possible maxi,
mum of five years, it would seem that six
months is not too long a term. Accordingly I shall suggest that.
There is one more or less formal matter
which I shall also suggest, and that is
I think it might be found advisable to
insert what is commonly known as a
commencement clause. We are now in
the middle of October, and as quarter
sessions will start operations in November,
and the next City Commission is not far
off and there will be the Ulster Winter
Assizes, I think it will probably be a
little inconvenient to let this Bill, when
it becomes law, come into force, which
it would do, on the day it receives the
Royal Assent, and therefore, I will suggest in the Committee Stage that we put
in a few words of the usual character
suggesting that the Act should come into
operation on 1st January next.
On the whole, I think and believe this
Bill will be found of service to us, and
that we shall reap the benefit of it both
in economy and, perhaps, in the accleration of our criminal procedure.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

18 OCTOBER 1933
THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 18th October, 1933.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
PRIVATE BUSINESS.
Richards' Divorce Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a Second time."
Hunter's Divorce Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a Second time."
Cinnamond's Divorce Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a Second time."
Joint Standing Committee on Divorce
Bills.
Moved, "That Colonel Pakenham be
named of the Joint Standing Committee
on Divorce Bills."-(Mr. Long.)
Mr. QUIGLEY: Is it not a fact that
Colonel Pakenham was a member of the
Joint Standing Committee on Divorce
Bills?
Mr. LONG: Colonel Pakenham and
Mr. Hill Dickson ceased to be Senators
in June, but at the election they were
re-elected, and the idea underlying this
Motion is that they should complete the
period of service this session, and at the
beginning of the new session a new Committee will as usual be nominated.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Kitchen Committee.

Colonel Pakenham be named of the Joint
Kitchen and Refreshment Room Committee."-(Mr. Long.)
Question to Minister.
STRANMILLIS TRAINING COLLEGE
MANAGEMENT COMMITTEE.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether he can state
which of the members of the management
committee of Stranmillis Training College
have been appointed by the Ministry as
the nominated representatives of churches;
by whom these members were nominated;
the outlay on the building and equipment
of the College; how much of such outlay
was met from moneys provided by the
British Government; and the average
annual cost of maintenance of the College.
In asking this question, I wish to make
some observations with regard to the
transformation effected by the Government in the case of Stranmillis Training
College which is as remarkable a metamorphosis in a public institution as ever
has been effected by any Government. I
simply unfold the facts. I make no
comment on them. The facts will be
their own comment.
Under the pre-partition national school
system the Catholics and Episcopalians
of Ireland provided at their own expense
their own training colleges in Dublin,
the former at Drumcondra, and the latter
at Kildare Place. Grants were made by
the British Treasury in aid of these institutions. The Government of the Irish Free
State still pays a per capita grant for
Protestant students at Kildare Place
Training College. There was also a
training college in Marlborough Street,
Dublin, supported altogether by the
Treasury and attended mainly by Presbyterian students from the North of Ireland.
When partition came the Government
of Northern Ireland received a sum of
£350,000 as compensation for the severance of the North from the use of public
buildings situated in Dublin, and doubtless to help to provide corresponding
buildings here. The Government has
provided Stranmillis Training College, at
a cost of £186,000. It spends on the
maintenance of the College about £22,000
a year. It has provided no building for
Catholics which would do for them what
Drumcondra had done. Neither the Presbyterians nor the Episcopalians, nor the
Methodists have at any time built in
Northern Ireland a training college of
their own. The Catholics of the North of
Ireland had before Partition built and
maintained one college here for the training of Catholic female teachers-St.
351 Stranmillis Training College
Stranmillis Training College 352
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
Mary's, Belfast. Their co-religionists had
built and maintained one in London for
the training of English Catholic Male
teachers-St. Mary's, Middlesex.
In theory of law Stranmillis is a State
College built and maintained out of
Parliamentary funds for the training of
teachers for the public elementary schools
of Northern Ireland. These funds are
contributed to by Catholics and Protestants alike according to their taxable
capacities. These three training colleges
receive capitation grants in respect of
their students. Stranmillis was opened
to lecturers and students in September,
1929. In Stranmillis there was in the
year 1931-32, 215 King's Scholars in
training, in St. Mary's, Middlesex, 31,
in St. Mary's, Belfast, 77.
a
bers had been members of the Board
of Education of the Presbyterian
Church. The teaching staff was
100 per cent. Protestant staff. What with
the daily Protestant services and instruction with the 100 per cent. Protestant
Committee and with the 100 per cent.
Protestant staff, there was an unmistakably denominational ring about the place.
Stranmillis held out no invitation to
Catholics to enter there. Catholics however have paid and must go on paying for
Stranmillis. They pay for Stranmillis
without the privilege of going in. The
door-keeper at Artemus Ward's waxworks used to announce to the crowd
round his door: "You cannot well expect
to go in without paying but you may pay
without going in." Catholics must pay
without going in
These
at Stranmillis in
theory a public State institution, in
actual working in living fact, an
and-out denominational institution.
students must all alike comply with the
regulations of the Ministry as respects
syllabus, examinations, and educational
standard.
The students in Middlesex receive instruction in subjects prescribed by the
Ministry of Education, and must pass
examinations conducted by the English
Board of Education in subjects which
fulfil the Ministry's regulations for the
training of teachers. They must go to
Middlesex because your régime vetoes
their entry to Stranmillis. You have not
rrovided any facilities for them in
Northern Ireland. and you will not
recognise their being trained in the Free
State.
In England there are training colleges
built and maintained at public expense.
The education authorities over there do
not provide in those colleges religious or
catechetical instruction for the teachers
in training. The Government here employs and pays chaplains of the principal
Protestant Churches to give such instruction at Stranmillis. In 1931-32 there
were only ten Methodist students at
Stranmillis and a chaplain was provided
for them.
I make no complaint about
that. The Episcopalian, Presbyterian
and Methodist chaplains act as chaplains
in the place, give lectures on religious
subjects, and the students join in the
saying of pravers and in service at fixed
times during the day. The atmosphere
is denominational through and through.
The committee originally put in charge
of the College was exclusively Protestant.
It was a 100 per cent. Protestant committee. Five of the eight original mem-
A
Then that was not sufficient.
peculiar agitation arose. The Education
Committee of the Episcopalian, Presbyterian and Methodist Churches claimed
that these churches should as churches
have a direct and distinct representation
on the management committee of this
State-built, State-maintained, and Stateequipped college. I suppose to "make
assurance doubly sure" with regard to its
Protestantism. The Minister of Education promised they would have it.
Whereupon fresh trouble began for him.
The foundation committee, Protestant
laymen, all jibbed at the introduction of
this clerical element and said they would
resign rather than accept it.
The Minister then dissolved the committee and set up in its stead a small
departmental committee. He intimated
that on reconsideration he was not prepared to grant the distinctive Church
representation promised, and that he was
not likely to recede from this new
position. Now when the Prime Minister
says he is going to tackle a question-say,
town tenants' grievances I know the
question is going to be adjourned.
When the Minister of Education says
he is not going to recede from the stand
he takes on such a topic as this I cannot
for the life of me avoid the suspicion that
ere long he will make a graceful surrender. The first token of his yielding
came soon in an offer by him to set up
an advisory committee of representatives
of the three Protestant Churches to advise
him on
any question relating to the
353 Stranmillis Training College
Stranmillis Training College 354
18 OCTOBER 1933
religious instruction and spiritual welfare of the students. The Protestant
Clergy were not satisfied. Then the
Prime Minister came down into the arena
with a batch of concessions in his pocket.
On the same day on which the representatives of the Protestant Clergy met
him-the 31st July, 1931-he sent a communication to them in the name of his
Cabinet, stating that "prescribed Bible
instruction is daily given to 128,000 boys
and girls by our teachers"-that is to the
total Protestant children in the primary
schools of all kinds-saying how anxious
he was "to maintain good feeling and
unanimity in our Protestant ranks,
and
inviting the heads of these principal Protestant Churches to nominate one representative each to serve with four other
members in the Departmental Committee
of Management of Stranmillis when, in
the opinion of the convening memberthe Parliamentary Secretary of the
Ministry of Education-any question
would arise affecting the religious teaching
and spiritual life of the students, with the
right of entry to the college at all times
to attend religious services and the classes
of religious instruction.
The Protestant Primate named the representative of his Church. The heads of
the other Protestant churches made no
nomination. The agitation did not die
down. It pressed on. The Ministerial
resistance was futile and half-hearted.
Then came a complete Ministerial retreat
all along the line. The sham fight was
over. From the beginning to the end it.
was nothing but a sham fight.
Some
people do not agree with sham fights, and
other people, I know, admire sham fights.
But a sham fight is very unreal, because
always you know beforehand who is
going to win in the end. That sham
fight came to a close. Terms of unconditional surrender were arranged at a
conference on 23rd March, 1932, of which
the following official record was issued
the same day :-
"An agreement was unanimously
arrived at between the United Education
Committee of the Protestant Churches,
Sir Joseph Davison (representing the
Loyal Orange Institution), and the
Prime Minister, under which all outstanding questions relating to the
management of the college were amicably
settled on the following terms:-The
Government to increase the number of
the Committee of Management to nine,
and to give the three principal Protestant Churches the right to nominate
one representative each on the Committee of Management, the names of
the nominees to be submitted to the
Minister of Education through the
medium of the heads of the three
Churches for the time being.
"Such three representatives to have
full status, the same as the other members of the Committee of Management,
subject to the ultimate constitutional
responsibility of the Minister of Education to Parliament for all decisions
arrived at.
"Viscount Charlemont signified his
approval of the terms of the settlement
by telegram to the Prime Ministersigned on behalf of the United Educathe
tion Committee of
Protestant
Churches: Wm. Corkey, W. H.
Smyth, James Quinn; on behalf of the
Loyal Orange Institution: Joseph
Davison."
The pretence of Stranmillis being an
undenominational establishment was at
last over and gone. Protestant denominational education had won. The Prime
Minister could proclaim to the Orange
Triennial Council in July, 1932. in
Belfast, that not a shadow of difference
existed between the Government and the
Orange Society. On the 31st March the
Ulster Teachers' Union Congress passed
a resolution in these terms:
"That we view with alarm, and we
direct the attention of the public to
the abject surrender of the Government
in acceding to the reactionary demands
of the Joint Committee of the Protestant Churches for special representation of the Churches as such on the
Committee of Management of Stranmillis Training College; and it is incomprehensible that such a a demand
should have the support of a democratic body like the Orange Order. It
is incredible that an institution built
and maintained out of public funds,
and preparing students for public service, should be handed over to the
management of a committee, a large
section of whose members will not be
responsible to any public authority."
One speaker to this resolution said the
Government had not the backbone of a
jellyfish-not a high compliment to the
jellyfish.
On the previous day the "Evening
Telegraph" described the settlement as a
355 Stranmillis Training College
THE SENATE
Stranmillis Training College 356
[Mr. Campbell]
sacrifice of principles of the highest importance.
On 9th April, at the Belfast Branch
of the Irish National Teachers' Organisation, the following resolution
unanimously carried:-
was
"That as the Stranmillis Training
College is a State institution, built and
maintained out of public funds, we
condemn the action of the Northern
Government in admitting the principle
of sectarian control by the appointment of representatives of Protestant
Churches as such on its Committee of
Management."
last
My
quotation is from the
"Evening Telegraph" of 29th April,
1932:
"As Professor Johnstone pointed
out sectarian education, which everyone wanted to see abolished, has been
re-established. The tragedy of it is
that to that deplorable result the
Orange Order has contributed so much,
contrary to the very principles upon
which it is based."
The storm of agitation has been succeeded by the calm of content. As Castor
and Pollux in the ancient pagan mythology guided into port mariners straining
to reach land, so Lords Craigavon and
Charlemont guided to their haven this
heterogeneous combination. I do not
think I have mis-stated a single fact
regarding this matter. It is a question
I would have brought forward sooner had
I been lately in this House, because it is a
matter of highest public importance. It
should at least be placed on record in the
Debates of this Chamber what the trend
of events has been. It will be useful
hereafter if not to-day. The broad result is that the United Education Committee is satisfied. A State-built,
State-equipped, State-maintained college
has been made a citadel of denominational religious activity and teaching.
The Protestant clergy and Protestant
students have been entrenched there in
a position of advantage and privilege.
The students have the backing of the
State to the last penny needed for their
upbringing. Yet the Government of Ireland Act, 1920, under which the Government functions, prohibits
prohibits giving a
preference to any religious denomination.
I read with interest the speeches the
noble Lord, the Minister of Education,
delivers from time to time when he
favours the public with his views, and I
remember a speech of his at Castlecaulfield, where he was speaking on 13th
July, 1931. In it he said that the Protestant churches here were more closely
identified with the education system here
than in any other country. The recent
history of Stranmillis emphasises the
closeness of this identification.
The State training college, like the
public elementary schools, is denominational through and through. To argue
that either the college or the schools are
undenominational would be to bolster up
a sham. In this College-built out of
the British Government's
of
gift
£350,000 for all denominations as in
these schools, a system of preference and
special privileges in favour of favoured
denominations has been stereotyped in
contrast to the scientific discrimination
concentrated against another denomination.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think I
had better confine myself to some extent
to answering the various questions raised
by the hon. and learned Member in the
question he has on the Paper rather than
go into any past history. The motto for
a Minister of Education, the ideal motto,
is laid down in the Bible, where it says:
"Let your communications be yea, yea,
and nay, nay.
Whatsoever is more than
these cometh of evil." It would probably make my speeches very dull if I
stuck to that, but on the whole I think
it would be safer.
The names of the members of the
Stranmillis Training College Committee,
to whom the hon. and learned Member
refers, and the persons by whom they
were nominated, are as follows:-The
Right Rev. William Corkey, M.A., D.D.,
representing the Presbyterian Church
and nominated through the medium of
the Moderator of the General Assembly;
the Very Rev. W. S. Kerr, B.D., representing the Church of Ireland and nominated through the medium. of the
Primate; the Rev. W. H. Smyth, M.A.,
representing the Methodist Church and
nominated through the medium of the
President of the Methodist Church.
Sums amounting in all to £186,264
have been expended on the buildings and
equipment of the College out of the grant
of £400,000 made by the Imperial
Government for this and other purposes.
357 Stranmillis Training College
Stranmillis Training College 358
18 OCTOBER 1933
The average annual cost of maintaining
the buildings and equipment in proper
condition (which is met from the ordinary
revenues of the College) amounts
£1,130 per annum over the seven years
ended August, 1932
Mr. CAMPBELL: Only £1,130?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Yes.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Per annum?
to
Viscount
Yes.
CHARLEMONT :
That is the average amount for seven
years. The hon. and learned Senator
made one other mistake in his speech.
The Government does not pay the
chaplains. They are paid out of the
ordinary revenue of the College, the
capitation grant, which is equally enjoyed
by St. Mary's, Belfast, as well as St.
Mary's, Middlesex. With regard to the
references the hon. and learned Senator
made to Stranmillis Training College
being purely denominational, I would
like for a moment to take the matter
out of the religious atmosphere and put
it on an analogy of ordinary everyday
life. If the hon. and learned Senator
was going along the platform of the
Great Victoria Street Station to get a
train and he saw a railway carriage door
open and the Archbishop of Canterbury
and myself sitting inside, would he be
justified in saying that the Great
Northern Railway Company ran only
Protestant trains? That is exactly the
position in regard to Stranmillis. It is
at the present time de facto Protestant,
but certainly not de jure, and I have
neither the will nor the power to refuse
admission to any Catholics who pass the
examination.
I would like also to say that the cost
of building St. Mary's Training College,
Belfast, for women Roman Catholic
teachers, has, in effect, been defrayed by
the Government. If the hon. and learned
Member will look at the Estimates for
the current year he will see that they
include a terminable annuity, 1911-36,
payable to St. Mary's College, Belfast,
to cover the original capital cost of the
College, £1,000 a year, that is, of the
Imperial Government. None the less,
St. Mary's has been paid for by moneys
provided by the taxpayers, Protestant as
well as Roman Catholic, and it stands in
exactly the same position, both de jure
and de facto, as Stranmillis may be said
to do de facto. The State pays a sum
for the students at St. Mary's exactly
the same as it pays for students at Stranmillis. The cost of maintaining Stranmillis is met out of the ordinary college
revenue, as in the case of St. Mary's.
Finally, the Roman Catholic Church has
the entire management of St. Mary's,
whereas, in the case of Stranmillis, the
Protestants have only representation-
Mr. CAMPBELL : Are all the
colleges not open equally to teachers of
all denominations?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I do not
think St. Mary's is. I am almost certain
it is not. Stranmillis is open to anybody who takes the examination, subject,
of course, to there being room in the
College to receive them. As I said, I
cannot emphasise sufficiently that I have
neither the will nor the power to keep out
Roman Catholics who, at any time, may
present themselves.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I would like to
say how disappointed I am with the
speech of the noble Lord, the Leader of
the House. After all, he has evaded the
real issues that have been raised by my
Colleague, Senator Campbell. He fails
to explain how it is that £186,264 should
be allocated to a particular college which
had to be run according to the lines
dictated by the three Protestant denominations of Northern Ireland, in
addition to the Orange Association, as
submitted in the case. Is it not shirking the issue to suggest that a college run
on lines dictated by these bodies is open
to Catholic students. Surely that
answer is not worthy of the noble Lordto say that Catholic students are entitled
to be admitted to the College on the
terms dictated as I have said.
It does not exactly state the actual
position to say that St. Mary's College
is on the same status. St. Mary's College
was built and erected practically at the
cost of the Catholic community, whereas
not only has Stranmillis been erected out
of the public purse, but as my Friend,
Senator Campbell, has stated we have
been compelled to contribute to the
erection of Stranmillis. To say that this
is an undenominational college which we
have been compelled to contribute to is,
I think, a very unfair representation of
the true facts. It was not sufficiently
denominational as originally fashioned
out by the Government, but as has been
359 Stranmillis Training College
Stranmillis Training College 360
THE SENATE
[Mr. McAllister]
stated the Protestant denominations
dictated certain terms which they believed were necessary to preserve what
they considered the religious status of
their students.
Now, I am sure that I am expressing
the views of my Colleague and myself
about it when I say we have no objection
to the proper education according to their
own wishes and their own views of Protestant students out of the public purse,
provided the Catholic students are also
maintained in equal status. I think it
is very desirable that students of all denominations should be properly trained
according to the dictates of their own
consciences. I think that is
a very
admirable idea. But we can only support that theory on the basis that
Catholic students are given the same
rights. That is the case which we are
making to-day. It is not that we object
to any method of training those students.
which the Protestant churches deem desirable for the purpose of training the
students of the future.
But I would like really to know how
that sum of £1,130 to which the noble
Leader of the House has referred is made
up.
We have been unable on these
benches to locate what that £1,130 is
composed of. And I would like the noble
Lord to inform the House how that substantial sum is made up. My Colleague
has covered most of the ground fully,
and I do not intend to repeat his arguments, but merely to endorse everything
he has said, and to express disappointment that the Government have more or
less ignored the vital points raised by
him. I think when you get a community
for whom my Colleague has been speaking, representing over one-third of the
population of Northern Ireland, and
making the case for the education of the
youth of one-third of the population, I
am sure the noble Lord on further consideration will give some more satisfactory
reply to the speech which has been made
by my Friend, Senator Campbell. It is
an extraordinary statement-I think the
figures were over £1,000,000 that has
been spent by the Government upon
transferred schools. Analysed that
means that over £1,000,000 has been
spent
Mr. SPEAKER: I think the hon.
Member is going outside the scope of the
question.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am
sorry that my original reply to the
question was not considered full. I
thought I had made it as plain as I
could. With regard to the last speaker's
point about the cost of building St.
Mary's not being defrayed by the Government, but originally by the Roman
Catholic community, is it not a fact that
in 1911 the Government-the Imperial
Government-undertook to
pay St.
Mary's this annuity of £1,000 for 25
years to recoup the Church for the
capital cost of building that college. I
do not see how that can possibly square
with the last speaker's statement.
Roman Catholic community provided the
cost of the college, but it has been entirely defrayed by the Government.
The
With regard to the part played by the
Protestant churches in the management
of Stranmillis you cannot say that the
nomination of three members of Protestant churches on the committee that
runs Stranmillis can possibly dictate the
whole course of the college. The curriculum is arranged by the Ministry of
Education, and is the same for all three
colleges. The only matter in which the
religious element can possibly come in is
the services of the chaplains and with
regard to religious education in the college. It is a minor part three representatives can play on the Committee as
a whole. I do not think there is anything whatever in the religious life of
Stranmillis that can possibly prevent
Roman Catholics making use of its
privileges.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: May
I just ask the Leader of the House is it
not a fact that the hon. Gentleman's
representatives are responsible for this
College being denominational. Is not
Stranmillis College open to all creeds,
the Roman Catholics as well as Presbyterians, Episcopalians, and Methodists?
I fail to see where the complaint is. I
understand that when the Bill was introduced it was made perfectly clear it was
not sectarian. The education system was
to be run on lines similar to Scotland,
where Roman Catholics would have the
same opportunity as was enjoyed by the
Protestant people, and if it is denominational it is only their fault and no other.
I do feel they are under a misapprehension when they complain about not
being fairly treated. Good gracious, in
the last amending Bill the Premier agreed
361 Administrative Provisions Bill—

18 OCTOBER 1933
Committee
to pay 50 per cent. of the cost of new
schools. Who was to get the benefit of
that 50 per cent.-only Roman Catholics?
I think it is unfair to complain that the
education system is not run on fair lines.
Mr. LESLIE: Still further, arising
out of that, may we take it that there is
nothing in the nature of a religious test
imposed in Stranmillis which could
operate to exclude any student who is
competent to pass the usual examination? Is it not true that there is no
obstacle to prevent a Jew, Turk, infidel,
or heretic from entering Stranmillis,
except what is imposed by their own
religious views? That seems to be the
short point.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I would
like to emphasise the fact that there is no
religious test of any kind. It is a State
college built by State money, and is open
to everybody who passes the King's
Scholarship examination.
There is one point I would like to refer
to in reply to Mr. McAllister, which I
regret I did not make sufficiently plain.
I said in my first speech that the average
annual cost of maintaining the building.
and equipment in proper condition, which
is met out of the ordinary revenue of
the College was £1,150 a year. That, of
course, is not the complete college expenditure covering food, lectures, and
salaries, which could hardly be done for
that even in Ulster. The total figures
are approximately as stated. I am sorry
I did not make that sufficiently plain.
Mr. CAMPBELL: May I ask the
noble Lord is there on the committee of
management or the staff of Stranmillis
one single Catholic representative?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: No, because the representatives are appointed in
proportion to the religious denominations
of the students.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I said staff also.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I would
like to have notice of that.
Administrative Provisions Bill.
Order read for the House to be put
into Committee.

Resolved, That this House do now
resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Mr. Long.)
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSE I (Transfer of Ordance Survey from Ministry of Agriculture to
Ministry of Finance).
Question proposed, "That the Clause.
stand part of the Bill."
Mr. LESLIE: As regards Clause 1, I
do not for a moment intend to oppose it.
I do not see that there was ever any
peculiar merit in assigning the work in
connection with Ordnance Survey to the
Ministry of Agriculture. Ordnance Survery is not confined to the country. I
do not in the least object to the transfer,
but I must say I am beginning to have
some little misgiving at the increasing
activities of the Ministry of Finance and
the increasing demands made upon them
of supervision over what I may call very
miscellaneous items in our scheme of
Government. I think, perhaps, some
day we shall have to go back to the older
system of having, perhaps, even one other
Minister of Public Works, something as
they had in Dublin before 1921. I think
we ought to bear that in mind whenever
these proposals come before us-the
chance that some day we may be overloading the Ministry of Finance and
drawing them away from their Treasury
functions, which, after all, are their
main duties.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.

matters), 4 (Adaptation of references to
the Lord Lieutenant in Acts of Northern
Ireland Parliament), and 5 (Short title),
ordered to stand part of the Bill.

Expiring Laws Continuance Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LONG: I do not propose to take
up the time of the House by making any
lengthy speech in moving the Second
Reading of this Bill. It is in its usual
form, with which hon. Members will be
familiar, and its purpose is to extend,
for one year, to the 31st December, 1934,
the operation of those Acts which are set
out in the Schedule, and which would,
in default of the passing of this Measure,
lapse on the 31st December next.
I beg
to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for to-morrow.
Constabulary Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This is a
short Bill extending the duration of certain Sections of the Constabulary Act
(Northern Ireland), 1928. In that Act
the "free quota" system, under which
local authorities were liable for portion
of the cost of extra police was abolished,
but a payment of £25,000 per annum
was required from the Belfast Corporation for a period of seven years, and
thereafter of such amount as might be
determined by Parliament. Under the
"free quota" system the Belfast Corporation would have been liable for a
contribution of approximately £40,000
per annum. The seven-year period expired on the 31st March last, and the
first Clause of the Bill provides for the
continuance of the payment of £25,000
by the Belfast Corporation for the current financial year. The question of payments after 31st March next does not
arise on the present occasion-that is a
matter which must be left for future consideration.
The second Clause of the Bill continues
until May, 1934, the power of the
Ministry of Home Affairs to acquire land
compusorily for the use or accommodation of the Royal Ulster Constabulary.
When the operation of the Constabulary
(Acquisition of Land) Act, 1924, was
extended until May, 1934, it was considered that by that date the necessity
for this power would have ceased.
was, however, found necessary,
financial grounds, to retard the progress
of the scheme for providing improved
police accommodation, with the result
that it will be some time before the
necessary works have been carried out.
It
on
The power of compulsory acquisition
is only employed in exceptional circumstances, but it is desirable that it should
be available until the scheme has been
completed. I beg to move, "That this
Bill be now read a Second time."
Mr. McMAHON: I would like, in my
first words in this House, to take exception to the action of the Government in
this Bill in imposing a charge of £25,000
a year on the ratepayers of Belfast. The
ratepayers of Belfast have had to carry
a very severe burden for the past seven
years owing to an agreement that was
forced on them by the Government in
connection with the cost of the Royal
Ulster Constabulary stationed in the
city. To compel them to pay this Bill
for another year is, to my mind, placing
a burden on the ratepayers of Belfast
which they are not able to bear. If this
expenditure was fairly distributed over
every local authority in Northern Ireland
it would not be so hard on the Belfast
Corporation, but I understand that no
other local authority in the Six Counties
is required under this Bill to pay any
portion of this sum. Belfast seems to be
the dumping ground for the extra police
of the Six Counties. Why that should
be so I do not know, because prior to the
passing of the Act of 1920 Belfast was
not the dumping ground, and every local
authority in Northern Ireland had to
pay their fair proportion of the cost of
the Royal Irish Constabulary.
The idea seems to prevail amongst the
Members of the Government that the
ratepayers of Belfast are able to bear any
burden. Within the past three or four
weeks the Government have informed the
Belfast Corporation that they are not in
a position to give grants in aid of relief
Constabulary Bill-
Second Reading
18 OCTOBER 1933
schemes, and that in future the cost of
these schemes will have to be borne by
the ratepayers. I understand also, that
within the past few days the Government
have notified the Corporation that they
cannot give a grant for the erection of
the new baths at Peter's Hill, although
the Government profess to be anxious to
promote relief schemes for the unemployed. The cost of all these things will
throw a very heavy burden on the ratepayers of the city and, having regard to
this fact, I think the city might very well
be relieved of this charge for extra police.
If this bill has to be paid this year I
trust the noble Leader of the House will
make representations to the Government
that there should be no similar demand
after this year.
I have been told there is a possibility
that this charge for police may be increased in the future to the extent of
a sixpenny rate in Belfast. If that is
so it will place the ratepayers of Belfast
in a difficult position next year, when
the re-valuation scheme will also take
effect, and that will hit the taxpayer as
well as the ratepayer.
Therefore, I
would appeal to the noble Leader of the
House to make representations to the
Government not to place any further
burdens on the ratepayers, because they
are carrying a very heavy load at the
present time.
Mr. LESLIE: I am not at all certain
that I ought not to join with the hon.
Member who has just sat down-and
whom, if I may, I should like to congratulate in having lost so little time
in making his views heard in our
counsels-in objecting to Clause 1 of the
Bill before us, because that Clause does
not exactly correspond with a certain
recommendation to which not very long
ago I set my hand as representing a
Committee who had had to consider,
amongst other things, the amount which
might justifiably be asked from the City
of Belfast towards the expenses of the
Royal Ulster Constabulary. However,
the full recommendations which that
Committee made have not yet been dealt
with by the Government. and I do not
see any objection to prolonging the period
for one year and carrying on at the old
rate; that is, asking the Corporation of
Belfast to find once more the sum of
£25,000. That £25,000 represents
approximately a rate of 3d in Belfast.
Belfast claims with justice to be one of
the great cities of the United Kingdom.
it is not in
with that
claim that there should be this tremendous outburst of anger at the request
to pay such a small rate as 34d towards
the cost of their police, the police that
protect them.
How does that rate of 3d compare
with what is borne elsewhere throughout
the United Kingdom. I believe that the
average police rate in large towns in
England-I am thinking of such towns
as Bristol, Leeds, and Sheffield for instance is 1s in the £. The rates of
Belfast are not unduly high when you
consider the size and amenities of the
city. I think I am right in saying that
the present rates in Belfast are about
12 5d in the £, or, at any rate, between
12s and 13s. On the other hand, the
rates in the City of Derry are 19s 6d,
and if it should happen that the recommendations of the Committee to which I
have referred ever become effective Derry
will be asked to shoulder a rate of 3d
in the £ as opposed to the 6d in the £
to which Senator McMahon alluded as
being the amount foreshadowed by the
Committee to be borne by Belfast.
I think it must be accepted that the
urban districts in Northern Ireland cannot continue to escape responsibility for
some small portion of the cost of the
Constabulary. I did not know when I
came here that this particular question
was to be raised here this afternoon, and
therefore I am not able to speak with
absolute accuracy as regards the figures
of the cost of the Royal Ulster Constabulary. But I am going to suggest
that the cost of the Constabulary is
approximately £750,000 a year, and that
takes no account of the sum necessary to
meet pensions or share of pensions due
to former officers and constables of the
Royal Irish Constabulary. If I am
right in giving £750,000 as the approximate cost, I may say also that the imposition of 6d on the rates of Belfast,
and 3d on urban district councils, including the City of Derry-if I may for
the moment class it as an urban district
council-would give us a sum of only
£50,000 a year. That Committee did
not propose to impose any rate on the
rural areas, on the ground that the
amount produced, assuming a
rate, would be something like only £2,000
or £2,500 at the outside, and the cost
of collection and other inconveniences
really put that matter out of court.
penny
Constabulary Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
The whole proposal was to raise from
the rates something like £50,000 per
annum to meet an expenditure of
£750,000. Contrast that with the
position across the water. It is normal
throughout England that the cost of the
police is borne, half by local rates and
half by the Government.
Mr. McMAHON: But in England the
local authority controls the police, and
we do not.
Mr. LESLIE: That point cannot
escape attention. It is a fact that, speaking generally, English counties have
their own police force the Metropolitan
Police is a separate force-and English
counties control their police. That is a
system which has endured for some time
in England, but it is giving more and
more dissatisfaction. It is becoming
increasingly evident that this county and
that county, acting separately, cannot
deal with organised crime. The motorcar and wireless has given the criminal
every advantage against the county constabulary. That is not a system to be
commended, and it is not the system we
have adopted in Northern Ireland. Here,
more than ten years ago, we adopted, and
rightly adopted in my judgment, the
centralised force.. It came to us with the
quota system to which the noble Viscount
alluded. Under that quota system
Belfast had 900 police alloted to it.
When we took over we increased the free
quota to 998, an increase of something
more than 10 per cent., and that continued until, I think, 1924, when the
free quota system was abolished and this
charge of £25,000 a year was imposed.
To deal with, what was said about the
control over the constabulary exercised
by the counties in England, I would ask
the House to remember that this is a
matter of proportion. If we were asking
Belfast to bear half the cost of the constabulary, if we were asking the county
councils to bear half the cost, their claim
to control of the police would be, perhaps, deserving of consideration. But at
the present time to put forward the claim
for control, when the contribution is so
meagre, is, I think, really scarcely
worthy of submission to a serious-minded
assembly. He knows himself that a rate
of 3d in the pound is not sufficient to
give any claim whatever to controlling
the force, which is necessarily a mobile
force, which has to operate, not only in
Belfast, but throughout the Six Counties.
I do not think any case whatever can
be made against the £25,000 which this
Bill assigns to it, or against the permanent rate which, perhaps I may not
speak about that. I have no authority
to say the recommendation will be
adopted. But if the recommendation is
adopted I venture to say a 6d rate in
Belfast is not an unfair burden, and
indeed Belfast will continue to be treated
with great and unjustifiable generosity.
So much for Clause 1.
Clause 2 deals with the simple matter
of extending, for a further period, the
power of compulsory acquisition of land,
which was first introduced in 1924 for a
five-years' period and was subsequently
extended in 1928 for a further four or
five years. The proposal now is to
extend the period for ten years. I think
that is too long. The power, the noble
Viscount told us, had only been exercised
in extreme cases, but I gather it has
been exercised once or twice. When the
former Parliamentary Secretary was
speaking-in this Chamber but not in
this House-he stated, in response to a
remark of mine, that the power at that
time had never been exercised at all. He
gave me the further assurance that,
although the power was there already it
was intended to confine it to barracks,
excluding rifle ranges and manoeuvring
grounds and so on. However, there is
that power to take land compulsorily, and
I think ten years is too long. I suggest
we should reduce it to six. I should say
five but, owing to the time of the year
and having regard to the life of this
Parliament and the probable duration of
the next one, I think six years is suitable.
I do not think, as the House knows,
these strong powers should be given for
too long a time. Parliament should never
relax control. Therefore, in this case,
just to mark the fact that we have
noticed it, I shall suggest reducing the
further period of ten years to six. On
the whole, I certainly support the Bill.
As regards the financial question, I think
it is a matter of necessity. As regards
the second Clause, it is a matter of
finance, and indeed my suggestion of
shortening the term may prove to be
futile, because it may be there is a
scheme already on the stocks which of
necessity may require a longer period.
Constabulary Bill-
Adjournment,
18 OCTOBER 1933
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Replying
on the first Clause of the Bill I should
like to say that Belfast is the capital
city and that it receives a great many
advantages from that fact. A great proportion of the business of the country is
done there. It has the staff of the
Government offices, and outside all that,
when you have a large mass of people
confined in a comparativly small area,
especially in the circumstances existing at
the present moment, I do not think,
taking these two facts together, you can
say that the force of police employed in
Belfast at the present moment is excessive
with the other duties they have to perform. Malicious injuries are a terribly
expensive matter to the ratepayers, and
I do not think that the contribution by
the Corporation of 9 per cent. towards
the cost of the police can be regarded as
an unduly heavy premium on a most
effective form of insurance, that is, the
magnificent police force. With regard
to the second Clause of the Bill, I should
like to say, in reply to the hon. Senator's
remarks, that the power of compulsory
acquisition has been used three times. I
will consider his other points at a later
stage.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for to-morrow.

Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am not
going to proceed with this Bill this
afternoon.

Bill, to which they desire the
currence of this House.
