Repairs to Schools
Repairs to Schools
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 19th October, 1933.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
DIVORCE BILLS.
The following Reports were made from
the Joint Standing Committee on Divorce
Bills:
Hunter's Divorce Bill.
That they had considered the said Bill,
and that the Averments in the Preamble
had been proved to their satisfaction,
and that they had gone through the said
Bill and made Amendments thereto.
Richards' Divorce Bill.
That they had considered the said Bill,
and that the Averments in the Preamble
had been proved to their satisfaction,
and that they had gone through the said
Bill and made an Amendment thereto.
Cinnamond's Divorce Bill.
That they had considered the said Bill,
and that the Averments in the Preamble
had been proved to their satisfaction,
and that they had gone through the said
Bill and made an Amendment thereto.
Questions to Ministers.
REPAIRS TO VOLUNTARY
SCHOOLS.
Mr. SPEAKER: Before calling on the
hon. and learned Member to put the
question standing in his name I should
like to point out that if any Member
wishes to speak on the question he should
do so immediately after the question is
put and before the reply is given. Otherwise it would mean that the Minister
replying on behalf of the Government
might have to make two speeches on the
same subject, which is undesirable and
is really out of order.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether it is a fact
that many voluntary elementary schools
are in urgent. need of repairs, and are
unable to effect such repairs without the
assistance of grants. In putting the question I wish to direct the attention of the
House to a very remarkable development
in the elementary school system in Northern Ireland. During the past two
years there has been a flowng tide of
transfer of voluntary elementary schools
belonging to certain bodies, and a steady
expansion of education privileges and
preferences in their favour.
In December, 1932, there were under
control of borough and regional committees 79 provided schools and 474 transferred schools, enrolling 85,961 Protestant pupils out of a total in all schools
of about 132,000 Protestant children.
Twenty-five schools have been transferred
since then. The broad result is that two
out of every three Protestant children are
now taught in schools built and maintained
or at least maintained entirely at the public
charge. There were 78 Four-and-Two
Committee schools, about 4 per cent. of
the total elementary schools of the area.
The Four-and-Two Committee type of
school is a negligible factor. It is a
sickly exotic of stunted growth. It has
found here an uncongenial soil, and it
really hardly counts. The total number
of pupils in 700 Catholic voluntary schools
at the same time was about 74,000, almost
9,000 above the Presbyterian total, and
20,000 above the Episcopalian total.
These schools cannot get one penny of the
public money for urgent repairs, besides
labouring under other galling disabilities.
£800,000 of public money has been cr
is being spent on the building of the provided schools. £227,000 has been spent
on the re-conditioning of transferred
schools. That totals over £1,000,000.
The Ministry have contributed £53,000
towards the erection of 13 Catholic
voluntary schools, and the managers have
contributed another £53,000. The Ministry have contributed £6,000 towards the
alteration of the improvement of five
voluntary schools, and the managers
another £6,000. That totals only £59,000
of public money. For every £1 of public
money spent on the one set of schools 1/-
is spent on the other.
One-third of the Northern school
accommodation of ten years ago has been
either superseded by new schools in the
shape of provided schools or modernised
in the shape of transferred schools. These
provided and transferred schools are the
19 OCTOBER 1933
Repairs to Schools
Repairs to Schools

of the little ones and to increase the
efficiency of the schools, and that such
repairs were urgent in many schools.
schools of the majority. They are not for
the minority and are not intended for the
minority. They have been built and
maintained or transferred and maintained
to the last penny at the public charge.
Fortunate denominational schools of
the majority! Bearing the euphemistic
titles of provided and transferred schools
--they are root and branch denominational in their pupils, their teachers, their
management committees, their education
committees, and their Bible instruction.
In every one of these schools Bible instruction is guaranteed.
The
It is only a question of time before the
500 schools still under Protestant
voluntary management are transferred.
Every year they are in process of transfer,
and the signs are that they will all
ultimately be transferred to friendly
borough and regional committees.
Catholic membership of these committees.
is perhaps 5 per cent. of the total
membership. When this result consciously.
and definitely aimed at by the Government is achieved, a large majority of the
700 Catholic voluntary schools will, as
regards buildings, be left in solitary
isolation-the Cinderella of Northern
education. It is a very obvious result
that the state of sanitation, repair and
equipment of many voluntary schools.
must become as a general rule markedly
inferior to that of the provided and transferred schools. You dip your hands deep
into the public purse for one set of
schools. You close the public pursestrings against the other. You then
taunt your opponents because their buildings have deteriorated.
Fair-minded men must be ashamed to
think how pitiable a portion of public
money falls under the Education Acts to
Catholics for building
building though the
Catholic children in voluntary schools
number more than a third of the total
number of children in all the schools. In
Scotland the voluntary schools contain
only one-seventh of the school population
but are treated with fairness. The
settlement under the Scottish Education
Act of 1918 is working well because it is
being worked without bias. In the Irish
Free State the elementary schools of all
denominations get even-handed justice
even the sparse Protestant schools in the
country districts.
The Government were well aware that
money given for the repairs of these
schools would go entirely to the benefit
The Government has, Pharoah-like,
hardened their hearts against this concession. In three respects the Education
Act of 1930 put the provided schools
on a denominational and Protestant basis
and secured the denominational and Protestant character of transferred schools.
First of all it became the duty of the
Minister of Education to nominate a
number of representatives of transferors
upon borough and regional education
committees. Secondly, that Act amends
the constitution and functions of
Management Committees of provided and
transferred schools so as to give onehalf the members to representatives of
former managers and transferors. These
Committees have the initiative in the
selection of teachers. Last of all the
Act did that by making compulsory
the imparting of Bible instruction in any
provided or transferred school, on the
requisition of the parents of ten schoolchildren attending such school, and by
compelling the teachers to give this type
of instruction if so required by the
Education Committee whose members are
almost all of one way of thinking. Such
instruction is given regularly in all provided and transferred schools. It is sectarian teaching through and through and
is compulsory.
The Act of 1930 made the path broad
and easy for the transfer of Protestant
schools but made the path practically
impassable for the transfer of Catholic
schools.
Such is the way in which the Government shows its fixed determination that
the children of the two competing sets
of schools in its area shall not start equal
in the race. Apart from the salary of
teachers the existing voluntary schools
are carried on free of all charge to the
public save one-half of the petty cash
expenditure on lighting, heating and
cleaning, while the provided and transferred schools can draw ad lib on the
public purse for every penny needed for
lighting, heating and cleaning, for current repairs, and for whatever improvement and re-conditioning are required
to bring and keep them up to date. The
provided schools are built from the ridge
to the ground at the common public
charge. Voluntary schools have been
built entirely by
by the sacrifices of
Repairs to Schools
Repairs to Schools
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
managers and generous benefactors, and
by grants under the British regime. A
handful of new Catholic voluntary
schools have been granted one-half of
the building outlay. A handful of old
Catholic voluntary schools have got from
the public funds one-half of the outlay
requisite for long overdue alterations.
A gushing stream of public money flows
for the one type of school, but a thin
and feeble trickle for the other.
Against the voluntary schools the
specious point is made that they are
not under local public control-hostile
control-and so must suffer. In truth
and fact they are under the strictest
public control. The real effective control over the schools is exercised by the
Ministry of Education. The Ministry's
tests must be met, its regulations obeyed,
its inspections satisfied, and its curriculum conformed to by all schools alike.
The borough and regional committees are
as conduit pipes through which pass the
public money for building, equipment,
and repairs. If these committees were
to disappear to-morrow public control in
all its plenitude would remain vested in
the Ministry. But that is not the real
reason why for every £1 of public money
spent on the building, reconditioning or
repair of provided and transferred schools
1/ is spent on the building and reconditioning of the Catholic voluntary
schools, and nothing at all on the repairs.
Mr. McMAHON: I think the House
will agree that Senator Campbell has made
a very strong case for better treatment of
the Catholic schools in Northern Ireland.
The burden that the Catholic people have
had to bear since the passing of the 1922
Act has been very great. Up to 1930 the
Government gave no grant whatever for
the building of Catholic schools, and it
was only the efforts of an hon. Member in
another House, the hon. Member for
Central Belfast (Mr. Devlin) that extracted from an unwilling Government a
contribution of 50 per cent. of the cost of
building Catholic schools. Even that is
very unfair when one considers that the
Catholic people, who are not the richest
people in this part of Northern Ireland,
have got to find 50 per cent. of the cost
of building their schools, and, at the same
time, pay rates and taxes towards the
building of Protestant schools.
The time has arrived when the Government ought to reconsider this whole
matter with a view to putting Catholic
schools on an equal basis with Protestant
schools. Why should Catholics be in any
inferior position to Protestants? I fail
to see why, except it be a question of religion. I know that members of the Government have often stated that this is a
Protestant Parliament for a Protestant
people. At the same time, they should
not extend that to the teaching of school
children. This may be a Protestant
Parliament, but they do not object to
taking rates from Catholic people.
They do not object to taking our
taxes. And whatever they may
say about their Parliament they
might be fair to the little children who
are growing up and see that they get a
proper education.
It is my opinion that the Government
have been doing everything to prevent
Catholic children getting the education
they should get and which they deserve.
They have used every device they can
against Catholic schools. Only recently
they withdrew the grant for the teaching
of Irish.
They teach Latin and Greek,
and yet they deny the Catholic children
the right to be taught the language of
their own country. We are not a wealthy
class. Our people are struggling under
the distress of unemployment, and they
find it very difficult to get jobs. Why
should that be so? The merchants are
being encouraged not to give employment
to Catholic people, and we are finding it
very difficult to build and maintain our
schools. The Government ought to do the
just thing.
With regard to the point mentioned by
Senator Campbell about the unsanitary
state of the schools, I think no Government in the world should tolerate unsanitary schools. If you have unsanitary
schools you have children being taught, in
a bed of disease, and, later on, you will
have to pay for that neglect by having to
contribute to sanatoria. The sanatorium
at Whiteabbey costs the ratepayers of
Belfast £80,000 a year, and in all probability that is largely due to bad sanitation of the schools. It is a very bad investment, if you can describe it as an
investment, to have unsanitary schools,
and I appeal to the Government to
reconsider this whole matter with a view
to putting Catholic children in the same
position as Protestant children.
19 OCTOBER 1933
Repairs to Schools
Repairs to Schools

I
amount of money that is spent on the
education of their children.
We Catholics pay our rates and taxes,
which go towards the building of these
large regional education committee
schools, and we feel very deeply that we
are not considered as far as the building
of these schools is concerned, and have
no voice in their management. We do
not want any voice in the management
of schools which are Protestant. We
merely want to look after the education
of our
own children, and we feel we
should get the support of the Government in bringing up our children first
as good Catholics and second as good
citizens, which they should be. It is
the duty of the Government to support
us in that object, and to help to make
the rising generation of our land satisfied
and contented citizens.
Sir DUDLEY McCORKELL: I have
been intensely interested in what the last
two speakers have said, but I feel
strangely out of my depth. For my own
information, and perhaps for the information of some other Members of the
House, I should like to know exactly
where we stand. When I look at the
Order Paper I find that a hon. Senator
is to ask the Leader of the House a certain
question, but instead of asking that question we have a series of speeches.
admire and compliment the hon. Senators
on their oratory, but in my opinion they
are not amplifying the question, but are
simply treating the House to a dissertation on education, which seems to me to
be outside the ambit of the question on
the Order Paper. I should like to compliment the hon. Member who spoke last,
and whose maiden speech I heard yesterday. I always listen to the hon. and
learned Member (Mr. Campbell) with
great interest, but I do feel on this occasion that to give the House a dissertation
on a subject covering very much ground,
including Northern Ireland and the Free
State, and many other matters, is quite
outside the ambit of the question.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: With all respect
to the Mayor of Derry, it is a wellknown fact that the parents of Catholic
children are ratepayers and taxpayers
and as good citizens as any under the
jurisdiction of the Northern Government.
Their children number about 10,000 more
than those of any other denomination
under our Government, and they are entitled to the same treatment, both as
regards the building of schools and the
education of their children. As ratepayers
and taxpayers they have eventually to
foot the bill, and they are at least entitled to equality of treatment. Out of
the huge sum of money spent on buildings £1,000,000 has been spent on
regional, or, as we call them, Protestant
schools, while the paltry sum of £60,000
has been given by way of grant-in-aid
of the building of Catholic schools.
Surely if the Catholic minority, which
number at least one-third of our population, are to be content and happy, as
it is the duty of our statesmen to make
them, they should be treated on an equal
basis with other sections of the community. If their children are not to
receive equal rights of education
cannot expect them to be happy and
ting equality of treatment as regards the
contented and to feel that they are getyou
When this Education Act was first
passed I think it was thought that after
a while our Catholic people would be
forced to come in under the Act. We
have now seen that Catholics have a
conscientious objection to the system of
education laid down by the Act, and
that under no circumstances will they be
forced in under that Act. What they
ask for is free and fair treatment, and
I would respectfully ask the Government,
in view of everything that has since.
happened, to re-consider this question of
education, and if possible to amend their
Act with a view to meeting the objections of the Catholic minority, and with
a view to bringing them in and helping
to have an Education Act which will be
impartial, fair, and just to everyone concerned.
Mr. BARCLAY: I should like to know
what these Gentlemen really want. Do
they want the Government to take the
taxpayers' money and hand it over to
a certain creed to be spent without any
public control? I do not think that that
can be the case. Where there is expenditure of public money there is always
public control and public representation.
If the demands of the hon. Members
were granted there would be no public
control, and, therefore, I cannot see how
the Government could accede to their
demands. As far as the Roman Catholics
are concerned, I do not think they are
suffering any disadvantage. They have
at their disposal all the benefits of the
Education Act of 1930 if they care to
embrace them, but it would seem to me
Repairs to Schools
Repairs to Schools
THE SENATE
[Mr. Barclay]
that they are putting up a strenuous
fight against that Act. I know that the
Government have nothing whatever
against the Roman Catholic community,
and that they intend to do equal justice
to the taxpayers of Northern Ireland as
a whole. Therefore, I would like to
know what it is those Members who speak
for the Roman Catholic community
really want.
Mr. McALLISTER : I should not
have intervened in this debate had it
not been for the speech to which we
have just listened. After the speeches
that have been delivered I hardly think
the hon. Member can be serious when
he says he does not know exactly what
the Catholic case is. I do resent his
suggestion that we want the Government
to hand over to us money to which we
are not entitled. I think it has been
very lucidly and clearly put here that
we represent one-third of the population
of Northern Ireland, and that we have
not received in money the amount to
which we are entitled per head of the
population. I do not really think
Senator Barclay can be sincere in the
speech he has made. Our position has
been put in a very moderate way, and
where the interests of the children are
concerned I would not like to introduce
any heat into this discussion, but I do
resent very much the tone and nature
and method of the speech to which we
have just listened. If as one-third of
the population we receive in grants onethird of the amount contributed in taxation in Northern Ireland we will be quite
content. But to suggest that we want
money to which we are not entitled
handed over for the upkeep, equipment,
and building of schools is a most unfair
interpretation to put upon very
moderate and very lucid speech which we
have just listened to from this side of the
House.
a
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am
quite aware that there are many voluntary
elementary schools in urgent need of repair, but I see considerable difficulty in
making grants for this purpose to those
who wish to retain the privilege of
private control of their schools. Might
I point out to the hon. and learned Member that, with the sole exception of
schools vested in the Ministry or the Commissioners of National Education, it has
never been the practice to make grants
for repairs.
Now there are as the hon. and learned
Senator mentioned about 500 Protestant
schools suffering from exactly the same
disability by which he claims to be so
penalised. Although there is a possibility
that all these will be transferred in due
course, still that due course is likely to
take a considerable time. In the meantime I have no complaints from them. I
might add that any school manager may
obtain grants for half the cost of repairs
by placing his school under a Four-and-
Two Committee, as provided in Section 15
of the Act of 1923. But it is to that particular committee the hon. Senator
objects.
The Government when it instituted the
first Education Act in 1923 showed that
they were not actuated by any design to
penalise any particular sect or to promote
the welfare of others. They founded their
Education Acts on previous Acts of the
National Commissioners, and also drew a
certain amount of their provisions from
other parts of the United Kingdom. Now,
I would like to point out that all the
voluntary schools in England have to be
under a Four-and-Two Committee in
order to be recognised at all, and that fact
has been accepted without question by
the Roman Catholics of England.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Not without question.
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
Well, it
has been accepted. The Roman Catholics
in England are agitating not against that.
I think the hon. and learned Senator is
wrong. I do not think they are agitating
against the formation of Four-and-two
Committees. They are agitating because
they cannot get grants, which is exactly
what they can do in the North of Ireland.
Roman Catholics in England have not the
same proportion to the rest of the population that they have here, but they are
far more numerous than they are in the
North of Ireland, and what they accept
there I think should be accepted here.
A great deal of comment has been made
by the hon. and learned Senator on the
fact that religion is absolutely safeguarded
in the Protestant schools under Four-and-
Two Committees and in those transferred
to the State. So it would be absolutely
safeguarded in Roman Catholic schools
should they be transferred to the State
at any time. I may say they have been
transferred to the State in Scotland.
Again, the same conditions might take
Old-Age Pensions
Criminal Justice Bill-
19 OCTOBER 1933
place here. It is inconceivable that any
future Government here should ever seek
to interfere with the teaching of religion
in Roman Catholic schools. The whole
object of the 1920 Act was to safeguard
religion in all schools. What would apply
to Protestant schools would apply equally
well to Roman Catholic schools. I do
think the system of education in the North
of Ireland-admitting imperfections, and
that applies equally to every activity of
the human race as far as we can go at
the present moment is perfectly fair to
all classes of the community.
CALCULATION OF OLD-AGE
PENSIONS.
I
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM:
beg to ask the Leader of the House
whether it is a fact that in calculating
old-age pensions the income is taken on
the basis of capital value and not the
actual income received; whether £25
capital is free, the next £375 is taken at 5
per cent., and any excess above this is
calculated at 10 per cent.; and whether,
now that payment on war stock and other
loans has been reduced from 5 per cent.
to 3 per cent., the Government will consider making a corresponding reduction.
in the calculation of old-age pensions.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The facts
are as suggested by the hon. Member.
The treatment of applicants for old-age
pensions, who are in possession of capital,
is not regulated by the amount of income
which is actually earned, nor by the
amount of income which could be earned
if the capital were invested at current
rates of interest. The sliding scale indicated in the question is properly more
liberal to applicants in possession of very
small savings than to those whose savings
are more considerable; and the Government does not think it ungenerous, having
regard to the fact that the annuity value
of capital at age 70 is at least 10 per cent.
The practice is fixed by statute, being the
same in Northern Ireland as in Great
Britain, and it is not proposed to introduce amending legislation.

Mr. LESLIE: Since I put on the
Order Paper the two amendments which
appear, and which have been circulated,
it has been pointed out to me that it
might be more convenient to move both
amendments together as one amendment,
and also, perhaps, for the sake of conformity the maximum fine, I suggest,
should be £25 rather than £20, because
£25 is the amount in the corresponding
section of the Unemployment Insurance
Act, 1920.
I gave on Second Reading a few
reasons why I think it should be desirable to give power to the magistrates to
impose a fine, and why I thought the
maximum term of imprisonment ought to
be six months rather than three. Therefore, I propose to move instead of the
amendments: "In Clause 4, page 3,
lines 19 and 20 leave out from "to" in
line 19 to "months" in line 20, and
insert "a fine not exceeding £25 or imprisonment to a term not exceeding six."
The effect is that a man will be liable
to a fine up to £25 or to imprisonment
up to six months. I beg to move.

THE SENATE
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept the Amendment.

the
Act may be cited
Criminal Justice Act (Northern Ireland), 1933.
Mr. LESLIE I beg to move at the
end to add:
(2) This Act shall come into operation
on the first day of January, nineteen
hundred and thirty-four.
This is a suggestion to add a few words
indicating when the Act is to come into
operation. It is very often found that
it is not very desirable in practice to
bring a new Act into operation immediately, and in the case of the corresponding statute in England, from which
so much of the Bill is taken, the Criminal
Justice Administration Act, a period of
six months was allowed before the particular clauses which reappear in this Bill
came into operation. It is, therefore,
a very ordinary suggestion to postpone
the commencement of the Act until some
convenient date. I have suggested first
January of next year, because I do not
think the Committee wants to delay the
operation of this Bill any longer than is
absolutely necessary.
We come then to the question of
whether it is absolutely necessary in this
case to wait until the 1st January or
whether the Bill can come into immediate operation. That is purely a
practical question, as to which it is very
likely the Government will be able to
give advice based on their knowledge of
what I may call the state of the books
at the moment. If the Government are
of opinion that there is no difficulty as
regards the assizes, the Court of Criminal
Appeal, and so on, then my Amendment
should not be proposed. If they think
there is some reason to expect inconvenience I suggest it would be wise to
make the Amendment I have suggested.
I beg to move.
The Gov-
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
ernment appreciates the motives which
prompted the hon. and learned Senator
to put down this Amendment, and it has
ment.
given a further opportunity for the matter
to be considered by the Legal Depart-
I am advised, however, that the
Chief Crown Solicitor's Departmentwhich has, of course, great experience of
these matters, and through which all
papers relating to prosecutions pass-is of
opinion that no difficulty will arise if the
Act comes into operation immediately,
and that it would be a pity to postpone
the advantages now given by the Act to
a later date. Accordingly, I do not
think that any useful purpose would be
served by postponing the commencement
of the Act, and I cannot therefore accept
the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: After that, I am sure
it would be the wish of the Committee
that the Amendment should be withdrawn. I beg leave to withdraw the
Amendment.

CLAUSE 2 (Further extension of
period for compulsory acquisition of land,
14 & 15 Geo. 5, c. 17).
The power of the Ministry of Home
Affairs to acquire land compulsorily for
the purposes mentioned in sub-section
(1) of section one of the Constabulary
(Acquisition of Land) Act (Northern
Ireland), 1924, may be exercised at
any time not later than the twentyeighth day of May, nineteen hundred
and forty-four, and the proviso to the
said sub-section, as amended by section
seven of the Constabulary Act (Northern Ireland), 1928, shall be further
amended accordingly.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie.
-Clause 2, page 2, line 6, leave out
forty-four (nineteen hundred and
forty-four) and insert "forty."
(4
""
Mr. LESLIE: The question raised by
the Amendment on the Paper is, If I may
say so, partly a question of principle and
partly a question of practice. I am, as
the Committee knows. always against giving anything approaching a blank cheque
to any Government department, especially
when it involves any interference with
private property. At the same time, we
all know that private property must
occasionally be interfered with for the
greater good of the greater number and
the point is whether it is necessary to extend this power of compulsory acquisition
for so long a period as ten years. That is
the practical part of the question. Originally, the power was given for five years,
and was then extended for a further
shorter period. This power had its origin
in 1924, and was extended in 1928. We
have heard that the barrack construction
programme has been unfortunately delayed
owing to financial pressure, and, perhaps,
other reasons; and we have also been told
that during the period of the current extension the powers vested in the Ministry
have been exercised on only three occasions. In the earlier period of currency
the powers were not, I understand, exercised at all.
The question is how often are those
powers likely to be exercised during the
next ten years, and ought we to leave that
sword hanging over the heads of possessors
of property in the vicinity of places where
police barracks are likely to be erected for
ten years.
I am inclined to think that
ten years is too long a period, and, therefore, I suggest a limitation to six years.
But again, as I said on the Second Reading, if there is a programme already in
hand which will be spread over the full
ten years it might be that the Committee
would think they had paid sufficient regard
to the principle by noticing the question,
and might leave the matter alone. However, that is a matter on which the Government must inform us. My own part
is simple, and that is to move that we
leave out "forty-four" and insert "forty,"
the effect of which will be to cut down the
period to six years.
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
While I
am inclined to agree with the hon.
Senator that it is undesirable that Parliamentary approval should be given in such
cases without a reasonable limitation, I
think the factors in this case are such
that the proposed ten years' extension of
the time is not unreasonable. The powers
conferred on the Ministry are only used
in cases where it is not possible to secure
sites by agreement at a reasonable figure,
and they would not be used after the
Barrack Reconstruction Scheme as a
whole is completed. The Act has now
been in operation for nearly ten years,
G
Drainage Bill—
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
and it has only been fully utilised in three
cases. Less than one-half of the police
barracks in Northern Ireland have been
dealt with under the scheme, and, owing
to the financial situation, it is not likely
that the remaining cases will be dealt with
in less than ten years.
As the hon. Senator is aware, the
primary reason for giving these nominally
drastic powers to the Ministry of Home
Affairs was to ensure completion of the
Barrack Reconstruction Scheme without
undue delay or difficulty and to avoid
excessive cost. It seems only reasonable,
therefore, that so long as the scheme is incomplete the powers referred to should
remain in existence. If, as we believe,
the scheme will not be nearly finished by
the date suggested by the hon. Senator,
it would not be expedient to allow the
powers to expire. I, therefore, regret
that I cannot accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: Again I do not think
it is desirable to press the Amendment.
The matter has been noticed, and I think
that is sufficient.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.

and citation), ordered to stand part of the
Bill.

Drainage Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The first
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
three Clauses of this Measure relate to
the drainage of the River Quoile, and
are designed to give statutory effect to
an agreement for this purpose which has
been made between the Ministry of Finance, the Down County Council, and the
Urban District Council of Downpatrick.
The terms of that agreement are set out
in full in the Schedule to the Bill, so
that I need not weary the House by
describing it in detail. Hon. Members
will, I am sure, join with the Government in expressing satisfaction that the
negotiations of the bodies concerned,
which have been proceeding for some
time past, have reached a satisfactory
conclusion, and that the way is now clear
for the carrying out of the works necessary to relieve the extensive flooding to
which this area has been subjected-an
object which has received the attentions
of many people during the past two
centuries.
Clause 4 of the Bill also deals with an
individual drainage area-that of the
Lough and River Erne. It will, no
doubt, be within the knowledge of hon.
Members that various extensive schemes
for the improvement of the drainage of
this waterway have been under consideration, but it has been found that any
comprehensive works would be wholly
uneconomic. It is, however, felt that
some amelioration might be achieved by
intelligent operation of the levels of Lough
Erne with the object of utilising that
vast reservoir in the interests of drainage, and the Government has decided to
carry out an experiment in this direction.
It was, however, necessary in the first
instance to purchase the water rights
enjoyed by the Belleek Pottery Company
at a cost of £3,250, of which the Government contributed £6,000 and the Fermanagh County Council agreed to pay
the balance. Clause 4 of the Bill gives
the necessary statutory authority to the
Fermanagh County Council for payment
of this amount.
In Clause 5 the Government is making
one more attempt to provide adequate
machinery whereby the County Councils
may be empowered to secure the cleaning
of drains and watercourses. Clauses
dealing with this subject appeared in the
Drainage Acts of 1925, 1929, and 1931,
but in each case difficulties were experienced in securing the desired end,
mainly owing to the fact that County
Councils have found it almost impossible
to prove liability. The Government,
therefore, proposes that the liability for
keeping a watercourse clean shall, subject to suitable limitations and safeguards, be definitely imposed by Statute
on the occupiers of the adjoining lands,
and it is primarily for this purpose that
Clause 5 is included in the Bill. Opportunity is, however, taken to gather to-
Finance Bill-
Second Reading
19 OCTOBER 1933
gether the relevant provisions of earlier.
Acts, and the Clause now consolidates
the law on the subject.
Clauses 6 and 7 deal with the Bann
Drainage provisions of the Act of 1929,
and are designed to remove difficulties
which have arisen in connection with the
The clauses are
acquisition of land.
self explanatory, and hon. Members will,
I am sure, readily appreciate the reasons
for the proposed Amendments.
Clause 8 is designed to enable the
normal percentage to be paid from the
Road Fund towards expenses incurred by
the Ministry of Finance in bringing the
swivel portions of the bridges over the
Lower Bann Navigation, now in the
Ministry's custody, into a condition fit
to bear modern road traffic. I beg to
move, "That this Bill be now read a
Second time."
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move,
this debate be now adjourned.'
"That
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to agree to that.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Finance Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LONG: The first three Clauses of
this Bill deal with stamp duties. In the
first place it is proposed to reduce, with
effect from the 23rd May last, the ad
valorem duty payable by companies and
limited partnerships on registration to
bring this duty into line with the corresponding duty payable in Great Britain.
Secondly, provision is made whereby
obligations arising out of bills of exchange
shall not become invalidated merely on
account of non compliance with stamp
laws; this provision is included in order
that Northern Ireland may conform to
an international agreement on the subject
entered into at Geneva in June, 1930.
In the third Clause certain exemptions
from stamp duties in favour of local
authorities are repealed as from the 1st
April, 1934. It is felt that the development and changes in local government
administration, and the difficulties which
have been experienced in administering
the existing law on the subject have
rendered the repeal of these exemptions
long overdue, and that it is only reasonable that local authorities should be
placed upon the same terms as other persons or bodies in this respect.
Clause 4 grants, as from the 23rd May
last, exemption from entertainment duty
in the case of Agricultural Shows, where
under existing law the Show would be
liable to duty merely by reason of the inclusion of programme items designed to
attract the general public. The exemption will only apply in cases where the
Ministry of Finance has approved the inclusion of such items.
An
The Bill finally makes provision for
exemption from death duties of Securities
issued by the Imperial Treasury, owned
by persons neither domiciled nor ordinarily
resident in the United Kingdom.
Imperial Act of 1931 established this
exemption as regards both income-tax and
British death duties. I beg to move,
"That this Bill be now read a Second
time."
Mr. LESLIE: I really cannot make a
practice of moving the adjournment of
debates, and I do not think that in this
case perhaps it is necessary. At the same
time, the time for considering this Bill in
this House has been extremely limited. If
I remember rightly we read it a First time
yesterday. A good many of its provisions
are what might be described as in common
form, and I think there are only two matters that are likely to provoke controversy
or, perhaps, even discussion.
Clause 3,
that is to say, the Clause suggesting the
removal of certain exemptions, has, I
understand, provoked a good deal of opposition already in the county councils and
in other local bodies.
I think it is suggested that the effect of
the withdrawal of the exemption would be
to impose a new liability on the funds of
the ratepayers. It has also been urged,
though I do not say so, that there is no
great justification for that imposition. I believe I am right in saying that the amount
of money expected to accrue to the Exchequer is limited to £4,000. I am
afraid I could not say for myself that I
see any real reason why payments made
by local authorites should be exempt from
stamp duty any more than payments
made by a private person or private firm.
Still the exemption has gone on for a long
time and I cannot but think that some
consideration, some opportunity of discussion ought to be given before Parlia-
Finance Bill----
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie
ment enacts anything which may result
in a change. Therefore, I am afraid I
have to ask the noble Viscount whether
it is the intention to suggest or support
the suggestion that this Bill should be put
into Committee. In fact, it would
possibly be a question for you, Mr.
Speaker, whether this is a purely financial
Measure which this House could not
amend in Committee. The fact is we
have a Measure containing some six or
seven Clauses, any one of which may be
described as a separate Measure on its own
account, so it might be considered pos
sible, without rejecting the Bill
whole, which we could if we wanted to,
to reject this or that Clause if that were
the deliberate opinion of the House.
as a
1S
I from that to the next point which
pass
I think may provoke discussion, and that is
the proviso to Clause 4 dealing with
entertainments duty. It provides that
the Clause is not to apply to any
entertainment unless the Ministry of
Finance approves of the inclusion in the
entertainment of such exhibition, display
or performance as is mentioned. From
a practical point of view I do see great
difficulties there. If men are proposing
to hold an agricultural show-it
usually a company or association-they
make their plans some months ahead.
Those who have anything to do with
agricultural shows know that things very
often go wrong, and it may easily happen
that at the last moment you may have a
sheep-dog trial impossible of production.
that day, and you may put on the programme a horse-jumping competition. Is
there likely to be time to get the approval
of the Ministry before the programme is
issued or revised. That seems to be a
practical point of some importance.
There is the other point whether if Parliament is going to give exemption at all
they ought to allow the Treasury to
whittle it down. Very possibly a further
explanation on the point could be afforded
to us at a later stage. I should like to
ask for that. Meantime I do not oppose
the Bill. It would be impossible to
oppose it as a whole. I at least infer that
some of the proposals are distinctly advantageous to the individual tax-payer.
I am not at all sure that we ought to pass
the Bill to-day if you decide that it cannot properly be dealt with in Committee.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: Personally I
agree that there should be an adjournment of this debate. We have not received copies of this Bill. I think I am
speaking on behalf of the public generally
when I state that this is being rushed
through rather quickly. At my County
Council on Tuesday a resolution was
unanimously passed against the Clause
repealing the stamp duty on receipts and
paying orders. That has been a privilege
they have had for a considerable time and
I hold it should not be taken away from
them. It is all very well to speak of
private firms having to stamp receipts,
but you must remember that if the public
bodies are now forced to stamp their
paying orders, receipts and other documents, they have made no provision for
that in their estimates for the present
year. The local authorities consider that
this is a thing that should not be placed
on them. From the ratepayer's point of
view it comes out of his pocket eventually
and it is certainly an increased burden on
him. From the point of view that the
Bill has not been circulated to the various
Members of the House, and also from the
point of view that this Bill is being
rushed through too quickly, I beg to
support the hon. Senator in the proposition that he has put forward, that the
debate be adjourned until we have time
to consider the Bill.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I think there is
much force in the arguments of the previous speakers, and I do suggest to the
Leader of the House to allow the Second
Reading to stand adjourned.
Mr. MCALLISTER: On a question of
principle and not on the question of the
Bill, I protest against the Measure being
rushed in this particular manner. There
are many Members in this House who
have never seen the Bill which has farreaching consequences, and yet the
Government propose that those Members
without having an opportunity of seeing
the Bill should vote
on it. I suggest that it is turning the business
into a farce if Members can be called
on at a moment's notice to investigate a
Measure with far-reaching consequences
and vote on it without having an opportunity of reading the Bill beforehand.
I do suggest to the Government that it
is unfair treatment. Members should
have an opportunity of considering the
Bill for a reasonable time. I am
speaking generally and not with reference
to this Bill alone.
19 OCTOBER 1933
Finance Bill-

Adjournmentand I am quite willing to put off the
discussion on this subject until next week.
On Question, Motion, "That the debate
be now adjourned," agreed to.
Sir DUDLEY McCORKELL: I feel
I am in agreement with certain speakers
for many reasons, one of which is that
the public as a whole have had very little
time, if any, to examine the Measure and
the local authorities in particular have
had no opportunity of seeing what the
result is going to be. I would respectfully suggest that on a matter that affects
the local authorities the Government, as I
expect they will, should give an opportunity to those local authorities to
examine the subject and see what is going
to happen. I formally move, "That this
debate be now adjourned."
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I feel in
agreement with what the Opposition say,

THE SENATE

Tuesday, 24th October, 1933.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.

Laid before the House (pursuant to

Questions to Minister.
MOTOR FATALITIES AND
ACCIDENTS.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Campbell:-
To ask the Leader of the House whether
he can state the number of cases of death
or of bodily injury to persons arising
out of the use of motor vehicles on the
public highways of this area since the
passing of the Motor Vehicles Act, 1926. .
Mr. CAMPBELL: In asking the
question which stands in my name I
wish to direct the attention of the House
to the very shocking loss of life and the
increasing number of persons injured
through motor accidents on our highways. The present state of things cannot be allowed to continue if we are to
exist as civilised beings. The magistrates and the police make the best of
the law as it stands, and, if the best is
not enough, a radical change must be
made. Desperate diseases require desperate remedies, and the state of the
road is desperate. Think of the 6,691
people killed, and the 202,119 injured in
Great Britain in the year 1931, and the
6,651 killed and the 206,410 injured in
1932. The horrors of a stricken battlefield could not be worse. Out of every
10 victims probably 8 were walking.
Most of the accidents took place in builtover districts, and only 25 per cent. in
open country.
The most recent figures are worse.
During the first six months of 1933 the
number of persons killed or injured on
the roads of Great Britain has considerably increased compared with the
first half of 1932. Eighteen people are
now killed every day and 565 are injured
every day, and seriously injured in most
cases.
The figures for Northern Ireland, too,
are disquieting and menacing. I believe that during the seven years that have
elapsed since the passing of the first Road
Traffic Act of 1926 the death roll on the
roads of Northern Ireland has been
almost 800 people.
It
In
Very few accidents are unavoidable.
five out
Probabaly five
of six are due
to human failure or errors of judgment. If all the people were careful
all the time there would be no
accidents. But the incidence of human
failure and error is a fixed quantity.
will persist. It must be reckoned on.
spite of cautions and codes the casualties
go on increasing. I can say at once that
most motorists drive with a strong sense
of responsibility and a deep regard for
human safety. But reckless and careless
drivers flourish, and their numbers are a
frightful danger to the community.
are reckless in their thirst for speed.
Their perversity makes them a danger to
all users of the road, both motorists and
pedestrians. Like drug addicts, they lack
self-control. Do these men care a twopenny tram-ticket for the old common law
which gives a person on foot the right to
walk on the highway, and entitles him to
care from all forms of carriages driven
upon it?
They
Then high-powered cars, capable of
doing a great speed, can be bought and
used by people who are epileptic, who
suffer from heart disease, who are colourblind, and who are deaf. There is no
real difficulty in their obtaining a driver's
licence. For £50 a three-year-old car
can be got by them capable of doing fifty
or sixty miles an hour, having brakes or
steering gear weakened by use.
Wellknown motor-cars of the 1933 type can
travel at 82 miles per hour. A motor-car
is a potentially lethal instrument, even in
the hands of a competent driver.
Motor Accidents
Motor Accidents
24 OCTOBER 1933
The Commissioner of the London
Metropolitan Police, Lord Trenchard,
recently urged drivers to realise that they
are in control of an instrument almost
as dangerous as a shotgun, and counselled
pedestrians to be as careful in crossing
roads as in crossing a railway line. A
man who keeps a tiger does so at his
peril and must answer for any damage
which the animal does to a person who
is not at fault. Some time ago a car
mounted a footpath and killed a man,
and his dependants got no compensation
for the loss of the bread-winner because
the accident was found to be due to a
latent defect in the steering-gear. A
"latent defect" in a tiger would be no
defence in an action against its owner.
(Laughter.)
There is a striking proposal by a House
of Lords Select Committee that a pedestrian who without negligence on his part
is injured by a motorist should be entitled
to recover damages though no negligence.
can be proved against the motorist.
Contributory negligence by the pedestrian is not to disentitle him to compensation, but it is to be taken into account
in assessing the damages. The Committee obviously had in mind the compulsory insurance against third party
risks which can be adapted, it is said,
at the cost of but a slight addition to
the present premiums to cover the extra
liability of the motorist. It would be a
strong departure from existing law, but
the situation is strange and desperate.
can
We cannot go on viewing the slaughter
of the aged and children with unconcern.
Is it necessary to society that people
should be maimed or killed on the roads
every day of the year? You
scarcely take up a morning or evening
newspaper in Belfast without finding that
in some part of the Province on that day
some old person or child has been killed
and someone injured. Is it not a scandal
and a reproach to our civilisation that
such a state of affairs should be allowed
to continue?
Mr. McMAHON: I am sure the House
must be alarmed at the figures presented
by the hon. and learned Member who has
asked this question. I should like to
say that while I agree to a certain extent with what Senator Campbell has
said regarding the drivers of motor-cars
who are reckless and careless, there is
at the same time a certain amount of
responsibility placed on pedestrians to
take reasonable care when crossing
streets in the city. For several years
past the Belfast Corporation have been
engaged in erecting automatic controls in
different parts of the city, and within the
past four or five months they have brought
in a by-law making it an offence for anyone to proceed against the red light. I
understand that pedestrians have not as
yet become accustomed to that by-law,
and I would suggest to the noble Lord, the
Leader of the House, that he should take
steps to see that the police are instructed
to be on duty at the different points
where these automatic signals are erected,
not with a view to prosecuting pedestrians, but with the object of educating
them to proceed only when the green
light is in their favour. I hope the day
will never arrive in Northern Ireland
when pedestrians, for the simple mistake
of proceeding against the red light, will
be prosecuted. But I hope they will be
sufficiently educated so that, like pedestrians in London, they will know how
to proceed. The present generation has
grown up with the motor-car, and the
result is they do not fear the motorcar as they might have feared it if they
had got proper education in school as
to its danger.
I again make the suggestion to the
noble Lord to pass on the matter to
the teachers of the public elementary
schools so that they will educate the
growing youth of Northern Ireland as to
the danger of the motor-car. I believe
it is only by instilling into the minds.
of the youth that we may accomplish
something in the way of reducing the
death roll.
There is another type of machine used
very frequently in the City of Belfast,
and that is the cycle. I think some control must be exercised in connection with
the bicycle. I see some boys racing along
at a tremendous speed on the highways,
and sometimes they are a menace to the
motor-car. After all, the motorist, if
he has a decent class of machine, will
have four brakes, and can pull up,
whereas a boy with a new bicycle-and
I speak in the interests of parents. One
of my own boys has a bicycle and I am
alarmed every time he goes out. I think
some control should be used in connection
with bicycles on the highway, because
they are a menace to the boys and girls
using them. I would suggest that the
noble Lord, the Leader of the House,
Motor Accidents
Motor Accidents
THE SENATE
[Mr. McMahon]
would be acting in the best interests cl
the people of Northern Ireland, and
especially of the parents if, in addition
to asking the teachers to educate the
youth in the danger of motor-cars, he will
also draw the teachers' attention to the
danger of the bicycle.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
would like to say a few words on this
subject. My feeling is that the motorists
have been very well looked after, but
those on foot, I am afraid, lay themselves out for accidents. Yesterday,
when I was motoring, three small boys
ran out and just ran up as close as they
could to the car. Now, if one of those
boys had slipped or been pushed into
the motor we would have been in trouble
and it would not have been our fault.
I think if the authorities are doing anything, if there is a footpath provided,
they should make people use it. The
motor cannot go on the footpath and
the people should use the footpath. In
that way the number of accidents would
be reduced.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I should like
to back up the previous speakers,
especially Senator Campbell. I think
there is a very great need in this country
for some more precautions. I have
driven a motor-car for 25 years, and I
have been in motors all that time. I
think I have always been a careful
driver, and I never had an accident.
But there are large numbers whose one
idea is speed.
I heard a story this morning which
illustrates what can be done in the way
of motoring. There is a firm in this city.
which hires out motors. A young man
came into one of these people to hire a
motor-car. The motor was duly hired,
and the young man got out of the garage
and drove into the street. Then he came
to one of the red lights and stopped.
When he got the signal to go on he went
back to the garage and asked "How do
you start again when it is stopped?"
That fellow had never been in a motorcar before and yet he was allowed to hire
a car and endanger the lives of people in
the street because he did not know how
to drive.
I do not believe in all those experts
when they say there is no good in an
examination. I am perfectly certain
that if a man is examined he will know
at least how to put the gears in, of course
the time may arrive when we will have
a gearless car. But there are hundreds
of men who go out in motor-cars and have
no idea of time and space, and I am very
much in favour of an examination.
Surely the Government should take some
steps to see if they can stop this killing
of people. If the law does not stop it
the law should be altered. I am very
much in favour of taking every precaution in the way of examining as to
physical ability to drive a car. The law
allows an epileptic, allows the blind, the
colour blind, and mere cripples to drive.
I do not think these people should be
allowed in a car at the wheel. I do not
think it should be beyond the power of
this kindly, autocratic Government of
ours to put a stop to this. I hope the
Government will take some steps to see
if they can save lives.
car
I
Mr. BARCLAY: I wish to support the
hon. Members who have spoken on this
question on the Order Paper. I want,
particularly, to emphasise what my hon.
Friend mentioned regarding cyclists. I
think the cyclists are very dangerous,
especially outside Belfast. I have been
along the County Down coast in a motorand I found on two or three
or more occasions on that particular
journey the driver had got to pull
up. In one instance he had to pull
right into the ditch to save cyclists
who were coming along three abreast.
understand that is against the law, but
the policemen cannot be over the whole
counties at one time. When these
young people get outside Belfast they all
get abreast. I think if the bicycle was
licensed it would put a great number of
them off the road, and that would, perhaps, be one way of saving life. If anything does happen to a motor-car in
trying to save a cyclist the cyclist is not
to blame. It is the driver of the motorcar who is to blame, if he has turned his
machine to save a cyclist and goes over
the ditch or, perhaps, strikes something
else on the roadside. I think some restriction should be put on cyclists, for
young women riding these things along
the road are dangerous to the travelling
public.
Mr. PORTER-PORTER: I would like
to support the hon. Senator who asked
the original question, but some of the
other Members seem to have Belfast on
the brain. They seem to think this is
Motor Accidents
Motor Accidents
21 OCTOBER 1933
the only place where things can be
rectified and the only place where mistakes occur. I live in the country and
am glad to say I never drove a motor-car
in my life and never shall. But I can
see from a motor-car where a great many
dangers lie. We are poisoned in my part
of the country by what I call young
flapper ladies and young men who do not
know anything about driving, but who
are driving. I think a great deal of the
blame rests with the resident magistrates
who do not sufficiently punish these
people who make mistakes. They have
done a good deal to the dangerous lorrydriver. Lorry-drivers used
pe an
infernal danger, because they kept the
crown of the road. They did not care
twopence about a motor-car, because the
lorry would be all right. I hope if the
Government does take notice and does
anything they will remember there are
people besides motorists.
Sir DUDLEY MCCORKELL: I wish
are
one
to be brief, so many Senators have
spoken, but I would also like to speak in
favour of what has been said, especially
in regard to the suggestion that something might be done in the schools. I
do feel that a concrete proposal is helpful, and I think that if a short time each
week were given to instructing children.
in school in what is commonly called road
sense, it would help the coming generation. There
or two things
especially dangerous. One of them is
playing that game of "last across" which
is dangerous, not only to the children
themselves, but to the motorists.
Throwing stones at cars is another thing
which is dangerous from many points of
view. The cars may swerve and there
may be broken windscreens. I would
suggest that it would be well worth while
to give a few minutes a week in schools,
because it would give road sense to the
coming generation of motorists and
pedestrians and cyclists.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I would like to support very strongly what Senator
Campbell has said in regard to this
matter. It is one that requires immediate attention. A short time ago in this
House I put a question suggesting that
motor-cars should stop where tramcars
stop to let passengers on or off. That is
a very important thing in the City of
Belfast. Conductors very often, but not
always, put their hands out, and sometimes the car stops. But when the conductor is on the top there is nobody to
put out a hand and the cars rush past.
That is a most dangerous thing.
We have not referred to the motorbicycles. Motor-bicycles, I think, are
very dangerous indeed, and the drivers
seem to have no care about people at all.
The Senator has referred to schools and
to bringing this matter before the children. I know that is being done in some
of the schools. I do not know whether
it is by the instruction of the noble
Viscount or not, but I know that in a
good number of schools children are
instructed in this very important matter.
But if it is not being done generally a
good suggestion has been made that it
really should be brought before the
children. It has been suggested that a
policeman should be at these red lights
to warn people. I think that if they
used the Press for special advertisements
it would be very much better and it
would be saving-
Mr. McMAHON: That has already
been done by the Belfast Corporation.
up,
Mr. QUIGLEY: I sincerely hope that
this matter will be taken because
almost every day, or every other day, we
read of some very sad accident to a father
or a child. I, myself, travelled in a car
through Victoria Street when a child was
killed by the car. She stepped out too
quickly as the car came along and, I
think, two days after she died. Something should really be done in connection
with the awful death roll among motorists
and cyclists. Undoubtedly, a great
number of motorists are very careful, but
when they are coming from a side
avenue or side street there is danger very
often. I hope something will be done.
I think Senator McMahon referred to
London and about the arrangements
there as regards pedestrians. You have
a hold-up and you hear "Pedestrians
forward. When he referred to this it
reminded me of an incident in London,
where a good Irishman who was on point
duty shouted, "Pedestrians this way.'
One pedestrian waited for a long time
and then, going forward to the policeman, said, "When are the Presbyterians
getting through?"
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I regret
that the way in which the question was
couched does not permit me to reply to
the general debate. I will, however,
draw the attention of the Government to
Motor Accidents
Motor Accidents
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
some of the suggestions made by hon.
Members, and I will draw the attention
of the Minister of Education to the proposal put forward by the Mayor of
Derry. I think some teachers do at the
present moment give instruction as regards motor traffic, but I do not know
that it is general. I thing a good deal.
might be done in that direction.
I regret that I am unable to give the
hon. and learned Member the information he desires in exactly the form
indicated in his question, as the statistics.
available are not compiled on this precise
basis.
A tabular statement has been prepared
which will perhaps meet his requirements.
This statement shows the total number
of accidents in each year; the number
of persons killed and injured in these
accidents; and information as to the responsibility for the accidents.
It would
appear that in a very
appreciable number of cases the accidents
were due to carelessness on the part of
pedestrians and pedal cyclists, and that
while the proportion of accidents caused
by pedestrians has varied little from year
to year, pedal cyclists have been responsible for a steadily increasing number.
That matter has been stressed by various
speakers, and I think anybody who has
experience of motoring will agree. When
you see a pedal cyclist a hundred yards
ahead you can be almost certain he will
swerve in front of you.
The accidents which remain when the
number due to pedestrians and pedal
cyclists have been deducted from the
total for each year are those arising out
of the use of vehicles of all kinds, except pedal cycles, and therefore includes
accidents arising out of the use of tramcars and horse-drawn vehicles.
As the table referred to is somewhat
long, perhaps the hon. and learned Member will permit me to circulate it with
the Official Report.
In conclusion, I am very pleased to be
able to add that the total number of
accidents, and the total number of
people injured within the first nine
months of the present year, is slightly
less than the corresponding totals for
1932.

RELATIONS OF LANDLORD AND
TENANT.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Gyle:-
Mr. GYLE: I beg to ask the noble
Lord, the Leader of this House, the
question on the Order Paper which
stands in my name, and in doing so, I
wish to direct his attention, and through
him the attention of the Government, to
the fact that long before this Government was set up this question of the
relationship
recombetween landlord and
tenant in urban areas was a subject which
was engaging serious attention. In the
early days of this Government questions
To ask the Leader of the House
whether the Government proposes to
bring in legislation in the immediate
future to give effect to the
mendations of the Committee on the
relations of landlord and tenant in urban
areas in Northern Ireland.
Landlord and Tenant
Landlord and Tenant
24 OCTOBER 1933
relating to this matter were asked and
answered in both Houses, but it was not
until 1925 that the Government seemingly gave this matter their attention.
I say seemingly deliberately, because the
manner in which this question has been
treated from that date until the present
time has convinced me that the Government took it up only to throw dust in the
eyes of the people of Northern Ireland
who were deeply interested in it.
In 1925 the Government promised that
a Commission would be set up to deal
with this matter. It took them twelve
months to appoint that Commission, and
after the Commission has sat and reported
to Parliament it took the Government
another twelve months to tell us anything
as to what their intentions might be.
The Government showed an anxiety to
shelve the question, because there is no
doubt that it has been shelved. We
could get nothing but evasive answers out
of the Government; in fact, up to this
time every move in the history of this
question has been one of driving, forcing,
and beating an unwilling Government
horse. Evasiveness all the time is what
I have found in reading over the records
of the debates and questions connected
with this matter in this House and in
another place.
To prove, if any proof is necessary, the
dilatoriness of the Government and their
want of sympathy with the movement to
go step by step with the British Government, I might point out that for the past
ten years there has been an Act on the
Statute Book of the Imperial Parliament giving some protection to a tenant
who may be in dispute with his landlord.
We boast in this Parliament that we go
step by step with Great Britain, and yet
we find that in Northern Ireland the
interests of tenants, and perhaps in some
cases of landlords also, have been
neglected to such an extent that it has
become a crying shame. I do not want
to introduce any personal note into this
question, but in 1926 I was evicted out of
premises, and I had no remedy whatever.
Had I been living in England I would
have been able to go to the appropriate
court and put my case before the
judge or Recorder, and I think I should
have been in a very different position
to-day. My landlord had a very
exaggerated idea of the value of the
property I was holding. They were leasehold premises, and when the lease expired
the landlord, through his solicitors, asked
me for a sum of £7,000 for a renewal of
the lease. Of course, I laughed at the
idea of it. I offered him £2,000, but he
refused and I was evicted out of the
property. He then put it up for auction
and did not get a solitary bid.
I know of many cases similar to that
which have occurred all over the North of
Ireland. The hon. Member opposite
(Mr. Porter-Porter) did not like Belfast
being referred to, particularly in connection with the deaths resulting from
motor accidents, and rather wanted the
districts
country
included in those
references. I can tell the hon. Senator
that throughout the Six Counties cases
similar to my own have been taking place
during the past ten years, and the
Government has absolutely refused to
take any action. The Government set
up their Committee, and after the Committee had reported the Minister of
Finance said that at some future time
there would be some Bill introduced.
Later on the Minister of Home Affairs
said a Bill was on the way. Later on
still we were told that the matter was
being brought forward, that steps were
being taken to bring in a Bill.
It is evident that the Gentleman who
was authorised to take steps to bring in
this Bill had not his feet encased in
Gulliver's seven league boots, because up
to the present time we have seen no sign
of this Bill being produced. In another
place Sir Robert Lynn in a speech quite
recently pushed the Government very
hard to bring forward a Measure, and
many of the Government back benchers
have been doing the same thing. When
I was sitting in another place we were
always told that if we brought forward a
concrete proposal the Government would
I found that while the Governaccept it.
ment would very seldom listen to anyone
on the Oppostion benches, if any Member
on the Government back benches made a
suggestion or brought forward a grievance the Members of the Government
tumbled over themselves to promise some
action.
What is the malign influence in the
Cabinet that prevents this Bill being
introduced? Something must have
happened. I remembered that on one
occasion I told the Government there
were differences in the Cabinet, and the
suggestion was pooh-poohed. I afterwards
discovered that I was correct, and that
Landlord and Tenant
Landlord and Tenant
THE SENATE
a
[Mr. Gyle]
the difference of opinion was very strong.
It is quite evident from the answers we
have received to questions on this subject of leaseholders that there is
difference of opinion in the Cabinet on
the question, and that some malign
influence is preventing a Bill being presented to Parliament. I would like to
know who that particular Member of the
Cabinet is. Who is the bogeyman of the
Cabinet? That is what the people outside are asking. Question after question
has been asked in both Houses, and one
answer is given one day and another
answer another day. The King's Speech
of 1922 foreshadowed a Bill on the subject, and we are still without it. The
question people throughout the Six
Counties are asking to-day is,
"Who is
the bogeyman of the Cabinet?"
The Prime Minister has told us more
than once that the people living in
Northern Ireland would be no worse off
than the people living in Great Britain.
Now I say and people believe it—that
those people whose leases are shortening
and running out and for whom nothing is
being done, although something was done.
for them years ago in England-those
people are commencing to say that they
are worse off than they would have been
if they had remained under the Imperial
Parliament.
I do not want to detain the House any
longer on this matter, although I tried.
to put my case as forcibly and in as
quiet a manner as possible, knowing as
I do the hardships these people have had
to suffer through the Government's want
of energy and want of desire. I am
afraid it is more want of desire than
want of energy. Through the Government's want of desire these people are
suffering, and I hope that when the noble
Viscount rises to reply he will assure us,
not that a Bill will be introduced some
day soon, but that he will give us a definite
date that he will be prepared to stand
over, and say that on a particular date
this matter will be rectified and the
people of Northern Ireland, who are at
the present time suffering sorely, will know
in their hearts that at last a remedy
is going to be provided for them.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I intervene for a
short time only on this question. I am
sure we all congratulate the hon. Member who has just sat down on his first
contribution to the debates of this
House. We hope it is only the first ci
many occasions on which he will bring
animation and vigour into our debates. He is entitled to speak on this
question because he has done spadework and he is a person who has suffered
from the present state of things, arising,
as I consider, from the unjust and
unequal state of the law. I regret that
my hon. Friend, Senator McAllister, is
not here to-day, because he also has
been one of the pioneers, if this reform
comes about in our time. He and I were
members of the Departmental Committee
which was set up by the Minister of
Home Affairs in 1929. We devoted a
great deal of time, and thought and
labour to the work of that Committee.
We did not see our way to agree in
every respect to the findings of the
majority and presented a minority report, to the compilation of which I put
in a great deal of time and study.
I have
I do not wish to anticipate the answer
the noble Viscount will give. But without being a prophet I think I can say
this, to use a Parliamentary phrase,
that the answer will be in the affirmative. A Bill will be promised.
not the least doubt about that. I am
also equally certain of this fact, that my
hon. Friend will get no definite date
such as he looks for. The reason is that
a very interesting event is looming ahead.
We are in for a General Election. It
may come in November; it may come in
February; and it cannot be delayed beyond May. I have no doubt the Government speaking here will promise the
very best terms my hon. Friend who has
just sat down could wish for. But we
had similar promises in the Speech of
His Grace the Governor in this House
on 11th March, 1930. That is a matter
of three and a half years ago. But we
have never seen the Bill. Our report.
the report of the majority and the report
of the minority, has been put into cold
storage. It has been a very frigid and
very freezing cold storage, and when it
will come out I do not know.
The hon. Senator must be aware of
this fact, if he thinks for a moment, that
this is not a tenants' Government.
Surely he must realise that this is a landlords' Government. You cannot expect
men to go against nature. You cannot
expect a landlords' Government to get
busy on tenants' grievances. They have
got this matter redressed in England.
They passed an Act there in 1927 which,


"
Landlord and Tenant
Landlord and Tenant
24 OCTOBER 1933
I think, realises the wishes of my Friend.
That Act was passed not by a Socialist
Government, not by a National Government, but by a Government with which
the Government of Northern Ireland
have many affinities. I think the matter
has been redressed in Southern Ireland,
and here we are alone with not the least
glimmer of daylight in regard to a matter
that presses heavily on many tenants.
Mr. McMAHON: Before taking part
in this discussion I would like to congratulate the hon. Senator Gyle on his
maiden speech. I think he must have
convinced the House very forcibly that
the case for the tenants and leaseholders
18 one that should be tackled by the
Government at a very early date. It has
been said that Governments move very
slowly, but in this particular regard they
have not moved at all. Mr. Gyle has
told the House of his own experience.
His experience is the experience of many
others in the North of Ireland.
They
are in the hands of people who have used
them for all the days they can remember
for place and power. Those same people,
many centuries ago, extracted the blood
from the bodies of the Irish people, and
this is the only remaining place in
Ireland where the landlords can still
demand their pound of flesh from the
leaseholders.
We go
We often hear from Members of the
Government that we are living under the
same laws as England. We have often
been told that Northern Ireland is the
best possible place to live in.
step by step with the Imperial Parliament. Yet here is one case where you
are out of step, very much out of step.
I could cite many other cases where you
are out of step, when it does not suit the
Northern Government to be in step. I
do not always believe it is a right thing
to go step by step. I do not believe this
Parliament should be a rubber-stamp
Parliament. I am afraid that is what
it is.
I have just got a copy of an answer
given to a question by the Leader of the
House on 27th April, 1932:
Viscount Charlemont: The question
of the introduction of the legislation
referred to by the hon. and learned
Member is one that must be determined in the light of the pressure of
other Parliamentary business, and I
am, therefore, unable at present to
state when it will be possible to introduce a Bill. Under the circumstances
it would not be appropriate for me,
pending the introduction of the Bill,
to make any statement as to its terms.
I trust that the noble Viscount will go a
step further than he has gone on the
previous occasion. It seems to me an
answer that might have been given in the
Imperial Parliament, rather than in
the Northern Parliament, because the
Imperial Parliament sits a good deal
longer than our Parliament, and to say
that the question must be decided by the
amount of Parliamentary time seems to
me farcical, because, after all, this
Parliament only meets about six weeks
-about three times in the year. The rest
of the year our Parliament is on holidays.
If legislation is required to lift the burden from a long-suffering people surely
it would not be too much to expect that
Members of the Government would come
back and do something for the people of
Northern Ireland, who are paying so
largely for the upkeep of this costly
establishment. I do hope, when the
noble Viscount rises to make his reply, he
will hearken to the appeal of the hon.
Senator and give a definite date when the
Government will be prepared to do something practical for the people.
Viscount CHARLEMONT : I also
would like to congratulate the Senator
who asked this question on his maiden
speech. I regret it is not in my power to
give an answer to the personal question
he asked as to who the bogeyman in the
Cabinet is. If one attends as many
Cabinet meetings as I have done and does
not see the bogeyman there, it compels
one to think it is oneself. I hope it is
not.
I can only refer the hon. Member to
the recent replies which have been given
in another place to the effect that the
matter is still the subject of consideration
by the Government with a view to the
introduction of legislation, but I cannot
give any undertaking or make any statement as to when a Bill will be introduced.
