Question again proposed.
Mr. LESLIE: I have first of all to
thank the House for consenting to the
suggestion I made for adjourning the
debate, the more so, perhaps, because I
did not give any reason at the time for
moving the adjournment. The point
was, that I was under the impression that
no Member of this House, at that time,
had received a printed copy of the Bill
in the form in which it left another place
and, therefore, I think it would be
universally agreed that this House was
ill-equipped to enter into a discussion at
that time.
The Bill is not in its nature a Bill of
a class likely to cause any very great difficulty. The first four Clauses are
practically confined to matters which
might almost have been dealt with
by local Bills, but for
one or two
instances where Parliamentary authority
is necessary. I presume that I am right
in assuming that there is no necessity for
any direct provision giving Downpatrick
Urban District Council power to act as
supervising authority in the Quoile River
Scheme if requested to do so. I think it
is plainly implied in the Bill that that is
the intention. I should like to be assured
that there is no necessity for statutory
permission to act.
The principal part of the Bill, of
course, is to be found in Clause 5, which
consolidates the existing law as regards
the cleansing of watercourses, and it at
least gets rid of the somewhat unsatisfactory method hitherto in vogue of
attempting to fix the liability to clear
a watercourse on the person required by
prescription to do it. I think the whole
Clause will be accepted as one fixing
liability on a very reasonable basis, and
yet giving sufficient protection to all persons who might be injuriously affected by
the requirements placed upon them by
an unreasonable local authority.
Several points arise, but I do not know
that they are of very great importance.
I should like to mention two matters
which have been brought before me, on
neither of which do I propose to suggest
to the House that they should take action
in Committee, but the matters ought to
be discussed if anybody thinks there is
anything in what has been said. The
first matter is the question whether there
is sufficient provision to ensure a reasonable length of notice by the county
council and so on, that somebody is required to do something. The second is
the question of whether, in the event of
it becoming necessary for the county
council to recover either a drainage rate
or the expense incurred in drainage, the
powers at present existing are sufficient.
A difficulty has been suggested on the
ground that it might happen that a
portion of a hereditament only might be
affected by a drainage rate or charge,
and that in default of payment by the
owner of that portion of the hereditament
there is no convenient power to go
against that part of the hereditament
which is outside that particular charge.
It is suggested that that might be the
cause of great difficulty and expense, and
I mention the matter in order that anybody who desires action to be taken
should put his Amendment on the Paper
and let the Committee consider it in due
Drainage Bill-
Second Reading
24 OCTOBER 1933
course. I do not myself say that I shall
support any suggestion of that kind, for
it seems to me that my proposal in that
direction would be a matter of very great
difficulty and would require serious consideration.
no
I pass to two other matters, as to which
I confess I am in some difficulty. The
first of them is in the same Clause, giving
the owner or occupier the right to recover from the other in certain circumstances. It seems we are rather unduly
tying ourselves down. There seems
certainty that the parties concerned will
always stand to each other in the relation
of owner and occupier, and the Bill does
not give us any definition of "owner."
I do not think either of the other
Drainage Acts give us any definition of
owner, and we do use the term "
reputed
owner more than once. I shall suggest
at a later stage that we make things more
simple in that direction.

The second point is more difficult. We
are introducing in the Bill-I think
probably on the advice of the Drainage
Advisory Committee the expression
"turf bog" as being comprised in agricultural land. So far as I know none of
these Drainage Acts have hitherto contained a definition of "agricultural
land." It is an expression that has more
than once been before this House. The
last occasion was in the Valuation Act
when, on the recommendation of the hon.
and learned Senator in front
front (Mr.
Campbell) we added a note that farm
land was to include pastoral land. That
was done, if I remember rightly, because
of a somewhat similar passage in one of
the Irish Land Acts. The Irish Land
Acts are referred to in this Bill, and I
confess I am very much afraid that we
shall have on this occasion to do something as regards the definition of agricultural land.
I am sure the whole House will agree
that the less we do the better, but I am
afraid that if we are introducing turf
bogs we do bring ourselves up against
that difficulty. I shall put something on
the Paper to serve as a basis of discussion,
and one of the points I shall endeavour
to make is that we ought not to talk of
"turf bogs," but we must simply call
them "bogs" because it seems to me that
"turf bog" is a somewhat indefinite expression. Of course, it means a bog
connected in some way with turf, but
whether cutting is going on there, has
been going on in the past, or may be
going on in the future is a question that
cannot be decided at once, and it is your
cut-out bog that will give you the
greatest difficulty in introducing
drainage scheme in an area of any size.
a
Passing to Clauses 6 and 7, which
are presumably necessary Clauses amending certain provisions of the Bann
Drainage Scheme, we are brought up
against the question of resorting to the
Road Fund in order to enable the Minister of Finance to get from the Road
Fund through the Minister of Home
Affairs grants, or rather advances is the
term, for improving roads and bridges.
maintainable in connection with the
Lower Bann navigation. It is, of course,
obvious that the bridges over the Bann
must be kept in a condition sufficient to
deal with the heavy motor traffic of the
present day. It seems to me that in so
far as any expenditure is limited to that
it is perfectly legitimate. There is, of
course, the precedent of the new bridge.
to the City of Londonderry which has
been supplied principally by moneys
voted by Parliament and which I understand will be made good in time by
annual instalments from the Road Fund.
It seems perfectly fair to regard a
bridge as part of a road, and therefore.
to resort to the Road Fund for its maintenance, but there is the question of what
I might call the swivel portion of the
bridges over the Bann. The Londonderry Bridge is not now a bridge which
can be opened in order that traffic may
proceed up the River Foyle. The bridges
over the Bann are constructed in order
to enable traffic to proceed up the river.
I think that when the Minister of Home
Affairs is consulting with the Minister of
Finance as regards the advances to be
made from the Road Fund the Minister
of Home Affairs should, in the interest
of the Road Fund, keep very careful
watch on the nature of the expenditure
to which he is asked to consent. I do
not suggest that this House should deal
with the Clause in any way, but I should
like to recall that one sentiment—that
the fund should be carefully watched and
the money applied in the interests of the
road rather in the interests of navigation as such.
The remaining Clause in the Bill,
Clause 10, is worth, perhaps, passing
notice, because its effect will be to give
us a little drainage code in Northern
Finance Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
consider-
Ireland which will be of very
abie advantage in the future. Taking
the Bill as a whole, there is nothing to
be done but to support it, but these
points I have mentioned ought, I think,
to receive consideration.
One
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Most of
the points raised by the hon. and learned
Senator can more suitably be dealt with
on the Committee Stage of the Bill, but
I should like to reply to his first point
about the statutory conditions imposed on
the Downpatrick Urban District Council
Clause 1 gives statutory force to the
agreement set out in the Schedule.
of the conditions in the Schedule-paragraph 6-is that the Downpatrick Urban
District Council shall, if so requested, act
as supervisor, and I am advised that no
furtner statutory authority is necessary.
Regarding the second point about the
liability of those through whose lands a
watercourse runs not being subject to any
unreasonable demand on the part of the
local authority, I should like to point out
that maintenance only is intended.
cannot be asked to either deepen the
channel or to blast rock. The Clause
merely requires that the watercourse shall
be kept clear in the interests of the community at large.
He
On Question, Motion, "That the Bill

Bill committed to a Committee of the

Finance Bill.
Order read for resuming Adjourned.
Debate on Question (19th October), "That
the Bill be now read a Second time."
Sir DUDLEY MCCORKELL: I moved
the adjournment of the debate on this
Bill because, in my view, and I think
also in the view of many other Members,
a little more time was required by those
interested to find out exactly what the
intentions of the Bill were. So far as
local authorities are concerned, there was
considerable doubt as to what the effect
of the Bill would be as regards Stamp
Duties to be paid by those authorities. I
think the matter has been looked into
now. I do not propose to make any
speech, but merely to thank the Government for having given us a little more
time in which to examine the Bill.
I
have no reason for opposing the Measure,
and I support the Second Reading.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I should like your
ruling, Mr. Speaker, on one point. As
this is a Finance Bill I believe it is competent for a Member of the House tc
move its rejection, but is it competent for
a Member to move the rejection of any
particular Clause, leaving the remainder
of the Bill unaltered?
Mr. SPEAKER: In my opinion this is
a purely Money Bill. This House is entitled to reject the Bill, but it is not
entitled to amend it.
Mr. McMAHON: I think that some of
the provisions of this Bill will have farreaching consequences. The Stamp
Duties to be incurred by the Belfast Corporation will probably amount to a very
large sum. If the Belfast Corporation
are to be compelled to stamp every
receipt for gas, electricity, rates, and
other services it will amount to a very
large sum annually. I am wondering
where the Minister of Finance will stop.
He seems to be taking every possible
opportunity not only to inflict hardships.
on the ratepayers but to give as little
money as possible in return. I think he
has robbed nearly every hen-roost in
Northern Ireland, and it is about time a
halt was called. I contend that even
more time should be allowed to elapse
before this Bill is proceeded with further.
I know the Belfast Corporation take a
very serious view of its effects, and no
one can estimate the amount of expenditure it may involve. It may mean
penny on the rates, and I think we ought
to have more time to consider it.
a
Mr. LONG: Senator McLaughlin on
the previous day's debate on this Bill
complained that there was no provision
in their estimates for it. Well, that is
a mistaken view, because if we have regard to sub-clause (2), of Clause 3, it
is provided that the Clause will not come
into operation until the next local financial year, and the local authorities will
therefore have plenty of time to make
the necessary provision in the final
estimates.
It has been suggested by one of the
Members that £4,000 will be a burden.
This will be payable by six counties,
three cities, a number of boroughs and
urban district councils. I can only give
Finance Bill-
Second Reading
24 OCTOBER 1933
an idea of what it will probably cost in
County Londonderry. Our expenditure in
County Londonderry averages a quarter of
a million. During the last three years it
has fluctuated between £248,000 and
£251,000 and it surely could not
seriously be contended that our proportion of this £4,000 would be an intolerable burden. It really would not be
very much.
a
In view of world conditions of finance
our Finance Minister, I think, deserves
credit for the prudent way he has
managed our finances. He has carried
out his duties with the fullest acceptance
and we have the greatest confidence in
him. It is occasionally necessary to
throw out an Irish sprat to catch
British salmon, but I think we will all
agree that our fishing has been to our
great advantage. Senator McMahon
seems to regard the Minister of
Finance as delighting in taxing all and
sundry and imposing a burden grievous
to be borne. I am quite perfectly satisfied that the Minister of Finance regards
this as an unpleasant duty. He would
not wish, I am sure, to impose a charge
on any person if he possibly could avoid
it. It is an unpleasant duty, but it is
nevertheless a duty.
In
No great criticism of any Clause of the
Bill except Clause 3 has been offered.
The arguments which have actuated the
Government in introducing this Clause
may be very briefly summarised.
the first place, there is the administrative difficulty. As hon. Senators
are aware, the exemption from Stamp
Duty of documents used by local
authorities has its origin in Statutes
passed almost a century ago. Since then
our whole system of local government has
been revolutionised by the passing of
such Acts as the Municipal Corporation
Act, the Towns Improvement Act, the
Public Health Acts, and, in particular,
the great Local Government Act of 1898.
This last mentioned Act adopted the poor
rate machinery for the purposes of
collecting the general revenue of county
councils and other rural authorities, and
thus there was attracted to local government documents generally the exemption
which originally applied only to documents connected with the relief of the
poor.
The effect of these changes in local government administration has been that the
exemption provisions as originally passed
bear little relation to the actual conditions to which they have to be applied,
and it is very often a matter of exceptional difficulty to determine whether a
particular document is or is not chargeable with Stamp Duty. One document
may be found to be exempt, whereas
another for some purely technical reason
may be found to be liable to duty. In
the opinion of the Government, there can
be no justification for this chaotic state
of affairs, and the only alternative
seemed to be either to extend the
exemption to all local government documents or to make duty chargeable upon
them all. The Government feel that
there are valid grounds for taking the
latter course, as I hope to explain before
I sit down.
In his speech in this House last Thursday the hon. Senator, Mr. Leslie, referred
incidentally to a second argument which
has had considerable weight with the
Government in bringing this provision.
before Parliament. Briefly the argument is this. Is there any real reason
why payments made by local authorities
should be exempt from Stamp Duty any
more than payments made by a private
person or firm? In the Government's
opinion there is not; and it seemed
proper that they should have due regard
to this argument, when the whole
question of these Stamp Duties had to be
re-opened as a result of the administrative difficulties and anomalies, to
which I have referred. Senator Leslie
raised the point about agricultural
shows. He feared there should be some
difficulty in securing the Ministry's
approval of the inclusion of a particular
item at short notice. From the experience of the Ministry it is not thought
there will be any difficulty in the
administration of the Bill even in the
exceptional circumstances the hon. and
learned Senator has in mind. There will
be an opportunity of getting in touch
with the Minister on the telephone.
Mr. McMAHON: Can the Deputy-
Leader of the House give any idea of the
amounts likely to be got from Belfast
Corporation in connection with Stamp
Duty?
Mr. LONG: No; I regret I cannot
,,
On Question, Motion, "That this Bill
be now read a Second time,' agreed to.
Moved, "That this Bill be now committed."-(Mr. Long.)
On Question, Motion negatived.

Adjournment-
THE SENATE
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move that this House do now adjourn
until Tuesday, this day week.
Mr. LESLIE: I am constrained to
wonder whether this House, with its
accustomed good nature, will bear with
me for a moment while I venture to take
this opportunity of suggesting, very respectfully, to hon. Members that when it
is desired to initiate a wide debate there
are some advantages in tabling a Resolution rather than putting a question. The
point is, as it occurs to me, that if a
Resolution is tabled other Members of
this House, that is, Members who are not
themselves tabling the Resolution, will
have a better opportunity of seeing where
the debate is likely to lead than if they
have a brief question put on the Paper
and if, perhaps, in practice, the debate
covers a very
wide and very varied
ground.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I beg to support the hon. Senator in his suggestion.
It struck me this afternoon that it was
or
not question and answer at all. It was a
debate on the subject that only one
two knew much of, whereas, if a Resolution is tabled it is quite another thing.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I would
like to add to what the hon. and learned
Senator has said. It puts the Leader of
the House under a great disadvantage if
a
mere question is tabled and in the
result, as this afternoon, calculations
and figures are wanted. He has not an
opportunity of going over what may be
said in the debate. He contents himself
with answering the question and has not
got an opportunity of looking up the
question which would enable him to
answer the debate with his customary
facility.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

Business of the House.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I beg to move, "That Standing Order No. XXV. be suspended for
this day's sitting for the purpose of
taking the Second Reading of
Electricity (Supply) Bill and the Housing
Bill."
the
very
Mr. GYLE: I object, and
forcibly object, to having a Bill handed
to me in the Vote Office, and then finding that a Motion is to be moved by the
Leader of the House enabling that Bill
to be introduced and read a Second time
on that very day. I am speaking particularly of the Housing Bill. I received
this morning a letter from a number of
very prominent people who are known as
the Building Trade Federation Employees,
and they ask me to look carefully through
this Bill, and, if I agree with what they
suggest, to move at least two Amendments to the Measure. I consider it is
not fair that we should be expected to
come here and discharge our duties carefully and efficiently and at the same time
be handed a Bill which we are expected
to read a First and Second time on
the same day. I enter a strong protest
against such a proposal, and I hope the
Noble Leader of the House will not insist on taking the Second Reading of this
particular Bill, the Housing Bill, to-day,
but will agree to give us a little more
time to consider it.
Mr. CAMPBELL: In principle I
strongly support the protest made by the
hon. Senator who has just spoken. I do
not think it is treating the House with
proper respect to ask it to adopt at stated
intervals these Motions for the Suspension
of Standing Orders. We are here as a
second Chamber and as a revising
Chamber, and I think we ought to have a
reasonable opportunity of considering
Bills, and especially a Bill of the magnitude and importance of the Housing Bill.
I also wish
to
Mr. McMAHON:
register my protest against this Motion,
particularly as it affects the Housing Bill.
Like Senator Gyle, I too have been asked
by the Building Trade Federation to see
if something can not be done to protect
skilled artisans. That has not been done
in the other House, and they are hoping
that something may be done in this
Chamber. I would appeal to the noble
Leader of the House not to press his
Motion to-day, but rather to give the
House an opportunity of fully considering the Bill and applying a calm, full,
and collected judgment on the whole
matter.
Mr. LESLIE: Speaking generally, of
course I should be in entire accord with
what has just been said. I object, and
have often objected, to taking the First
and Second Readings together, but I
think we have to remember that it is
sometimes necessary to choose the lesser
of two evils. If it should be the will of
the House to give the Housing Bill a
Second Reading this afternoon there will
then be a reasonable time, or rather more
time for putting down Amendments for
the Committee, if the Committee stage
were taken on Thursday. But if the
Second Reading were postponed I think
the time for Committee discussion would
be curtailed. Although I am bound to
agree in principle with the objection,
in practice on the present occasion I think
it would not be wise for the House to
insist.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I also
agree in principle with hon. Members
who oppose this Motion, but I would like
to point out that the Housing Bill was
circulated to Senators on Thursday last,
and should certainly have reached them
on Friday morning. I am sorry if the
hon. Member opposite (Mr. Gyle) did not
get his copy until to-day. It must also
be remembered that the Bill was before
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
another place for some considerable time
a fortnight ago, so that hon. Members
have had ample opportunity of considering the Measure as well as any possible.
Amendments. I am afraid the situation
is as Senator Leslie has adumbrated. If
this Motion is not agreed to we shall
have to take the Second Reading tomorrow and the Committee stage on
Thursday, which would be rather quick
work if hon. Members desired to put
down Amendments. It would be very
much more advantageous if the House
would, while sympathising in principle
with the objection, pass this Motion today.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

NEW INDUSTRIES GRANTS FOR

Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House if he can state
the number of applications for grants
towards the acquisition of sites for new
industries in Northern Ireland under the
New Industries (Development) Act, 1932,
received by the Ministry of Commerce;
the amount of such grants, and the
number of factories or workshops built
on such sites.
Viscount
CHARLEMONT: Apart
from inquiries of a general nature, seven
concrete applications have been received
by the Ministry of Commerce for grants
under the Act to which the hon. and
learned Member refers. Two grants,
amounting to £95 and £52 per annum,
respectively, have already been made,
and two applications are under consideration at the moment.
None of the undertakings, in respect
of which applications have so far been
received, has involved the erection of new
buildings, although six of them have entailed either the acquisition of new ΟΙ
the adaptation of existing premises.
LIFE INSURANCE POLICIES.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Campbell:
:-
To ask the Leader of the House whether
the Government has had under consideration the findings of the Committee on
Industrial Insurance in Great Britain on
the excessive competition among insurance companies for life insurance policies,
resulting in masses of uneconomical contracts into which people are induced to
enter, and the lapse of inordinate numbers of policies with heavy losses to poor
persons; whether such evils prevail in
Northern Ireland; and, if so, whether
any remedy is contemplated.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Many thousands of
life insurances here are effected through
British companies. The report of the
Industrial Insurance Committee in Great
Britain shows the existence across the
water of excessive competition, with a
feverish pressure for increase, first by the
offices upon their staffs, and then by the
latter upon the working-class population.
£54,000,000 was the immense sum devoted
to British industrial assurance in 1930,
and the business is increasing in face of
the downward course of wages and the
widespread unemployment. In that year
there were over 80,000,000 policies in
force in Great Britain. The annual
premium income of the insurance companies is more than the annual cost of
the British Navy. That is a very reinarkable fact. The Committee which
has considered this matter with very great
care in England has come to certain
findings, which are of very considerable
importance.
The Committee 'recommend limiting to
£20 the amount of insurance which may
be effected on any one life, and that there
should be a right to a free policy in every
case after two years' premiums have been
Oral Answers
31 OCTOBER 1933

Oral Answers
Northern Ireland to-day in quarter-stone
bags without any indication as to the
country of origin, and that patriotic
housewives in Northern Ireland are
buying this meal under the impression
that it is made from home-grown oats,
and what steps he proposes to take to
put an end to this and so protect the
interests of Northern Ireland farmers.
paid. With regard to children, they
advise stringent limitations upon insurance for the benefit of others that only
parents, step-parents or persons in loco
parentis should be allowed to insure for
their own benefit a child under 14 years
of age; that the premium should not
exceed one penny a week; and that such
insurance should be effected with a
friendly society, trade union, or industrial assurance company. The due protection of the assuring public demands
drastic changes, and I am sure these
matters, which are of great moment, will
be duly considered by the Minister responsible for the protection of the insured.
public. The Committee foreshadow that
the transference of the vast insurance
business to a single organisation under
the control of the State may ultimately
have to be faced.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Report
of the Committee on the law and Practice
relating to Industrial Assurance is under
consideration by the Ministry of Commerce. As the hon. and learned Member
is no doubt aware, the Committee, so
ably presided over by Sir Benjamin
Cohen, K.C., made recommendations of
a far-reaching nature in regard to industrial insurance law.
During the course of the Committee's
deliberations the Permanent Secretary of
the Ministry of Commerce, in his capacity
as Industrial Assurance Commissioner for
Northern Ireland, gave evidence and a
transcript of his evidence before the Committee has been published.
Industrial assurance business in Northern Ireland is largely in the hands of
companies and societies with headquarters
in Great Britain, and the hon. and
learned Member will, therefore, appreciate readily the desirability of keeping
the law relating to industrial assurance
in Northern Ireland as closely in line with
that existing in Great Britain as circumstances permit. The whole question of
remedial legislation based on the Committee's Report is receiving close consi
deration.
SALE OF GERMAN OATMEAL.
The following question appeared on the

Mr. McMAHON.-To ask the Minister of Agriculture if he is aware that
German flake oatmeal is being sold in
Mr. McMAHON: I am in a rather
peculiar position here to-day. I am asked
to put a question to the Leader of the
House which I never put down. I understand that during the past few days
-I have been told by an hon. Senator
of this House-I did not read it myself
-I have been described by one Belfast
newspaper as having put down this
question either by design or accident.
Another Belfast newspaper alleged I had
some sinister motive. I have no knowledge of whether German oatmeal is sold
in Belfast or not, and I really think some
explanation is due to me in view of the
statements published in the newspapers.
It is very unfair that any hon. Member's name should be bandied about for
putting down a question of which he has
no knowledge.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I cannot
conceive a more terrible possibility than
that which has been outlined by the hon.
Senator who has just sat down.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Is the noble
Viscount in order in answering a question
which is not a question.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I must
admit that I may not be, and I ask your
ruling, Mr. Speaker, before proceeding
with the answer.
Mr. SPEAKER: I think as the
question appears on the Paper it may just
as well be answered. The noble Viscount
is at liberty to answer the question.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will,
therefore, answer the question put down
on the Paper without any reference to
I who asked it.
I am informed by my right hon.
Friend, the Minister of Commerce, that
his attention has been called to the fact
that certain oat products of foreign
origin are being sold in Northern
Ireland in packets of less than 14lbs.
weight which bear an indication
