Mr. McALLISTER: In view of the
question which has just arisen-a question having been put down on the Parliamentary Paper without the authority of
a Member of this House-I think the
House should take up some practical
attitude with regard to it. I would respectfully ask you, Mr. Speaker, as the
custodian of the rights, liberties, and
privileges of this House to suggest some
means whereby the answer which has
been given by the noble Lord, the Leader
of the House, to a question which was
not put on the Parliamentary Paper by
the authority of any hon. Member should
be deleted from the Official Reports of
this House. It would be very unfair to
any Member of this House that somebody
should put down a question for some
motive of his own in the name of a
Member of this House and the answer
appear in the Official Report. That
would more or less-I know the noble
Lord would not be a party to it-assist
and support the mischievous person who
put the question on the Parliamentary
Paper.
I would further suggest that you, as
the custodian of the rights and privileges
of Members of this House and in control
of the operations of this House, have a
full investigation into the manner in
which this question erroneously appeared
on the Order Paper. It would be a very
serious thing if a precedent of this
character was created and allowed to go
by default. It would only bring the
House into ridicule. The question at
issue is a very serious and substantial
one, and I am sure every Member will
support me in vindicating the rights of
Members whose names are put to questions on the Paper without their knowledge or authority.
Mr. CAMPBELL: On a question of
order, may I ask your ruling as to
whether the Official Reports of this House
can contain any report of an answer by
the noble Lord to a question which is not.
before us and is put by no one. I do
submit that, on a point of order, the
answer should not appear on the records
of this Chamber.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I beg to ask by
whose authority this question was put on
the Paper, in view of the fact that
Senator McMahon has denied that he
gave any authority. The hon. Senator
says he has no knowledge of the question,
and yet he has been criticised in the Press.
Although I am the baby Member I do
not think that is the way to treat Members of this House, and some action
should be taken.
Mr. SPEAKER: The question will not
appear in the official publication of the
proceedings of the House, and I will make
inquiries as to how it came to be printed
on the Paper.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the Electricity Supply Bill and the Housing Bill,
to which they desire the concurrence of
this House.
Housing Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Long).
of
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The purpose of this Bill is to continue for a
further period of two years the policy
which the Government has pursued with
regard to housing for a considerable
number of years past. This policy has
met with a very large measure of success,
in that it has resulted in the erection of
something in the neighbourhood
26,000 houses, and I think that the
House will agree that this is not a small
contribution towards the solution of this
very important matter. At the same
time it must be admitted that there is
still a shortage, particularly of the type
of houses which can be let at a rent within the means of the poorer classes of the
community, and, therefore, the Government has come to the conclusion that it
is essential to continue for a further
period assistance towards the building of
such houses.
Housing Bill—
Second Reading
31 OCTOBER 1933
While the policy of the grant of a
subsidy is continued by this Bill, provisions have been included which are important departures from the previous.
Housing Acts. The previous legislation
enabled the Government to pay a subsidy
of £25 in respect of any house erected
which complied with the conditions, and
local authorities were also empowered to
make a supplemental grant of the same
amount, or to grant an exemption from
rates. The Government has come to the
conclusion that the State subsidy should
be made dependent upon the local
authority, which is primarily responsible
for the housing in its district, also making
a grant of the same amount or giving an
equivalent benefit in the shape of exemption from rates; accordingly the State
subsidy will only be given where the
local authorities agree to give a similar
amount, or to exempt the houses from
rates for a period of ten years. This
period of ten years has been fixed on the
basis that exemption for this period is
approximately equivalent to the cash
value of a grant of £25.
As the House is aware, representations
have been made from time to time during
the passage of the various Housing Acts
through Parliament to the effect that a
limitation should be placed upon the rent
at which houses erected with the aid of
a subsidy might be let. The view which
the Government has always held was that
it was undesirable to impose such a restriction, as it would probably tend to
limit the supply of houses. The matter
has been strongly pressed again, particularly by the Belfast Corporation, and
my right hon. Friend, the Minister of
Home Affairs, has been assured by representatives of that body that they understand that a number of reputable builders
are prepared to take advantage of this
subsidy under such conditions. In these
circumstances the Government is of
opinion that the experiment may be tried.
Provisions have, therefore, been inserted
in the Bill during the Committee stage
in another place whereby a local authority
which agrees to give a subsidy may attach
conditions to such grant, including a condition as to the maximum rent which
may be charged during a period of three
years from completion.
Where such conditions are imposed the
procedure will remain as heretofore until
the houses are completed, and the Ministry of Home Affairs will continue to
supervise the administration up to that
point. The State subsidy, however, will
be paid not to the builder, but to the
local authority, who will pass it
together with its own grant, to the
builder. The local authority will then
be responsible for enforcing the conditions which it has attached to the grant.
The local authority will further be responsible for enforcing the statutory conditions as to alteration and enlargement
which now exist, and which are now
enforced by the Ministry of Home Affairs.
For the purpose of enabling the local
authority to carry out its duties in this
respect the Bill empowers it, in a proper
case, to recover the subsidy, and gives
the local authority a charge on the house
for that purpose.
Apart from the grant of a subsidy,
assistance may be given to builders in
another way under the provisions of the
Bill with a view to making it easier for
them to obtain the necessary finance to
carry out building operations. As the
House is no doubt aware, building
societies and other institutions agreed to
make advances for the purpose of
enabling persons to build houses for
the purpose of letting, but such
advances are, of course, normally
limited to something in the nature of
70 per cent. of the value of the work.
The Bill now enables local authorities to
guarantee two-thirds of the difference
between the advance which would
normally be made and 90 per cent. of
the value, and the Government undertakes to reimburse the authority one-half
of any losses sustained by it under the
terms of the guarantee. Further, as an
alternative, the Bill empowers local
authorities to advance money to persons
or bodies of persons for the purpose of
enabling them to build houses at a rate
of interest less by 10/- per cent. than
the rate at which the local authorities
themselves can borrow the money, and
the Government undertakes to make good
to the local authorities this 10/- per cent.
for a period not exceeding 20 years.
It should, however, be clearly understood that where such a guarantee is
given, or where a contribution towards
the rate of interest is given, the builder
cannot at the same time obtain a subsidy.
The Bill also enables local authorities to
advance money for the purpose of building houses for letting on the same lines
as a local authority can now, under the
Small Dwellings Acquisition Act, lend.
money to individuals for the purpose of
Housing Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
enabling the individual to buy or build a
house for his own occupation; but in this
case, where the borrower is paying the
normal rate of interest, he may in addition
to the advance obtain the building
subsidy.
The Government hopes that if the local
authorities co-operate with it by exercising the powers which are now given to
them under this Bill, many houses available for letting to the working classes
will be built. Since the local authorities
for the past year or two have ceased
giving supplemental grants the rate of
building of these houses has fallen to a
very low ebb, but the Government is confident that if the local authorities will
resume their former policy of giving the
supplemental grants large numbers of
houses will be built. I beg to move:
"That this Bill be now read a Second
time."
Mr. McMAHON: While welcoming this
Bill as something in the way of relieving
congestion amongst the working classes,
I am not entirely satisfied that the
Government have done all they possibly
could to relieve the situation.
I am
concerned greatly that there is no provision made in the Bill about the number
of houses per acre.
If this Bill goes
through in its present form it means that
speculative builders can start jerry building again. That will be a very serious
matter for the people in 20 years' time.
It is all very well to look at the new
houses that have been erected. Even
though they are small they look very
nice for three or four years, but after
a number of years they become what are
known as slums. The Government might
have put some provision in the Bill whereby builders could not have erected houses
at a density of, say, more than thirty
to the acre; twenty-five would be much
better, but I would say there should not
be more than thirty to the acre.
I do say that the Government, from
the very outset of their building legis
lation, have bungled. In the first
Housing Bill which they presented to
Parliament they gave a subsidy of £100,
and allowed local authorities to give a
£50 subsidy, with the result that people
without money at all have become capitalists. They simply put up the houses,
pocketed the money, and they are to-day
charging rents of anything from 15/-
to 17/6 per week.
I do not know whether this House is
aware of it or not, but, with the Government subsidy of £100, at one time
builders could come along to the Belfast
Corporation and get an interest re-loan
for forty years of £200. That was £200
before they started to build at all. 1
understand houses have been erected at
£240, which makes that house only cost
£40. In other words people without one
penny in their pockets could start building operations and enrich themselves at
the expense of the taxpayers and ratepayers.
even
The Government have got to the stage
now when they are prepared to control
rents for the first time.
I say that the
Government who gave the £100 subsidy
should have controlled rents in the first
instance. Now at this late stage they
are going to control rents. But for how
long? For three years. At the end of
three years these houses will be decontrolled, and if building operations do not
go on as speedily as one would like these
rents will go up again. I say the Govern
ment are mainly responsible for the condition in which the poor people of Belfast
find themselves, because they did not
control the rents in the first instance,
and they are going to incur an
greater responsibility by allowing houses
to be decontrolled after three years. The
Government should consider this matter
on the Committee stage of the Bill and
insert something to protect the people
who go into these houses. I do not know
how many houses will be built under this
scheme. It may simply be a case of
certain people coming to the aid of the
Government and, in view of the coming
elections, making the people of Belfast
believe that houses are going to be built.
After the elections there may be no houses
built at all, but something should be done
in this Bill to protect the people who will
go into those houses, and to see that
they are not charged greater rents than
they should be charged.
With regard to the imposition which
they are going to place on the local autho
rities, again that is a bit of sharp practice
on the part of the Government. They are
going to hand over £25 to the local
authority and make the local authority
responsible for seeing that the houses are
properly built. In other words, the
Belfast Corporation, in addition to having
to free these houses of rates for a period
of ten years or give a subsidy of £25, are
31 OCTOBER 1933
Housing Bill-

Second Reading
of "To him that hath shall be given . . .
Many middle-class tenants have benefited
by these Acts which were never intended
for them. Many hundreds of better-paid
artisans have benefited, but the code was
never intended for them either. Builders
and owners have done exceedingly well out
of this Housing code. In fact, of
all classes in the community they
have done the best. But the people
for whom it was really meant, who cannot
afford to pay the higher rents, they, as I
say, are out in the cold.
also going to be called upon to see that
the houses are properly built, while the
Government go off scot free. The Belfast
Corporation or any local authority should.
be allowed the salary of the inspector or
the clerk of works, who is to see that these
houses are properly put up.
For the
Government to go to the people, as they
will in the very near future, tell them of
the wonderful Government they have been
and how they have now put housing on a
firm basis, and, at the same time, shirk
their responsibilities, is not playing the
game with the people. I do say that the
local authority should be paid the amount
of the salary of the inspector or clerk of
works, who is going to supervise the
building of these houses.
Mr. CAMPBELL: This is a disappointing Bill. At the same time, one would
wish it well. Anyone interested in the
housing problem must wish well to the
Bill, no matter how small and timid it
may be.
As I understand the Bill, it is
proposed to allow the Government to give
a grant of £25 to any local authority,
corporation, urban council, or rural
council, for the erection of houses of the
small kitchen or parlour type, provided
the local authority exempts those houses
from rating for a period of ten years.
There has been a long procession of these
Housing Acts across our Parliamentary
stage. As a rule, they have been rather
puny things, and the broad result is that
too little has been accomplished for the
Province as a whole. I doubt very much
whether, now that all these Acts have
been in operation for a period of ten years,
Northern Ireland has any more subsidy
houses than the single city of Manchester,
Birmingham or Glasgow.
These Acts were intended primarily for
the poorer class of tenants who could not
house themselves. That was the primary
intention of this Housing code, to provide
houses, for instance, for people in slum
areas who could only afford to pay 4/- or
5/- a week in rent. Under all these
different Acts, including the present Bill,
this deserving class has been left out in
the cold. They cannot face the rents of
these subsidy houses. Corporation houses
are letting at about 17/- a week in Belfast.
There are many subsidy houses in Belfast
letting at from 16/- to 17/- a week. I
think the average rent over all Northern
Ireland would be 9/- or 10/- per week for
State subsidy houses. These people cannot afford to pay those rents.
It is a case
was
In England they had tried an experiment towards providing for the wants
of these people by way of clearing away
slum areas. They passed a magnificent
Housing Act there in 1930, under which
a subsidy called the Greenwood subsidy
to the extent of 40/- or 50/- a year for
40 years was given to a local authority in
respect of each man, woman or child it
took out of a slum house and put into a
house fit for human accommodation. That
a magnificent Act, if suffered to
operate. In due time, I believe, they will
solve the slum problem. It may come rather
late, but it will come some day, perhaps
sooner than later. Not only that, but I
see that under the Report of a recent
Departmental Committee, presided over
by Lord Moyne, they propose to expropriate compulsorily bad slum landlords,
to buy them out, and to replace them by
housing management commissioners and
public utility societies.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I feel
very pleased to see this Bill introduced
and the Second Reading moved to-day.
I have no complaint to make about the
Bill. I feel that it is a good Bill and an
improvement on previous Housing Bills,
and I do think, now that the Government have agreed to pay a subsidy of £25
on condition that the local authorities
bear their fair share of the burden, there
is every possibility in the near future of
the right class of house being built. Up
to recently the houses that have been
built were not suitable for the more lowly
paid workers. Scarcely any, or at any
rate very few, of that type of house have
been built in the city of Belfast. They
did not appear to be attractive enough
for the speculator. The better class
house could be built by a builder and sold
immediately it was built, whereas a workman's house, if it is built, is not likely
to be sold, and such a man has no money
to pay for the purchase of a house.
Housing Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
I do feel that this is the time when a
great improvement will be made in the
housing of the working classes. The cost
of material is fairly low at the present.
time. It is gradually rising I am told--
and it is a question if we have not missed
our opportunity-but it is still very low.
There are a considerable number of building trades operatives unemployed and
very anxious for work, and I feel that
this is a very opportune time to encourage local authorities and builders to
go ahead and provide working-class
houses. There is no doubt whatever that
there is a tremendous demand for this
smaller type of house at a small rent. I
had a call from a man on Saturday night
-I just want to give the information to
the house, because there are a number
of people who imagine that there is no
demand and no necessity for these small
houses. This man told me he lived in a
house in Dock Ward. I am not going to
mention the street, but I could give the
name and the number of the street. The
person who rents the house pays 13s per
week for the house, and she has four
sub-tenants. Each of these sub-tenants
pays her 5/- per week for rent and 1/-
per week for gas. That is 20/- per week
she is getting for the letting of that house
and she is herself living rent free.
That is the state of affairs that is
current in Belfast, and I want the House
to recognise this. A good number of
people tell us that it is the rent agents
and the property owners who are imposing on the working classes. Unfortunately
it is not so. The greatest scoundrel is.
the man who has a fairly big house and
room to let to other working-class people
like himself, and who is not a bit backward in fleecing them. It is a glaring
scandal to see a person renting a house
at 13/- a week and obtaining 20/- a week
out of it as well as 4/- a week for gas.
I do feel that the Belfast Corporation
deserves some little credit for pressing
this matter forward. They have in the
past provided the finest type of house for
the working classes. They have provided
the best possible house at the smallest
rent that has been produced up to the
That is on the Seaview site.
present.
It is a pretty spot and anybody who visits.
that place comes away satisfied that the
people housed there are living comfortably and contented. The Corporation
have endeavoured, after careful
sideration, to put forward another scheme.
con-
The Housing Department of the City
Council came to the conclusion that they
could get houses built and let them at
7/- per week. That is something better
even than the Seaview type, something
lower. I am quite satisfied that, if the
Belfast City Council are able to go ahead
with this scheme, they will produce another lot of houses that will be a credit
to the city of Belfast, and at the same
time be an encouragement and an inducement to builders to persevere with the
building of such houses.
I
Another great inducement to builders in
the Bill is that the Government have made
it possible for those people who have not
the money to borrow money to build.
am told that is one of the big drawbacks
in the building trade at the present time.
They have not sufficient money to permit
of their putting it into houses and waiting
until they obtain their money back. This
in itself is a great concession on the part
of the Government in arranging for this
loan. It has been said that at the expiration of three years the builders would be
able to extract a higher rent. I hold that
before the expiration of three years there
will be sufficient houses built to meet the
demand, and once the demand is metand we all recognise that the law of supply
and demand regulates prices-the people
who have houses will not be able to increase their rents because of the competition. Therefore, I am satisfied that at
the end of three years if the builders take
advantage of the offers in the Bill there
will be a supply of houses to meet the
demand.
I
The local authorities have held up the
building of houses to a considerable extent
during the past year or two. The Government have given £25 for some time past,
but the local authorities refused to supplement that amount, with the result that
no one would attempt to build houses.
do not think it would have paid them to
do so and let them at a low rent. Now
the Government have provided that local
authorities must shoulder their responsibilities, I hope that some pressure will be
put on the local authorities to see that
they do not handicap the building of
houses, as it is possible for them to do.
If they refuse to pay the £25 there is no
possible chance of houses being built.
The matter now lies entirely with the
local authorities. The Government have
given their portion on condition that the
local authorities give theirs. I do say with
Housing Bill—
Second Reading
31 OCTOBER 1933
all sincerity that houses should be provided for this class of people, and everything possible should be done to enable
them to live comfortably, even in the
smallest type of house, at a rent within
their means, so that they will not be
fleeced by these other people who attempt
to take all they can.
Mr. LESLIE: There
if I
was,
may
say so, a note of optimism about the
commencement of the speech of the hon.
Senator, who has just sat down, which was
sadly missing, perhaps, in the two speeches
which immediately followed that of the
noble Viscount who introduced the Bill.
I do not think that I can join with the
first two speakers in complaining of the
Bill on the ground that it does not go
far enough. That really seemed to be
what we might call the main point of complaint. The complaint was that the Government was only giving a measly £25
when they used to give £100, and that, at
all events, they ought to pay something
very much more substantial in order to
promote the building of small houses.
That argument might have more weight
if the Government happened to be in possession of a bottomless purse; but the Government has not got money, rather I
should say the Government have not got
control of money because the money that
is to provide these subsidies is not in any
sense the money of the Government, but
the money of the people.
I was sorry to notice in one of the
speeches that a suggestion seemed to be
made that the Government and the people
were in opposite camps. I think the intention was to suggest something in the
way of a contest between rates and
taxes, but although there are rates
and there are taxes the ratepayer
and the taxpayer in Northern Ireland are much the same person. That,
I think, was rather overlooked. I do not
think that we can complain against this
Bill that it is insufficiently generous in the
way of subsidy when we consider the
extent of the demands upon the resources
of the country at the moment, and the
very limited amount of those resources
that are yet undrawn. We are suffering
at the moment from over-taxation on the
one hand, and our local authorities are
suffering from over-rating on the other.
The limit of taxation has, in my judgment, been passed and the limit of local
government assessment is not far off.
The Government has not any money
under the control of Parliament which it
can throw about recklessly, and I am
afraid that the local authorities have no
great funds either. I should have said
myself that this Bill might have been
fairly described as a Bill which will help
the local authorities in performing what
is really their primary duty, because it
is more the duty of the local authority
than of the Government to promote
housing. I think this Bill does help,
and I think it helps not only in the city
of Belfast, of which we have heard so
much, but in other parts of the Six
Counties. It ought never to be forgotten
when we are discussing housing questions
that there is a need for housing accommodation outside Belfast altogether. There
are other towns besides Belfast in Northern Ireland, not perhaps so large, and
there is the great question of housing in
the rural districts.
But there is nothing here that I can
find that will impose any serious obstacle.
There is nothing that I can find which
would encourage sub-letting. There is a
possibility of increasing the superficial
area of the approved houses from 550
feet to 600 feet under certain conditions,
and I do not think that my hon. Friend
(Mr. Joseph Cunningham), on my left,
can regard that slight concession to the
local authorities as being an inducement
to sub-let. After all, the whole question
of sub-letting was dealt with not very
long ago in our last Rents Restriction
Act. I am not sure that that Rents
Restriction Act is regarded as being the
great success that some people seemed to
think it was likely to be.
Passing from that, I do not think we
have any complaint that we can practically make. The local authorities are
being helped, and I suggest that they are
being left to manage their own business
in their own way. That is chiefly shown
perhaps by the concession in regard to
the increase in size. As regards pressure,
the question of the Government of the day
bringing pressure to bear on the local
authorities, I think, is outside the scope
of this Bill altogether. If pressure is to
be put on the local authorities, it is
surely for their own constituents, who
are the ratepayers, to move in the matter.
If the population of a particular locality,
be it great or small, want money spent
on houses, it is for them to bring pressure
Housing Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie
on their representatives, which they can
do, in a perfectly constitutional manner.
The only point which gives me any
difficulty in the Bill, and it is a point
to which I will direct attention in the
Committee stage-although I may say
at once that I have very little hope of
being able to shake the Government on
the matter the only point that does
trouble me at all is whether it will really
help housing to insist on the ten-year
limit, or whether we should do more by
giving the local authorities the option of
a five-year exemption from rates instead
of ten years. I will mention that again.
I will not develop it to-day. It is an
important question, even at this late
hour, whether we should not provide that
the exemption from rates should not include exemption from a rate raised to
meet claims in respect of criminal injury.
Those are points in the Bill which
seem to me worthy of discussion, although
I think it extremely probable that
when they are before us it will be on an
occasion not altogether suitable for their
introduction. Taking it all round, I
support this Bill. I think it will be
received with approval in the country,
and I hope it will do a certain amount
of good. In fact, I hope that the optimism of my hon. Friend on the left will
be justified rather than the pessimism
existing in the earlier speeches.
Mr. GYLE: As I intimated earlier, I
have not had an opportunity of perusing
this Bill with the care which I would
have liked, but like the previous speakers
I welcome the Measure. I think it is a
step in the right direction. From conversations I have had with people deeply
interested in the building of houses I am
afraid that the safeguards that are undoubtedly in the Bill for almost every
person are left out so far as the artisan
class who help the builders are concerned.
There are not the safeguards in this Bill
that I would like to see. I had a letter
from the National Federation of Building
Trades Operatives. Those are the bricklayers, carpenters, plumbers, and other
tradesmen engaged in the building of
houses, and they feel that in this Bill
their interests are not being adequately
safeguarded.
I have been asked to bring before this
House the fact that they want to have
an Amendment inserted in this Bill. I
may say that the operatives have interviewed whoever is in charge of the drafting of this Bill, and also the right hon.
Gentleman (Sir Dawson Bates) who introduced it in another place, and, I am
sorry to say, they have not succeeded in
getting the Government to agree to what
they suggest should be part and parcel
of this Bill. I would like to direct the
attention of the noble Viscount who introduced the Bill in this House to these
facts, and I would like that he and the
right hon. Gentleman in charge of it in
another place might reconsider their
decision, and perhaps agree to the principle I am putting forward in suggesting
that certain Amendments should be put
in.
The first Amendment is to, the effect
that the workmen engaged on such houses
should work the agreed building trade
hours under the agreed building trade
conditions and be paid the agreed standard
rate of wages obtaining in the district
in which such houses are constructed.
The most important point is that the
houses shall be constructed by recognised
building trade operatives and by a recog
nised builder. My reading of that
Amendment is that the operatives desire
not only that skilled workmen will build
these houses but that recognised builders
will be those who will have the contracts
for having the house properly erected.
When the last subsidy was given we
found there were houses erected at something like £240 by certain speculative
gentlemen. You will find there are many
speculative gentlemen in the city of
Belfast who are always prepared to take
advantage of any little move whereby they
can enrich themselves no matter whom
they make the poorer. We found that
those gentlemen who got £140 of a subsidy, and who erected houses which cost
something like £240 each, did not engage
skilled operatives. They brought in all
sorts of handymen-" hedge carpenters"
is an expression I have heard applied to
them. Certainly agricultural labourers
were brought in to do carpentering and
plumbing work in many instances. Some
of those houses will not be much better
than slum property by the end of ten
years, and I suppose that in a future
Senate someone will get up to propose
that a large sum of money be voted by
the Government in order that those houses
may be demolished. We want to avoid
that sort of thing. We want to see that
these houses are properly built, and are
suitable for working-class people.
31 OCTOBER 1933
Housing Bill-

Second Reading
think that is common cause so far as the
Government and those who criticise the
proposals are concerned. Thousands upon
thousands of pounds have been spent on
these housing schemes. At one time the
people who built the houses received £140
per house, £100 from the Government
and £40 from the local authorities. It
is common cause that the whole of the
housing scheme has miscarried, that the
whole of those colossal sums of money
voted by the House of Commons and
sanctioned by this House have been misapplied.
So far as I can remember, one of the
original proposals was that these houses
should contain baths, but for some reason
or other-I do not know whether the
Belfast Corporation are to blame or notthe baths were not put in.
I find generally among those best able to judge, the
builders themselves, the contractors, and
workmen who do this class of work, the
opinion is held that we have got from previous schemes a proportion of jerry-built
houses. The gentlemen for whom I am
speaking, who want to get this Amendment inserted in this Bill, are very
desirous that that sort of thing should not
occur again. They do not want to see
farm labourers brought in to do work of
this kind. Those farm labourers hang on
to their jobs, get a certain number of
stamps, although they do not come under
the Unemployment Insurance Act, and
then go on the dole. That is another
matter that the Government should take
into consideration. If they give us what
we are asking for, this sort of thing will
be prevented. I want to quote a definition of a building trade operative which
has been given to me. It is :
A craftsman who has served his time
to his trade, and is employed in that
trade only, or an apprentice to such
trade, or a labourer who has been employed in the building trade for a period
of something like one or two years.
That is the position I am going to ask the
Government to take into consideration. I
hope that they will see their way to meet
the views not only of the operatives, but
of the builders or contractors themselves.
I understand that the architects are also
in favour of a Clause such as I have read
out being inserted in the Bill. I will not
detain the House any longer on this matter. I should like the Government to give
these matters that I have raised due consideration, and I hope that when the noble
Lord the Leader of the House rises to
reply to the Second Reading debate he
will be able to give us an assurance that
these points will be reconsidered by the
Government.
Mr. McALLISTER: I should like to
intervene for a moment before the noble
Lord rises to reply. It appears to me,
looking at the whole history of this
housing question, that it is common cause
that legislation was originally introduced.
for the purpose of providing houses at
reasonable rents that the lowliest of
working men would be able to pay. I
If houses could not be built for the
lowliest and the poorest of the community
when a subsidy of £140 was being paid,
I fail to see by what process of logic it
can be argued that houses can be provided
for them when only £25 is the inducement. Do not take me as being against
the principle of the subsidy in this Bill.
I am as strongly in favour of it as Mr.
Cunningham, and I hope that his
optimism will be duly fulfilled. I think,
however, he is stressing the probable
results too much when he suggests that in
three years' time, with a subsidy of £25,
the builders of Northern Ireland will have
every worker suitably housed. I quite
accept the view that Mr. Cunningham
is heartily anxious to see the lowliest in
the community properly housed, but I
think he is too optimistic from that point
of view. I think we should hear more
of the details of this scheme before the
House is asked to give its approval to the
Bill.
com-
I should like the noble Lord to show
us the provision in this Bill which will
ensure that the money will be appropriated to the building of houses which
will be rented at a reasonable figure to
the working-class people of the
munity, and that it will not go in the
same direction as the amounts previously
devoted to this object. I think that is a
fair onus to put upon the Government,
to ask them to show within the four
corners of this Bill any definite provision,
any definite condition, which will ensure
that the money approved of here will not
go the same way as the huge and colossal
sums which have been approved of both
here and in Great Britain in the past.
I fail to see where this Bill differs from
the other Bills, and where special provision has been made to remedy the
errors of the past. I think that is
common cause both on the part of those
who support the Government and the
Electricity (Supply) Bill 444
Housing Bill
THE SENATE
[Mr. McAllister]
Opposition. The other Bills have miscarried, and that being common cause, I
ask the Leader of the House to show us
the particular provision in this Bill that
was not in the other Bills which will safeguard the purpose we all have in view.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I certainly join
with the other speakers in complimenting
the Government on bringing forward this
Bill, but at the same time I should like
to see inserted in the Measure some of
the provisions suggested by Senators on
this side of the House. In a town like
Armagh, where I come from, we have
a number of slums and houses that have
been condemned, but difficulties have been
experienced owing to the way the leases
have been drawn up, with large fines for
renewal, and to the fact that the property
is held in such a way that the landlord
between the head landlord and the tenant
is not in a position either to repair the
houses or have them knocked down and
rebuilt. I should like the Government
to do something for a town like Armagh,
where space is so restricted, whereby
houses could be knocked down and
rebuilt.
There is another point that strikes me
in regard to this Bill, and that is: Where
are the local authorities to get the £25?
I will give you an instance of what is
in my mind. Four grants under this Bill
in Armagh would involve a penny in
the £, and if we had 100 houses built
within a year you can easily realise how
much would fall upon the rates. Therefore, I respectfully suggest to the Leader
of the House that he should amend
Clause 6 of the Bill by inserting after
the word "advances," "and grants,"
with a view to allowing urban district
councils and rural district councils to have
loans where they cannot budget for the
sums necessary.
I certainly think this is a step in the
right direction. I should have liked to
see the Government more generous from
the point of view of making grants. It
does not seem right that a Government
with a big exchequer purse such as they
have should ask a small local council or
rural district council to make the same
amount of a grant as the Government
themselves. I could have understood the
position if the Government had come forward boldly and faced this matter in a
proper manner, by saying they would
give £50 if the councils concerned would
One
give £25. That would certainly be an
encouragement to the local authorities to
build houses, as I feel that the Government are anxious they should do.
thing I am anxious about, and that is
that something should be done in these
small towns where slum areas exist and
where there is not sufficient space for the
rebuilding of new houses until the old
houses have been knocked down.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think it
is rather a difficult matter when hon.
Senators in the course of a Second Reading debate ask the Leader of the House
for all kinds of details, because it is
really impossible for him to give them
about a Bill which does not arise in his
own Department. The Leader of the
House, I think everybody will agree, is
an extremely able and intelligent man-
(laughter)—but at the same time when
he gets out of his own Department he
cannot possibly be expected to know and
foreshadow every possible question that
can be asked of him in the course of a
Second Reading debate, when only the
principle of the Measure is being decided.
I should be only too happy at a later
stage to give answers to the questions
raised. If any hon. Senator has any
question to ask on a definite Clause in the
Bill, I will answer to the best of my
ability on the Committee Stage or at a
later stage on the Third Reading. If
hon. Senators who have put questions today will allow me to answer when the
discussion of the various Clauses is
reached on the Committee Stage, I think
that would really be the most satisfactory
way, because I can then deal with them
in their order. As far as I can see the
criticisms of the Bill have been to the
effect that the subsidy is not enough,
and that it should be increased. That
might be done if the Government were
possessed of more money than they are.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Thursday next.
Electricity (Supply) Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Long.)
Mr. LONG: This Measure has for its
purpose the amendment in certain
respects of the law relating to electricity
supply. Hon. Members will be well
445 Electricity (Supply) Bill-

31 OCTOBER 1933
Second Reading
aware of the rapid development which
has taken place, and is still taking place,
in the methods by which electricity is
supplied to an ever-widening circle of
consumers. New means and new methods
have created conditions which those who
framed the existing Statutes did not, and
could not, foresee, and it is accordingly
necessary for us to review our legislation
from time to time, in order that it may
be made to cover these new conditions
and to clarify the meaning of the provisions of the existing Acts as to which
some doubt may have arisen. Such is
briefly the purpose of the Bill now before
the House.
Hon. Members will observe, for instance, that power is taken to enable a
supply undertaking to utilise a transformer installed on private property for
distributing electricity to other consumers,
and the rapid development of the practice.
of providing cookers and other electrical
appliances on hire, or hire-purchase, has
made it desirable to extend the powers
which undertakers already possess to discontinue supply, where charges for
current or fittings remain unpaid, to cases
of arrears on instalments, or rent charges,
in respect of appliances.
This
In certain streets, either by reason of
the fact that they are narrow, or because
of special amenities to be preserved, it has
recently become the practice to attach the
low tension distribution lines to the fronts
of houses, where they occupy an inconspicuous position under the eaves.
practice has proved valuable in avoiding
the erection of poles in certain cases, and
it has in general been welcomed by owners
In
and occupiers of the houses affected.
one or two cases, however, there has been
difficulty in obtaining a way-leave, and it
is accordingly desirable that powers
should be afforded to the undertakers in
this respect with a right of appeal to the
Ministry similar to that which already
exists in the case of electric lines placed
across lands.
Another feature of the Bill is the power
given to authorised undertakers to make
by-laws with respect to the workmanship
and the materials used in domestic
installations, which they are required to
connect up from the public supply.
A question has arisen, owing to a
recent court decision in England, as to
whether the Ministry in giving a wayleave consent has power to award the
amount of compensation or rental to be
paid in respect of the way-leave. Under
the 1931 Act the Ministry may give its
consent on such terms and subject to
such conditions and stipulations as it
thinks just, and it is desirable that it
should be made clear that the Ministry
has power to award compensation; provision has been made to this end in the
Bill before you. The principle enunciated
is not a new one and the Clause follows
the wording of the Telegraph Act of 1878,
which deals with the erection of telephone
and telegraph lines.
This brief review of the more important
Clauses of the Bill, will, I trust, be
sufficient to enable hon. Members to
appreciate its nature and its purpose.
I
beg to move, "That this Bill be now read
a Second time."
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: Might I just
mention one matter in connection with
Clause 4 with regard to persons who may
object to pay for electric current or
appliances. I know it is the duty of this
House to protect, as far as they possibly
can, the consumer, especially when
there is a bona fide dispute regarding an
account furnished. I suggest that
provision might be inserted in this Bill
to ensure that the current would not be
cut off from the consumer until that bona
fide dispute had been placed before the
Ministry concerned. We have had cases
where either managers or authorised
a
undertakers who have an order from the
Electricity Board have taken themupon
selves the responsibility of cutting off the
light from the consumer without any
notice whatever. The point I would like
to make is that where a bona fide dispute
arises, on the question of whether an
account furnished has been paid, whether
or not there has been an over-charge, or
whether there are items in the account
that should not be in it, if the consumer
is prepared to lay the case before any
tribunal, and if he is prepared, if
necessary, to lodge the amount of
the account in dispute with a responsible third party, no manager,
undertaker, or official should have the
power to cut off the light without first
getting the authority of the responsible
Ministry.
I think that is an important matter.
If such a suggestion is incorporated in
this Bill it will be a protection to the
consumer so that he will not be at the
mercy of any particular individual. We
all know perfectly well that in small
447 Sea Fishing Industry Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. McLaughlin]
villages where electric current is sup
plied we may have an official who may
be the manager or undertaker, and who
may have taken some umbrage against a
particular person. If the account he furnishes is disputed on the ground, perhaps,
that a portion of it has been paid, or that
the consumer has been over-charged, or
that he has been charged for appliances
that have been put into his premises without his authority, he should have the
opportunity of appealing to the Ministry,
and a manager, undertaker, or official
should not be in a position to cut off the
light until he has received authority from
the Ministry or the Electricity Board. I
would ask the Government to give careful
consideration to my suggestion.
Mr. McALLISTER: I would like to
support the suggestion made by my hon.
Friend, because I think it is a very
reasonable one. It is not a question of an
appeal to a court or tribunal which sits
at intervals, but to the responsible
Ministry. The Ministry of Commerce
and the Ministry of Home Affairs are
functioning all the year round, and consequently there would be no advantage in
any person making a fictitious appeal.
On the other hand, it would protect the
public generally against a public official
or local authority deciding whether a man
was to sit in light or in darkness.
Mr. LONG: The very important suggestion made by the hon. Member will
receive the consideration of the Government, and I will give a definite answer
on the Committee Stage.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Sea-Fishing Industry Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The provisions of this Bill are rendered necessary
by reason of the Sea-Fishing Industry
Act passed by the Imperial Parliament a
few months ago. That Act, as hon.
Members will be aware, represented the
British Government's attempt to place.
the sea-fishing industry of the United
Kingdom upon a sound economic footing
by ensuring to British sea fishermen fair
and remunerative prices for their catches.
The methods adopted to achieve this
object were first of all the limitation of
the quantities of sea fish which may be
landed in the United Kingdom by foreign
fishermen and, secondly, the exclusion of
British fishermen from certain fishing
grounds during the part of the year when
the catches landed from those grounds
tend to create a glut in the British market. A third method was the fixing of
the minimum size of mesh which British
fishermen are allowed to use in waters
outside the three-mile territorial limit.
These three Measures all apply equally
to Great Britain and Northern Ireland,
but the fourth and last method employed
under the British Act applies only to
Great Britain, namely, the prohibition of
the sale of fish under certain prescribed
sizes. That particular provision in the
Westminster Act was confined in its
application to Great Britain because the
control of sale within Northern Ireland is
a matter within the jurisdiction of the
Parliament of Northern Ireland and, in
so far as control of the sale of sea fish
is considered to be necessary, it was considered that such control should be derived from an Act passed by the Northern
Ireland Parliament.
The British Act is now operative and.
as hon. Members will readily appreciate,
a situation has been created in which the
Northern Irish market is liable to find
itself flooded with fish of less than the
minimum sizes prescribed for sale in
Great Britain. While there has not up
to the present been any sign that such
undersized fish are, in fact, being landed
in Northern Ireland the House will. I am
sure, agree that protection against such
a possibility must be provided with as
little delay as possible. The present Bill is
intended to provide that protection just in
the same way as the British Act has provided it for Great Britain. Under the
Bill the Ministry of Commerce is empowered to prescribe the mnimum sizes
of sea fish which may be sold in North-
Ireland and it is constituted an
offence for any person to have or to sell
fish of less than the prescribed sizes. The
Bill further fixes the maximum penalty
which may be inflicted for an offence
against an Order made by the Ministry
and specifies the particular officers who
are empowered to seize fish which do not
conform to the requirements of any
Order made by the Ministry.
ern
The Bill is quite simple and the purpose which it is intended to serve is, I
think, beyond argument. Accordingly,
I beg to move, "That the Bill be now
read a Second time."
Sea Fishing Industry Bill-
31 OCTOBER 1933
Drainage Bill
Mr. McMAHON: The danger that I see
arising from this Bill is that it will in
all probability increase the price of fish
to the poorer classes of the public.
Another matter that gives me great concern is that this Government seems to be
constantly interfering with the liberty of
the subject. They are constantly controlling farmers, and now they are going
to control fishermen, and every form of
control means an additional horde of inspectors. I have been wondering how
the Government finds sufficient money to
pay the number of officials they have all
round this place. The noble Lord has
not told us how many inspectors will be
necessary in connection with this Bill.
Nor has he told us what the qualifications
of the inspector will be before appointment, whether he will have to carry a
rule in his pocket to measure these fish.
This matter requires a great deal of
consideration. I think it will hit the
poorer classes of the people, because I
know that in Ardglass, for instance, the
people get fish practically for nothingthe small fish. The same thing applies
at Kirkcubbin. These are two places in
Northern Ireland that will be hit very
badly. I think the Government would be
better employed, instead of injuring the
fishermen, building decent harbours to
enable them to land the fish in a satisfactory manner. I hope the noble Lord
will see to it that no unnecessary hardship is placed upon the fishermen because
unless
will
the inspectors
who
be
appointed under this Bill are instructed
in connection with their job I am afraid
the whole thing will end in chaos.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
approve of this Bill, and I think instead
of raising prices to the poor it will rather
lower prices. If we destroy young fish
or ruin them the food will not be there.
We have seen that happen in regard to
oysters in Strangford Bay. There are no
oysters there now. I think this Bill will
be a help rather than a hindrance to the
supply of fish.
There is just one point I would like
to ask the Leader of the House about.
Under this Bill we do not give the size
of the fish, and the Ministry of
Commerce states the size, but it
does not state how that will be
published, and how the fishermen and
users will know what the size of the fish
is. I think it would be better, instead
of depending on the "Gazette," which a
great many people do not see, that there
should be some means of publishing the
ruling of the Ministry of Commerce. If
that is put in the Bill it would be easy
to understand. I just mention that in
the hope that the Leader of the House
will see that something is done.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I do not
think it can be the object of the first
speaker to ensure that the supply of fish
shall be entirely deficient in the various
areas. That is what the Bill in the
Imperial Parliament is designed to prevent the exploitation by fishermen of
the smaller sizes of fish to the detriment
of the fishing industry in general. The
Bill is really with a view to rendering
our sea fishing more profitable in the
future rather than less so. I should like
to add that no additional inspectors will
be appointed under this Act. The inspection will be done by the police and by
the Ministry's fishery officer and by the
existing fishery inspector of Belfast Corporation. With regard to the suggestion
made by the right hon. Senator who last
spoke, I will see about that by the time
the further stages are reached.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill read a Second time and committed
to a Committee of the Whole House for
Thursday.

the Ministry), 3 (Provisions as to expenses of scheme), and 4 (Contribution

CLAUSE 5 (Maintenance of water
courses and power of county council to
execute work).
(1) It shall be the duty of the
occupier of any agricultural lands on
or through which a watercourse runs
or of which a watercourse forms a
boundary to scour out and cleanse so
much of the watercourse as runs on or
through or bounds the lands occupied
by him and to maintain it in such a
condition that the proper flow of the
water is not obstructed:
*
*
Provided that-
*
*
*
*
(iii) nothing in this section shall
affect the operation of any scheme.
made under section twenty-seven of
the Northern Ireland Land Act,
1925.
*
*
*
*
(10) Nothing in this section shall
affect the right of an owner or occupier
to recover from the other under the
terms of any lease or other contract
for the time being in force the amount
of any expenses incurred by him under
the foregoing provisions of this section
or recovered from him by the county
council.
*
*
*
*
(12) For the purposes of this
section-
(i) the expression "scour out and
cleanse" includes the cutting, topping
or removal of any bush, shrub or
tree causing or likely to cause, or
contributing to the causing of, an
obstruction to the proper flow of any
watercourse;
(ii) the expression "agricultural
lands" includes turf bog;
(iii) the expression "watercourse"
does not include so much of any
rivers as may be prescribed by the
Ministry of Home Affairs after consultation with the Ministry.
(13) Section twelve of the Act of
1925, section twenty-two of the Act of
1929, and section three of the Drainage
Act (Northern Ireland), 1931, are
hereby repealed.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I propose
to make the Amendment standing in my
name. I should like to explain that these
two paragraphs have been drafted in such
a way as to overcome technical difficulties
which arose out of the drainage scheme in
connection with the land settlement. It
was felt in the case of land intended to
benefit by the purchase money set apart
for the purpose of drainage, that it would
be unfair that the county council should
be given power to proceed against the
Occupier notwithstanding the existence of
trustees holding funds for that very purpose. On the other hand, it would be
inequitable to render trustees liable without limit for the drainage of the land.
Accordingly, their liability has been
limited to the amount of money at their
disposal.
The drafting of these Acts, I may mention, has given rise to great difficulty, and
the present form should prove satisfactory
in practice. It is not anticipated that the
effect of the Clause will be very great because the area covered by such a scheme
is relatively small. It was necessary that
this special circumstance should be provided for. I beg to move, to leave out
Sub-section (1), paragraph iii, and to insert:
"(iii) this section shall not be
deemed to impose upon an occupier any
liability in respect of a watercourse in
any case where sums have been transferred or set apart in order that the income of a fund may be applied in or
towards the cleansing or maintenance
of the watercourse, either under subsection (2) of section twenty-seven of
the Northern Ireland Land Act, 1925,
or otherwise in connection with a sale
under the Land Purchase Acts:
(iv) in any case where trustees have
been appointed to apply the income of
such a fund as aforesaid, this section
shall have effect with the substitution
of those trustees for the occupier, but
the county council shall not be entitled
to recover from the trustees, as expenses incurred in executing works, any
greater sum than the trustees have
power to apply in or towards the cleansing or maintenance of the watercourse."
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page
7, Sub-section (10) to leave out from "of"
("right of an owner") to under "
("other under the terms ") and insert
any person to recover.'

Drainage Bill-

Committee
The idea behind this Amendment is not wise, for the Committee will remember
so much to unravel the knot as to prevent that there is no more difficulty in getting
our tying the knot. The point, as it a horse on to a turf bog than there is in
seems to me, is to maintain the rights of getting him on to a cut-out bog. Therepeople under the terms of leases or other fore, I move the Amendment on the

whether they are owner or occupier or what

,,
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page
8, line 9, to leave out "turf bog" ("the
expression 'agricultural lands' includes
turf bog") and to insert, "meadows,
pastures, bogs, and woods.'"
This is very much more difficult; in
fact I do not know what the Committee
will say about it. The point is that
hitherto we have relied with indifferent
success on the broad expression "agricultural lands' and we have not descended
to any definition. We have, I suppose,
explained it to ourselves in this way:
"Agricultural lands" mean any lands
which are under tillage or which may be
under tillage, lands which are tilled or
capable of being tilled. Now, lands
capable of being tilled necessarily include
both pasture and meadow and lands
under crop include woods, because timber
is a crop just like anything else. I think
we have done very well as we were, but,
now, the suggestion in the Bill is to include turf bog. Directly we say that
agricultural land includes turf bog, it
seems to me we get into difficulty, because
if we are going to include turf bog, what
are we going to do about cut-out bog or
bog which is partly cut out and partly
still being cut?
The
Again what are we going to do about
mountain land, rough mountain which
is only grazed or cut-out or partly cutout bog, and so on? I think we get into
very great difficulties and, therefore, if
we are going to do anything at all I think
we must try to put up a definition.
definition I suggest is to leave out "rurf
bog'-and I am not at all sure whether
the Committee would not do well to
accept that portion of the Amendment and
to reject the following portion-and then
insert the words, "meadows, pastures,
bogs, and woods." I have, of course,
put in meadows and pastures to meet the
case of the mountain, and the bogs to
cover all bogs, whether turf bogs or other-

Mr. CAMPBELL: I think the Amendment is a most reasonable one, for at the
moment we have no definition of agricultural lands and this give us a clear
definition.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I regret
that it is not possible to accept this
Amendment, and possibly when I have
explained the precise significance which
attaches to it the hon. and learned Senator
will be satisfied with my explanation and
withdraw it.
Under the Valuation Act of 1852
separate lists were kept of lands, but by
Section 12 of that Act turf bogs were
excluded from the list of lands and indicated separately, because these turf bogs
were not subject to rating. Following
this the Rating and Valuation Act of
1928 defined agricultural land as being
land within a rural area, etcetera, and
derating was applied to agricultural land
on this basis, that is to say still excluding
turf bogs, which had been derated in
1852.
Coming to the present position as regards drainage, it was thought that
possibly, in view of these existing definitions, agricultural land might be held to
exclude turf bog, and consequently this
special item in the 'valuation lists is
added back to agricultural land.
As I have said the explanation is purely
technical, but at the same time it has
been represented very strongly by a
certain county council that there should
be no doubt about the inclusion of turf
bogs, because the hon. Senator will understand that numerous stretches of bog
intersect other lands in such a way that,
if the turf bog were not subject to proper
drainage supervision the drainage of the
other lands would be rendered impossible.
In these circumstances, therefore, I regret I cannot accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I think the Committee
would prefer that the Amendment should
be withdrawn. I do not think it much
matters.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Drainage Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr, Leslie]
This Amendment is not on the Paper,
and it is rather a troublesome one. As
the Committee will have noticed, this
whole Clause is devoted to the question
of the scouring and cleaning out of watercourses, but it has been suggested that
we might scour and clean out the various
enactments dealing with the matter. The
text of the Bill repeals Section 12 of the
Act of 1925, Section 22 of the Act of
1929, and another section, but leaves in
certain words in Section 26 of the Act
of 1925 which are no longer wanted, and
which are really rather misleading as
suggesting a double form of appeal. All
the appeal necessary is already provided
for in this Clause, and it would really
clear up the Statute Book a little if the
Committee consented to accept the
Amendment. The Amendment is to leave
out the whole of Sub-Clause (13) on page
8, and to insert instead of it-
"The following enactments are hereby repealed:
Section twelve of the Act of 1925;
In section twenty-six of the Act of
1925, as amended by subsequent
enactments, the words "or by the
service upon him of a notice, or by
anything done by a county council
under section twelve of this Act";
Section twenty-two of the Act of
1929;
Sub-section (1) of section twentythree of the Act of 1929;
Section three of the Drainage Act
(Northern Ireland), 1931."
I beg to move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause 5, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

Question proposed,
stand part of the Bill."
Mr. ARMSTRONG: In regard to line
28 on page 6, there is a suggestion I
should like to make, and that is to add
the word "deepening" to the word
"widening" there. I have had a good
Ideal to do with the care of these watercourses, and I have often found that when
the water is running freely, owing to a
lump of clay or something of that kind
in the drain lower down, the water is
obstructed, and all the work done above
is rendered useless. I think deepening
in certain cases is more important even
than widening, and the inclusion of an
amendment to that effect would be an
improvement on the Bill.
The CHAIRMAN: I would like to ask
the hon. Member with which Clause he
is dealing.

line 28. It says therebe

reasonably necessary."
I suggest that it should be made to read-

deepening or widening of the watercourse as may be reasonably necessary."

in Clause 5 with which the Committee
have finished.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: Clause 6.

of Clause 5 on page 6. The Committee
will notice that Clause 5 is a very long

words to which the hon. Member is alluding at the moment are really in Clause 5
with which the Committee have finished.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: I am sorry.
I
noticed "(6)" and I took it to be Clause 6.
That is sub-Clause

(6) of Clause 5.

A Repudiated Question 460
A Repudiated Question
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 2nd November, 1933.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the
Chair.
A Repudiated Question.
EXPLANATION BY MR. SPEAKER.
Mr. SPEAKER: With regard to the
question on the Paper last Tuesday in
the name of Mr. McMahon, I first of all
wish to say that owing to the fact that a
rather complicated situation was sprung
on me, I allowed the question to be
answered. I wish to make it clear that
the fact that a question is down on the
Paper does not mean that it must be
asked, and any Member who has a
question on the paper, even if it is a
question which he really did hand in
himself, has the right to say, when called
on, that he does not intend to ask the
question.
With regard to the mistake itself, I
have satisfied myself that it was entirely
due to an error in my Department,
owing to the fact that the name as
written on the question looked very like
that of the hon. Member, and I can only
ask him to accept my sincere regret, that
owing to this mistake his name was put
to the question. At the same time, I
would draw the attention of Members
to the fact that, although this is the first
time that such a mistake has occurred,
my Department is only human, and thus
not infallible, and if hon. Members would
draw the attention of my Department to
a mistake of this kind it would at once
be rectified by a reprint of the Order
Paper.
Mr. MCMAHON : I accept your
assurance as to how this mistake occurred,
and I readily agree with you that a mistake of this character should be brought
to notice. With regard to the statement
you have made about the desirability of
drawing the attention of your Department to such a mistake before the question is put, I should like to say that that
was also put to me by one of my
colleagues, but it was put to me rather
late, because when he asked me if I had
drawn the attention of the official concerned to the mistake on the Order
Paper, I had already raised the question
in the House. I recognise now that I
might have done better by going to the
official concerned. However, as far as I
am concerned, the incident is entirely
closed, and I accept the explanation
given by Mr. Speaker.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I am sure I express the feelings of every section of the
House when I say that we sympathise
with your Lordship in what has happened,
and we also sympathise with your Lordship's officials. I may say this for our
Party, and I am sure for every Party in
the House also, that we have always
found
your Lordship's officials most
efficient and courteous in every respect.
We have had abundant evidence of their
extreme courtesy on
every possible
occasion. A mistake of this kind could
happen in the best regulated assemblies.
I think such a thing has never happened
before in this House, and I am sure it
will not happen again in a hundred years.
Mr. Chichester is a very courteous and
a splendid official, and I should like him
to realise that we sympathise with him
in this matter, as we sympathise with
Senator McMahon.
Mr. GYLE: While agreeing with the
sentiments expressed by the previous
speaker, there was one point in your
explanation, Mr. Speaker, that I rather
regretted, and it is this. You say that
a mistake was made in reading the name
of the party who sent in the question.
I would rather you had given us the
name of the party who did send in the
question. I think that is due to this
House. As a recent Member I would
not like a question I had sent in to be
ascribed to someone else, and I think the
name of the party who sent in the question should be given to the House.
Mr. SPEAKER: In reply to Senator
Gyle, I may say that the question was
sent in by a Member of another place,
but I am not at liberty to give the name.
Mr. GYLE: I quite accept your
explanation.
