Questions to Minister.
APPOINTMENTS OF DISTRICT
INSPECTORS, R.U.C.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Campbell:
To ask the Leader of the House whether
several recent appointments of District
Inspectors of the Royal Ulster Constabulary have been made by nomination
instead of by Civil Service examination;
and whether, in regard to future appointments, it is the intention of the Government to substitute nomination for Civil
Service appointments.
Mr. CAMPBELL: In putting the
question which stands in my name, I
wish to express the hope that the Government do not contemplate returning to the
system of appointing District Inspectors
by nomination, under which system in
times past appointments were made and
given to candidates of strong political
influence. I hope it is not intended to
drop the system of Civil Service examination under which men are appointed who
have proved their merit and ability in
open public competition.
Mr. LONG: The selection of gentlemen
for cadet appointments-as distinct from
promotion appointments-to the officer
rank of the Royal Ulster Constabulary
has been made in the last three cases by
a Selection Board instead of by Civil
Service examination.
It is not anticipated that further
vacancies for such appointments will arise
until 1935. If and when such appointments are being made the selection will
be made by a Selection Board.
MARKETING OF PIGS.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr.
McLaughlin :-
To ask the Leader of the House
whether it is a fact that there is considerable delay in disposing of the pigs.
placed in cold storage by the Armagh
Urban Council on behalf of the owners;
and whether he can say when these pigs
will be sold; and whether he is aware
that pigs are being purchased alive at
live weight prices at farmers' homes,
killed by the farmers, and then delivered
dead to the curers; and whether the
Government will take action in the
matter, or request the Pigs Marketing
Board to have the pigs in cold storage
sold.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: It will be within
the recollection of the House that the
market held in Armagh on 3rd October
was one of the first markets to come under
the regulations of the Pigs Marketing
Board. I think it is generally agreed
that the congestion of pigs in the markets
at that particular time, and the fact that
there was no demand for them, was due
to the Government having brought in
regulations for the marketing of pigs for
which one might say the ground had not
been prepared. The quota of exports was
not fixed before these regulations were
brought into force. That being so, the
farmers who brought their pork into
Armagh market on 3rd October were
placed in a very awkward position, and
they were unable to sell their pork.
About eleven o'clock in the morning of
that day, when the real position of affairs
was discovered, the Town Clerk of the
Armagh Urban District Council telephoned to the Ministry of Agriculture and
requested them to give some help in the
difficulty. The Ministry referred him to
the Pigs Marketing Board, but when that
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
[Mr. McLaughlin]
body was communicated with they practically referred the Town Clerk back to the
Ministry. The result was a conference
between the Ministry and the Pigs Marketing Board with a view to getting the
pigs purchased by the curers. Negotiations went on until seven o'clock in the
evening, and at that time a message was
received from the Ministry stating they
could do nothing.
As chairman of the Urban Council I
was in the market at the time and the
only thing I could advise the farmers to
do was to get the pigs put on a lorry and
sent to Belfast and put into cold storage.
They have been in cold storage ever since,
and we have not been able to get the Pigs
Marketing Board to sell them. No doubt
they are doing their best to sell them, but
the position at present is that the pigs are
still in cold storage and have not been
sold. A number of the unfortunate
owners of these pigs have been decreed in
respect of their land annuities, and it
does not suit them to lie out of this money
for such a length of time. We all know
that the money for the pig is generally
put away for the payment of rent or some
other bill that is urgent.
In the case of these poor farmers their
pork has been in cold storage since 3rd
October, and, at the moment, there is
very little prospect of it being sold. In
view of the Government's decision to
indemnify the curers against any loss,
surely it is the duty of the Government
now to try to do something for the owners
of these pigs by having the pork placed
with curers as quickly as possible at the
controlled price. I further suggest that
the owners of these pigs should be indemnified against the cost of keeping these
carcases in cold storage since 3rd October.
With regard to live pigs, it seems to me
that the curers are not really trying to
carry out their obligations under the
regulations of the Pigs Marketing Board.
If they were I think this congestion that
has occurred could have been lessened to
a great extent. In my county at the
present time I understand that the curer
goes out and buys the pig alive. The
farmer is then told to kill the pig and the
pig is delivered as dead pork to the curer
after being sold at live weight. I do
suggest that is not fair. There is some.
thing wrong. I go further and suggest
that if the curers were doing their best
and making an honest endeavour to help
the Government the congestion we have
had during the past six weeks would not
have occurred, particularly the 120 pigs
in cold storage in Belfast from Armagh
market on 3rd October would not have
been there. These pigs would have been
disposed of long ago.
Mr. HILL-DICKSON: I do not wish
in any way to encroach on the rules of
the House by rising to debate the
question and answer, but I am very much
obliged to my Friend, the Senator from
Armagh, for raising the matter. It does
not apply to Armagh alone. From
Newry, in my own county, 150 pigs have
been sent into cold storage since 3rd
October. At the Down County Council
meeting to-day, at Downpatrick, this was
brought to our notice by the representative from the district. Some of those
people whose pigs have gone into
cold storage have to meet difficulties in
the way
of annuities and other debts.
Then, again, a curer who was there told
us that these pigs, when they came out of
cold storage, might not be worth the full
controlled price. Our opinion is the
Board should make up the difference, and
these people should be compensated. It
is a very cruel thing for the Government
to recognise and establish a marketing
board and then leave the people to suffer
the loss. I hope the answer of the
Deputy Leader of the House will give
some satisfaction to these people.
is
Sir DUDLEY MCCORKELL: I do not
wish to carry on the debate as I know it
would not be within the rules to do so,
but perhaps as a member of the Pigs
Marketing Board before the answer
given I might say this Pigs Marketing
Board has no power to deal with the
disposal of pigs in cold storage. The Pigs
Marketing Board particularly asked the
farmers at that time not to kill the pigs
and bring them into the market. But
that warning, I am sorry to say, was not
regarded as carefully as it might have
been. Be that as it may, the Pigs Marketing Board have every sympathy with
these farmers whose pigs are in cold
storage and, in so far as they are enabled
by the authority at their disposal, would
be glad to do anything to relieve them.
There are bound to be certain hitches at
starting a scheme. It would be a very
remarkable thing if there was no hitch.
I most respectfully suggest that to cast
aspersions on the Bacon Marketing Board
and the curers would not help things.
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
2 NOVEMBER 1933
Both the pork boards are working together and I have no doubt the scheme
will in time work well. The Pigs Marketing Board has every sympathy with the
farmers but they are debarred from doing
anything at the moment.
Mr. LONG: It is a fact that some delay
has occurred a delay which was inevitable under the circumstances-but I
am informed that arrangements are being
made by the Pigs Marketing Board for
the disposal of these pigs at an early date.
The hon. Member will be glad to know
that there is no reason to believe that the
delay in marketing has caused any deterioration in the value of these pigs for
bacon purposes.
With regard to the purchase by local
curers of live pigs I would refer the hon.
Member to the Government statement
which appeared in the Press on Monday
and Tuesday of this week and from which
he will see that the proposals made by
the Government with a view to solving
existing difficulties are contingent upon
the Northern Ireland Bacon Marketing
Board agreeing to purchase their supplies
of pigs in Northern Ireland in accordance
with the custom which has always prevailed in the past.
Mr. LESLIE: Arising out of that
may I ask the Parliamentary Secretary,
whether the effect of what he has said
and the general opinion entertained by
the Ministry, is not that such difficulty
as has arisen has arisen because of a
certain number of farmers having held
too long and then sold too quickly?
Mr. LONG: I understand that the
position is as has been stated by the hon.
and learned Senator.
THE REPUBLICAN FLAG.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
put down this question to draw attention
to what I consider neglect of duty on
the part of the police with reference to
that particular flag. I think most of us
are aware by reading the speeches of a
number of statesmen of Southern Ireland that they have longing eyes on the
North of Ireland. But I think that
until such time as the people of Northern Ireland show a desire to connect
themselves with them, I do not think
they should be allowed to fly this flag
of theirs just as they care or think fit.
It is rather provocative, in my opinion,
amongst people who are Imperialistically
inclined and are opposed to a republic.
More especially I am surprised at the
police since they recognised this, that
they did not ask the players to have the
flag removed. Evidently they passed on
and allowed it to stay. It is a little
strange, because in Belfast I have said
over and over again the police are not
very backward in having the Union
Jack removed if it is in a place
objectionable to the Nationalists.
I beg to ask the Leader of the House,
whether the Government are prepared to
acquiesce in the order of the Gaelic Athletic Association that the Republican flag
must be flown at all games of that body in
Northern Ireland, and whether he is
prepared to take action in the case of
the police at Corrody, outside Derry,
last Sunday,
Sunday, who, after protesting
against the presence of this flag, did not
insist on its removal.
Mr. CAMPBELL: This question
raises another, especially in view of the
speech of the hon. Member who has just
sat down. What is the flag he speaks of
and calls the Republican flag? Is it the
flag of the Irish Free State? The hon.
Senator is silent, and I take it therefore
it is. What is the colour of that flag?
One of the colours of that flag should
please him. The Irish Free State is a
neighbouring and friendly state inhabitated by 3,000,000 people. I am
not sure that there is any illegality whatever in flying the flag of the Irish Free.
State.
If it was illegal, what has made it
illegal? There is no suggestion that
there was any breach of the peace or any
provocation given by the people hoisting
the Free State flag. There being nothing
of that kind, what is the grievance? I
believe this flag is hoisted over the
Government Offices in Whitehall. Can
it be legal in London and illegal in
Londonderry with the Derry added ou?
I believe it has actually been flown over
the City Hall. The Lord Mayor will
know.
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH indicated dissent.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I believe it has
actually been seen on the Carlisle Bridge,
the Craigavon Bridge at Derry. That
being so, what is wrong? What is particularly complained about in flying this
Oral Answers
Housing Bill-
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
flag any more than flying the flag of
Canada, the flag of South Africa, or the
flag of New Zealand? I would like, if
I may, to supplement the question put
by the hon. Senator and follow his own
words. Taking away the last few words
of the question, I would like to ask the
Deputy Leader of the House do the
Government propose to take any action
against the police for their conduct on
this occasion in-if the facts set down on
the Paper by the hon. Senator are right
-interfering with the lawful rights of
law-abiding, peaceful people in the
exercise of those rights.
Mr. McMAHON : I am rather surprised that this question has been put
down in such a way as to be misleading,
because the flag has been described as
the Republican flag. I have no knowledge up to the moment that there is a
republic in Ireland. As far as I know,
and as far as everyone knows, the South
of Ireland is a Dominion. If the flag
that was flown in Derry happened to be
the flag of any other Dominion than the
Free State, would Senator Cunningham
have taken the same exception to it? I
would like to know if Senator Cunningham
is more Imperialistic than the Chief
Whip of the Northern Government, who
admitted in another House that he stood
to attention under that flag while the
Soldiers' Song was sung. He saw nothing
wrong in doing that in the South of
Ireland. Why should Senator Cunningham take exception to the Gaelic
Athletic Association?
The
After all, that association must have
some flag, and they decided to adopt the
flag of the majority in Ireland. They do
not confine their activities to Derry. They
go to France and different other countries.
That flag, I am proud to say, has been
honoured in foreign countries, although
it is dishonoured in part of Ireland.
Athletic Association has done good work.
Quite recently I noticed at some games
in France they played special Irish music
for the Gaelic Athletic Association, who
are not confined to the South of Ireland,
but are mainly drawn from the North of
Ireland. They are not confined to any
religious sect, because I understand it is
non-political and non-sectarian.
Why
there should be any objection to this flag
I do not know. I think the hon. Senator
might be better engaged in putting down.
a question drawing attention to the
poverty in Northern Ireland rather than
directing attention to something that
might irritate people and cause disturbance and dissatisfaction in other parts of
the country.
Mr. LONG: The primary consideration
governing the action of the police in cases
of the kind referred to by the hon.
Senator is that of the maintenance of
law and order, and where any republican
or other flag is flown in circumstances
which would be likely to endanger the
public peace the police in the discharge
of their duty take the necessary steps
for the removal of such flag, and will
continue to do so.
The hon. Senator may rest assured that
the Government is not prepared to accept
the dictation of the Gaelic Athletic Association or any other organisation in this
respect.
That answer is an answer also to the
supplementary question by Senator Campbell.
Mr. McMAHON: Arising out of that
reply, would the Deputy Leader of the
House inform us what republican flag he
is speaking of?
Housing Bill.
Order read for the House to be put
into Committee.
Resolved: "That this House do now
resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Mr. Long.)
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSE 1 (Amendment of s. 1 of 22
& 23 Geo. 5, c. 3, as to grants in respect
of construction of houses)-
*
*
(3) A general resolution may be
adopted by a local authority, rural
district council or county council under
and in pursuance of sub-section (9) of
section one of the Housing Act (Northern Ireland), 1923, or section three of
the Housing Act (Northern Ireland),
1925, and applying to houses in the
area of the local authority or in the
rural district (as the case may require)
in respect of whose construction the
Ministry will, after the adoption of such
resolution, have power to make grants
by virtue of sub-sections (1) and (2)
of this section:
Housing Bill—
Con.mittee
2 NOVEMBER 1933
Provided that, for the purposes of
any such general resolution, the grants
shall be at the rate of twenty-five
pounds in respect of each house constructed, and the period for which
exemption from rates shall be given
shall be ten years and loans free of interest shall not be made.
""

""
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause
1, sub-Clause (3) after be ("shall be
ten years") to insert "not less than five
or more than. The point I wanted to
raise when I put the Amendment on the
Paper was as to whether Parliament will
really be acting prudently in insisting so
to speak on its view that in the provision
of houses under this Clause Parliament
and the local authorities are to go at a
rate which is aproximately fifty-fifty.
It is generally agreed, I think, that the
subsidy of £25 inserted in the Bill is
supposed to approximate roughly to the
value of the rates for ten years. It seems
to me very questionable whether we shall
not be defeating our own object if we insist on a hard and fast limit of ten years
instead of allowing a local authority the
option of giving the exemption from rates.
for a somewhat shorter period.
It does not seem to me that any change
we make will make any difference to the
Exchequer except in so far as it may
encourage the building of more houses, and
that, I imagine, is the object in view. I
do not think it will really make any
difference to the tenant of the house, for
although at first sight it may seem to be
a great advantage to a man to get an
exemption from his rates for ten years
instead of five, because it would be in
effect a reduction of rent, we must bear in
mind the fact that the local authority will
have the option of fixing the rent at such
figure as they may think fit, subject to the
approval of the Ministry. I think we
cannot dismiss from our minds the fact
that the local authority in fixing the rent
will take into account that there is exemption from rates for such-and-such a
period.
a
The whole problem is a little difficult.
because it seems to me that for local
authority to give an exemption for so long.
a period as ten years is distinctly a leap
in the dark, as during those ten years all
sorts of local questions may arise. It
may be a large question, such as the provision of waterworks, or it may be minor
matters that need not be gone into. The
first thing that occurs to one's mind is the
further provision of schools which may
arise very acutely if a new district is
formed in an urban district, or there is an
extension. These extensions may arise in
ten years, and although they may result.
in very serious burdens on ratepayers as a
whole, they will not necessarily be in the
minds of the local authority at the time
they give the exemption from rates which,
as the Bill stands, is limited to ten years.
Those of us who have served, perhaps,
longer than others in this House will
remember that we have had this question
before us from time to time. We have
had arguments in more or less the same
form as those now being put before us.
We have had the other point, what is
called the long view, that is to say that
we should look beyond the ten years to
the advantages which the rates will derive
at the end of the ten-year period when
the houses become productive from the
ratepayers' point of view. We have to
set those two questions, the one against
the other, and it seems to me that
opinions are extremely likely to differ.
The question is whether to give the local
authority the option of a shorter period
of exemption will not tend to induce the
local authority to give the exemption and
so get a grant and get the houses built,
while to make a hard and fast condition
that the exemption shall be for ten years,
which shall be approximately half and
half the other grant, might have the
opposite effect. Those are the two opposite
points, and I think the Committee ought
to decide between them. Meantime, I
beg to move the Amendment as printed
on the Paper.
Mr. LONG: The Government cannot
accept this Amendment. The object in
fixing on a period of ten years was to
find a period of exemption which would
be equivalent on the average to a cash
grant of £25, and having regard to the
usual valuation placed upon this type of
house, it may be taken that the annual
rates would average something between
£2 10s and £3 per annum. It is true,
of course, that the rate varies in different
districts according to the needs of the
district, but it appears to be better to
have a fixed period based on an average
than to have a period varying from district to district.
It must, of course, be borne in mind
that the grant of £25 is paid at once and
the builder has the immediate benefit,
whereas where the benefit is in the form
of exemption from rates it is spread over
Housing Bill—
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
a period of years, and, therefore, to be
equivalent to a cash grant the total cash
benefit under the exemption from rates
must exceed the amount of the subsidy
which is paid immediately. The hon. and
learned Senator, who has moved the
Amendment, has suggested that we should
take a long view of the matter. My submission is that this is taking a long view
of the matter.
Mr. LESLIE : I do not think that
anybody else cares the least about it, and
I shall ask leave to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. GYLE: I beg to move in Clause 1,
at end of paragraph (b) to add:
"(c) Any such general resolution
shall provide that the workmen engaged
on
such house, work the agreed
building trade hours and under agreed
building trade conditions and are paid
the agreed standard rate of wages as
obtain in the district in which such
house is constructed, and that the house
is constructed by recognised building
trade operatives and by recognised
builder.
'Recognised building trade operative' means a craftsman who has served
his time to his trade and is employed in
that trade only, or an apprentice to
such trade or labourer who has been
employed in the building trade for a
period of not less than two years.
'Recognised builder' means a person or body of persons or a local authority having an established building connection with a yard and all the necessary
plant and staff for the carrying out of
building contracts.'
I do not wish to reiterate the arguments
which I put before the Senate on the
Second Reading of the Bill. At the same
time, I wish to impress upon this Committee that a very large and important
class of the community ask that this
Amendment should be inserted in the Bill.
The National Federation of Building
Trade Operatives have asked that this
Clause should be put into the Bill in
order to safeguard their interests, and
more especially the interests of those
people who will occupy the houses to be
built, by seeing that they are properly
built. Under the last Housing Bill
speculative builders took advantage of the
subsidy, which amounted to something
as
like £140, and brought in what my hon.
Friend on my left described to me
hedge carpenters. We want to try to prevent that class of men getting control of
the building of these small houses, because
we want to have really good houses fit for
the people to live in.
are
I believe that the Government
really in earnest in trying to bring about
a condition in which the lower-paid classes
of the community will have houses that
are worth living in, houses with baths and
all the other conveniences that should be
Not
made applicable to those people.
alone do the operatives-that is the
plumbers, the bricklayers, the carpenters,
and all the other people in the Federation
-want this Amendment, but the master
builders themselves think the proper
course would be to have it inserted in the
Bill.
The reason, as far as I can gather, why
the master builders want to have this
Clause placed in the Bill is this: If we
take a town like Belfast we know there is
organisation, that there are building inspectors and all sorts of men whose duty
it is to see that building is properly carried out and the contracts properly
finished so as to enable the builders to get
the subsidy, but in some of the smaller
towns there is no organisation to see that
the houses are properly built. We know
there are many men in some of the smaller
towns and villages of Northern Ireland
who carry out work of this description,
but they do not carry it out in the best
possible way. They are really good,
handy men at building byres and pigstyes and that sort of thing, but their work
is not sufficiently good for the building
of houses for people to live in, and so we
desire that the Government should put
into this Bill something which, if it is
not in the exact terms of the Amendment
set down, would agree with the principle
of the Amendment. If they do that I
will be satisfied.
We feel that something must be done
to prevent slum property being erected
by speculative builders. That is really my
point in connection with my Amendment.
We want some sort of supervision, and
we believe that if this Amendment, or
a similar Amendment in other words, is
put into the Bill it will be sufficient safeguard. I do now wish to labour this
point. I believe that the Government are
very sympathetic and are really desirous
of having houses built properly for the
working-class people. I am quite satisfied
Housing Bill—
Committee
2 NOVEMBER 1933
of that, but unfortunately they do not
put in the necessary safeguards to prevent speculators from trading on their
kindness, if I may put it that way. I do
not think, under the circumstances, that
I should detain the Committee any
longer, except to say that the operatives,
the builders, and the architects,
experts in matters of housing, feel they
are giving the Government good advice.
as
I
Mr. McMAHON: I would like to
associate myself with the appeal that has
been made by the hon. Senator, who has
just sat down, that we might have something inserted in this Bill which would
be a protection for the skilled artisan
engaged in this particular industry.
think the Government should listen to the
appeal made to them to protect the
skilled artisan. After all, he made
tremendous sacrifices in becoming a skilled
artisan. His parents in his early youth
might have gained a great deal more
income from his activities in connection
with his work if he was not becoming
a skilled artisan. We must remember
that when he was a boy he had in all
probability to start at 2/6 or 3/- a week,
which was the limit at the time the
artisans of to-day were serving their
time.
That was a great sacrifice. He has
now become skilled, and as a skilled man
his work will be of the best class. In the
erection of houses we cannot have too
many skilled artisans. In connection
with the suggestion that only proper contractors should be allowed to erect houses,
I think there is a great point in that also.
It might be asked how a proper contractor would be described. I would say
that a competent contractor would be a
man who has all the necessary tools and
a builder's yard, and has been in business
for some considerable time.
I have been told that during the building boom certain foremen engaged by
certain contractors in Belfast started to
erect houses themselves. They erected
these houses after hours. The men who
were engaged during the day under them
were asked to come along and assist in
the erection of those houses. Of course
those men may not have wished to work
at the houses after hours, but there was
the danger that if they did not comply
with the foreman's request they might
lose their jobs. That was very unfair to
the contractor, who, if he wanted overtime worked had to pay the recognised
overtime rates and, in addition, had to
pay for the upkeep of his yard and so
forth, whereas the individual who could
command a certain number of men without keeping up an establishment could
start to erect houses at a great deal less
cost than the ordinary contractor.
With regard to seeing that fair wages
and conditions of employment are
observed, I would remind the Government that if they fall behind in this
matter they will be falling far behind
the local authorities. For instance, the
local authority in Belfast, even in ordinary contracts even if it is only for a
ton of tram lines and so forth-insist that
fair conditions must prevail in the firms
from which those rails are ordered. No
contract is given out by the local authority in Belfast in which the contractor has
not to sign, and his sureties must sign
also an undertaking that he will recognise
the full trade union rates and all the
conditions attached to the principle of
trade Unionism. If that is a good policy
for the local authorities to adopt it should
not be a bad policy for the Government
to adopt. I would make a special appeal
to the Government to accept the Amendment proposed by Senator Gyle, or if
they cannot accept it in its entirety let
us at least have some compromise.
There
But
Sir DUDLEY MCCORKELL: I do not
wish to enter into controversy with regard
to this Amendment as a whole.
may be something to be said for it, there
may be something to be said against it,
and it remains for the Government to say
whether they can accept it or not.
there are one or two items which, perhaps,
the mover of the Amendment himself
might consider carefully, even if the
Government are going to assist in the
I do suggest that the words
"or labourer who has been employed
in the building trade for a period of
not less than two years"
matter.
may raise difficulties of a most extraordinary kind. In the first place, how
are we going to know? Does that mean
a labourer who in the whole course of
his natural life has done two years' work
in the building trade, or does it mean a
labourer who has been consecutively employed for two years in the building trade,
or what does it mean? I suggest that if
this part of the Amendment is carried
to its logical conclusion it means that no
workman can get a job in the building
trade at all, even though all the other
Housing Bill—
Committee
THE SENATE
[Sir Dudley McCorkell]
old gentlemen who have been employed
in the building trade are drawing the oldage pension or are dead, and it seems
to me to rule out the chance of giving
some very deserving people work.
The next point, perhaps, is not of so
very great importance, but it seems to
me that every recognised builder must
have had a start somewhere. He did not
come into this world with an established
building connection and a yard, even
though he may have inherited the latter
from somebody else, and it seems to me
to rule out people who may have started
on their own account, lock, stock, and
barrel. But particularly am I exercised
about that condition about the ordinary
labourer, who is assisting the craftsmen
or the artisans, not being allowed to work
if he has not previously worked for two
years in the building trade. It is, in my
opinion, going to deprive a lot of very
honest men of the chance of getting work.
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH: My
difficulty about this Amendment is that,
while I am in agreement with a good deal
of it, I certainly cannot support it as it
stands. The idea apparently is, in the
first place, to ensure that the fair wages
clause will be observed in the building
of houses to which the Government and
the local authorities contribute. I take
that to be the object of the first part of
the Amendment. I just wish to point
out that the local authorities have power
to enforce these regulations before they
pay over the £25 subsidy.
Mr. McMAHON: Only in so far as it
applies to their own building work.
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH: As
far as the Corporation of Belfast are
concerned, they do, as the hon. Member
has said, insert that fair wage clause in
all their contracts, and I have no doubt
whatever that the same clause will be inserted in the regulations made by the
Belfast Corporation under this Bill.
But apparently certain of the operatives
want this Amendment, and the builders
themselves want it, because on the 2nd
October the Belfast Builders' Association
requested the Belfast Corporation that in
any regulations they should make this
clause should be inserted, and they said
that they were going to ask the Government to have it put in the Bill.
While I say it is not necessary to put
it in the Bill at all, as far as I am personally concerned I do not object to it,
though it is going to lead to some unpleasantness and trouble. I do not think
it is at all necessary but, as far as that
part of the Amendment is concerned, I
want to make it quite clear that I am
absolutely and entirely in agreement
with the idea of it. But if the hon.
Member reads on he will see that the
Amendment would put the local authorities, and certainly the Government, in a
very awkward position. I do not see how
it could possibly be carried out. If you
read on you will see that it says:
"under agreed building trade conditions and are paid the agreed standard
rate of wages as obtain in the district
in which such house is constructed-—-—____”,
If the Amendment were to stop there
there would be some commonsense in it
and it would be workable. but it goes on
to say:
"and that the house is constructed by
recognised building trade operatives and
by recognised builder."
Can anyone here tell me what a recognised
builder is? If my hon. Friend—and I
asked him the question outside-were to
take advantage of this Bill and build a
street of houses himself, he is not a recognised builder. He could not claim to be a
recognised builder, and certainly he could
not claim the subsidy either from the Government or the Corporation.
Again, if any recognised tradesman
wished to build a house for himself he
could not do it under that. Assuming
that this is put into the Bill as it is, such
a man could not claim the subsidy because
he is not a recognised builder.
In my
opinion, while there might be a Clause such
as the hon. Member suggests, although I
say it is unnecessary, if the House and the
Government are agreeable there might be
a Clause put in somewhere or other in the
Bill to cover the difficulty. But to go
the length suggested in the two last paragraphs of the Amendment would, in my
opinion, be absolutely wrong and unworkable and would lead to no end of trouble.
As the Amendment stands to-day,
although I am in favour of part of it, I
am bound to and certainly will vote
against it.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: The
Lord Mayor has referred to the matter in
the Amendment in which I am particularly interested. If this had been introduced by any other hon. Member than
Senator Gyle or Senator McMahon I
Housing Bill-
Committee
2 NOVEMBER 1933
would certainly have said immediately
that it was class legislation. Here they
are putting forward an Amendment requiring that only builders who have been
building for years-capitalists if you like.
-shall be allowed to build these houses
and to draw the subsidy. A joiner, a
bricklayer, or any other tradesman is
barred and prevented from attempting to improve his position in life.
by undertaking the building either
of a few houses or a number of
houses. During the last few years,
when trade has been so very bad in the
city of Belfast, I have noticed many cases
of where joiners have been able to obtain
just sufficient money and have undertaken
the building of two, four, or even six
houses. And it has always been for the
benefit of the community, because it has
produced the houses which the people required. Why should we put anything of
a restrictive nature in this Bill that would
be likely to prevent us from getting houses
built? It is houses that we want. We
do not care who is going to build them,
whether they be capitalists or decent
tradesmen.
I know the trades unions have put this
forward with the best intentions in the
world. I know they have done it for the
purpose of preventing unscrupulous employers from taking advantage of this
scheme. But it will never be by an Act
of Parliament that you will be able to
watch that. The trades unions are able
to watch it, and I do find it a great advantage to have been acquainted with the
work of trades unions, as I have been for
the last twenty years, when I hear the
remarks of the mover and seconder of this
Amendment, remarks which show how
little they really know about the work and
duties of the trades unions. If you leave
it to the trades unions I am perfectly
satisfied that they will see that proper
conditions and hours are maintained.
As the Lord Mavor of Belfast has
pointed out most local authorities insist
that the fair wages clause is inserted in
all building schemes, so that, as far as the
local authorities are concerned, it is quite
all right. But you have to remember that
there are some out-of-the-way districts.
where the people require houses just as
much as they do in the city of Belfast,
where there are no trades unions, and
where the men-good men, too; not men
who merely make byres-are prepared to
work at the building of houses. Many
good men have come to the city of Belfast
after having served their time to the
joinery trade in the country. In fact, it
is such men who have made up the trade
in the shipyards and elsewhere in Belfast.
And, whether these men have served their
times at byres or even at wheelbarrows,
they will probably undertake the building
of houses; and I am told that they require
houses just as much as and in some cases
very much more than we do in industrial
places like Belfast.
Then our hon. Friends have said that
they want to prevent slum property being
erected. But all these houses are inspected and examined by a Government
inspector before the subsidy is paid.
Mr. McMAHON: Not a Government
inspector, a Corporation inspector.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
think the Leader of the House will tell
us that the Government have an inspector
who examines these houses before the
subsidy is paid over. The Government
undertakes the inspection of these houses
to see that they are erected correctly and
in a workmanlike manner. I do not
really see any necessity for the Amendment. I have great sympathy with the
trades unions, and I can claim to have
given them all the assistance one man
could possibly give over a number of
years. I would be only too ready to
support the Amendment in this case if
it were at all likely to benefit the trades
unions. But this is not going to benefit
the trades unions, and it is going to
restrict the supply of houses. I hope the
Amendment will be withdrawn, and that
there will be no vote on it, because I
think it would be injurious rather than
helpful.
that
Mr. BARCLAY: I am very sorry
I cannot vote for this Amendment as a
whole. I agree with the Lord Mayor of
Belfast that if it had stopped at providing
that the standard rates of wages should be
paid in the districts in which the houses
were built it would have been quite sufficient. If the standard rate of wages is
paid in any district the builder himself
will find the most suitable men to do the
building and any other work that is
necessary. It would seem to me that it
would be too much like dictating to the
local authorities or to the Government
who should be employed and who should
not be employed, when at the same time
they have to go to the general public
for the money to build the houses. I
Housing Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Barclay]
cannot agree with that at all. I believe
in acting fairly all round, and I believe
that the builder will get trade union men
to come forward to do the work. The
builder is not going to throw away good
money for bad workmanship. If he is
going to pay the standard rate of wages
he will try to find the men most capable
of doing the work that he requires to be
done. Therefore, on the whole, I cannot
support this Amendment.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I think there is
general agreement on the part of all
sections of the House that the intention
of the hon. Senator who has introduced
this Amendment is to ensure that these
houses will be built under trade union
conditions, and that the men engaged in
the building work will be paid trade union
wages. I do not think that at this time
of the day there can be a dispute in any
legislative body with regard to the wisdom
of that intention. The only point in
question in this House is does this
Amendment carry out that intention?
The Lord Mayor of Belfast, as I understood his speech, says he is in perfect
agreement with the first portion of the
Amendment.
We all agree, I hope,
that the men shall work under agreed
trade union conditions, and be paid the
agreed standard rate which obtains in the
district in which the houses are being constructed.
That is the major intention of this
Amendment, and I am perfectly certain
that if any of the latter part of the
Amendment is inconsistent with the
general intention expressed at the beginning, the hon. Senator will be willing
that the matter should be reconsidered
between this and the Report stage. Unless
the Government are prepared to accept
the principle underlying this Amendment
then this Bill will go through the House,
and there will be no guarantee whatever
that any of these houses will be put up
under trade union conditions. I say,
with all deference, that it is in the best
interests of the people at large that these
houses should be built by properlyqualified and skilled workmen. I also
think that most of the Imperial Government contracts of to-day contain a fair
wages clause.
I do not see any reason
why we should depart therefrom in this
House. It would be far better that the
Government who contribute this sum, and
the local councils who are interested in
the exemption from rating of these
houses, should set a headline, a standard,
for ordinary employers. I ask the Deputy
Leader of the House to indicate whether
or not he is prepared to accept the paramount principle underlying this Amendment or to reject it in toto.
Mr. LESLIE: We are really dealing
with a Bill intended to promote the provision of further houses for the working
classes, and this is an Amendment to one
of the Clauses of the Bill. I suppose I
shall be told, in saying what I am going to
say, that I am an unrepentant Conservative, and altogether a fossil of the most
complete description. I cannot help that.
I am firmly convinced in my own mind
that the application and the maintenance
of what are called trade union principles
are as much responsible as anything else.
for the shortage of houses for the working
classes which we experience to-day. I am
quite sure of that. I am also certain that
one of the explanations of the great growth
of national expenditure to-day-and I am
speaking, perhaps, more for the moment
of the conditions across the water-is the
fact that Parliament from time to time insists on the insertion of fair wages clauses.
Consequently, the complaint is made that,
while people with one breath lift up their
voices and say how horrible a thing it is
that this or that should cost such an
immense sum of money, with the other
they demand that Parliament shall put in
fair wages conditions and so on and so
on.
That is to say, they weight the dice
perpetually against the economists.
Again, passing from that, the suggestion
in the Amendment seems to me really
directed towards something in the nature
of Parliamentary recognition or re-establishment of the trade guilds of the Middle
Ages. Still as an unrepentant Conservative, I can see no public advantage whatever in such a course. It seems to me
that all that Parliament has any right to
require is expressed in paragraph (b) of
sub-Clause (2) of Clause 2 of the Bill,
that the houses are to be such as will
satisfy the Ministry as regards their construction and so on. I give the reference
in full in order that any hon. Senator who
wants to can look at it himself and see
exactly what is said, that the houses are
to be completed in a proper and workmanlike manner." That, I suggest, is all
that Parliament can rightly require, and
subject to that the local authorities
throughout the Province should be left
to manage their own affairs. By local
"
Housing Bill-
Committee
2 NOVEMBER 1933
authorities I do not mean to confine myself to the Corporation of Belfast. There
is a great deal too much, if I may say so,
of the suggestion that the housing problem
is entirely a Belfast problem. Those of
us who do not live in Belfast think somewhat differently, but there is a great tendency to regard the whole matter as a
Belfast problem. It is not.
It is common to the entire Province. Therefore,
I say that all the local authorities, including, of course, the Belfast Corporation,
should so far as possible be left to manage
their own affairs subject to the over-riding
conditions that the houses must be built to
the satisfaction of the Ministry, a satisfaction which can be derived either by
their own inspection or by accepting the
report of some thoroughly competent inspector appointed by the local authority.
That is a matter of administration with
which Parliament is not called upon to
deal. I shall be obliged, if this matter
goes to a vote, to oppose the Amendment.
Mr. LONG: This proposal goes infinitely further than any fair wages clause for
which there is any precedent. Not only
does it seek to impose conditions as to
hours and wages, but in effect it would
confine the employees to trades unionists
and the builders to members of builders'
associations.
In so far as it is within their power
the Government have encouraged fair
wages clauses. Such clauses are inserted
in their own contracts, and on the 22nd
March, 1928, the Ministry of Home
Affairs circularised local authorities urging
them to insert a similar clause in contracts for works to be carried out on their
behalf, and I understand that the majority
of local authorities do insert such clauses.
The conditions in the different parts
of the area vary very much indeed, and
what might be a suitable provision in the
City of Belfast or in the larger towns
might be entirely unsuitable in the rural
areas. There is, therefore, very great
difficulty in putting in any provision of
this character which would be of
general application. The matter is one
which can be very much more appropriately dealt with by the authorities in
each district, having regard to the circumstances obtaining there and the
practicability of enforcing such a condition.
This Bill makes provision enabling the
local authorities to impose conditions
which are to govern the payment of
grants, and under these provisions the
local authorities can deal with the matters to which this Amendment relate, if
they consider such a condition expedient
and could be administered in the district.
This appears to the Government to be a
very much more suitable way of dealing
with the matter than by amendment of the
Bill, and I am, therefore, unable to accept
the Amendment. I should like to emphasise the statement of Senator Joseph
Cunningham that the Ministry of Home
Affairs will supervise the building of the
houses up to the time of their completion.
Mr. GYLE: I am very sorry indeed
that the Government, after the expression of opinion in this House that this
Amendment, speaking generally, would
be acceptable to this House if couched in
difierent termsno.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: No,
Mr. Campbell said that.
Mr. GYLE: I listened to the speeches,
and I know that that was the general expression of opinion, and that the Amendment was moved in all good faith, that
it was moved by two Members who knew
little about trade union conditions--
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: That
is right.
Mr. GYLE:
or about anything
relating to the position of the speculative
builder. I want to disabuse my hon.
Friend's mind on that point. I know
that he has taken part in trade union
business practically all his life, but,
although I had not the privilege of being
a trade union member, not being brought
up to a craft that was able to have a
trade union, still I have always taken a
very intelligent interest in the trade
union movement, and I want to ask the
hon. Senator who sits on my left (Mr.
Joseph Cunningham) if this is not a trade
union application why should these
gentlemen, representing practically all the
trade unions of the city, the National
Federation of Building Trade Operatives,
the men who build the houses, sit in conclave, knowing exactly what they want,
and sponsor this Amendment? Still the
hon. Senator says, because it happens to
be proposed by one who is not a trade
unionist, that the operatives know nothing
at all about what they are asking.
I listened with great pleasure indeed to
the Lord Mayor of Belfast. I see from the
way that the Government have treated this
Housing Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Gyle.]
Amendment that we are not going to get
much in this House, and I should like to
put one question to the Lord Mayor.
Will he, before any payment of this subsidy is made, have declared by resolution
in the Corporation of Belfast that the
trade union fair wages clause--everything
practically that is in this Resolution-will
be passed by the Corporation? The Lord
Mayor very kindly said that he was
quite in sympathy with this Amendment,
and I should like him to give an undertaking in this House that that will be
done. Then we will have moved forward
to some extent so far as the city of Belfast is concerned.
My point-and I tried to make it perfectly clear, although I may not have
made it as clear as I should have done-is
that while we are almost satisfied so far
as Belfast is concerned, there are no
organisations in the country districts to
see that the fair wages clause is observed.
I was prepared to meet the Deputy-Leader
of the House on this matter, to try to get
something put into this Bill that would
prevent speculative builders, either in
Belfast or in the country, using this subsidy for their own profit and building slum
property, engaging "hedge carpenters,"
as my Friend called them.
I say this, and I have no fear in saying
it, that if this Bill goes through as it is
and there is no compromise made and
nothing inserted in it, we will have the
same conditions prevailing in the country
places as we had in Belfast under the last
subsidy Bill. We will have speculative
builders grabbing the subsidy and putting
it in their pockets and building houses
which are hardly fit for people to live
in, so that in ten years' time you will
be coming to another place and asking for
money to pull down slum property.
Again I ask the Deputy Leader of the
House to reconsider the matter and perhaps in conjunction with the employers
and trade unions try to arrive at some
form of words that might be inserted in
the Bill to safeguard the interests of those
with whom almost every Member of
this House has proclaimed his sym-
Campbell, Mr. T. J.
Gyle, Mr. J.
The Lord Mayor of Belfast.
The Mayor of Londonderry.
Armstrong, Mr. Henry B.
Barclay, Mr. William.
Clark, Sir G. S.
CONTENT.
McAllister, Mr. T. S.
McLaughlin, Mr. T.
I
pathy. ask him would he not
listen to a deputation from this
Association and try to come to an
arrangement whereby some form of words
may be inserted in the Bill that would
arrive at the conditions we want to arrive
at, that is that the subsidy will not be
used for the purpose of building slum
property and speculative building. I
would ask the Deputy Leader would he
not reconsider this matter and do as I
suggest.

Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH:
The hon. Member has asked me a question
which it would be absolutely impossible
for me to answer. I had hoped I made
myself clear in the statement I made.
said then and say now-I am speaking
more particularly of Belfast than of the
other counties-that as far as this Amendment is concerned it is not necessary
because the local authorities have the
power to insert this fair wages clause.
My hon. Friend asked me to give a
guarantee. That would be impossible,
but I will go as far as to say that I will
do my best to see that the clause is inserted in our regulations. As far as the
other areas are concerned I do not want
to deal with them as I have no knowledge
of them. I am merely dealing with
something I know. I will do my best.
Indeed, I can almost promise that the
clause will be inserted.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: You
will not promise that only the big builders
will be allowed to build?
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH: Certainly not.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I did
not think so.
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH: I
am only referring to the fair wages clause
as affecting Belfast-
The CHAIRMAN: Does the hon.
Member wish to press his Amendment?
Mr. GYLE: Oh, yes.
Question put: "That those words be
there added."

Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Payment of grants where
conditions are improved by local authority).
2. (1) A local authority or rural district council may resolve that the grant
or exemption from rates to be given by
the authority or council under section
one of this Act in respect of an approved
house may be made subject to such conditions as the authority or council may,
with the approval of the Ministry, impose, including a condition that for a
period of three years the house shall not
be let at a rent in excess of that fixed
by the authority or council with the
approval of the Ministry.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:

pro-
In page 3, after line 4 (" with the
approval of the Ministry ") insert
vided that no exemption shall be given in
respect of any item of poor rate raised to
meet a charge in connection with compensation for criminal injuries."
Mr. LESLIE: I am not moving the
Amendment on the Paper because,
although I think the principle that the
Amendment suggests is right, the Committee will recognise that the setting of
the Clause is not altogether appropriate
for a complete and novel procedure as
regards exemption from liability for
criminal injury. Since we started on the
campaign for the procuring of better housing we have derated agricultural land
generally, but left it underated in respect
of claims for compensation for malicious
or criminal injury, and, therefore, it is
illogical that newly-built houses, although
exempt from ordinary rates, should have
exemption from that special class of rate.
Perhaps there may be some more favourable occasion for asking Parliament to
consider that matter. I do not think that
this afternoon is the appropriate occasion.
Therefore, I do not propose the Amendment.
Amendment not moved.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Provisions as to rents of
houses constructed by local authorities).
(2) For the purposes of sub-section (3)
of section eleven of the Housing (Ireland) Act, 1919 (as amended by
section one of the Housing Acts
Amendment Act (Northern Ireland),
1924), an approved house let at a rent
fixed by a local authority with the
approval of the Ministry shall be
deemed to be let at the best rent
that can be reasonably obtained.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page
4, line 30, after "house" ("an approved
house to
house") insert "including a
which paragraph (d) of sub-section 2 of
section two of this Act applies?
The object of this small and simple
Amendment is to secure for the new
pattern of approved houses of something
over 550 superficial area the same protection as that given in Clause 3, subsection 2, to
to the ordinary approved
houses, that is the protection in regard
to the rent charged being considered and
accepted as the best obtainable rent
which is fixed by the local authority, subject to the approval of the Ministry.
Apart from some additional words as I
suggest these new approved houses between 550 and 600 superficial feet would
not have that advantage.
Mr. LONG: This Amendment clears up
a small point about which there might be
some doubt, and I am therefore prepared to accept it.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

purpose of increasing housing accommodation), and 5 (Power of the Ministry
to reimburse part of losses sustained
under guarantees) ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
CLAUSE 6 (Power of local authorities
to borrow for purpose of making
advances).
(1) The raising of money for the
purpose of making advances in pursuance of section two of this Act shall
be a purpose for which—
(a) a local authority may borrow
for the purposes of Part III of the
Housing of the Working Classes Act,
1890.
Housing Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I beg to move,
in page 6, line 36, after "advances” (“of
making advances') insert "and grants.'
I hope this Amendment will meet with
the approval of the Committee. It is
with regard to urban councils and rural
councils who may not be able out of their
budget in a particular year to give a grant
for a number of houses that might be
erected. For instance, in Armagh in
one particular year we had 100 houses
erected under the Act and had to give a
hundred grants which would mean a rate
of 2s 6d in the £. Therefore, I do suggest that the Government should allow
urban and rural councils to procure loans
for the grants and that we should insert
after the word "advances," "and
grants."
Mr. LESLIE: I do not want to anticipate
the Parliamentary Secretary, but I would
like him to say in answer whether I am
correct in assuming that so far as grants
are concerned the local authority has
already power to borrow, as is suggested
by the Amendment. As regards the
second part of the Clause, I have some
slight difficulty, and that is whether
under the second part of it money borrowed by the authority or council will or
will not have to be reckoned as part of
the total. I am quite satisfied that the
position is already safe as regards grants
and with the first part of the Clause, but
I am not satisfied about the second. That
is what I would like the Parliamentary
Secretary to tell us, perhaps, at a later
stage.
Mr. LONG: I think that the difficulty
which the honourable Senator sees in this
Clause does not exist. Local authorities
are authorised to borrow for the payment
of grants under Section 1, Sub-section (9)
of the Housing Act of 1923, which is still
in force. Clause 6 is inserted in this Bill
for the purpose of enabling local authorities to borrow in connection with the new
methods of giving assistance, namely, by
the lending of money to builders.
borrowing for the purpose of making
grants is already provided for, it is unnecessary to provide for it in this Clause.
I, therefore, cannot accept the Amendment. I should like to reply to the question raised by the hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Leslie) at a later stage.
As
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: As there is
already power given to the local authorities in this respect, I beg to withdraw the
Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.

to make contribution towards interest

SCHEDULE:
(7) References in this section and in
section three of this Act to the payment
of a building grant shall be construed
as references to the payment thereof by
the authority or council, under paragraph (c) of sub-section (2) of section
two of the Housing Act (Northern Ireland), 1933, to the person or body of
persons constructing the house.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page
10, line 43, after the first section"
("References in this section') insert "except in sub-section 1 thereof."
If the Committee will look at the first
part of the Schedule they will see reference to the Ministry of Home Affairs, and
it is necessary to save this reference by a
few words of explanation when we come
to paragraph 7. Accordingly, I am suggesting to insert in the appropriate place
except in sub-section 1 thereof.'
Mr. LONG: I am prepared to accept
this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.

Electricity (Supply) Bill.
Order for the House to be put into Committee read.
Resolved: "That the House do now
resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Mr. Long.)
489 Electricity (Supply) Bill-

2 NOVEMBER 1933
Committee
CLAUSES 1 (Use of transformers), 2
(Area of supply of electricity) and 3
(Amendment as to serving notice of
works on local authority), ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 4 (Recovery of payments
due for apparatus let on hire).
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I beg to move
to add at the end of Clause 4:
"Provided that the undertakers shall
not cut off or discontinue any supply of
electricity to any person where, in the
opinion of the Ministry of Commerce, a
bona-fide dispute exists between any
undertakers and such person as to any
such sum claimed to be due to the undertakers by such person."
The object of this Amendment is to give
the consumer some protection in a case
where there is a bona-fide dispute regarding an account furnished by an authorised
undertaker or any official of an authorised
undertaker. It is to ensure that a particular official should not have the power
to cut off the supply to a consumer without first having obtained the sanction of
the Ministry. My contention is that there
might in some part of the Six Counties,
be a dispute between a consumer and an
official of an authorised electricity company regarding an account furnished by
the company, and I want to ensure that
before the light is cut off the Ministry concerned should be consulted and give their
decision as to whether the supply should
be cut off or not. There have been many
disputes regarding accounts, but my
Amendment is only intended to cover the
case of a bona-fide dispute.
I am not concerned about the person
who deliberately holds back the payment
of his account, but I am speaking on behalf of the person who have a bonamay
fide objection to paying
to paying a bill on the
ground, possibly, that some part of it has.
been paid before, or that it includes
charges for the hire of appliances which
have been installed in the consumer's premises without his instructions. In the case
of such a dispute, the undertaker, manager
or any official should not have power to
cut off the light until he has first placed
the matter before the Ministry concerned.
Mr. LONG: Careful consideration has
been given to the Amendment which
appears on the Order Paper in the name
of Senator McLaughlin. Whilst the Government appreciate the confidence reposed
in the Ministry of Commerce by the hon.
Senator, I would ask him to consider that
it is the business and, in fact, the prime
interest of an electricity undertaker to
sell as much electricity as he can. He
would, accordingly, be very reluctant to
do anything which would lose him a customer, and, in normal cirmustances, it is
unlikely that supplies would be cut off
unless all ordinary remedies had failed.
In point of fact, cases of the cutting off
of electricity brought to the Ministry's
notice have been very rare.
The hon. Senator will observe that both
in the Clause of the Bill as it stands, and
in the Clause of the 1882 Act to which it
refers, power is given to the undertaker
to cut off the supply only where the consumer "neglects" to pay arrears. Clearly
where there is a bona-fide dispute as to
payment in progress, the undertaker could
have no cause to cut off the supply on
the ground of neglect.
In the absence of any substantial evidence that authorised undertakers are
abusing their power of cutting off supplies and of any evidence that any such
abuse cannot be dealt with adequately
under the present law, there would appear
to be no necessity to seek the intervention
of the Ministry of Commerce in disputes
of this kind between authorised undertakers and consumers. It would obviously
be inadvisable that the Ministry should be
required to intervene in the day-to-day
administration of the Electricity Supply
Acts in regard to consumers' accounts for
electrical energy. In view of these considerations I regret that the Government
cannot accept the hon. Senator's Amendment.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I am rather
surprised at the answer given by the
Deputy-Leader of the House. My
Amendment is directed only to those
cases where there is a bona fide dispute
between a consumer and an authorised
undertaker, and where the consumer is
prepared to lodge the amount in dispute
with a responsible third party. I do
know of cases where there has been a
dispute between a consumer and an
official of an undertaker and where the
offer of the consumer to lodge the amount
in dispute with a responsible third party
was ignored, and the light was cut off.
The result is that such a gentleman is
looked upon as a man who has refused
to pay his electricity bill. I want the
Electricity (Supply) Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
from
[Mr. McLaughlin]
Ministry to protect such a man
having his light cut off when he is prepared to lodge the sum in dispute with a
third party pending the settlement of the
dispute.
Mr. LESLIE: May 1 suggest another
possible reading of this Clause. In the
case of a bona fide dispute arising between
a consumer and a supplier, I cannot for
the life of me see why the consumer
should not say to his supplier, "I shall
not pay.
I shall lodge the money in dispute in the County Court," which is the
proper place to lodge it. That could not
lead to the cutting off of the supply. The
supply is only to be cut off if the person
neglects to pay any sum due. In the
event of a real dispute the money is not
due until a court of competent jurisdiction has decided it is due.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: But what if the
light is cut off in the meantime?
Mr. LESLIE: Then the suppliers
would be liable for a claim for damages,
and the damages would probably be such
that they would not do it again.
Mr. CAMPBELL: But how is the
consumer to lodge the money in court?
I do not know of any method by which
he could do so.
Mr. LESLIE: Of course, he need not
if he does not want to.
Mr. CAMPBELL: He may be able to
do such a thing under this Bill, but I do
not know of any machinery whereby he
could lodge money in court.
Mr. LESLIE: I was only giving that
as an example of how the matter might
be dealt with. Of course, you cannot
lodge money in court like you can lodge
it in a bank or in the Post Office Savings
Bank.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I am afraid the
answer given to Senator McLaughlin is
not very logical.
Mr. LONG: I can only repeat what I
have already said; that in the view of
the Government the point is adequately
dealt with under the existing law.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: In my view,
where there is a bona fide dispute or even
in a case where proceedings are pending
for the recovery of an account furnished,
and which is in dispute, no undertaker
or official should be allowed to cut off
the supply until the dispute has been
decided either by the county court or
some competent authority. If the
Deputy Leader of the House will give me
an assurance that the matter will be
further looked into with a view to safeguarding the interests of consumers in
this respect I will withdraw the Amendment.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
would suggest that the Government
should give an undertaking to consider
further the point with a view to dealing
with it on the Report stage, if it is
thought necessary to do so.
Mr. LONG: I am sorry I could not
undertake to do so.
Question put, "That those words be
there added."
The Committee divided: Content, 5;
Not Content, 12.

Gyle, Mr. J.

CLAUSES 5 (Explanation of powers of
the Ministry of Commerce under s. 37
(8) of 21 & 22 Geo. 5, c. 9) and 6 (Attach-
McMahon, Mr. J.
Leslie, Mr. James G.
Long, Mr. John A.
Quigley, Mr. A.
Sinclair, Colonel Thomas
ment of fixtures to buildings for carrying

CLAUSE 7 (By-laws as to wires
apparatus and fittings).
493 Electricity (Supply) Bill

2 NOVEMBER 1933
Adjournment
(3) The undertakers may by any bylaws made under this section impose on
offenders against the same such reasonable penalties as they think fit not exceeding the sum of five pounds for each
offence and in the case of a continuing
offence a further penalty not exceeding
forty shillings for each day after written
notice of the offence from the undertakers and such penalties may be recovered in a summary manner.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause
7, Sub-clause (3) at the end to add:
"Provided that all such by-laws imposing
any penalty shall be so framed as to allow
of the recovery of any sum less than the
full amount of the penalty." The Committee will probably recognise that that
Sub-clause is really our little friend
Section 220 of the Public Health Act,
1878, slightly altered. The only thing is
they have left out the concluding words as
they appear in the Public Health Act, and
these words ought to be inserted. Unless
we insert the words on the Paper there
seems to be some danger of something in
the nature of a fixed penalty being prescribed by the by-law-making authority.
That is not, I think the intention of the
Government and it does not meet with
my own approval.
There are different ways of dealing with
the situation. One would be to cut out
the whole sub-section and introduce a
new one. I think the Committee will
agree that as the procedure generally
follows that of the Public Health Act
the words of the enactment should also
follow the wording. Consequently I
trust the Committee will agree to the
words on the Paper.
Mr. LONG: The Government are prepared to accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 8 (Penalty for interfering
with works), 9 (Interference with works
with intent to cut off supply of electricity), 10 (Application of Act to the
Electricity Board), and 11 (Short title,
construction and citation) ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
Preamble and Title agreed to.

Drainage Bill.
