Oral Answers
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 7th November, 1933.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Papers Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Agriculture:
The Marketing of Potatoes Rules
(Northern Ireland), 1933, No. 2.
Ministry of Finance:
The Stormont Estate Regulations,
1933.
The Registrar-General's Annual Report for 1932, containing General
Abstract of Births, Deaths and Marriages
registered in Northern Ireland during the
year.
Ministry of Home Affairs:
Regulation 22b under the Civil
Authorities (Special Powers) Act (Northern Ireland), 1922.
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act), and ordered to lie on the Table.
Business of the House.
Resolved, "That Standing Order No.
XXV be suspended for this day's
sitting for the purpose of taking the
Third Reading of the Housing Bill and
the Electricity (Supply) Bill."-(Mr.
Long).
Questions to Minister.
CLEARANCE OF SLUM AREAS.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether he can
state the number of unhealthy areas
cleared or improved, and the number of
unsanitary dwelling-houses demolished or
repaired under the Housing and Town
Planning Act, 1931.
Mr. LONG: No schemes for clearance
of areas have yet been put forward by
Local Authorities under the powers conferred on them by the Planning and
Housing Act of 1931. This does not,
however, mean that nothing has been
done in the meantime to improve the
condition of unsanitary dwellings.
The sanitary authorities throughout the
area are continuously exerting pressure
on the owners of such property to have
them put in proper repair and improvements made. I am not able to give the
figures asked for by the hon. and learned
Member, as it is part of the ordinary
duty of the authorities to deal with these
matters, and individual cases do not
come before the Ministry of Home Affairs.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Is it not a fact
that the net result is that the Act is a
dead letter?
Mr. LONG: I do not think the hon.
and learned Member is entitled to come
to any such conclusion.
Mr.
McMAHON: I would like to
point out to the Deputy Leader of the
House that we have been told from many
platforms, and in particular in this
House and in another place, that we
follow step by step with England, but
here is one particular instance where we
are out of step. Some two years ago
the British Parliament passed an Act for
slum clearance, and the Northern Government, in pursuance of their step by step
policy, also passed an Act for the same
object, but with this difference: that
this Government made no financial provision in their Act to enable the local
authorities to find the wherewithal for
the clearance of slums. It may be said,
and I know it has been said in this
House, that there are no slums in Belfast. I say there are slums in Belfast
just as there are in other cities, and I
would suggest to the Deputy Leader of
this House that the Act passed by this
Government fell very far behind the
English Act.
So far as the clearance of slums is concerned, this Government made that Act
a dead letter when they made no financial
provision to enable local authorities to
clear slum areas. When and how are
slums in Northern Ireland going to be
cleared. I fail to see how a local
authority can undertake the clearance of
slums in its area. It has been suggested
in this House that there are houses that
in a few years will become slums if some
thing is not done to save them. If the
Government had inserted in their Bill
some financial provision whereby it would
have been possible to renovate houses
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
7TH NOVEMBER 1933
that are to-day decaying for want of
repair, they would have been doing
something to solve the housing problem
in Northern Ireland. But the Government has failed to do anything practical
so far as slum clearance is concerned,
and they will continue to fail until they
make some financial provision whereby
local authorities can clear unhealthy
areas.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I am
very much interested in seeing unhealthy
houses replaced by good healthy dwellings. It is quite true that while our
Government passed an Act to enable local
authorities to remove slums they did not
make any financial provision, as they did
in England, for that work to be undertaken. In fairness to the Government,
however, I want to point out that the
Ministry of Home Affairs informed local
authorities that once they had their plans
prepared and submitted them to that
Ministry they would see what financial
assistance they could give. Therefore I am
satisfied that the Ministry of Home Affairs
is not in any way callous as to the position
of tenants occupying unhealthy houses.
Indeed, I think the local authorities
themselves might move a little faster in
this matter than they have done. They
seem to be somewhat dilatory about providing better homes for the people, and
I do not think it is fair that the Government should be blamed because the local
authorities neglect their duty in this
respect.
Mr. LESLIE: Is that not really what
it comes to? There is a double question
involved. First there is the question of
responsibility, and the primary responsibility is surely on the local authority.
Secondly, there is the question of funds.
The local authorities have powers to raise
very considerable funds for application in
this direction if they see fit or are provoked to see fit by the action of their
own constituents. I do not think it is
a matter for the central Government in
the first instance. The Town Planning
Act did give local authorities very considerable liberty, but it is quite true, as
has just been said, that that particular
Act did not provide any funds from the
resources of the taxpayers' pocket-that
is to say the ordinary resources open to
the Government as such as opposed to
the local government.
EXPENDITURE ON RATES AND
TAXES.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether it is a fact
that about one-third of the income of
the people of Northern Ireland is expended each year on British and Northern
Ireland taxes and on local rates.
Mr. LONG: The hon. and learned
Member is no doubt well aware that a
precise answer to his question would
require a great deal of investigation,
which cannot at the moment be carried
out, and, in any event, would only result
in an expression of intelligent opinion,
because there are so many factors about
which economists are not in agreement.
However, rather than give no information on the subject, I should like to
say that the hon. and learned Member's
estimate of the proportion of the national
income of Northern Ireland which is extracted from the pockets of the people
in the form of rates or taxes is much too
high. I have no reason to believe that
the figure for Northern Ireland differs
greatly from that assessed in respect of
the United Kingdom by the Committee
on National Debt, which reported six
years ago, and gave an estimate of under
20 per cent.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Can the Deputy
Leader of the House say whether taxation per head of the population in
Northern Ireland is higher than in Great
Britain?
Mr. McMAHON: I think the question
asked by the hon. and learned Senator
(Mr. Campbell) is one that might have
been more fully answered by the Deputy
Leader of the House. Again we are out
of step with England, because taxes in
Northern Ireland are higher than they
are in England. In my opinion the local
authorities here are bearing a greater
burden than are the local authorities in
England. Take, for instance, the taxes
on motor lorries. They are a great deal
heavier in Northern Ireland than they
are in England. Why that should be so
I do not know, because the commercial
men who have got to employ lorries to
deliver their goods and chattels all over
the Six Counties have in many cases to
compete with English merchants. Yet
the Northern Ireland merchant has to pay
a tax far above what the English
merchant has to pay for his transport.
Housing Bill—
Third Reading.
THE SENATE
[Mr. McMahon]
Therefore, to say that we are just as
well off in Northern Ireland as they are
in England is not strictly true.
I do not think there are any people in
any part of these islands that are taxed
more than are the people of Northern
Ireland. Indeed, I would say they are
taxed out of face. They have got to pay
the taxes imposed by the Imperial Government, because really we fix no taxes
ourselves, except to increase the tax on
motor lorries, for the reason that we are
a rubber stamp Government. Not only
have we to bear that burden, but we
have also got to shoulder the burden this
Government is imposing on the ratepayers
of Belfast. The Government has refused
to contribute to relief schemes for the
relief of unemployment. A very large
scheme has been abandoned quite recently,
and unless we are prepared to let the
people starve the local authorities will be
asked to shoulder that burden. That
will mean a very big addition to the rates
next year.
It has been suggested that
the remedy is to reduce the allowance
given to the people drawing outdoor
relief. If that happens I do not know
how the people are going to exist. They
are certainly not living at the present
time; they are merely existing. I think
this Government should be a little more
human in its treatment of the people of
Northern Ireland, and especially the
poor people, and not impose any additional burden on them, as the ratepayers
of Belfast will probably have to shoulder
an addition of from 2/6 to 3/6 in the £ on
the rates next year.
Housing Bill.
Order for receiving Report of Amendments read.
Mr. LONG: Before moving that this
Report be now received I should like to
deal with a point raised by the hon. and
learned Senator Leslie during the Committee stage, and to say that loans for
the purpose of making a grant or subsidy
by urban district councils are loans under
the Housing of the Working Classes Act,
1919, and are not, therefore, reckoned
against the total borrowing power of these
bodies. In rural districts such loans are
reckoned against the borrowing power,
but this is not of practical importance,
because there is always an ample margin.
I beg to move, "That this Report be
now received."
Mr. LESLIE: I am obliged to the
Parliamentary Secretary for being so kind
as to look into the point I raised. The
House may remember that the question
really arose in consequence of an Amendment which had been moved to the Clause
in the Bill. I was satisfied at that time
that so far as grants were concernedand grants were the matter specially
brought before the Committee on the
Amendment the position was secure as
regards the urban district councils. I
was not in complete possession of the full
facts which the Parliamentary Secretary
has just been so good as to give us.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Accordingly said Amendments were reported and agreed to by the House.
Moved, "That this Bill be now read
the Third time."-(Mr. Long.)
Colonel SINCLAIR: May I intervene
for a few moments on the Third Reading
stage of this Bill to draw attention to a
recent deliverance of Sir Hilton Young,
the Imperial Minister of Health, which
appears to me to be of importance in
regard to the payment of subsidies by
local authorities and the Government.
With your permission I will read it:
Sir Hilton Young says:-
"It is essential to success in this
attack upon the slums that the subsidy
should not be available for a new house
except on the condition that a slum
house should be pulled down. It was
the absence of this condition, in his
opinion, that was the root cause of
failure to make progress with slum
clearance in the past. The subsidy
could be earned by local authorities for
building new houses without having to
pull down bad ones. Very naturally
they went forward with the comparatively easy task of building the good
new houses, and left untackled the
much harder task of demolishing the
slums against the wishes of many with
rights and interests therein."
I. am
aware that many slum dwellers,
habituated to the unsanitary surroundings, are unwilling to leave them, either
from the convenient proximity to their
work or from general apathy and disinclination to change. Unless some inducement or mild compulsion can be
adopted, it is difficult to see how a rapid
clearance of slum areas can be attained.
No inducement can be so potent as a
ready-made house for occupation at a
rental suited to the means of the occupier.
Housing Bill-
Third Reading
7TH NOVEMBER 1933
May I stress this point, that a reduction in mortality and an amelioration in
morality may be secured by the authorities concerned by applying this Housing
Bill, which we are about to pass, with a
primary view to slum clearance, and regarding the provision in suburban areas
of as many new houses as possible as a
secondary object to the primary one of
slum clearance.
That bears a little upon the question
asked by the hon. and learned Senator
Campbell, supported by hon. Senator
McMahon. I only wanted to draw attention to the point that unless something
is done by the way of compulsion to cause
evacuation of slum houses, I do not see
what progress we are going to make in
what most of us recognise as very important particularly in regard to health and
morality-the clearance of slums.
Far be it from me to discourage the
building of cheap houses for the working
classes in suitable areas, suburban or
otherwise. But the point is, as hon.
Senators opposite have pressed, something
should be done of a more thorough-going
nature for slum clearance. I wished to
draw attention to what the Minister of
Health in England has stated after a
very exhaustive personal survey of the
slum problem in England, particularly in
the north of it, that further advance in
that way lies in making new houses available. If new houses are not available
there is little prospect that slum houses
will be cleared.
Mr. CAMPBELL: The House always
listens with interest to the hon. and
gallant Senator who has just sat down,
because each one of us recognises his very
deep and very constant interest in every
question affecting the welfare of the community. He speaks always with authority
in this House, and I am sure we are
delighted this afternoon with his contribution to the debate. He has touched a
matter of very great importance. Remarks from this side of the House do not
carry so much weight, but I do earnestly
trust that what he has said will carry
due weight in the proper quarter. In
our area we have no element of compulsion on the local councils. We have
no element of encouragement for the
local councils. Failing these two elements
the local authorities will "gang their ain
gate" and the result will be that in future
I fear, as it has been in the past, the
local authorities left to themselves will
make a very poor contribution to the
solution of this long-vexed problem of
the housing of the poorer classes.
Mr. McMAHON: I want to say that
I was deeply interested in the speech
delivered by the hon. and gallant
Senator. He has made some practical
suggestions to which the House should
give special consideration. He refers to
the people who live in the poorer class
districts, because it happens to suit them
and is nearer their work and so forth. I
know from practical experience that that
is so.
He speaks about compulsion, but
I think there would be no necessity for
compulsion if the Government made
proper provision in the Bill that I spoke
about a moment or two ago in regard to
the clearance of slums. There should be
no need for compulsion, because under
the English Act you simply take down.
one side of a street and provide temporary
accommodation for the people while building new houses, and when the houses are
erected the people displaced are allowed
to go into the new ones.
You will never solve this problem of
slums until the Government do something practical as far as finance is concerned. People are reared in a particular
district. They have grown up there. It
is convenient to their work. They know
everyone about the district. You ask
them to go to Andersonstown or the
Woodvale Road, where it is going to cost
them a twopenny tram to travel. I
know one particular family who live in.
the Glen Road district, and who would
not be there if they could yet a house in
the centre of the city. It costs them
something like 30s a week to travel to
the Labour Exchange or to their workthree or four times the cost of the house.
Therefore you must provide houses
within the penny tram stage. There
you will have the difficulty of finding
ground. There is no ground unless you
demolish slums and erect houses in their
place. I do say that unless the Government make up their mind to make
financial provision for slum clearance
you are only tinkering with the housing
problem.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I wish to endorse what Senator Sinclair has said. I
suppose from their own point of view the
Government are trying to tackle the
problem in a proper manner. But it
strikes me this Housing Bill will not get
Electricity (Supply) Bill-
Third Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. McLaughlin]
rid of the slums. I can visualise in our
own town-we have slums there-new
houses will not get rid of the slums. I
put this question to you, what are you
going to do with the old houses when you
erect new ones. Are you going to leave
the old walls standing? Why not give
some authority for clearing away those
houses? Then again we come back to
the question as to how the slum houses
are held. In a great many instances the
trouble is with the head landlords. The
leases are either too short or the fine is
too large for getting a new lease. The
lease is too short for the local bodies to
build, or the renewal fine is too large for
any individual to come in and build new
houses. I respectfully suggest that the
Government should consider very seriously
the question of setting up a commission.
to go into the question of slums generally
in the Six Counties, and also to go into
the question of how these slum premises
are held with regard to leases and everything else in that connection. I respectfully suggest that.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Resolved, "That this Bill do now pass."
-(Mr. Long.)
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill, with
some Amendments, to which they desire
their concurrence.
Electricity (Supply) Bill.
Order for receiving Report of Amendments read.
Resolved, "That this Report be now
received."-(Mr. Long.)
Accordingly said Amendments were
reported and agreed to by the House.
Moved, "That this Bill be now read
the Third time."-(Mr. Long.)
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I am returning
to the question I raised the other day with
the object of appealing again to the Government. Is there to be no protection
for Mr. John Citizen, as he is called,
otherwise the consumer? Is it the reply
of the Government that the consumer if
there is a bona fide dispute as to an
account is to have no redress? Is either
the local manager or the official undertaker of the electricity to be the be-all and
the end-all as far as matters
concerned.
are
I do suggest respectfully to the Government that if an account is furnished to
me as a consumer and I have a bona fide
objection to portion of it, possibly that I
have paid it before, or possibly on the
ground that some apparatus has been put
into my house that I never gave an order
for, or some for which I am charged too
much I suggest to the Government in
cases such as that I should have some redress. I should not be at the mercy of
a particular individual.
If that particular individual refuses to meet my
legitimate point I should have power of
appeal to the Electricity Board or to some
independent Ministry that would give
attention to my case and consider it.
The consumer should have the right before
the light is cut off, of at least appealing
to somebody and asking for some advice
in settling the case before the undertaker
or manager has power to cut off the light.
The consumer has undoubted rights and
should not be at the mercy of any particular individual.
Pos-
We all know perfectly well that we
have individuals who, when they get into
authority, take the reins into their hands
and wield them rather severely. They
may have a personal animosity or personal objection to a particular consumer.
They may go to that consumer and say,
"There is your account, pay it."
sibly there is in that account some item
the consumer is not responsible for.
I
say in a case like that the undertaker or
manager should not have the right to cut
off the supply immediately, but that the
consumer should at least have the privilege
of making his case before some home Department and asking that the light should
not be cut off pending a decision in the
case.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I should like
to join with the hon. Senator in his
remarks about the consumer. After all,
the consumer is the man who pays the
piper. I think there should be some
allowance for accidents of this kind. I
agree that in certain cases officials have
a very hard way of treating certain
people, and there should be some
form of appeal. I was
away on
other business last week and could
not be here. If there is nothing in
the Bill to protect the consumer I suggest
to the Government that there should be
some means provided of keeping him
straight.
Electricity (Supply) Bill—
Third Reading
7TH NOVEMBER 1933
""
Mr. LESLIE: Of course the proceedings this afternoon are the last hope
we have of getting any Amendment in
the Bill. The question before the House
is, That this Bill be now read the Third
time," and it is open to any hon. Member,
without notice, to move a definite Amendment if Mr. Speaker is prepared to accept
it as a proper Amendment. However, on
this particular question of the consumer,
those of us who were here last week will
remember that the hon. Senator (Mr.
McLaughlin) raised the question on the
Committee stage, and he raised it, if I
may say so, very forcibly. He put the
case for the consumer very well. An
answer was given from the Front Government Bench, directing attention to the
expression "neglects to pay," which
appears in the wording of the Bill-"If
any person neglects to pay." It was
pointed out that in the opinion of the
Government and its advisers the expression "neglects to pay" meant something
distinctly definite, and could not be held
to apply to any case in which there was
a bona fide dispute pending.
I think I told the hon. Member I would
try to look into the matter between the
Committee stage and the Third Reading,
and I have been as best I can looking
into the matter. I am perfectly satisfied
in my own mind that the consumer has
ample protection already, and by way of
supporting my opinion I propose to direct
the attention of the House to Section 18
of one of the other Electricity Acts, the
Act of 1909. I thought at one time we
might have incorporated a reference to
that Section in the present Act, but for
reasons I came to the conclusion that it
would possibly be mischievous to do so. I
am going to read the brief Section from
the Act of 1909, one of the Electric Light
(Northern Ireland) Acts, which has to be
read in conjunction with those Acts
mentioned in the Bill before us. Therefore, what appears in the Act of 1909
will have a distinct bearing on anything
that
in this Measure. The partiappears
cular Section 18 reads as follows:-
The undertakers may refuse to supply
electrical energy to any person whose
payments for the supply of electrical
energy are for the time being in arrear
(not being the subject of a bona fide
dispute), whether any such payments.
be due to the undertakers in respect of
a supply to the premises in respect of
which such supply is demanded or in
respect of other premises.
The last part of that Section is a
distinct example of the sting being in the
tail. It is not proposed to apply that to
the case before us which deals with electric
wires or appliances. I do suggest that
the power to refuse to supply electric
energy is again clearly exemplified by the
case, such as that with which the Clause
deals, of cutting off and discontinuing.
I do not think you could have better
evidence of the fact that you are refusing
to supply energy then if you cut it off. It
seems to me that the words "neglects to
pay" are enough protection for any consumer in such cases adumbrated by the
hon. Senator. I think we had just better
leave the Bill alone and not put in any
more which would further discourage the
undertaker. We were reminded on the
last occasion that the object in life of
the undertaker is to supply electricity.
He is not in the business to supply it
for health; he is in it for profit and is
not out to quarrel with his customers
and cause trouble with any shareholder
when the dividend question arises.
think it is really perfectly safe. If a man
has a real subject for discussion-he is
charged too much or is charged with
something he did not order-he has only
got to write to the suppliers and say,
"There is a dispute pending between us,
and if in the face of that the supplier
does disconnect his supply he does it at
I
own peril, and probably exposes
himself to a successful action in the
courts and to risk of heavy damages.
Sir DUDLEY McCORKELL: A great
deal has been said about the rights of the
consumer, and I think every Member
would sympathise with him. I am bound
to confess that I would like to see the
consumer safeguarded up to the hilt. He
is reasonably so at the moment but for
one thing. A great amount may be
made out of "bona fide dispute,'
29 but
nobody who has discussed the matter has
told us who is to decide whether a dispute is bona fide or not.
Mr. McALLISTER: I would ask the
hon. and learned Senator (Mr. Leslie)
who has made a study of the Act of 1909
if he could clarify the position at present by stating under Section 18 of the
Act who is to be the person who is to
determine where a bona fide dispute arises
or by what authority the undertaker is
to be prevented from cutting off the light
pending the decision as to what is a
bona fide dispute?
Electricity (Supply) Bill-
Third Reading
THE SENATE
Mr. LESLIE: I can only speak by
leave of the House.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I am very glad
and thankful to the hon. and learned
Senator for the help he has given me in
this discussion. The only thing I am
concerned about is that where a dispute
arises the light should not be cut off
pending the settlement of that dispute.
The Mayor of Derry has asked who is to
settle that dispute. The Ministry concerned should give the undertaker instructions that pending the settlement of
a bona fide dispute the light is not to be
cut off. That is my point.
no
Mr. LONG: The Ministry has
to intervene in the manner
powers
suggested. May I remind the hon.
Senator that, as I pointed out in my
statement in Committee, neither this Bill,
nor any of the Acts which precede it,
gives an undertaker power to cut off
supplies, where there is a bona fide dispute over an account between him and a
consumer. Such powers are only operative
where the consumer neglects to pay for
current or fittings supplied. The considered view of the Government, as
stated during the Committee stage, is
that the granting of such power as
suggested by Senator McLaughlin is
neither necessary nor desirable.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
would like to refer to Clause 7, which
authorises the undertakers to make bylaws in respect of the nature, material,
workmanship and arrangement of the
wires. I have received a communication
from a large body of electrical representatives and they point out to me that if
there was something inserted in the Bill
requiring the undertakers to make it imperative under the by-laws for registered
electricians and contractors to do the work
it might prevent a great deal of trouble.
There is no doubt about it that electrical
work at present is being tinkered with by
a good number of amateurs. With a
little carelessness and want of knowledge
a great deal of harm can be done in the
way of fusing, fires, and such like. I
believe that those who draw up the bylaws should insert a compulsory Clause
requiring properly registered electricians
and contractors to do the main portion
of the work in connection with electrical
undertakings.
Mr. McMAHON: It is rather amusing
to hear the last speaker wanting to protect electricians, although at the last
sitting of this House he was not prepared
to assist another hon. Senator who moved
an Amendment to protect people in the
erection of houses. That is really class
distinction. Perhaps electricians are a
more respectable body than bricklayers,
carpenters, and so forth. I am really at
a loss to know how he makes a distinction
between electricians and the Building
Trades Federation who wanted protection
for themselves and also for the small
contractor.

"That
Mr. LONG: I beg to move,
this House do adjourn until Thursday

Mr. McMAHON: Will the Deputy

Adjournment--
Unemployment
7TH NOVEMBER 1933
Mr. LESLIE: Before the Parliamentary Secretary answers that question.
perhaps he would say what time
Thursday it is proposed that we should
meet. I do not say anything about the
other question.
on

Mr. McMAHON: Perhaps that is a
Cabinet secret.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not know whether
it is a question for this House. It would
seem to be a matter within the perogative
of the Crown, with which we have nothing
to do. The newspapers of course have
already announced the date. Everyone
knows that this Parliament is moribund,
and that the register is in articulo mortis.
The matter is really not one for the
House. The question is the time we are
to meet.
Mr. LONG: The question is that we
adjourn until Thursday at Five minutes
past Twelve o'clock.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I wish, with your
permission to refer to a matter in respect
of administration; that is in regard to the
figures for unemployment in Northern
Ireland. They are very little changed
for the better. If allowances were made
for the pruning of the registers by the
referees perhaps the figures would be as
bad as any on record. There is no appreciable re-absorption of labour into industry. To-day the spectre of unemployment
is stalking from one end of the Six
Counties to the other. The Government
administer the dole, but apparently
beyond its administration they have no
foresight in regard to a policy of a constructive kind dealing with unemployment.
We have learned during the past
few days that the Sites for Industries
Act of last year is practically a dead
letter. There have only been a paltry
half-dozen or so applications received for
sites for new industries in the Province.
The working classes are directly affected.
by the administration of the dole, and
what hits them reacts on other classes.
The middle classes are impoverished
beyond belief and their purchasing power
is at a low ebb. How can it be otherwise when, according to the answer of the
Deputy Leader of the House, they are
spending upon rates and taxes one-fifth
of their income. It is flowing from gushing fountains on princely salaries,
enormous staffs and grandiose buildings.
The unfortunate taxpayer is groaning
under it all.
Instead of rallying to the cause of
prosperous administration every force
which sincerely seeks the betterment of the
Province the Government has recourse to
appeals to the undisciplined passions of
supporters whom they look down on as
altogether docile and without ideas. We
on this side of the House are as anxious
for the betterment of Northern Ireland
Some
as are hon. Members opposite.
time ago we were told by a Tory chief
that what Ulster wanted was more drums,
not, note you, more looms or keels.
A distinguished scholar lectured lately to
a Belfast audience upon the drum as the
primitive music of pre-historic man.
(Laughter.) That may explain a lot.
are
I do not want to say anything to hurt
anyone, but Members of the Government
downright hard-hitting thumpers
of the drum. They know their cues.
The drums lead to Stormont. I remember one of the characters from Dickenshe was not a very dignified character, I
must say who said that one has really no
idea how the human bees will swarm to
the beating of any old tin kettle. That
is the lesson the Government have acted
upon. One cannot conceive a barer or
more barren record of administration than
that of the Government for the past
twelve years.
As far as the working
classes are concerned the record, except
for a chosen few, is a record of blank
despair.
Mr. McMAHON: I think this House
will agree that the figures published in
this morning's Press of 61,000 men unemployed in Northern Ireland must be very
disquieting. This Parliament is coming
to an end. It is really in its death
throes. When we consider what the Government have done during the past four
or five years we see that they have not
been so very active in regard to the solving of the unemployment problem. Some
ten years ago Lord Craigavon stated that
whatever happened in England he would
solve the uenmployment problem here.
How did he proceed to solve that problem ? I remember a speech which he
delivered shortly after he came back from
Australia in which he told us the solution
of all our difficulties was to make fertile
land out of the swamps of Northern Ireland.
Just imagine a Prime Minister
who is drawing such a large salary coming
back from Australia and telling the
people of Northern Ireland, the people
who live on the land and by the land how
Adjournment-
Unemployment
THE SENATE
JMr. McMahon]
they should deal with their boglands in a
country where the land is the best in the
world.
That is how the unemployment question
was tinkered with by the present Government. That is how it will always be
tinkered with. If we could only change
the Government I believe we could alter
the whole condition of things, but, unfortunately, there is such a division of
opinion amongst the working classes that
they do nothing for themselves. Hence,
it is with regret that I see this Parliament dying out with nothing done for
unemployment. I trust the electors when
they come to face the polls will recognise
who are their true friends.
to
I have
Mr. QUIGLEY:
listened
the hon. and learned Senator
(Mr. Campbell) and the hon. Senator
(Mr. McMahon). One would think
they have not been living in Northern Ireland at all. It is a well-known
fact that the Government here has done
more than any other Government has
done. It is unfair for the hon. and
learned Senator to make such a speech in
regard to the work of the Government.
Both he and Senator McMahon, whom
we are pleased to hear so frequently since
he came into this House, should at least
be fair and considerate. The Government have built some 26,000 houses in
Northern Ireland, and a great many
people have benefited by those houses.
There is one thing I wish to say, and I
have said it before in this House when
dealing with working men's houses the
rents are too high.
Mr. J. CUNNINGHAM: Hear, hear.
Mr. QUIGLEY: The rents are far too
But in the Bill which we had behigh.
fore us to-day Lord Craigavon, who has
been referred to, has seen to it that the
people will get houses at a reasonable
rent, and that the houses when they are
being built will be supervised in such a
way that there will be no complaint to
find in connection with them.
gards the other social services provided
in Belfast, what could be better anywhere? The Government during the
past twelve years has a record of good
work, and I think it is entitled to credit
for it. The speeches we have heard, as
far as I can see, are simply election
speeches. I hope that the hon. and
learned Senator (Mr. Campbell), whom
As re-
I am always pleased to hear, will admit
that the Government has done good
work. In their heart of hearts the hon.
Members know that the Government has
done good work, and is deserving of the
thanks of the people of the Six Counties.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I must congratulate the last speaker upon his
courage,
if not upon his logic. I think it required
superhuman courage, even in a friendly
atmosphere like this, to defend the
administration of the Government of
Northern Ireland for the past twelve
years. He told us that the Government
have built 26,000 houses. Surely that is
not an accurate statement. So far as I
know, the Government have built no
houses except Stormont, and that was
built by the British Government. They
have provided subsidies towards the
building of a number of houses in
Northern Ireland, but now they have
reduced the subsidy and thereby curtailed the building of houses.
I say it is beyond contradiction that
twelve years ago, when the Government
took over control of the administration
of the Six Counties, this country formed
part of the most progressive, most stable,
and most successful agricultural community in Europe. And what do we
find to-day? The farming industry is
practically in bankruptcy. The Government
have contributed by way of loans guarantees something like £9,000,000 to the
shipbuilding industry, which I would not
object to if they had funds to do it, and
contributed proportionately to agriculture. The shipbuilding industry and
trade unions secured these guarantees
through their efficient organisation at
the expense of the farming community,
although the farming community had to
contribute towards that £9,000,000
guaranteed to the shipbuilding industry
in Belfast.
There has been absolutely no effective,
or sufficient, or substantial, or courageous
effort made to grapple with the position
which the farming community in Northern Ireland are in. I think the most
recent proof of the inefficiency of the
Government is in the tremendous chaos
in which we find the pork industry in
Northern Ireland. The farmers were led
to believe, through the agricultural
authorities and the Government, that
their great salvation, the great outlook
for their regeneration was the mass production of pork, and we all know the
Adjournment-
Unemployment
7TH NOVEMBER 1933
tragic and bitter disappointment the
farmers have suffered from the optimism,
ill-founded, illogical, and inefficient, of
the Government in that particular
regard.
It requires super courage for anyone
with the facts before him, if he has eyes
to see what is going on in Northern Ireland, and is in touch with the labourer,
the farmer, and the skilled worker, to
say that the Government of Northern
Ireland have been a successful or efficient administration.
I agree
with
Senator Campbell that we are as anxious
naturally as Members on the other side
of the House, and as Members of the
Government, to see the Six Counties
flourishing It is in our interest as well
as theirs.
We represent one-third of the
people of Northern Ireland. I would be
glad if, instead of condemning the inefficiency of the Government, I could say
they had been efficient and had contributed to the social and material interests,
and the peace and happiness and contentment of the people of Northern
Ireland, instead of, as a responsible
public representative, being called upon to
condemn their inefficiency in the most
bitter way.
we
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I have listened
to Senator Quigley talking about the fairmindedness of this Government, but we
must remember that as far as the minority
in the Six Counties is concerned-I will
not call it Northern Ireland, because we
cannot claim to be Northern Ireland; we
are Six Counties, and the sooner
recognise that fact the better-the
Government have been anything but fair.
Let us take their record for the first
First
twelve years of their existence.
they bring forward their Franchise Act
You
and the redistribution of seats.
have two counties in your six which were
overwhelmingly Catholic counties. What
have the Government done in those two
counties? They have changed the representation on the county councils of those
two counties; they have given the
majority on those county councils over to
the minority; they have disfranchised the
Catholics, who have the majority in those
two counties, so that they have now the
minority in the county councils and have
no control over the spending of money in
connection with those two bodies.
After they have done that they bring
along their Education Act, which they
protest to the world is
a very fair
Measure. But they make sure that the
county councils, to whom they are going
to give over the working of this Education Act, are overwhelmingly Protestant,
and that the Protestants are provided
with splendid schools while we, the
Catholics, who pay our share of the rates
and taxes, see our children deprived of a
proper education. What is the position.
in the country at the present time? Go
round the country and you will find big
regional, and as I say Protestant, schools
built by the ratepayers' money. Alongside them you will find the Catholic
schools, which our people cannot afford to
keep in a proper manner, for they cannot
afford to provide the proper building
facilities. And when we come to this
Government for a grant they give us half,
whereas they should give us the same
treatment and the same conditions as
they give for the Protestant children.
What is the result of this going to be?
Is it going to create content amongst our
Catholic people? At the present time
you are giving what might be called a
super-education in splendid schools to
Protestant children, but you are keeping
the Catholic children in schools that are
not up-to-date. Surely to goodness, if
the Government are fair and wish the
minority to join with them in trying to
make a success of our country, they
should consider the minority to the extent
of giving them fair educational facilities.
I certainly join with the hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Campbell) in saying that
our interests are bound up here with the
Six Counties. We are not anxious to be
complaining always, but we are anxious
for fair play. We are anxious to help in
the working of our Government if we get
the fair play to which we are entitled
and, I do say that the record of this
Government shows that they have not
been fair, particularly to the minority.
Sir DUDLEY MCCORKELL: As I
have no electioneering speech to make I
am not going to make one, but there is
one remark I would like to make. I
always listen with great pleasure to the
hon. Senator (Mr. McAllister) who made
reference to the Pigs Marketing Board.
I agree with much of what he has said.
I agree that farming is in a depressed
condition. That is common knowledge,
and it is not in Northern Ireland alone
that that is the case. But as a member
of the Pigs Marketing Board, I have to
defend it to this extent, that I think the
I

it
hon. Senator would be fairer if he gave

I have just one other remark. It is

was
hardly
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: It
necessary for Senator
McAllister to tell Senator Quigley that he
had courage to get up here and to state
facts. I want to say that Senator
McAllister is more than courageous to
make the statements he has made regarding the want of effort on the part of the
Government. Surely hon. Members in
front of me must understand that this is
not the only place in the British Isles that
is in a depressed state. Everybody
knows that the whole United Kingdom,
indeed the whole world, is in a bad state.
They might, at any rate, take into consideration the fact that Belfast and
Northern Ireland as a whole are not in
any worse condition than any other place,
but as a matter of fact are a great deal
better under the Northern Government.
Take, for instance, our shipbuilding
yards. They are in a better position than
dozens of other shipbuilding yards either
in England or Scotland-a much better
position. There are few shipyards in the
United Kingdom that have more tonnage
under construction at the present time
than our shipyards have. Again, the
linen industry is in a very good position.
No one complains about that. In that
industry they are employing a considerably larger number of workers than they
were some years ago. Again, take
tobacco. Our tobacco factories in Belfast are booming. The position at the
Ropeworks is good. Where is the black
spot? Where is the place where the conditions are so terrible as to be deplored in
this manner by hon. Members in front?
Mr. McMAHON: Sixty thousand
people unemployed.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: Other
places are in the same plight. During
unthe past year the numbers of our
employed have been reduced from 69,000
to 61,000 odd. Surely that is something
to the credit of the Government. The
Government are being blamed for all
these things of which we have heard today, for the Roman Catholic children not
being properly educated, for pigs not
being sold and having to be put into cold
storage, but all that is frivolous compared
with the tremendous amount of work
carried out by the Government throughout the Six Counties. The Government
have done remarkably well in encouraging the placing of orders for ships in Belfast through their Loans Guarantee Acts.
Further, the Government have gone a
considerable length in assisting to provide
for our unemployed. I do not know of
any place where the unemployed have been
assisted more or where local authorities
have been more assisted in providing for
them than they have by the Government
of Northern Ireland. Up to recently
the Government were giving fifty per
cent. of the cost of wages and material
for relief works in order to keep these men
in employment. Belfast certainly has its
unemployed. I am somewhere about
forty years connected with the engineering
trade, and I never yet knew that trade,
even in the very best of times, not to have
some unemployed. Undoubtedly to-day
it is pretty bad, but it is worse in some
other places.
Whether hon. Members have introduced these matters for electioneering
purposes I do not know, but it is not
going to help in any way to say that the
Government have not done some good.
When the Government go to the country
I am perfectly certain that their record
will stand the strictest investigation and
that they will get considerable credit for
what they have done during the past few
years. It has been said that agriculture
is in a deplorable state, but it is much
better here than it is in the other part of
Ireland. The farmers here have the
benefit of derating, and have no rates to
pay worth speaking of, so that they are a
great deal better off here than are their
brethren over the Border. Hon. Members have also introduced the Pigs
Marketing scheme. That scheme is only
in its infancy. Nobody expects it to be
in perfect working order all at once, but
we do expect it eventually to restore to
the farmers a reasonable return for their
pork. There is no use in condemning the
scheme before it is properly tried, but
Adjournment-
Unemployment
7TH NOVEMBER 1933
our
to-day our farmers, along with
industrial workers, are in a much better
position in Northern Ireland than they
are in other parts of this country or of
the United Kingdom.
I
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM :
There is one matter to which I would like
to draw attention. Twelve years ago we
Members of this House coming out here
would have been in danger of our lives.
Houses were being burned.
You may
say that Lisburn was burned out and that
others towns were also burned out.
know that Ballymena was always in a
better position than most other towns.
There the leaders of the different religious
bodies got together, put down their foot
at once, and prevented any rows. When
we are considering the merits of the
Government we should give them credit
for that. Northern Ireland-the Six
Counties, as some hon. Members call it—
twelve years ago was in a state of
anarchy; to-day we are perfectly quiet.
If we look to our neighbours we see that
six years ago they were in a state of
anarchy; to-day they still seem to be
bordering on it. Our Government
deserves credit for turning such a state
into the quietness in which we now live.
There is another matter for which the
Government should not be blamed. Hon.
Members speak about education. It
strikes me that these schools which have
been built by the Government are open to
everybody. As far as I know there is
nothing to prevent any child, whether it
be a Protestant, a Roman Catholic, a
Mohammedan, or a Jew, from going into
those schools.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Are not all schools
open to all children?
am
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
speaking now about Government
schools. They are open to all children.
I really cannot see where, from the point
of view of religion, any harm can be done
in teaching children reading, writing,
mathematics, or science. If they choose
to do so they can go and take their
classes there. But one section chooses to
say "No. We are going to build our
own schools," and go and build their own
schools, and have certain religious forms
that the children must go through in
those schools. The Government schools
do not ask that any forms should be
observed, and it is not fair to say that
the Protestants have those schools to themselves. They have not them to themselves.
We hear
Then there is another thing.
of the injustice done to the teachers. I
was making some inquiries and I found
that a Protestant teacher is educated and
trained for a less sum of money than is
a Roman Catholic teacher. A Roman
Catholic teacher is sent to some religious
body in London and given his training
there, but in fair play to the Protestants,
it must be said that it costs more to do
that than if he were trained in Stranmillis.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Are you quite sure
of that?
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
am perfectly sure. When you hear people
talking about injustice it is time to let
that fact be known. Our Government
have something to their credit. They
have made mistakes like everyone else.
They have made mistakes for which I,
too, could criticise them, but in fairness
it must be admitted that they have done
something.
Mr. McHUGH: I do not like to intervene on this very important subject, but
the last speaker did not speak quite
impartially. He pointed out very clearly
to the House that he had no fault whatever to find, because no fault could be
found, with regard to those particular
Protestant schools which have been built
for Protestants only with a Protestant
atmosphere. (Hon. Members: No, no.)
The word "No" is sounded from these
benches, but if you go into those schools
you will find that every teacher is a
Protestant, and that the very atmosphere
is Protestant. Yet it is suggested that
we should send our children to those
particular schools. Why does the right
hon. Gentleman who has just sat down
(Mr. Samuel Cunningham) not send his
children to the Roman Catholic schools,
where there are Roman Catholic teachers,
if he thinks there is no danger?
One of the most remarkable things in
connection with this talk about educating
the people is that until the Northern
Government came into power and gave
grants for the building of schools, the
Protestants built no schools, while the
Roman Catholics were building year
after year, and spending thousands upon
thousands of pounds in that way. That
Adjournment-
Unemployment
THE SENATE
[Mr. McHugh]
is the difference between the two peoples.
The Protestants did nothing until the
Government built their schools and gave
them every assistance possible. Take the
town of Enniskillen, which I know perfectly well. In that town the Roman
Catholic people spent something like
£22,000 in building schools for their
children, and the Protestants did not
spend one penny until the Government
came to their rescue and handed them
the money. Then they built the most
splendid schools and obtained the services of the best of teachers. That
money was taken out of the taxpayers'
pockets, irrespective of what their
opinions might be. I think that when
the right hon. Gentleman (Mr. Samuel
Cunningham) suggests that we have no
right to complain, I have the right to
ask him why do the Protestants not send
their children to schools in which there
are Roman Catholic teachers? I challenge the right hon. Gentleman to point
out any schools in Northern Ireland
where Protestant children are sent to be
instructed by Roman Catholic teachers,
but they say to us: Why not come in
to our atmosphere and we will destroy
your religion in course of time? (Hon.
Senator: No, no.)
""
Mr. LONG: This simple Motion for
the Adjournment of the House has
apparently given rise to a full-dress Vote
of Censure on the Government. The
hon. and learned Senator who initiated
the discussion (Mr. Campbell) referred
mainly in his speech to the figures for
unemployment, and said that the
Government had done nothing, or next
to nothing, in that regard. I quite
agree that there is a considerable number of people still unemployed that we
should like to see employed. We should
like to see everyone in employment, but
I suggest that the Government have done
everything that they can be reasonably
expected to do.
I should like to enumerate a few of
the things they have done. The unemployed persons on the register have
decreased since the 1st January by over
15,000, and it will be pleasing to every
hon. Senator to know that this decrease
has been steady and gradual. Then the
Government have started 69 relief
schemes and works of public utility.
These are in operation at the moment.
The cost to the Government of those 69
relief schemes and works of public
utility is £780,000. In regard to that
matter, I should like to draw the attention of hon. Senators to the fact that
relief schemes have been discontinued in
Great Britain since the National Government came into office; they have not
been discontinued in Northern Ireland,
and an expenditure of £780,000 has been
incurred by the Northern Government
in that respect.
Another matter I should like to mention is that about ten months ago the
Minister of Labour approved of 180 road
schemes in the city of Belfast, at a total
cost of £288,000. Of that total cost of
£288,000, the Government would have
had to pay in grants £84,000, but the
Corporation of the city of Belfast have
not yet started any of those 180 schemes.
Then again, there were nine programmes
submited by the Belfast Corporation for
distress relief schemes, at a total cost of
£1,129,000; the cost to the Government
of those distress relief schemes amounted
to £589,000. These schemes that I have
enumerated are employing at the present time 4,718 persons.
Another matter I may mention is that
£18,000 per week is being expended by
the Government in unemployment benefit, and £18,500 per week in transitional
payments. Then again, the Government
assist industry through the Loans
Guarantee and Trade Facilities Act.
From the hon. Senators' remarks one
would gather that this is the only country in the world in which this state of
affairs prevails, but it is well known that
this is a world-wide evil, that it has
arisen from world causes, and that no
single Government in the world is able
to provide a cure. I do not complain of
this of Vote of Censure which has been
moved by the Opposition. It has been
said that the business of an Opposition is
to oppose. However, some of their
statements have been characterised by
exaggeration and omission, and I complain of that.
I fearlessly assert that
no Government in the world has done so
much for unemployment, having regard
to their resources, as the Government of
Northern Ireland.
I should just like to make a few remarks
in reply to the speech of Senator
McAllister regarding agriculture. I am
sorry he is not here. Derating has cost
the Government £860,000, and land purchase £280,000, per annum. Therefore
the taxpayer has to pay £1,140,000 per
Adjournment-
7TH NOVEMBER 1933
Black Scab
annum for the benefit of the farmers.
That is a very large sum, indeed, but we
all think it well spent money, and the
farmers are grateful for the assistance
they have had. I submit that the
Government
are entitled, as Senator
Joseph Cunningham has said, to credit
instead of blame for what they have done.
BLACK SCAB IN SOUTH DERRY.
Mr. BARCLAY: Just before you put
the Motion to the House, I should like to
draw attention to a grievance of the farming community of South Derry. I did
not give the Deputy Leader of the House
notice of this question, and therefore I
do not expect an answer, but I take this
opportunity of bringing this very
important matter-it is important to the
farmers to his notice, in order that something may be done. Nine years ago Black
Scab existed in two different parts of
South Derry. One part was a place called
Black Hill, lying between Tobermore and
Draperstown, and the other part was at
Moneymore. There is a distance of about
12 miles between those two places. The
Minister of Agriculture placed a ban
upon this district, which practically takes
in South Derry, 12 miles each way. I
reckon that for the last nine years the
farmers of South Derry have not been
allowed to send their potatoes into the
local markets, nor into the market in the
city of Belfast. Therefore, they are
deprived of the local markets, and they
are deprived of the market in the city of
Belfast. Of course they are permitted to
send to England.
I think that, after the lapse of nine
years, during which they have complied
with the regulations of the Ministry of
Agriculture, it is high time that this ban
was removed.
They have done away
with all the old seed and the old potatoes
that they used to plant years ago, and
they have got new seed, which costs some
of them £1 per cwt. They tell me that
Black Scab has not been found in any
district during the past nine years. The
farmers of South Derry would take it as
an honour, I may say, from the Minister
of Agriculture if he would remove this
ban, and give them an opportunity once
again of sending their potatoes to the
City of Belfast. I raise this question in
the hope that something may be done.
Mr. LONG: I shall be quite pleased to
make representations to the Government
on the subject which Senator Barclay has
raised.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Twenty minutes before
Five o'clock until Thursday next.
Oral Answer
Royal Assent
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 9th November, 1933.
The Senate met at five minutes after
Twelve of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Question to Minister.
INTERROGATION OF REPUBLICAN
PRISONERS.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Joseph
Cunningham:-
To ask the Leader of the House whether
it is a fact that the proceedings at an
interrogation of 34 Republican prisoners
in Belfast Jail on Friday last were published in a Dublin newspaper controlled
by Mr. de Valera while not available to
the Belfast Press, and, if so, whether the
Government will make inquiries as to how
the leakage of information occurred.
Mr. SPEAKER: I believe it to be
the wish of the hon. Member in whose
name the question on the Order Paper
stands that it should be answered through
Hansard.
The following is the answer to the
question:-
I am in-
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
formed that a paragraph purporting to
give the result of an inquiry held on the
1st instant under the provisions of Regulation 22B of the Civil Authorities Act
appeared in the Irish Press-the paper to
which no doubt the hon. Senator referson the 4th instant.
Inquiries have been made in the matter,
but it has not yet been possible to ascertain the source from which the information
was obtained.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons agree to the Amendments
made by the Senate to the Drainage Bill,
the Criminal Justice Bill, the Housing
Bill and the Electricity (Supply) Bill
without any amendment.
Royal Assent.
Mr. LONG: I am authorised by the
Prime Minister, on his behalf, to inform
the Senate that His Grace the Governor
of Northern Ireland, in the name of and
on behalf of His Majesty the King, has
been pleased to give his Assent to the
following Bills:-
Exchequer and Financial Provisions
(No. 2),
Administrative Provisions,
Expiring Laws Continuance,
Constabulary,
Finance,
Sea Fishing Industry,
Drainage,
Criminal Justice,
Housing,
Electricity (Supply),
Hunter's Divorce,
Richards' Divorce,
Cinnamond's Divorce.
Prorogation of Parliament.
Moved, "That the Gentleman Usher of
the Black Rod be commanded to inform
the Commons that the proclamation
proroguing the present Session of Parliament is to be read forthwith, and inviting
the Commons to attend in order to hear
the same."-(Mr. Long.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The Commons, being at the Bar, with
their Speaker, the Proclamation was read
to both Houses of Parliament by the
Speaker of the Senate (in pursuance of
His Grace's Command), as followeth :-
BY HIS GRACE THE GOVERNOR OF
NORTHERN IRELAND.
A PROCLAMATION
For Proroguing the present Parliament
of Northern Ireland.
WHEREAS it is provided by Section
eleven of the Government of Ireland Act,
1920, as modified by and in pursuance of
certain enactments amending the said Act,
that the Governor of Northern Ireland
shall, in His Majesty's name, summon,
prorogue and dissolve the Parliament of
Northern Ireland:
Prorogation
End of Session
9 NOVEMBER 1933
NOW I, JAMES ALBERT EDWARD,
DUKE OF ABERCORN, KNIGHT OF
THE MOST NOBLE ORDER OF THE
GARTER, KNIGHT OF THE MOST
ILLUSTRIOUS ORDER OF SAINT
PATRICK, Governor of Northern Ireland, by this Proclamation do publish
and declare His Majesty's Pleasure that
the said Parliament be, as from the
reading of this Proclamation in the
Parliament of Northern Ireland prorogued to Tuesday, the fifth day of
December, one thousand nine hundred
and thirty-three, to be then holden.
Given this eighth day of November,
one thousand nine hundred and thirtythree.
(Signed by his own hand) ABERCORN.
GOD SAVE THE KING.
End of the Fifth Session (opened
